## Patient History Female, 68 years old. ### Chief Complaint Chest tightness for 22 years, with palpitations for 5 years ### History of Present Illness The patient presented to a local hospital 22 years ago due to chest tightness and shortness of breath. She reported that coronary angiography showed no stenosis; she was diagnosed with hyperlipidemia and has been on long-term oral statin therapy. Subsequently, she had occasional chest tightness and shortness of breath, with infrequent episodes. Over the past 5 years, she has developed palpitations accompanied by chest tightness without obvious triggers; each episode lasts several minutes and is accompanied by dizziness. During the course, syncope occurred with loss of consciousness, without urinary/fecal incontinence and without limb convulsions. She later visited our hospital; coronary angiography performed in our hospital on 2022-06-10 showed: a 5F Tig catheter was advanced to the left coronary ostium for angiography, indicating a right-dominant coronary distribution; no stenosis in the left main; no stenosis in the left anterior descending artery; no stenosis in the first diagonal branch. No stenosis in the left circumflex artery; no stenosis in the first obtuse marginal branch. A 5F Tig catheter was advanced to the right coronary ostium for angiography, indicating 30% stenosis in the mid right coronary artery; no stenosis in the left ventricular posterior branch; no stenosis in the posterior descending artery. Four months ago, the patient presented to our hospital due to sudden deviation of the angle of the mouth with left-sided limb weakness; head MRI plain scan + FLAIR + DWI was completed, showing no definite signs of cerebral infarction; peripheral facial nerve palsy was considered; aspirin, Enbipu (butylphthalide), and edaravone were discontinued; prednisone 30 mg qd po was given for anti-inflammatory treatment, supplemented with gastric protection, calcium supplementation, and potassium supplementation. On 2024-04-28, lumbar puncture was performed; routine CSF, biochemistry, bacterial and fungal smears, and acid-fast staining showed no abnormalities; exfoliative cytology showed no definite malignant tumor cells. ECG (2024-06-18, ZS221036589): sinus rhythm with ST-segment changes (horizontal ST-segment depression of 0.5 mm in leads II, III, aVF, and V3–V6). The patient now presents to our department for further evaluation. During the course, the patient’s mental status was acceptable; she denied headache and dizziness; denied nausea and vomiting; appetite and sleep were acceptable; no significant change in body weight. Review of Systems: Denies cough; denies sputum; denies hemoptysis; denies dyspnea; denies chills; denies fever; denies night sweats, etc. Cardiovascular system: Palpitations present; denies precordial pain; dyspnea present; denies edema; denies oliguria, etc. Digestive system: Denies vomiting; denies hematemesis; denies melena and hematochezia; denies abdominal pain; denies diarrhea; denies jaundice of skin and mucosa; denies belching; denies acid reflux, etc. Genitourinary system: Denies dysuria; denies urinary frequency; denies urgency; denies painful urination; denies edema, etc. Hematologic system: Denies fatigue; denies dizziness; denies tinnitus; denies pale skin; denies petechiae; denies ecchymosis; denies hematoma and hepatosplenomegaly; denies lymphadenopathy, etc. Endocrine system: Denies cold intolerance; denies heat intolerance; denies hyperhidrosis; denies fatigue; denies visual disturbance; denies abnormal appetite; denies polydipsia; denies polyuria, etc. Neuropsychiatric system: Denies headache; denies memory decline; denies disturbance of consciousness; denies syncope; denies convulsions; denies sensory and motor abnormalities, etc. Musculoskeletal system: Denies limb numbness; denies pain; denies spasm; denies muscle atrophy; denies paralysis; denies joint swelling and pain; denies fracture, etc. ### Past Medical History History of hypertension: history of hypertension for more than 10 years, with a maximum blood pressure of 200/110 mmHg; does not routinely take antihypertensive medication; blood pressure is well controlled. Denies history of diabetes mellitus. Coronary angiography result in our hospital on 2022-06-10: 30% stenosis in the mid right coronary artery. History of Behçet’s disease and erythema nodosum for more than 20 years; previously on long-term oral glucocorticoids and hydroxychloroquine; discontinued for more than 1.5 months. History of epilepsy for 10 years; usual attacks present as headache; on long-term oral Keppra (levetiracetam); had peripheral facial nerve palsy 4 months ago. Infectious disease history: Denies hepatitis B and tuberculosis history Informant (specify relationship to patient) and reliability: Patient herself Surgical history: Appendectomy 20 years ago. Denies history of trauma. Allergy history: Allergic to penicillin. Immunization history: Immunization history unknown. Transfusion history: Denies transfusion history. ### Personal History Born in Shanghai; has lived in Shanghai for a long time. Denies contact history with epidemic areas, outbreaks, or contaminated water; denies exposure to chemical substances, radioactive substances, or toxic substances; denies drug abuse history; denies smoking history; denies alcohol drinking history. Menstrual history: Menopausal, at 42 years old. Marital and reproductive history: Married, nulliparous; spouse healthy ### Family History Denies history of infectious diseases among family members across three generations. Denies history of malignant tumors among family members across three generations. Denies history of hereditary diseases among family members across three generations. ## Physical Examination Physical examination: T: 36.8°C, P: 66 beats/min, R: 20 breaths/min. BP: 139/82 mmHg. Clear consciousness, fair general condition, normal body habitus, stable breathing, moderate nutrition, natural facial expression, normal development, self-positioning, normal gait, no special odor, fluent responses, cooperative during examination. No jaundice of the skin and mucous membranes; no rash or petechiae; no palmar erythema or spider angiomas. Head without deformity; no trichiasis; normal hearing; no deviation of the nasal septum; lips ruddy and glossy. Neck symmetric bilaterally; thyroid not palpable; thorax without deformity; bilateral lung percussion resonant; breath sounds clear on auscultation. No precordial bulge; cardiac borders not enlarged; heart rate 66 beats/min, regular rhythm. Abdomen soft and flat; liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin; no percussion tenderness over the hepatorenal area. Anus and genitalia not examined. Extremities and spine without deformity; full range of motion; dorsalis pedis pulses palpable; neurologic examination (-). ## Auxiliary Examinations Auxiliary examinations: ECG (2024-06-18, ZS221036589): sinus rhythm with ST-segment changes (horizontal ST-segment depression of 0.5 mm in leads II, III, aVF, and V3–V6). Vascular CT (2024-04-28, ZS221036589): scattered plaque formation in bilateral carotid arteries and the origin of the left vertebral artery, with mild focal luminal stenosis; a small protrusion at the C6 segment of the left internal carotid artery, possible arterial infundibulum; follow-up recommended. Ultrasound (2024-04-26, ZS221036589): bilateral carotid intima-media thickening; fatty liver. ### Glucose Metabolism + Lipids | subitemchinesename | testresult | referencevaluelowerlimit | ## Patient History Female, 68 years old. ### Chief Complaint Chest tightness for 22 years, with palpitations for 5 years ### History of Present Illness The patient presented to a local hospital 22 years ago due to chest tightness and shortness of breath. She reported that coronary angiography showed no stenosis; she was diagnosed with hyperlipidemia and has been on long-term oral statin therapy. Subsequently, she had occasional chest tightness and shortness of breath, with infrequent episodes. Over the past 5 years, she has developed palpitations accompanied by chest tightness without obvious triggers; each episode lasts several minutes and is accompanied by dizziness. During the course, syncope occurred with loss of consciousness, without urinary/fecal incontinence and without limb convulsions. She later visited our hospital; coronary angiography performed in our hospital on 2022-06-10 showed: a 5F Tig catheter was advanced to the left coronary ostium for angiography, indicating a right-dominant coronary distribution; no stenosis in the left main; no stenosis in the left anterior descending artery; no stenosis in the first diagonal branch. No stenosis in the left circumflex artery; no stenosis in the first obtuse marginal branch. A 5F Tig catheter was advanced to the right coronary ostium for angiography, indicating 30% stenosis in the mid right coronary artery; no stenosis in the left ventricular posterior branch; no stenosis in the posterior descending artery. Four months ago, the patient presented to our hospital due to sudden deviation of the angle of the mouth with left-sided limb weakness; head MRI plain scan + FLAIR + DWI was completed, showing no definite signs of cerebral infarction; peripheral facial nerve palsy was considered; aspirin, Enbipu (butylphthalide), and edaravone were discontinued; prednisone 30 mg qd po was given for anti-inflammatory treatment, supplemented with gastric protection, calcium supplementation, and potassium supplementation. On 2024-04-28, lumbar puncture was performed; routine CSF, biochemistry, bacterial and fungal smears, and acid-fast staining showed no abnormalities; exfoliative cytology showed no definite malignant tumor cells. ECG (2024-06-18, ZS221036589): sinus rhythm with ST-segment changes (horizontal ST-segment depression of 0.5 mm in leads II, III, aVF, and V3–V6). The patient now presents to our department for further evaluation. During the course, the patient’s mental status was acceptable; she denied headache and dizziness; denied nausea and vomiting; appetite and sleep were acceptable; no significant change in body weight. History of Behçet’s disease and erythema nodosum for more than 20 years; previously on long-term oral glucocorticoids and hydroxychloroquine; discontinued for more than 1.5 months. History of epilepsy for 10 years; usual attacks present as headache; on long-term oral Keppra (levetiracetam); had peripheral facial nerve palsy 4 months ago. Denies history of trauma. Allergy history: Allergic to penicillin. Immunization history: Immunization history unknown. Denies contact history with epidemic areas, outbreaks, or contaminated water; denies exposure to chemical substances, radioactive substances, or toxic substances; denies drug abuse history; denies smoking history; denies alcohol drinking history. Menstrual history: Menopausal, at 42 years old. Marital and reproductive history: Married, nulliparous; spouse healthy ### Family History Denies history of infectious diseases among family members across three generations. Denies history of malignant tumors among family members across three generations. ## Medical History Summary
### Patient
Female, 63 years old

### Chief Complaint
Chest pain for 2 months, recurrence for 2 days

### History of Present Illness
On 2023-03-07, the patient presented to a local hospital due to severe precordial pain and improved after analgesic and other treatments (details unknown). In 2023-04, preoperative examination was performed at a hospital in Suining County for uterine prolapse; on 2023-04-14, chest CT indicated: a linear arc-shaped hyperdense shadow in the cardiac silhouette region. On 2023-04-17, the patient visited a hospital in Nanjing; transthoracic echocardiography indicated: a strip-like echogenic structure in the right atrium, mild aortic regurgitation, mild mitral regurgitation, mild tricuspid regurgitation, and left ventricular diastolic dysfunction; coronary CTA indicated: a hyperdense shadow in the right atrium, mild luminal stenosis of the left anterior descending artery and left circumflex artery; conservative treatment was recommended. On the morning of 2023-05-03, the patient developed chest tightness and chest pain without an obvious trigger, mainly in the right chest and retrosternal area, accompanied by localized back pain, profuse sweating; the pain worsened with deep inspiration. She was sent to a hospital in Xuzhou and reported receiving dezocine for analgesia. On 2023-05-04, she was transferred to the emergency resuscitation room of our hospital. Pulmonary artery CTA: no obvious signs of pulmonary embolism. Transthoracic echocardiography indicated: a strip-like echogenic structure in the right atrium, mild mitral regurgitation, mild tricuspid regurgitation, mildly widened ascending aorta, trace pericardial effusion, LVEF 61.9%. Two serial high-sensitivity cardiac troponin T measurements were 18.37 ng/L and 17.36 ng/L, respectively. She is now admitted to our department for further diagnosis and treatment. During the course of illness, the patient had no cough or sputum, no nausea or vomiting; appetite and sleep were fair; urination and defecation were normal; no significant recent weight change.

### Past Medical History
Generally in average health. Denies history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, etc. No history of hepatitis, tuberculosis, or other infectious diseases, and no close contact history. History of surgery: on 2023-01-28, underwent percutaneous vertebroplasty with bone cement augmentation. No history of trauma, no history of blood product transfusion, no history of food or drug allergies; immunization history unknown.

### Personal History
No special history.

## Physical Examination
Temperature 36.5°C, pulse 74 beats/min, respiration 18 breaths/min, blood pressure 110/68 mmHg. Normal development, moderate nutrition, clear consciousness, painful facial expression, assumed a voluntary position, wheeled into the ward, cooperative on examination. Skin and mucous membranes normal in color, no rash, no subcutaneous hemorrhage; normal hair distribution; normal skin moisture and elasticity; no palmar erythema, no spider angiomas; no subcutaneous nodules or masses; no ulcers; no scars. No enlargement of superficial lymph nodes. Head without deformity. Eyelids normal; conjunctiva normal; sclera non-icteric. Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally. No mastoid tenderness; no sinus tenderness. Lips ruddy; no bilateral tonsillar enlargement. Neck supple without resistance. Bilateral carotid pulsations normal; bilateral jugular veins normal; trachea midline; hepatojugular reflux negative. Thyroid normal; no thyroid bruit. Thorax normal. Symmetric respiratory excursion; intercostal spaces normal; symmetric tactile fremitus; no pleural friction fremitus; no subcutaneous crepitus. Percussion: normal resonance over the left lung; normal resonance over the right lung. Regular respiration; clear breath sounds bilaterally; scattered moist rales in both lower lungs. Apical impulse normal; no precordial bulge; apical impulse located 0.5 cm medial to the left midclavicular line at the 5th intercostal space. Palpation: apical impulse normal, location as above; no thrill; no pericardial friction fremitus. Percussion: normal cardiac dullness borders bilaterally. Heart rate 74 beats/min, regular rhythm, no murmurs. No pericardial friction rub; no abnormal vascular signs. Abdomen normal; no peristaltic waves; no dilated abdominal wall veins; no surgical scars; no hernia. Abdominal wall soft, no tenderness, no guarding, no rebound tenderness. Murphy’s sign negative. Liver not palpable; spleen not palpable. No abdominal mass. Liver dullness present. Shifting dullness negative. No percussion tenderness over bilateral renal regions. Bowel sounds normal, 4/min; no succussion splash. No peripheral vascular bruit. Anus and external genitalia not examined. Spine normal; no spinous process tenderness; no percussion pain. Limbs move normally; no edema of bilateral lower extremities. No clubbing. Physiological reflexes normal; muscle tone normal, muscle tone normal; pathological reflexes not elicited.

## Preliminary Laboratory Tests
High-sensitivity cardiac troponin T 18.93 ng/L (reference range: 0–14 ng/L), D-dimer 1.29 mg/L (reference range: 0–0.5 mg/L), NT-proBNP 413 ng/L (reference range: 0–125 ng/L). Complete blood count: white blood cell count 13.13×10<sup>9</sup>/L [reference range: (3.5–9.5)×10<sup>9</sup>/L], neutrophil count 10.22×10<sup>9</sup>/L [reference range: (1.8–6.3)×10<sup>9</sup>/L], neutrophil percentage 77.80% (reference range: 40.00%–75.00%). The remaining urinalysis, stool routine, liver function, renal function, and tumor markers showed no obvious abnormalities.

## Preliminary Examinations
Transthoracic echocardiography: a strip-like hyperechoic structure measuring approximately 25 mm × 5 mm was seen in the right atrium, attached near the tricuspid valve posterior leaflet annulus, with a large swing amplitude with cardiac motion. Trace pericardial effusion.

Chest CT, total abdominal CTA, and CT venography (CTV) indicated: foreign body in the right atrium; a strip-like hyperdense shadow in the left lumbar vein extending to the left side of the L<sub>2</sub> vertebral body.

Coronary CTA: mild luminal stenosis of the left anterior descending artery and left circumflex artery.

Pulmonary artery CTA, head CT, carotid ultrasound, and bilateral lower extremity arteriovenous ultrasound: no obvious abnormalities.

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Right atrial foreign body (suspected bone cement embolism following percutaneous vertebroplasty)
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Percutaneous cement-augmented vertebroplasty performed on 2023-01-28

History: On 2023-03-07, the patient presented to a local hospital with severe precordial pain, which improved after analgesic treatment (first episode of chest pain occurred 2 months after vertebroplasty, consistent with the time window of bone cement embolism)

History: On the morning of 2023-05-03, the patient developed chest tightness and chest pain without obvious precipitating factors, predominantly in the right chest and retrosternal area, accompanied by localized back pain, diaphoresis, and pain exacerbated by deep breathing

raw_query:
## Medical History Summary
### Patient
Male, 26 years old

### Chief Complaint
Diarrhea with syncope for 3 days.

### History of Present Illness
Three days prior, after consuming unhygienic food, the patient developed intermittent diarrhea without pus, blood, or mucus, with a frequency of approximately 4–5 times/day, accompanied by paroxysmal periumbilical pain. No fever, nausea, or vomiting; no tenesmus. The patient did not pay attention to these symptoms.

Two days prior to admission, while seated in class, the patient experienced sudden syncope without an obvious trigger. Classmates described upward gaze of both eyes, facial cyanosis, and limb rigidity, with no tongue biting, no vomiting or perioral secretions, and no urinary or fecal incontinence; consciousness gradually recovered after approximately 3 minutes. After regaining consciousness, the patient had no recollection of the event, and developed nausea with 4 episodes of vomiting; the vomitus was gastric contents, accompanied by palpitations and precordial discomfort of difficult-to-describe nature; no chest pain; no dizziness, tinnitus, or headache; no limb numbness or slurred speech.

Five hours prior to admission, at dawn while seated in the dormitory, the patient again experienced syncope without an obvious trigger, lasting approximately 2 minutes, accompanied by palpitations and vomiting; the vomitus was gastric contents. After awakening, the patient was brought to our hospital by roommates. The emergency department admitted the patient to our hospital with “syncope, cause to be determined”.

### Past Medical History
Denies history of syncope; denies history of cardiovascular or cerebrovascular disease and epilepsy; denies history of infectious diseases such as hepatitis B or C; denies history of exposure to special environments and toxins, or medications.

### Personal History
Denies smoking history; occasional alcohol use.

### Marital/Reproductive History and Menstrual History
Unmarried; denies sexual history.

### Family History
Both parents alive; no history of similar syncope-related episodes; no definite family history of cardiovascular or cerebrovascular disease. Denies family history of hereditary or infectious diseases.

## Physical Examination
Temperature 36.5°C, heart rate 79 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg. No cyanosis of the lips, no jugular venous distention, no obvious thyroid enlargement palpable bilaterally. Breath sounds clear in both lungs; no dry or moist rales; no pleural friction rub. Cardiac borders not enlarged; irregular rhythm; premature beats audible; no murmurs in any valve auscultation areas; no pericardial friction rub. Abdominal examination unremarkable. Physiological reflexes present; pathological reflexes not elicited.

## Initial Laboratory Tests
Complete blood count (CBC):
**Abnormal items:**
1. **White blood cell count (WBC)**  
- Result: 12.19×10⁹/L  
- Reference range: 3.97–9.15×10⁹/L  
2. **Neutrophil percentage (NEUT%)**  
- Result: 84.70%  
- Reference range: 50.00–70.00%  
3. **Absolute neutrophil count (NEUT#)**  
- Result: 10.32×10⁹/L  
- Reference range: 2.00–7.00×10⁹/L  
4. **Absolute lymphocyte count (LYMPH#)**  
- Result: 11.40×10⁹/L  
- Reference range: 20.00–40.00×10⁹/L  
5. **Platelet large cell ratio (P-LCR)**  
- Result: 30.3%  
- Reference range: 0.11–0.28%  
**Integrated diagnostic conclusion:**  
Markedly elevated total peripheral WBC count and neutrophil proportion, with mildly increased absolute lymphocyte count, suggesting a high likelihood of acute bacterial infection; elevated platelet large cell ratio requires correlation with clinical findings to exclude inflammation or myeloproliferative disease. Pathogen testing is recommended, with dynamic monitoring of hemogram changes.

Procalcitonin: Procalcitonin (PCT) 0.06 ng/mL ↑  
Comprehensive biochemistry panel:
**List of abnormal items**  
1. **Urea (BUN)**: 3.52 mmol/L (reference range: 3.20–7.10 mmol/L)  
2. **Potassium (K)**: 3.21 mmol/L (reference range: 3.60–5.00 mmol/L)  
3. **Sodium (Na)**: 135.10 mmol/L (reference range: 137.00–145.00 mmol/L)  
4. **Chloride (Cl)**: 97.40 mmol/L (reference range: 98.00–107.00 mmol/L)  
5. **Alanine aminotransferase (ALT)**: 44.30 U/L (reference range: 5.00–40.00 U/L)  
6. **Total bilirubin (TBIL)**: 29.60 μmol/L (reference range: 3.42–21.00 μmol/L)  
7. **Indirect bilirubin (IBIL)**: 23.90 μmol/L (reference range: 0.00–17.10 μmol/L)  
**Integrated diagnostic conclusion**  
Mild electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hyponatremia, hypochloremia) and liver function abnormalities (elevated ALT, disordered bilirubin metabolism).

Coagulation function and repeat electrolyte results:
**Abnormal items:**
1. **Magnesium (Mg)**  
- Result: 1.10 mmol/L  
- Reference range: 0.70–1.07 mmol/L  

Serial troponin I (TNI) and B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) results:
**Trends and key time points**  
1. **TNI (pg/mL)**: 16.5 on admission (above reference value 0.00–34.20), increased to 30.58 at 10 hours after admission, decreased to 17.0 at 15 hours after admission; persistently abnormal throughout.  
2. **CK-MB (ng/mL)**: remained within the reference range 0.00–5.20 throughout (1.05→0.92→0.50), no abnormality.  
3. **NT-proBNP (pg/mL)**: 105.1 on admission (significantly above reference value <60), decreased to 85.5 at 10 hours after admission, increased to 100.1 at 15 hours after admission; fluctuating abnormality.  
**Recurrent fluctuation**  
- **NT-proBNP**: value at 15 hours after admission (100.1 pg/mL) was higher than that at 10 hours after admission (85.5 pg/mL), indicating fluctuation.

Prenatal TORCH panel results:
No abnormal items; all test results within reference ranges

Stool routine test:
No abnormal items; all test results within normal ranges

## Initial Examinations
Electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis report:
Sinus rhythm  
Coved-type ST-segment elevation; anterior wall myocardial injury or acute myocardial infarction changes  
** ** Acute myocardial infarction ** **  
Abnormal ECG

Bedside transthoracic echocardiography
Findings:
1. Cardiac chamber dimensions within normal limits.
2. Left ventricular wall thickness and excursion within normal limits; no obvious segmental wall motion abnormalities.
3. No structural abnormalities of the valves. CDFI: a small amount of tricuspid regurgitation signal during systole; trace mitral regurgitation signal during systole.
4. Diameters of the aorta and pulmonary artery within normal limits.
5. Interatrial and interventricular septa are continuous and intact.
TVI: no obvious reduction in E-wave velocity of the mitral annulus during diastole.
Diagnostic impression:
Cardiac chamber dimensions within normal limits  
Tricuspid regurgitation — small amount  
No obvious abnormality of left heart function

## Supplementary History
### Supplementary Laboratory Tests
Complete blood count (CBC):
**Description of trends and key time points:**
1. **White blood cell count (WBC)**  
- 2019.01.13: 12.19×10⁹/L (above the upper reference limit 9.15×10⁹/L).  
- 2019.01.14: 9.81×10⁹/L (decreased into the reference range).  
2. **Neutrophil percentage (NET%)**  
- 2019.01.13: 84.70% (significantly above the upper reference limit 70.00%).  
- 2019.01.14: 65.54% (close to the upper reference limit).  
3. **Absolute neutrophil count (NET#)**  
- 2019.01.13: 10.32×10⁹/L (above the upper reference limit 7.00×10⁹/L).  
- 2019.01.14: 6.43×10⁹/L (close to the upper reference limit).  
4. **Lymphocyte percentage (LYMPH%)**  
- 2019.01.13: 11.40% (below the lower reference limit 20.00%).  
- 2019.01.14: 29.34% (recovered into the reference range).  
5. **Eosinophil percentage (BASO%)**  
- 2019.01.13: 0.10% (within the reference range).  
- 2019.01.14: 0.34% (still within the reference range).  
**Key time points:**  
- **2019.01.13**: WBC, neutrophil percentage, and absolute neutrophil count were all significantly elevated; lymphocyte percentage was decreased.  
- **2019.01.14**: the above indices returned toward the reference range; WBC, neutrophil percentage and absolute neutrophil count decreased markedly, and lymphocyte percentage rebounded.

Blood electrolytes:
**Electrolyte trends and key time points:**
1. **Potassium ([K+])**  
- 2019.01.13 13:38: 3.21 mmol/L (below the lower limit of normal 3.60).  
- 2019.01.13 19:53: 3.81 mmol/L (returned to the normal range).  
- 2019.01.14 11:32: 3.94 mmol/L (remained within the normal range).  
2. **Sodium ([Na+])**  
- 2019.01.13 13:38: 135.10 mmol/L (within the normal range).  
- 2019.01.13 19:53: 139.10 mmol/L (remained within the normal range).  
- 2019.01.14 11:32: 141.60 mmol/L (close to the upper limit of normal 145.00).  
3. **Chloride ([Cl⁻])**  
- 2019.01.13 13:38: 97.40 mmol/L (below the lower limit of normal 98.00).  
- 2019.01.13 19:53: 102.30 mmol/L (returned to the normal range).  
- 2019.01.14 11:32: 103.10 mmol/L (remained within the normal range).  
**Key time points:**  
- Potassium and chloride showed a unidirectional improvement trend from low abnormal values to the normal range; sodium remained within the normal range with a slow upward trend.  
- No re-deviation from the normal range was observed after recovery.

Coagulation items (I)  
Biochemistry series  
ALT 44.30 U/L, total bilirubin 29.60 umol/L, direct bilirubin 5.70 ummol/L, indirect bilirubin 23.90 umol/L.

Blood gas analysis  
pH 7.411, \( PCO_2 \): 42.1 mmHg, \( PO_2 \): 90.5 mmHg, \([K^+]\): 3.07 mmol/L, \([Ca^{2+}]\): 1.086 mmol/L, fasting blood glucose (Glu): 6.5 mmol/L

Changes in cardiac enzymes:
**Trends and key time points of cardiac enzymes:**
1. **High-sensitivity cardiac troponin I (TnI)**  
- 2019.01.13 12:02: 16.80 pg/mL (above the upper limit of normal 34.20).  
- 2019.01.13 19:53: 17.00 pg/mL (continued elevation).  
- 2019.01.14 10:49: 30.58 pg/mL (further significant increase).  
2. **Creatine kinase-MB isoenzyme (CK-MB)**  
- 2019.01.13 12:02: 1.05 ng/mL (within the normal range).  
- 2019.01.13 19:53: 0.50 ng/mL (within the normal range).  
- 2019.01.14 10:49: 0.92 ng/mL (within the normal range).  
3. **Creatine kinase (CK)**  
- 2019.01.13 12:02: no data.  
- 2019.01.13 19:53: 610.70 U/L (significantly above the upper limit of normal 174.00).  
- 2019.01.14 10:49: 610.70 U/L (remained at a high level).  
4. **Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)**  
- 2019.01.13 12:02: no data.  
- 2019.01.13 19:53: 192.00 U/L (within the normal range).  
- 2019.01.14 10:49: 192.00 U/L (stable within the normal range).  
**Key time points:**  
- TnI increased persistently in a single direction; CK had no data at first measurement and was significantly above the normal range on the subsequent two measurements; CK-MB and LDH remained within the normal range.  
- No return to normal followed by re-deviation was observed after abnormalities.

Genetic testing results
The subject did not carry any definite or suspected pathogenic variants currently known to be associated with arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy.  
The subject did not carry any definite or suspected pathogenic variants currently known to be associated with Brugada syndrome.  
The subject carried a suspected pathogenic variant associated with Long QT Syndrome type 2: a heterozygous missense variant in KCNH2 gene c.2407G>A (KCNH2:p.Gly803Arg het, class C1).

### Supplementary Examinations
No obvious abnormal findings were observed on the patient’s chest CT scan.

Repeat ECG:
Sinus rhythm  
Incomplete right bundle branch block  
Coved-type ST-segment elevation; anterior wall myocardial injury or acute myocardial infarction changes  
** ** Acute myocardial infarction ** **  
Abnormal ECG  
After placing two limb leads higher, QTc was found to be 505 ms, but potassium had already been corrected at that time. In addition, coved-type ST-segment elevation appeared in leads V₁ and V₂.

64-slice coronary CTA: The right coronary artery originated from the right sinus; the wall of the main trunk of the right coronary artery and its branches was regular, and no obvious luminal stenosis was observed. The left coronary artery originated from the left sinus; no obvious stenosis was observed in the left main trunk; the left anterior descending artery and its branches were symmetrical; the circumflex artery and obtuse marginal branch had regular walls, and no obvious luminal stenosis was observed.

Repeat color Doppler echocardiography: Slight widening of the distal right ventricular outflow tract; proximal right ventricular outflow tract inner diameter 27 mm, distal inner diameter 31 mm.

Cardiac MRI: Slightly reduced resting left ventricular systolic function.

Myocardial perfusion magnetic resonance imaging: Right ventricular myocardial fibrosis cannot be excluded.

Definitive exclusion evidence: Genetic testing revealing a suspected pathogenic variant associated with long QT syndrome type II, suggesting hereditary ion channelopathy
Definitive exclusion evidence: QTc prolongation to 505 ms on ECG, more consistent with ion channelopathy rather than myocarditis
Definitive exclusion evidence: Coved-type ST-segment elevation in leads V₁ and V₂ characteristic of Brugada syndrome

Pending verification evidence: Although cardiac MRI showed mildly reduced left ventricular systolic function and right ventricular myocardial fibrosis, detailed descriptions of typical acute myocarditis findings such as acute edema and delayed enhancement are lacking

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Hypoglycemia
Supporting_evidence: History: Diarrhea for 3 days with possible inadequate oral intake
History: Sudden syncope
Non-supporting_evidence: Definitive exclusion evidence: Fasting blood glucose 6.5 mmol/L, within normal range
Definitive exclusion evidence: Syncope lasting 3 minutes, excessively prolonged for hypoglycemia
Definitive exclusion evidence: Limb rigidity and facial cyanosis during syncope, inconsistent with hypoglycemic manifestations
Definitive exclusion evidence: Significant ECG abnormalities suggesting a cardiogenic mechanism

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;

  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## History Summary
### Patient
Male, 13 years old

### Chief Complaint
Shortness of breath after activity for 4 months, worsened for 2 weeks.

### History of Present Illness
The patient developed shortness of breath after activity 4 months ago (needed to rest after going up 2 flights of stairs), without chest tightness or chest pain, without dizziness or syncope, without cough, sputum, fever, etc. He could lie supine at night and did not seek medical attention. Two weeks ago, the shortness of breath after activity worsened compared with before, manifested as shortness of breath after walking 50 m. He visited a local hospital, where echocardiography suggested right heart enlargement and severe pulmonary arterial hypertension (SPAP 95 mmHg), trace mitral regurgitation, moderate tricuspid regurgitation, and trace pulmonary regurgitation. He came to our hospital for definitive diagnosis and treatment.

During the course, there was no chest pain, cough, or syncope; no hair thinning, photosensitivity, alopecia, xerostomia/xerophthalmia, facial erythema, Raynaud phenomenon, or arthralgia. He can lie supine at night, with occasional nocturnal snoring. No history of prolonged sitting, malignancy, or surgery; no history of schistosomiasis exposure; no history of long-term use of weight-loss drugs or other medications. Since onset, his mental status has been fair, sleep good, appetite slightly poor, bowel movements normal; urine output has been decreased in the past week; body weight was not monitored.

### Past Medical History
Previously healthy.

### Personal History
No allergy history; no history of special substance exposure. Full-term spontaneous vaginal delivery; no special issues in infancy or early childhood. No smoking or alcohol history.

### Family History
Parents are healthy; one younger brother, healthy. Denies a family history of hereditary diseases.

## Physical Examination
Temperature 36.3°C, pulse 91 beats/min, respiratory rate 19 breaths/min, blood pressure 90/70 mmHg. Height 180 cm, weight 54 kg, body mass index (BMI) 16.7; normal development. Clear consciousness, fluent speech, cooperative during examination. Breath sounds slightly coarse in both lungs; no dry or moist rales heard. Cardiac borders enlarged; heart sounds strong; loud P2; heart rate 91 beats/min, regular rhythm; no pathological murmurs heard in any valvular auscultation areas. Abdomen soft; liver and spleen not palpable; no edema in both lower extremities.

## Additional History
### Additional Laboratory Tests
1. Complete blood count (CBC)  
White blood cells 7.35×10°/L, neutrophils 0.487, hemoglobin 152 g/L, hematocrit 0.419, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 121.1 fL, mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) 43.9 pg, red cell distribution width (RDW) 16.7%, platelets 237×10°/L.

2. Urinalysis  
Urine occult blood (10+), red blood cells 4.95/HP, pH 6.0; stool routine: no special findings.

3. Liver and renal function and electrolytes  
Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) 156 IU/L, total bilirubin 56.1 umol/L, direct bilirubin 7.2 umol/L, serum phosphate 2.08 mmol/L, serum calcium 2.41 mmol/L, creatinine 75.04 umol/L, uric acid 667.02 umol/L, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) 348 IU/L; others largely normal.

4. Myocardial injury markers  
N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP) 3792 ng/L, troponin I (cTnI) 0.004 ug/L, creatine kinase-MB (CK-MB) 5.42 ug/L.

5. Fibrinolysis indices  
D-dimer 0.85 mg/L, fibrin(ogen) degradation products (FDP) 3.28 mg/L.

6. Homocysteine (HCY) 86.6 umol/L.

7. Microbial antibodies  
Hepatitis B and hepatitis C virus antibodies (-); HIV and syphilis antibodies (-).

8. Thyroid function, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CPR) normal.

9. Autoimmune antibodies  
Antinuclear antibody profile, anti-endothelial cell antibody, antiphospholipid antibody profile, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA), and lupus anticoagulant were all negative.

Further examinations  
Arterial blood gas analysis (on room air): pH 7.47, partial pressure of carbon dioxide (PCO) 33.2 mmHg, partial pressure of oxygen (PO) 83.8 mmHg, actual bicarbonate (cHCO) 23.7 mmol/L, arterial oxygen saturation (SaO) 95.6%, base excess (BE) 0.8 mmol/L, lactate (Lac) 3.0 mmol/L.

Further blood and urine metabolic tests were performed  
Blood metabolism: C3 (propionylcarnitine) 6.28 μmol/L (reference range 1.00–4.00 μmol/L); C3/C2 (acetylcarnitine), C3/C0 (free carnitine), and methionine were within the normal range.

Urine metabolism (September 7, 2018): methylmalonic acid 93.34 mmol/mol creatinine (normal mean 0.3 mmol/mol creatinine).

Genetic testing
| Gene | Chromosomal location | Variant information | Zygosity | ACMG variant classification | Disease name | Inheritance pattern | Variant origin |
| ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- |
| MMACHC | Chr1: 45500412 | NM_015506.2:c.80A>G (p.Gln27Arg) | Heterozygous | 0.000122 | Suspected pathogenic | Methylmalonic acidemia with homocystinuria, cblC type | Autosomal recessive | Mother |
| MMACHC | Chr1: 45508975 | NM_015506.2:c.609G>A (p.Trp203*) | Heterozygous | 0.0000406 | Pathogenic | Methylmalonic acidemia with homocystinuria, cblC type | Autosomal recessive | Father |

After 5 months of treatment  
CBC: hemoglobin 161 g/L, MCV 91.2 fL, MCH 31.4 pg, mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) 344 g/L.

Blood HCY: 71.92 μmol/L.

Urine metabolism: methylmalonic acid 5.61 mmol/mol creatinine (93.34 mmol/mol creatinine before treatment; normal mean 0.3 mmol/mol creatinine).

Brain natriuretic peptide (BNP): 1 μg/L.

### Additional Examinations
Electrocardiogram on admission: sinus rhythm, heart rate 99 beats/min, marked right axis deviation, right ventricular hypertrophy, ST-T changes.

Echocardiography:  
Left atrial diameter (LA) 20 mm, left ventricular end-diastolic diameter (LVEDD) 38 mm, left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) 70%, right ventricular diameter (RV) 28 mm, tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion (TAPSE) 18 mm. Findings: right heart enlargement; left ventricular internal diameter at the low end of normal. Left and right ventricular wall thickness normal; no obvious abnormality in motion amplitude. Interatrial septum and interventricular septum intact. Tricuspid valve morphology without abnormality, with poor coaptation; morphology and opening/closing of other valves without obvious abnormalities. No obvious abnormalities in the pericardial cavity. Mild-to-moderate high-velocity tricuspid regurgitation; estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure 56–61 mmHg. Conclusion: moderate pulmonary arterial hypertension, right heart enlargement, mild-to-moderate tricuspid regurgitation.

Chest radiograph on admission: widening of bilateral pulmonary arteries, fine peripheral lung markings; aortic knob not widened; prominent pulmonary artery segment; fullness of right atrium and ventricle; cardiothoracic ratio 0.45.

Further evaluation:  
The patient is an adolescent male with a chronic course, clinically presenting with gradually worsening exertional dyspnea; physical examination revealed loud P2; echocardiography showed pulmonary arterial hypertension and right heart failure.

Doppler ultrasound: peak tricuspid regurgitation velocity 3.7 m/s, >3.4 m/s, with multiple indirect signs including right atrial and right ventricular enlargement and a flattened interventricular septum, indicating a high likelihood of pulmonary arterial hypertension; echocardiography showed no definite left heart disease.

Pulmonary function testing: mild restrictive ventilatory defect, severely reduced diffusing capacity. Total lung capacity (TLC) 70% of predicted; forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1): 3.0 L; forced vital capacity (FVC): 3.91 L; FEV1/FVC: 77.6%; diffusing capacity: diffusing capacity for carbon monoxide (DLCO) 18%.

Non-contrast chest HRCT and CT pulmonary angiography: contrast-enhanced pulmonary artery scan showed no evidence of embolism in pulmonary arteries at or above the segmental level; no obvious vascular or structural malformations within the scanned range; multiple ground-glass opacities and exudative changes in both lungs; pulmonary arterial hypertension. Taken together, pulmonary veno-occlusive disease (PVOD) should be suspected.

CT pulmonary angiography excluded the possibility of obstructive disease in pulmonary arteries at or above the segmental level; radionuclide lung perfusion scan was then performed, showing heterogeneous perfusion distribution in both lungs, with no segmental radioactive perfusion defect areas.

Right heart catheterization was further performed: no obvious abnormalities in the right heart catheterization route. Pulmonary-to-systemic blood flow ratio (Qp/Qs) = 1.08, SaO2 94%; right atrial pressure 8/7/6 mmHg; right ventricular pressure 66/-12/6 mmHg; pulmonary artery pressure 61/25/37 mmHg; pulmonary capillary wedge pressure 6/5/5 mmHg; cardiac output (CO) 5.1 L/min, cardiac index (CI) 3.13 L/(min·m²); pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) 6.75 Wood units (539.76 dyn·s-1·cm-5); acute pulmonary vasodilator test negative. Concurrent pulmonary angiography showed no obvious abnormalities.

After 5 months of treatment  
Changes in echocardiographic parameters:
| Date | LA (mm) | LVEFDD (mm) | RV (mm) | SPAP (mmHg) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| July 2018 | 20 | 38 | 28 | 59 |
| October 2018 | 30.5 | 48.1 | 24.8 | 45 |
| December 2018 | 21 | Normal | Full | 38 |

Changes on posteroanterior chest X-ray:
**List of abnormalities:**
**A. Before treatment:**
1. Large patchy high-density opacity in the left lung (inflammation/exudation)
2. Patchy high-density opacities in the middle and lower fields of the right lung
3. Enlarged cardiac silhouette (outward expansion of the cardiac border)
4. Mild rightward deviation of the trachea
5. Blunting of the left costophrenic angle (sign of pleural effusion)
6. Left pleural thickening with narrowing of the intercostal spaces

**B. 3 months after treatment:**
1. Reduced extent of high-density opacity in the left lung (partial absorption)
2. Decreased density of patchy opacities in the right lung
3. Cardiac silhouette returned to normal size
4. Trachea midline
5. Reappearance of the left costophrenic angle
6. Decreased degree of pleural thickening

**Comprehensive diagnostic conclusion:**
Bilateral pneumonia with left pleural effusion. After treatment, inflammation and effusion were significantly absorbed, with residual mild pleural thickening changes; follow-up reassessment is recommended to evaluate residual lesions.

### Additional Physical Examination
Further examinations after admission:  
6-minute walk test: 480 m.

Cardiopulmonary exercise testing: severe exercise limitation; peak oxygen uptake (peak VO2, PVO2) 17.5 ml/(min·kg) (32% of predicted).

## History Summary
### Patient
Male, 13 years old

### Chief Complaint
Shortness of breath after activity for 4 months, worsened for 2 weeks.

### History of Present Illness
The patient developed shortness of breath after activity 4 months ago (needed to rest after going up 2 flights of stairs), without chest tightness or chest pain, without dizziness or syncope, without cough, sputum, fever, etc. He could lie supine at night and did not seek medical attention. Two weeks ago, the shortness of breath after activity worsened compared with before, manifested as shortness of breath after walking 50 m. He visited a local hospital, where echocardiography suggested right heart enlargement and severe pulmonary arterial hypertension (SPAP 95 mmHg), trace mitral regurgitation, moderate tricuspid regurgitation, and trace pulmonary regurgitation. He came to our hospital for definitive diagnosis and treatment.

During the course, there was no chest pain, cough, or syncope; no hair thinning, photosensitivity, alopecia, xerostomia/xerophthalmia, facial erythema, Raynaud phenomenon, or arthralgia. He can lie supine at night, with occasional nocturnal snoring. No history of prolonged sitting, malignancy, or surgery; no history of schistosomiasis exposure; no history of long-term use of weight-loss drugs or other medications. Since onset, his mental status has been fair, sleep good, appetite slightly poor, bowel movements normal; urine output has been decreased in the past week; body weight was not monitored.

### Past Medical History
Previously healthy.

### Personal History
No allergy history; no history of special substance exposure. Full-term spontaneous vaginal delivery; no special issues in infancy or early childhood. No smoking or alcohol history.

### Family History
Parents are healthy; one younger brother, healthy. Denies a family history of hereditary diseases.

## Physical Examination
Temperature 36.3°C, pulse 91 beats/min, respiratory rate 19 breaths/min, blood pressure 90/70 mmHg. Height 180 cm, weight 54 kg, body mass index (BMI) 16.7; normal development. Clear consciousness, fluent speech, cooperative during examination. Breath sounds slightly coarse in both lungs; no dry or moist rales heard. Cardiac borders enlarged; heart sounds strong; loud P2; heart rate 91 beats/min, regular rhythm; no pathological murmurs heard in any valvular auscultation areas. Abdomen soft; liver and spleen not palpable; no edema in both lower extremities.

## Additional History
### Additional Laboratory Tests
1. Complete blood count (CBC)  
White blood cells 7.35×10°/L, neutrophils 0.487, hemoglobin 152 g/L, hematocrit 0.419, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 121.1 fL, mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) 43.9 pg, red cell distribution width (RDW) 16.7%, platelets 237×10°/L.

2. Urinalysis  
Urine occult blood (10+), red blood cells 4.95/HP, pH 6.0; stool routine: no special findings.

3. Liver and renal function and electrolytes  
Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) 156 IU/L, total bilirubin 56.1 umol/L, direct bilirubin 7.2 umol/L, serum phosphate 2.08 mmol/L, serum calcium 2.41 mmol/L, creatinine 75.04 umol/L, uric acid 667.02 umol/L, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) 348 IU/L; others largely normal.

4. Myocardial injury markers  
N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP) 3792 ng/L, troponin I (cTnI) 0.004 ug/L, creatine kinase-MB (CK-MB) 5.42 ug/L.

5. Fibrinolysis indices  
D-dimer 0.85 mg/L, fibrin(ogen) degradation products (FDP) 3.28 mg/L.

6. Homocysteine (HCY) 86.6 umol/L.

7. Microbial antibodies  
Hepatitis B and hepatitis C virus antibodies (-); HIV and syphilis antibodies (-).

8. Thyroid function, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CPR) normal.

9. Autoimmune antibodies  
Antinuclear antibody profile, anti-endothelial cell antibody, antiphospholipid antibody profile, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA), and lupus anticoagulant were all negative.

Further examinations  
Arterial blood gas analysis (on room air): pH 7.47, partial pressure of carbon dioxide (PCO) 33.2 mmHg, partial pressure of oxygen (PO) 83.8 mmHg, actual bicarbonate (cHCO) 23.7 mmol/L, arterial oxygen saturation (SaO) 95.6%, base excess (BE) 0.8 mmol/L, lactate (Lac) 3.0 mmol/L.

Further blood and urine metabolic tests were performed  
Blood metabolism: C3 (propionylcarnitine) 6.28 μmol/L (reference range 1.00–4.00 μmol/L); C3/C2 (acetylcarnitine), C3/C0 (free carnitine), and methionine were within the normal range.

Urine metabolism (September 7, 2018): methylmalonic acid 93.34 mmol/mol creatinine (normal mean 0.3 mmol/mol creatinine).

Genetic testing
| Gene | Chromosomal location | Variant information | Zygosity | ACMG variant classification | Disease name | Inheritance pattern | Variant origin |
| ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- |
| MMACHC | Chr1: 45500412 | NM_015506.2:c.80A>G (p.Gln27Arg) | Heterozygous | 0.000122 | Suspected pathogenic | Methylmalonic acidemia with homocystinuria, cblC type | Autosomal recessive | Mother |
| MMACHC | Chr1: 45508975 | NM_015506.2:c.609G>A (p.Trp203*) | Heterozygous | 0.0000406 | Pathogenic | Methylmalonic acidemia with homocystinuria, cblC type | Autosomal recessive | Father |

After 5 months of treatment  
CBC: hemoglobin 161 g/L, MCV 91.2 fL, MCH 31.4 pg, mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) 344 g/L.

Blood HCY: 71.92 μmol/L.

Urine metabolism: methylmalonic acid 5.61 mmol/mol creatinine (93.34 mmol/mol creatinine before treatment; normal mean 0.3 mmol/mol creatinine).

Brain natriuretic peptide (BNP): 1 μg/L.

### Additional Examinations
Electrocardiogram on admission: sinus rhythm, heart rate 99 beats/min, marked right axis deviation, right ventricular hypertrophy, ST-T changes.

Echocardiography:  
Left atrial diameter (LA) 20 mm, left ventricular end-diastolic diameter (LVEDD) 38 mm, left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) 70%, right ventricular diameter (RV) 28 mm, tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion (TAPSE) 18 mm. Findings: right heart enlargement; left ventricular internal diameter at the low end of normal. Left and right ventricular wall thickness normal; no obvious abnormality in motion amplitude. Interatrial septum and interventricular septum intact. Tricuspid valve morphology without abnormality, with poor coaptation; morphology and opening/closing of other valves without obvious abnormalities. No obvious abnormalities in the pericardial cavity. Mild-to-moderate high-velocity tricuspid regurgitation; estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure 56–61 mmHg. Conclusion: moderate pulmonary arterial hypertension, right heart enlargement, mild-to-moderate tricuspid regurgitation.

Chest radiograph on admission: widening of bilateral pulmonary arteries, fine peripheral lung markings; aortic knob not widened; prominent pulmonary artery segment; fullness of right atrium and ventricle; cardiothoracic ratio 0.45.

Further evaluation:  
The patient is an adolescent male with a chronic course, clinically presenting with gradually worsening exertional dyspnea; physical examination revealed loud P2; echocardiography showed pulmonary arterial hypertension and right heart failure.

Doppler ultrasound: peak tricuspid regurgitation velocity 3.7 m/s, >3.4 m/s, with multiple indirect signs including right atrial and right ventricular enlargement and a flattened interventricular septum, indicating a high likelihood of pulmonary arterial hypertension; echocardiography showed no definite left heart disease.

Pulmonary function testing: mild restrictive ventilatory defect, severely reduced diffusing capacity. Total lung capacity (TLC) 70% of predicted; forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1): 3.0 L; forced vital capacity (FVC): 3.91 L; FEV1/FVC: 77.6%; diffusing capacity: diffusing capacity for carbon monoxide (DLCO) 18%.

Non-contrast chest HRCT and CT pulmonary angiography: contrast-enhanced pulmonary artery scan showed no evidence of embolism in pulmonary arteries at or above the segmental level; no obvious vascular or structural malformations within the scanned range; multiple ground-glass opacities and exudative changes in both lungs; pulmonary arterial hypertension. Taken together, pulmonary veno-occlusive disease (PVOD) should be suspected.

CT pulmonary angiography excluded the possibility of obstructive disease in pulmonary arteries at or above the segmental level; radionuclide lung perfusion scan was then performed, showing heterogeneous perfusion distribution in both lungs, with no segmental radioactive perfusion defect areas.

Right heart catheterization was further performed: no obvious abnormalities in the right heart catheterization route. Pulmonary-to-systemic blood flow ratio (Qp/Qs) = 1.08, SaO2 94%; right atrial pressure 8/7/6 mmHg; right ventricular pressure 66/-12/6 mmHg; pulmonary artery pressure 61/25/37 mmHg; pulmonary capillary wedge pressure 6/5/5 mmHg; cardiac output (CO) 5.1 L/min, cardiac index (CI) 3.13 L/(min·m²); pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) 6.75 Wood units (539.76 dyn·s-1·cm-5); acute pulmonary vasodilator test negative. Concurrent pulmonary angiography showed no obvious abnormalities.

After 5 months of treatment  
Changes in echocardiographic parameters:
| Date | LA (mm) | LVEFDD (mm) | RV (mm) | SPAP (mmHg) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| July 2018 | 20 | 38 | 28 | 59 |
| October 2018 | 30.5 | 48.1 | 24.8 | 45 |
| December 2018 | 21 | Normal | Full | 38 |

Changes on posteroanterior chest X-ray:
**List of abnormalities:**
**A. Before treatment:**
1. Large patchy high-density opacity in the left lung (inflammation/exudation)
2. Patchy high-density opacities in the middle and lower fields of the right lung
3. Enlarged cardiac silhouette (outward expansion of the cardiac border)
4. Mild rightward deviation of the trachea
5. Blunting of the left costophrenic angle (sign of pleural effusion)
6. Left pleural thickening with narrowing of the intercostal spaces

**B. 3 months after treatment:**
1. Reduced extent of high-density opacity in the left lung (partial absorption)
2. Decreased density of patchy opacities in the right lung
3. Cardiac silhouette returned to normal size
4. Trachea midline
5. Reappearance of the left costophrenic angle
6. Decreased degree of pleural thickening

**Comprehensive diagnostic conclusion:**
Bilateral pneumonia with left pleural effusion. After treatment, inflammation and effusion were significantly absorbed, with residual mild pleural thickening changes; follow-up reassessment is recommended to evaluate residual lesions.

### Additional Physical Examination
Further examinations after admission:  
6-minute walk test: 480 m.

Cardiopulmonary exercise testing: severe exercise limitation; peak oxygen uptake (peak VO2, PVO2) 17.5 ml/(min·kg) (32% of predicted).
## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Response Format
- When generating the final answer: you must output the diagnostic results directly in Json format (example below), and do not output any other irrelevant content;

- {"Preliminary diagnosis": {"Primary diagnosis": {"Name": "XXX", "Etiologic subtype": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}, "Secondary diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}]}, "Differential diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Selection rationale": "Consider rare but potentially fatal diseases", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": [], "Excludable": true, "Recommended corresponding tests": []}, {"Name": "YYY", "Selection rationale": "Consider differential diagnosis of adjacent organ lesions", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1"], "Excludable": false, "Recommended corresponding tests": ["Recommendation 1", "Recommendation 2"]}]}

## Brief History
### Patient
Female, 33 years old

### Chief Complaint
Chest tightness and palpitations for more than 7 years, worsening for 3 months.

### History of Present Illness
The patient developed chest tightness, palpitations, and joint swelling 7–8 years ago. In 2016, she was diagnosed with “arthritis” at a local hospital and started oral glucocorticoid therapy (prednisone acetate tablets 5–10 mg, once daily); symptom improvement was not significant. One year ago, she discontinued the medication on her own and switched to traditional Chinese medicine; symptoms improved in the early phase of treatment, but she had long-term insomnia with frequent dreaming and tachycardia. At a local hospital, she was given oral metoprolol tartrate tablets 25 mg twice daily to control heart rate. Three months ago, she gradually developed worsening chest tightness and dyspnea compared with before, paroxysmal in nature, lasting about 15 min each episode; exacerbated after activity and slightly relieved after rest. She had nocturnal asthma and could not lie flat. One month ago, she developed swelling of the right cheek, accompanied by swelling and pain behind the ear, hoarseness, choking cough when drinking water, cyanosis of the lips, and a marked decrease in exercise tolerance; no chest pain or fever; no obvious cough, wheezing, or sputum production. On January 3, 2019, she came to our hospital from another city for medical care.

### Past Medical History
Denies other chronic diseases; denies history of mental disorders.  
Denies history of infectious diseases.  
Denies history of surgery or trauma.  
History of exposure to important medications and toxins: traditional Chinese medicine (black pills; specific components unknown), taken for more than 1 year.  
Denies food and drug allergy history.

### Personal History
Grew up in her native place; denies smoking, alcohol drinking, and other adverse habits; denies long-term residence in epidemic areas; denies history of prostitution/promiscuity.

### Marital/Obstetric and Menstrual History
Married, no history of pregnancy; menstruation normal.

### Family History
Parents and younger sister are healthy; denies family history of hereditary diseases.

1. Temperature 37.3°C, heart rate 115 bpm, respiratory rate 40 breaths/min, blood pressure 120/92 mmHg, SpO₂ 65% (on room air, not receiving oxygen).

5. Parasternal heave; heart rate 115 bpm, regular rhythm; loud P₂; grade II systolic murmur at the 3rd–4th intercostal spaces along the left sternal border, without obvious radiation.

Genetic testing report for metabolic myopathy: suspected pathogenic variant in the SYNE2 gene. SYNE2 gene–related Emery–Dreifuss muscular dystrophy type 5 is autosomal dominant. Family verification is recommended.

question:
## Brief History
### Patient
Female, 33 years old

### Chief Complaint
Chest tightness and palpitations for more than 7 years, worsening for 3 months.

### History of Present Illness
The patient developed chest tightness, palpitations, and joint swelling 7–8 years ago. In 2016, she was diagnosed with “arthritis” at a local hospital and started oral glucocorticoid therapy (prednisone acetate tablets 5–10 mg, once daily); symptom improvement was not significant. One year ago, she discontinued the medication on her own and switched to traditional Chinese medicine; symptoms improved in the early phase of treatment, but she had long-term insomnia with frequent dreaming and tachycardia. At a local hospital, she was given oral metoprolol tartrate tablets 25 mg twice daily to control heart rate. Three months ago, she gradually developed worsening chest tightness and dyspnea compared with before, paroxysmal in nature, lasting about 15 min each episode; exacerbated after activity and slightly relieved after rest. She had nocturnal asthma and could not lie flat. One month ago, she developed swelling of the right cheek, accompanied by swelling and pain behind the ear, hoarseness, choking cough when drinking water, cyanosis of the lips, and a marked decrease in exercise tolerance; no chest pain or fever; no obvious cough, wheezing, or sputum production. On January 3, 2019, she came to our hospital from another city for medical care.

### Past Medical History
Denies other chronic diseases; denies history of mental disorders.  
Denies history of infectious diseases.  
Denies history of surgery or trauma.  
History of exposure to important medications and toxins: traditional Chinese medicine (black pills; specific components unknown), taken for more than 1 year.  
Denies food and drug allergy history.

### Personal History
Grew up in her native place; denies smoking, alcohol drinking, and other adverse habits; denies long-term residence in epidemic areas; denies history of prostitution/promiscuity.

### Marital/Obstetric and Menstrual History
Married, no history of pregnancy; menstruation normal.

### Family History
Parents and younger sister are healthy; denies family history of hereditary diseases.

## Physical Examination
1. Temperature 37.3°C, heart rate 115 bpm, respiratory rate 40 breaths/min, blood pressure 120/92 mmHg, SpO₂ 65% (on room air, not receiving oxygen).
2. Small and thin habitus, height 153 cm, weight 33 kg, BMI 14.09; conscious, chronic ill appearance, sitting upright; answers appropriately and cooperates with examination.
3. Generalized dry skin with chapping and scaling; cyanosis of lips and distal extremities; right cheek swelling, red in color, increased skin temperature, palpable fluctuation with tenderness; no obvious jugular venous distension.
4. Coarse breath sounds in both lungs; no dry or moist rales heard in either lung.
5. Parasternal heave; heart rate 115 bpm, regular rhythm; loud P₂; grade II systolic murmur at the 3rd–4th intercostal spaces along the left sternal border, without obvious radiation.
(6) Abdomen flat and soft, without tenderness or rebound tenderness.  
(7) Poor neck mobility; deformities of finger joints and wrist joints bilaterally; deformities of both knee joints; left hip joint unable to abduct; edema of both lower extremities; nervous system (–).

## Initial Laboratory Tests
(3 January 2019)  
(1) Complete blood count: WBC 17.14×10^9/L ↑, neutrophils 0.904 ↑  
- **Hemoglobin**: 154 g/L ↑  
- **Red blood cells**: \(6.39 \times 10^{12}/L\) ↑  
- **Hematocrit**: 55.3% ↑  
- **Platelets**: \(162 \times 10^{9}/L\) within normal range  
- **C-reactive protein**: 128.80 mg/L ↑  
- **Plateletcrit**: 1.37 ng/ml ↑  

(2) Tuberculosis tests: T-SPOT (–), ESAT-6 (–), CFP-10 (–).

(3) Liver function:
- **A/G ratio**: 1.0 ↓

- **Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)**: 270 U/L ↑  
- **Alkaline phosphatase (ALP)**: 143 U/L ↑  
- **Direct bilirubin (DBil)**: 6.8 umol/L ↑  
Others normal.

(4) Renal function:
- **Blood urea nitrogen (BUN)**: 15.0 μmol/L ↑  
- **Serum creatinine (Scr)**: 68 μmol/L  
- **Uric acid (UA)**: 692 μmol/L ↑  

(5) Electrolytes:
- \([Na+]\) 141 mmol/L  
- \([K+]\) 4.3 mmol/L  
- \([Cl-]\) 90 mmol/L ↓  
- **Serum calcium**: 2.1 mmol/L ↓  
- **Serum phosphate**: 0.75 mmol/L ↓  
- **Urine calcium**: 0.82 mmol/L ↓  
- **Urine phosphate**: 11.4 mmol/L ↑  

6 **Heart failure and myocardial injury markers**: BNP 1786 pg/ml ↑; troponin (TNI) (–), creatine kinase (CK) (–).

7 **Coagulation function**: INR 1.18 ↑, D-dimer 1.22 mg/L ↑, others normal.

8 **Arterial blood gas analysis** (nasal cannula with high-flow oxygen 5 L/min):  
- pH 7.18 ↓,  
- PCO₂ 115 mmHg ↑,  
- PO₂ 20 mmHg ↓,  
- SpO₂ 73% ↓.

9 **Arterial blood gas analysis** (under noninvasive ventilatory support):  
- pH 7.55 ↑,  
- PCO₂ 58 mmHg ↑,  
- PO₂ 101 mmHg ↑,  
- SpO₂ 98%.

10 **Rheumatology and immunology tests**: ANA profile (–), rheumatoid factor (–), HLA-B27 (–), anti-neutrophil antibody (–), ESR (–).

## Initial Examinations
Pulse oximetry SpO₂ 65%; after high-flow oxygen (5 L/min), the patient developed impaired consciousness. Emergency arterial blood gas: pH 7.18, PCO₂ 115 mmHg, PO₂ 20 mmHg, SpO₂ 73%.

Admission ECG showed sinus rhythm, right axis deviation, P pulmonale, and high voltage in the right ventricle.

Admission echocardiography (January 3, 2019): right atrial (RA) internal diameter 49 mm × 50 mm ↑; right ventricular (RV) internal diameter 44 mm ↑; left ventricular end-diastolic diameter (LVEDD) 33 mm ↓; synchronous interventricular septal motion; tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion (TAPSE) = 12 mm ↓; pulmonary artery systolic pressure (PASP) = 63 mmHg ↑; ejection fraction (EF) 79%. Conclusion: enlargement of right atrium and right ventricle, small left ventricle, decreased systolic motion of the RV free wall, moderate tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary hypertension.

Chest PA and lateral radiographs showed increased lung markings in both lungs, a nodular shadow in the right lower lung, enlarged cardiac silhouette, prominent pulmonary artery segment, cortical distortion of the right 1st–2nd ribs; degenerative changes of both shoulder joints were mentioned.

## Supplementary History
### Supplementary Laboratory Tests

Muscle biopsy: marked variation in myofiber size; complement deposition seen on sarcolemma of numerous necrotic fibers; mild diffuse upregulation of p62 in some fibers; mild atrophy of type II fibers. Conclusion: pathological changes of myogenic injury; possible necrotizing myopathy combined with steroid myopathy.

| Weak staining                  | +      | Weakly positive |  
| Strong staining                | ++     | Positive |  
| Same intensity as QC band      | +++    | Strongly positive |  
1. Antigen band “co” is the quality control band; 2. Suspicious results are interpreted as negative.

Genetic testing report for metabolic myopathy: suspected pathogenic variant in the SYNE2 gene. SYNE2 gene–related Emery–Dreifuss muscular dystrophy type 5 is autosomal dominant. Family verification is recommended.

Laboratory tests (at discharge on January 19, 2019)  
1) Complete blood count: WBC 7.89×10^9/L, neutrophils 0.884 ↑, hemoglobin 140 g/L, RBC 6.12×10^12/L ↑, hematocrit 52.2% ↑, platelets 130×10^9/L, C-reactive protein 15.14 mg/L ↑, plateletcrit 0.25 ng/ml ↑.  
2) Liver function: ALT 47 U/L, AST 56 U/L ↑, LDH 617 U/L, ALP 93 U/L, total bilirubin 15 umol/L.  
3) Renal function: BUN 8.5 μmol/L ↑, creatinine 34 μmol/L, uric acid 186 μmol/L.  
4) Electrolytes: [Na+] 136 mmol/L ↓, [K+] 4.9 mmol/L, [Cl-] 86 mmol/L ↑.  
5) BNP: 110 pg/ml ↑.

### Supplementary Examinations
**Chest CT with contrast**: pulmonary hypertension, enlarged cardiac silhouette; multiple infiltrates in the right middle lobe and both lower lobes; small bilateral pleural effusions; scanned bilateral shoulder joints, clavicular heads, spine, sternum and部分 ribs showing abnormal bone mineral density. Main pulmonary artery trunk widened; enlarged cardiac silhouette.

**Pulmonary function test**: severe restrictive ventilatory dysfunction; bronchodilator test negative.

Sleep respiratory monitoring: mild sleep apnea, severe hypoxia.

Contrast-enhanced mandibular CT: annular enhancing lesion in the right pharyngeal muscles and medial pterygoid muscle, abscess? others? exclude tracheal compression.

Lower abdominal ultrasound: sludge-like gallstones; multiple bilateral renal calculi with hydronephrosis.

Joint ultrasound: small amount of effusion; markedly irregular cortical margins; synovial hyperplasia.  
Ultrasound findings:  
1. Small effusion in the right wrist joint; markedly irregular cortical margin of the left wrist joint—further evaluation in conjunction with clinical findings is required;  
2. Mild synovial hyperplasia with small effusion in the right suprapatellar bursa; very small effusion in the left suprapatellar bursa; rough medial cortical bone of the left knee; thinning of articular cartilage in both knee joints;  
3. Moderate synovial hyperplasia with calcification on the ulnar side of the left wrist, with rough bony surface; mild synovial hyperplasia with small effusion on the ulnar side of the right wrist;  
4. No obvious abnormalities in both ankles.

Muscle strength examination: muscle strength grade IV− in both upper limbs, grade III− in both lower limbs.

Electromyography: myogenic damage on EMG, prominent in proximal limb muscles; no obvious active denervation; respiratory muscle power insufficiency considered.

Plain muscle MRI: mild fatty infiltration of muscle groups in both calves; mild fatty infiltration of muscle groups in both thighs.

Follow-up echocardiography (March 22, 2019): PASP 64 mmHg; RA internal diameter 45 mm × 54 mm; RV internal diameter 42 mm (smaller than the previous 444 mm); TAPSE 19 mm (significantly improved vs. 12 mm previously); LVEDD 38 mm (improved vs. 33 mm previously); LVEF 81%. Conclusion: enlarged right atrial and right ventricular internal diameters, pulmonary hypertension with mild tricuspid regurgitation.
## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;
