## Brief Medical History
### Patient
Male, 53 years old

### Chief Complaint
Admitted due to “one episode of sudden palpitations 1 year ago, recurrence with dizziness for half a day”

### History of Present Illness
One year ago, the patient developed sudden palpitations without an obvious trigger and perceived tachycardia. Coronary angiography performed at an outside hospital showed no abnormalities. The outside-hospital diagnoses were arrhythmia (premature ventricular contractions, ventricular tachycardia) and acute myocarditis; details are unknown. After discharge, the patient had been on long-term oral Betaloc (metoprolol tartrate) therapy. At noon on the day of admission, the patient suddenly experienced palpitations in the office, accompanied by dizziness, sweating, and throat discomfort. There was no significant relief after rest. There was no obvious chest tightness or chest pain, no dyspnea, no nausea or vomiting, no transient visual blackout or syncope, no headache, and no loss of consciousness. The patient then presented to the Emergency Department of our hospital. Blood pressure was 190/80 mmHg. ECG suggested sustained ventricular tachycardia. Lidocaine and Cordarone (amiodarone) were ineffective; subsequently, electrical cardioversion was performed once, restoring sinus rhythm.                Outside-hospital coronary angiography (1 year ago): normal coronary arteries; no stenosis in the LM, LAD, LCX, or RCA; TIMI flow grade 3.

### Past Medical History
Hypertension for 6 years, with a maximum blood pressure of 190/101 mmHg; treated with oral losartan; the patient reports blood pressure is fairly well controlled. History of partial thyroidectomy for malignant thyroid tumor; currently taking Euthyrox (levothyroxine) 37.5 μg orally. Denies other chronic disease history. Denies allergy history. Denies history of infectious diseases.

### Personal History
No special history

### Marital/Fertility History and Menstrual History
No special history

### Family History
Denies family history of hereditary diseases. No family members with heart disease.

## Physical Examination
Blood pressure 107/77 mmHg. Clear consciousness; breathing steady; answers appropriately; cooperative with examination. Neck supple; no jugular venous distension. Breath sounds clear in both lungs; no obvious dry or moist rales. Cardiac borders not enlarged; heart rate 64 beats/min; regular rhythm; no murmurs. Abdomen soft and flat; no tenderness or rebound tenderness; liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin. No edema in both lower extremities.

## Initial Laboratory Tests
Complete blood count, coagulation function, liver and renal function, thyroid function, and D-dimer: no abnormalities.

Electrolytes: potassium 3.56 mmol/L, sodium 138.37 mmol/L, chloride 103.84 mmol/L, calcium 2.17 mmol/L, magnesium 0.74 mmol/L.

Cardiac biomarkers: troponin T 0.134 ng/mL, creatine kinase-MB 2.09 ng/mL, myoglobin (MYO) 63.73 ng/mL, NT-proBNP 304 ng/L.

## Initial Examinations
ECG on emergency admission: the admission ECG showed wide-QRS tachycardia, ventricular rate 190–200 beats/min. The QRS complex displayed a left bundle branch block morphology, with negative concordance of the predominant QRS deflection in precordial leads V<sub>1</sub>–V<sub>6</sub>, strongly suggestive of ventricular tachycardia. Meanwhile, the predominant deflection in leads II, III, and aVF was negative, and in leads I and aVL was positive; the axis was leftward, suggesting that the origin of the ventricular tachycardia was located at the right ventricular apex. This site is not a common origin of ventricular tachycardia. After cardioversion, the sinus-rhythm ECG showed T-wave inversion in right precordial leads V<sub>1</sub>–V<sub>3</sub>, suggesting abnormal right ventricular repolarization.


Post-emergency electrical cardioversion ECG:
**List of abnormalities:**
1. **Right bundle branch block** (rsR' pattern in lead V1, deepened S wave in lead I, increased R wave in lead aVR)
2. **Left anterior fascicular block** (increased R wave in lead I with disappearance of q wave, rS pattern in lead III, markedly leftward axis)
3. **ST–T changes** (mild ST-segment elevation with tall, peaked T waves in leads V3–V6)
4. **P-wave abnormality** (biphasic/notched P wave in lead II, suggesting left atrial enlargement)
**Comprehensive diagnostic conclusion:**  
Right bundle branch block combined with left anterior fascicular block, with left atrial enlargement and abnormal myocardial repolarization (myocardial ischemia should be excluded in conjunction with clinical findings).

Echocardiography: trace aortic regurgitation; mildly reduced left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 51%).

## Additional History
### Additional Examinations
Repeat echocardiography  Analysis of characteristic ECG findings highly suggested abnormalities in the right heart system. Echocardiography was repeated with emphasis on right-heart parameters and function. Results indicated: right ventricular enlargement (42 cm × 62 cm), widened right ventricular outflow tract (long-axis internal diameter 30 mm, short-axis internal diameter 35 mm), and right ventricular fractional area change (FAC) 40%, at the lower limit of normal.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging  T1WI: fatty tissue infiltration at the right heart border; fat-suppressed T2WI: fat signal at the right heart border was suppressed; LVEF 54%; right ventricular ejection fraction (RVEF) 15%, markedly decreased; right ventricular end-diastolic volume (RVEDV)/body surface area (BSA) 117.72 mL/m2. LGE: on the long-axis four-chamber view, an enhanced signal was seen in the outer layer of the apical septum.

Follow-up  24-hour Holter monitoring 1 month later: baseline rhythm throughout was sinus rhythm; total beats 77,273; average heart rate 55 beats/min, maximum heart rate 88 beats/min, minimum heart rate 42 beats/min. Premature ventricular contractions 210; no ventricular tachycardia observed.

24-hour Holter monitoring 1 year later: baseline rhythm throughout was sinus rhythm; total beats 79,036; average heart rate 61 beats/min, maximum heart rate 110 beats/min, minimum heart rate 43 beats/min. Premature ventricular contractions 1,619, approximately 2%; one episode of nonsustained ventricular tachycardia (3 beats).

Echocardiography 1 year later: right ventricular enlargement (approximately 60 mm × 42 mm), FAC approximately 40%, normal left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 69%).

## Brief History
### Patient
Female, 44 years old

### Chief Complaint
Recurrent exertional shortness of breath for more than 2 months, worsened for 2 days.

### History of Present Illness
The patient developed shortness of breath after strenuous physical activity 2 months ago (early August 2018), accompanied by stabbing pain in the retrosternal area and pallor, which was relieved after rest. Thereafter, exercise tolerance progressively declined; emotional agitation could also precipitate shortness of breath, accompanied by cyanosis and a weight loss of 4 kg. Two days ago (October 4, 2018), after a quarrel with another person, the patient developed shortness of breath again, accompanied by hemoptysis with blood-streaked sputum; the symptoms persisted without relief. She sought care at an outside hospital, where D-dimer was negative and pulmonary artery CTA showed multiple filling defects; pulmonary embolism was diagnosed and intravenous thrombolytic therapy was administered. After thrombolysis, shortness of breath and hemoptysis still progressively worsened, and she was transferred to our hospital for further diagnosis and treatment.

### Past Medical History
A pulmonary nodule was detected during a physical examination 1 year ago. Denies a history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, rheumatic/autoimmune diseases, cardiac disease, etc. No history of trauma or surgery. No history of blood transfusion.

### Personal History
No history of contact with contaminated water. No special medication history. Denies smoking and alcohol abuse.

### Marital/Obstetric and Menstrual History
Menstrual and marital/obstetric history unremarkable; no history of spontaneous abortion.

### Family History
Denies similar illnesses among family members; denies family history of hereditary diseases.

## Physical Examination
Temperature 38.5°C, pulse 111 beats/min, respiratory rate 31 breaths/min, blood pressure 117/71 mmHg, body mass index 18.03.

Alert and oriented, fatigued, jugular venous distension. Tachypnea; coarse breath sounds bilaterally; no dry or moist rales. Regular rhythm, heart rate 111 beats/min; accentuated second heart sound at the pulmonary valve area (P2 > A2). No other abnormalities on cardiac and pulmonary examination. Abdominal examination unremarkable; no edema in both lower extremities.

## Initial Laboratory Tests
Laboratory examinations  
(1) Arterial blood gas analysis (FiO2 41%): PaO2 57 mmHg; PaCO2 29 mmHg; pH 7.50; HCO3− 22.6 mmol/L; base excess (BE) −0.6; lactate (Lac) 1.8 mmol/L; SpO2 92%.  
(2) D-dimer: 510 μg/L (reference range 0–500 μg/L).  
(3) N-terminal pro–B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP): 2977 ng/L.  
(4) Coagulation profile: activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) 45.8 s (reference range 30–45 s); prothrombin time (PT) 14.5 s; international normalized ratio (INR) 1.14; fibrinogen (FIB) 4.67 g/L; thrombin time (TT) 17.7 s.  
(5) Complete blood count: WBC 12.28×10^9/L, neutrophils 0.797, hemoglobin 121 g/L, platelets 268×10^9/L.  
(6) Liver function: ALT 17 U/L, AST 28 U/L, total bilirubin 12.1 μmol/L, direct bilirubin 2.4 μmol/L, total protein 58 g/L, albumin 34.7 g/L.  
(7) Renal function: creatinine 60.06 μmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 2.8 mmol/L.  
(8) Cardiac biomarkers, viral hepatitis markers, HIV antibody, Treponema pallidum particle agglutination assay (TPPA), routine urinalysis, routine stool test, lipid profile, and thyroid function tests were all unremarkable.  
(9) Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 31 mm/h; C-reactive protein 60.9 mg/L; procalcitonin 0.13 ng/mL.  
(10) Autoimmune antibodies: antinuclear antibody, ds-DNA, ANA profile, complement, rheumatic and rheumatoid indices, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) (−).  
(11) Thrombophilia panel: antithrombin III (AT-III), plasma protein S, plasma protein C activity, lupus anticoagulant—all unremarkable.  
(12) Tumor markers: neuron-specific enolase (NSE) 25.5 μg/L (reference range 0–16.3 μg/L); the remaining CEA, AFP, CA153, CA724, CA199, CA125, CA242, and non-small cell lung cancer–associated antigen were all (−).

## Initial Examinations
Electrocardiogram: sinus tachycardia; T-wave inversion in leads V~V; poor R-wave progression  
Portable chest radiograph: cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio 0.58); hilar enlargement  
Bedside echocardiography (October 7, 2018):  
Right atrium (47 mm) and right ventricle (left ventricular outflow tract 38 mm, longitudinal diameter 60 mm) enlarged; right ventricular wall thickness 6.1 mm; tricuspid regurgitation area 6.3 cm²; estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure 80 mmHg. Echocardiography suggested severe pulmonary arterial hypertension of undetermined etiology, with moderate tricuspid regurgitation.  
Pulmonary artery CTA: multiple strip-like filling defects were seen within the distal main pulmonary artery and the lumens of the left and right pulmonary arteries; no opacification was seen in the left upper lobe pulmonary artery, with filling defects in its branch vessels and partial truncation. A patchy filling defect was seen at the origin of the left lower lobe artery, and filling defects were also seen in the lumen of the lateral basal segment branch. Multiple filling defects were seen within the lumens of the right middle lobe artery and the basal segment branch arteries of the right lower lobe artery. Pulmonary embolism was considered.

## Supplemental History
### Supplemental Laboratory Tests
Postoperative pathological diagnosis  
The submitted specimen showed extensive thrombus and intima-like tissue. Tumor was seen at the margins of the thrombus and intima. The tumor cells were pleomorphic; some cells were elongated spindle-shaped with uneven cellularity and relatively bland morphology; some cells had abundant cytoplasm with epithelioid appearance, marked nuclear atypia, and frequent mitotic figures. Stromal collagen fiber proliferation with hyaline degeneration or myxoid degeneration was present, with inflammatory cell infiltration.

Immunohistochemistry: tumor cells Desmin (focal +), SMA (−), Myogenin (−), S100 (−), ALK (5A4) (−), Vimentin (+++), Ki-67 (80%+), CDK4 (−), LCA (−)

Molecular pathology: tumor cells MDM2 FISH (+)

### Supplemental Examinations
Repeat echocardiography (October 22, 2018): thrombosis in the distal main pulmonary artery and the left and right pulmonary arteries; severe pulmonary arterial hypertension of undetermined etiology (estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure 95 mmHg); moderate tricuspid regurgitation

Pulmonary artery CTA: multiple nodular and quasi-round hypodense lesions were seen from the main pulmonary artery to the level of the left and right pulmonary artery branches; the density of some lesions was heterogeneous; the pulmonary arterial wall margin was irregular; contrast enhancement was not obvious; in the pulmonary arteries with filling defects, there was accompanying vascular enlargement. These signs were not consistent with typical pulmonary thromboembolism and warrant further suspicion of nonthrombotic pulmonary embolism.

Cardiac MRI: multiple lesions in the main pulmonary artery, bilateral pulmonary arteries, and their branches, considered tumor-related changes. Abnormal signals in both lungs, considering possible pulmonary infarction; bilateral pleural effusions. Moderate left ventricular enlargement with normal global systolic function (LVEF 65.12%). Right ventricular size normal with moderately reduced global systolic function. Moderate tricuspid regurgitation and moderate pulmonary regurgitation; small pericardial effusion.

PET/CT: filling defects in the main pulmonary artery, left pulmonary artery, and right upper pulmonary artery with increased glucose metabolism, highly suggestive of malignancy, with possible arterial sarcoma; emboli in bilateral pulmonary arteries and branches with scattered pulmonary infarctions in both lungs; right pleural effusion; small pericardial effusion. No malignant tumor metabolic imaging was found elsewhere on whole-body ^18F-FDG PET/CT. Left maxillary sinusitis. Scattered fibrotic foci in both lungs.

## Brief History
### Patient
Female, 44 years old

### Chief Complaint
Recurrent exertional shortness of breath for more than 2 months, worsened for 2 days.

### History of Present Illness
The patient developed shortness of breath after strenuous physical activity 2 months ago (early August 2018), accompanied by stabbing pain in the retrosternal area and pallor, which was relieved after rest. Thereafter, exercise tolerance progressively declined; emotional agitation could also precipitate shortness of breath, accompanied by cyanosis and a weight loss of 4 kg. Two days ago (October 4, 2018), after a quarrel with another person, the patient developed shortness of breath again, accompanied by hemoptysis with blood-streaked sputum; the symptoms persisted without relief. She sought care at an outside hospital, where D-dimer was negative and pulmonary artery CTA showed multiple filling defects; pulmonary embolism was diagnosed and intravenous thrombolytic therapy was administered. After thrombolysis, shortness of breath and hemoptysis still progressively worsened, and she was transferred to our hospital for further diagnosis and treatment.

### Past Medical History
A pulmonary nodule was detected during a physical examination 1 year ago. Denies a history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, rheumatic/autoimmune diseases, cardiac disease, etc. No history of trauma or surgery. No history of blood transfusion.

### Personal History
No history of contact with contaminated water. No special medication history. Denies smoking and alcohol abuse.

### Marital/Obstetric and Menstrual History
Menstrual and marital/obstetric history unremarkable; no history of spontaneous abortion.

### Family History
Denies similar illnesses among family members; denies family history of hereditary diseases.

## Physical Examination
Temperature 38.5°C, pulse 111 beats/min, respiratory rate 31 breaths/min, blood pressure 117/71 mmHg, body mass index 18.03.

Alert and oriented, fatigued, jugular venous distension. Tachypnea; coarse breath sounds bilaterally; no dry or moist rales. Regular rhythm, heart rate 111 beats/min; accentuated second heart sound at the pulmonary valve area (P2 > A2). No other abnormalities on cardiac and pulmonary examination. Abdominal examination unremarkable; no edema in both lower extremities.

## Initial Laboratory Tests
Laboratory examinations  
(1) Arterial blood gas analysis (FiO2 41%): PaO2 57 mmHg; PaCO2 29 mmHg; pH 7.50; HCO3− 22.6 mmol/L; base excess (BE) −0.6; lactate (Lac) 1.8 mmol/L; SpO2 92%.  
(2) D-dimer: 510 μg/L (reference range 0–500 μg/L).  
(3) N-terminal pro–B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP): 2977 ng/L.  
(4) Coagulation profile: activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) 45.8 s (reference range 30–45 s); prothrombin time (PT) 14.5 s; international normalized ratio (INR) 1.14; fibrinogen (FIB) 4.67 g/L; thrombin time (TT) 17.7 s.  
(5) Complete blood count: WBC 12.28×10^9/L, neutrophils 0.797, hemoglobin 121 g/L, platelets 268×10^9/L.  
(6) Liver function: ALT 17 U/L, AST 28 U/L, total bilirubin 12.1 μmol/L, direct bilirubin 2.4 μmol/L, total protein 58 g/L, albumin 34.7 g/L.  
(7) Renal function: creatinine 60.06 μmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 2.8 mmol/L.  
(8) Cardiac biomarkers, viral hepatitis markers, HIV antibody, Treponema pallidum particle agglutination assay (TPPA), routine urinalysis, routine stool test, lipid profile, and thyroid function tests were all unremarkable.  
(9) Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 31 mm/h; C-reactive protein 60.9 mg/L; procalcitonin 0.13 ng/mL.  
(10) Autoimmune antibodies: antinuclear antibody, ds-DNA, ANA profile, complement, rheumatic and rheumatoid indices, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) (−).  
(11) Thrombophilia panel: antithrombin III (AT-III), plasma protein S, plasma protein C activity, lupus anticoagulant—all unremarkable.  
(12) Tumor markers: neuron-specific enolase (NSE) 25.5 μg/L (reference range 0–16.3 μg/L); the remaining CEA, AFP, CA153, CA724, CA199, CA125, CA242, and non-small cell lung cancer–associated antigen were all (−).

## Initial Examinations
Electrocardiogram: sinus tachycardia; T-wave inversion in leads V~V; poor R-wave progression  
Portable chest radiograph: cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio 0.58); hilar enlargement  
Bedside echocardiography (October 7, 2018):  
Right atrium (47 mm) and right ventricle (left ventricular outflow tract 38 mm, longitudinal diameter 60 mm) enlarged; right ventricular wall thickness 6.1 mm; tricuspid regurgitation area 6.3 cm²; estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure 80 mmHg. Echocardiography suggested severe pulmonary arterial hypertension of undetermined etiology, with moderate tricuspid regurgitation.  
Pulmonary artery CTA: multiple strip-like filling defects were seen within the distal main pulmonary artery and the lumens of the left and right pulmonary arteries; no opacification was seen in the left upper lobe pulmonary artery, with filling defects in its branch vessels and partial truncation. A patchy filling defect was seen at the origin of the left lower lobe artery, and filling defects were also seen in the lumen of the lateral basal segment branch. Multiple filling defects were seen within the lumens of the right middle lobe artery and the basal segment branch arteries of the right lower lobe artery. Pulmonary embolism was considered.

## Supplemental History
### Supplemental Laboratory Tests
Postoperative pathological diagnosis  
The submitted specimen showed extensive thrombus and intima-like tissue. Tumor was seen at the margins of the thrombus and intima. The tumor cells were pleomorphic; some cells were elongated spindle-shaped with uneven cellularity and relatively bland morphology; some cells had abundant cytoplasm with epithelioid appearance, marked nuclear atypia, and frequent mitotic figures. Stromal collagen fiber proliferation with hyaline degeneration or myxoid degeneration was present, with inflammatory cell infiltration.

Immunohistochemistry: tumor cells Desmin (focal +), SMA (−), Myogenin (−), S100 (−), ALK (5A4) (−), Vimentin (+++), Ki-67 (80%+), CDK4 (−), LCA (−)

Molecular pathology: tumor cells MDM2 FISH (+)

### Supplemental Examinations
Repeat echocardiography (October 22, 2018): thrombosis in the distal main pulmonary artery and the left and right pulmonary arteries; severe pulmonary arterial hypertension of undetermined etiology (estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure 95 mmHg); moderate tricuspid regurgitation

Pulmonary artery CTA: multiple nodular and quasi-round hypodense lesions were seen from the main pulmonary artery to the level of the left and right pulmonary artery branches; the density of some lesions was heterogeneous; the pulmonary arterial wall margin was irregular; contrast enhancement was not obvious; in the pulmonary arteries with filling defects, there was accompanying vascular enlargement. These signs were not consistent with typical pulmonary thromboembolism and warrant further suspicion of nonthrombotic pulmonary embolism.

Cardiac MRI: multiple lesions in the main pulmonary artery, bilateral pulmonary arteries, and their branches, considered tumor-related changes. Abnormal signals in both lungs, considering possible pulmonary infarction; bilateral pleural effusions. Moderate left ventricular enlargement with normal global systolic function (LVEF 65.12%). Right ventricular size normal with moderately reduced global systolic function. Moderate tricuspid regurgitation and moderate pulmonary regurgitation; small pericardial effusion.

PET/CT: filling defects in the main pulmonary artery, left pulmonary artery, and right upper pulmonary artery with increased glucose metabolism, highly suggestive of malignancy, with possible arterial sarcoma; emboli in bilateral pulmonary arteries and branches with scattered pulmonary infarctions in both lungs; right pleural effusion; small pericardial effusion. No malignant tumor metabolic imaging was found elsewhere on whole-body ^18F-FDG PET/CT. Left maxillary sinusitis. Scattered fibrotic foci in both lungs.
## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Response Format
- When generating the final answer: you must output the diagnostic results directly in Json format (example below), and do not output any other irrelevant content;

- {"Preliminary diagnosis": {"Primary diagnosis": {"Name": "XXX", "Etiologic subtype": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}, "Secondary diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}]}, "Differential diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Selection rationale": "Consider rare but potentially fatal diseases", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": [], "Excludable": true, "Recommended corresponding tests": []}, {"Name": "YYY", "Selection rationale": "Consider differential diagnosis of adjacent organ lesions", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1"], "Excludable": false, "Recommended corresponding tests": ["Recommendation 1", "Recommendation 2"]}]}

## Brief History
### Patient
Female, 18 years old

### Chief Complaint
Recurrent shortness of breath after activity for 3 years; one episode of syncope 5 hours ago

### History of Present Illness
2020/02 (age 15) 3 years ago, the patient began to experience recurrent shortness of breath after activity. A local hospital considered “asthma” and provided “anti-asthmatic therapy”; symptom improvement was not obvious, and no further evaluation or treatment was pursued.  
2021/01 (age 16) 2 years ago, the patient dropped out of school and stayed at home without going out to work; she often felt fatigued and short of breath after activity, which could be relieved after resting. No blurred vision/near-syncope or syncope, no dizziness, no abnormal sensation or motor disturbance of the limbs, and no vertigo.  
2023/04 (age 18) 5 hours ago (2023.4.26), the patient suddenly developed syncope while walking outside and fell to the ground. Family members reported spontaneous awakening in about 2 minutes; there were no convulsions during the episode. She then presented to the emergency department of our hospital. ECG indicated “third-degree atrioventricular block”; after implantation of a temporary pacemaker, she was admitted.

### Past Medical History
Denies chronic diseases such as hypertension, diabetes mellitus, cerebral infarction, etc. Denies history of surgery, trauma, and infectious diseases.

### Personal History
Denies smoking and alcohol use. Previously had fair academic performance. Denies history of long-term medication use.

### Marital/Obstetric and Menstrual History
Unmarried, nulligravida. Regular menstruation with normal menstrual flow.

### Family History
Parents are healthy; no siblings. No similar history in the family; no family history of premature cardiovascular disease.

## Physical Examination

No special facial appearance; normal development; no skin erythema; extremities warm; facial contusion visible. No jugular venous distention; hepatojugular reflux negative. Right internal jugular vein indwelling catheter in place; no local redness, swelling, or exudation. Scattered moist rales in both lower lungs. Cardiac dullness enlarged to both sides; heart rate 60 bpm, regular rhythm; no murmurs in any valve auscultation areas; no pericardial friction rub. Liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin; shifting dullness (-). Mild edema of both lower extremities.

## Preliminary Laboratory Tests

Blood lipids: TC: 2.73 mmol/L, TG: 1.28 mmol/L

## Preliminary Examinations
Emergency ECG (day of admission) ECG: sinus rhythm; third-degree atrioventricular block; junctional escape rhythm (42 bpm);  
Non-contrast chest CT (day of admission) Chest CT: multiple patchy ground-glass density exudative opacities in both lungs, with thickening of the interlobular septa in both lungs and diffuse thickening of the bronchial walls in all lobes bilaterally; pulmonary edema is highly suspected. Cardiomegaly. Temporary pacemaker in situ. Small bilateral pleural effusions; mild bilateral pleural thickening.  
Non-contrast cranial CT (day of admission) Cranial CT: no abnormalities detected;

### Additional Examinations
Changes in myocardial enzymes (day of admission and day 2) After admission, myocardial injury markers were elevated, and the ECG showed ST-T changes in the precordial leads;  
Coronary angiography (day 2 of admission) 1. CAG: left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries showed no significant stenosis; distal flow TIMI grade 3. 2. Temporary pacemaker lead dislodgement; lead readjusted;  
Echocardiography (day 2 of admission) 1. Myocardial involvement disorder 2. Ventricular wall thickening 3. Mildly decreased left ventricular function 4. Biatrial enlargement 5. Trace pericardial effusion;

Cardiac magnetic resonance (day 9 of admission) MRI conclusion summary: 1. Biatrial enlargement; symmetric thickening of the left ventricular wall with fibrotic changes 2. Mildly reduced left heart function 3. Small pericardial effusion;  
Electromyography (day 11 of admission) MG suggested myogenic injury changes, with more prominent involvement of proximal muscles of both upper and both lower limbs;  
Gastrocnemius muscle biopsy (day 12 of admission)  
HE pathology result: slight swelling of striated muscle; indistinct borders and striations; widened perinuclear spaces; no inflammatory cell infiltration observed;  
Holter re-examination (day 10 of admission) Paced + intrinsic rhythm, average 68 bpm; frequent premature atrial contractions, 14,260 beats; short runs of atrial tachycardia; frequent multifocal premature ventricular contractions, 12,497 beats; no long RR intervals or long QT  
Repeat Holter (day 10 of admission) Sinus rhythm, average 58 bpm; premature atrial contractions 265 beats, some in pairs; multifocal premature ventricular contractions 8,468 beats, some in trigeminy; short runs of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, duration 3 seconds  
Summary of ultrasound and imaging assessment No edematous changes consistent with the acute phase of myocarditis were observed. Findings of symmetric left ventricular myocardial hypertrophy were present, with biatrial enlargement; mildly decreased LV ejection fraction, with HCM and RCM phenotypes. Immunofluorescence staining: uneven distribution of desmin with abnormal accumulation

Consciousness not fully clear; general mental status; breathing stable; neck supple. No jugular venous distention bilaterally. Coarse breath sounds in both lungs; no obvious dry or moist rales. Muscle strength and muscle tone of the limbs normal. Physiological reflexes present; pathological signs negative.

### Chief Complaint
Recurrent chest pain for more than 20 days, worsened with 1 episode of syncope

### History of Present Illness
The patient reported that 20 days ago, without an apparent trigger, she developed persistent xiphoid/subxiphoid pain; each episode lasted from several minutes to several hours, accompanied by chest tightness and shortness of breath. She denied cough and sputum production, denied chills and fever, and denied nausea, vomiting, or other discomfort. Fourteen days ago (August 28), at approximately 8:00 PM, she developed sudden severe chest pain (8–9/10) with dizziness and diaphoresis. The location was the same as before but more severe, accompanied by left hypochondrial pain. About half an hour later, she felt an urge to defecate; while getting out of bed and walking to the toilet, she developed transient visual blackout, followed by loss of consciousness and fell to the ground (duration unknown). After spontaneously regaining consciousness, she still had persistent chest pain (6–7/10), and therefore visited a local hospital the next day. Troponin and NT-proBNP were reported as normal. Echocardiography suggested pericardial effusion; details of diagnosis and treatment were unknown. Her symptoms did not improve significantly. She then presented to the outpatient clinic of our hospital for further evaluation and treatment, and was admitted to our department from the outpatient clinic with a working diagnosis of “chest pain, syncope—etiology to be investigated.” Since onset, she has had poor精神状态 (general condition), appetite, and sleep; urination and defecation were normal; no significant weight loss was noted.

## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;

## Brief Medical History
### Patient
Male, 73 years old

### Chief Complaint
Paroxysmal palpitations and shortness of breath for more than 1 year, aggravated for 1 week

### History of Present Illness
More than 1 year ago, the patient developed palpitations without an obvious trigger, occurring 3–5 times daily, lasting from several minutes to approximately 1 hour each time, and resolving spontaneously. The episodes were accompanied by chest tightness and shortness of breath, worsened after activity, with occasional dizziness, without amaurosis or syncope. Holter monitoring at an outside hospital suggested frequent premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) and short runs of ventricular tachycardia (VT). During an emergency department visit at the outside hospital, the patient experienced transient loss of consciousness; ECG monitoring showed ventricular fibrillation (VF). Electrical defibrillation was performed with conversion to sinus rhythm and recovery of consciousness, after which the patient was transferred to our hospital.

### Past Medical History
Three years ago, due to “acute myocardial infarction,” the patient underwent emergency coronary angiography (CAG) + percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI), with implantation of 1 stent in the LCX.

## Physical Examination
No obvious positive findings

## Initial Laboratory Tests
Cardiac enzymes: hs-TnI 0.463 ug/L  
BNP: 499 ng/L

## Initial Examinations
Electrocardiogram: sinus rhythm, heart rate 60 beats/min, no ST-T changes observed  
Echocardiography: LV 55 mm, LVEF 32%, segmental wall motion abnormality of the left ventricle  
Pre-coronary angiography Holter monitoring: sinus rhythm, frequent interpolated PVCs (14,356 beats), some in couplets, some in bigeminy and trigeminy, 2 runs of nonsustained VT, the longest consisting of 5 PVCs  
Coronary angiography: LMT normal; LAD segment 6 stenosis 85%, segment 7 stenosis 80%; mid-segment in-stent stenosis of the prior LCX stent 85% (ISR); RCA segments 1–2 diffuse stenosis 30–50%.  
Two stents were implanted in the LAD, and balloon dilatation was performed within the LCX stent. Postoperatively, secondary prevention therapy for coronary artery disease was given: metoprolol sustained-release tablets 23.75 mg/day, amiodarone 600 mg/day, potassium and magnesium supplementation

Post-coronary angiography repeat Holter monitoring: sinus bradycardia, mean heart rate 57 beats/min, frequent PVCs and short runs of VT

A single-chamber ICD (BIOTRONIK Iforia 7 VR-T DX) was implanted for sudden death prevention. ICD settings were as follows: mode = VDI; VT1/VT2/VF detection rate = 167/180/200 BPM; detection/redetection counter = VT1: 40/30, and VT2: 16/14.

Beginning the night after ICD implantation, the patient developed recurrent palpitations without hemodynamic compromise. The ICD delivered frequent ATP therapy and delivered 2 shocks. The patient received amiodarone continuous infusion, intravenous continuous infusion of a β-blocker, potassium and magnesium supplementation, and symptomatic treatment. Symptoms gradually improved after 1 hour.

The patient was given oral amiodarone and metoprolol sustained-release tablets and was followed up after discharge. There was no recurrence over the following nearly 4 months. Palpitations recurred 4 months later, and the oral amiodarone dose was increased again. Over the past nearly 1 month, ICD remote monitoring indicated frequent VT episodes that resolved spontaneously or converted to sinus rhythm after ATP therapy. The patient was therefore recalled by telephone and was admitted for a second time on 2019.9.23. Remote monitoring prior to admission indicated 3 additional VT episodes, and the patient perceived 1 ICD shock.

Second admission:
Electrophysiological study:
During tachycardia, the CS electrode recorded two V waves occurring regularly after each A wave  
The His-bundle electrode showed two H and V waves occurring after each A wave, respectively  
AH2 interval − AH1 interval = 280 ms  
H1V1 interval = H2V2 interval = 80 ms  
With ventricular pacing, there was no VA retrograde conduction or only weak retrograde conduction

Diagnostic_evidence: History: Paroxysmal palpitations and shortness of breath for over 1 year, worsened for 1 week, accompanied by chest tightness and dyspnea, aggravated upon exertion, with intermittent dizziness
Ancillary examination: Electrophysiology study revealed two V waves regularly appearing after each A wave recorded by CS electrode
Ancillary examination: His bundle electrode demonstrated two H and V waves appearing after each A wave, with AH2 interval - AH1 interval = 280 ms (high specificity)
Ancillary examination: H1V1 interval = H2V2 interval = 80 ms (high specificity)
Ancillary examination: Ventricular pacing showed no VA retrograde conduction or weak retrograde conduction (excluding reentrant mechanism)

Ancillary examination: Coronary angiography revealed 85% stenosis of LAD segment 6 and 85% stenosis of the mid-segment of the LCX stent
Ancillary examination: Coronary angiography showed 85% stenosis of LAD segment 6, 80% stenosis of segment 7, and 30–50% diffuse stenosis of RCA segments 1–2

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Status post coronary artery stent implantation
Diagnostic_evidence: History: 1 stent was implanted in the LCX 3 years ago; during this admission, 2 stents were implanted in the LAD, and balloon angioplasty was performed within the LCX stent

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: In-stent restenosis of the coronary artery (85% stenosis of the mid-segment of the original LCX stent)
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Coronary angiography revealed 85% stenosis of the mid-segment of the original LCX stent (ISR)

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Chronic heart failure (NYHA functional class III)
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Worsening dyspnea upon exertion
Ancillary examination: Echocardiography showed LVEF 32% and LV dimension 55 mm (high specificity)
Ancillary examination: BNP 499 ng/L

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Frequent premature ventricular complexes
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Holter monitoring revealed frequent interpolated premature ventricular complexes (14,356 beats), some in couplets, and some in bigeminy and trigeminy patterns

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Non-sustained ventricular tachycardia
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Holter monitoring revealed 2 episodes of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, with the longest run composed of 5 premature ventricular complexes; post-ICD implantation follow-up still indicated frequent ventricular tachycardia

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC)

Ancillary examination: Echocardiography did not indicate right ventricular structural abnormalities (high specificity)

Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Electrocardiogram showed no QT interval prolongation (high specificity)

## History Summary
### Patient
Female, 60 years old

### Chief Complaint
Intermittent chest and back pain for 16 years

### History of Present Illness
The patient developed chest and back pain 16 years ago, which was aggravated after exertion and accompanied by dyspnea. Electrocardiogram showed “myocardial ischemia” (details unknown), with intermittent irregular treatment. Ten years ago, she was diagnosed with “hypertrophic cardiomyopathy” at an outside hospital and began oral diltiazem and other symptomatic treatment. During this period, due to recurrent symptoms, she visited multiple hospitals on multiple occasions. Recently, she was admitted to our department for further diagnosis and treatment. Recently, she has had no headache, dizziness, syncope, or transient visual blackout. She has long-term insomnia, fair appetite, poor mental status and physical strength, and intermittent constipation and diarrhea at baseline. For further diagnosis and treatment, she was readmitted to our hospital for treatment.
2022-03-07 Echocardiography (outside hospital): hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, decreased left ventricular diastolic function
2022-03-08 Contrast-enhanced cardiac MRI (outside hospital): hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, with myocardial fibrosis involving the anterior septum and the anterior wall of the left ventricle.
Patient, female, 60 years old at the time of visit in 2022.
At age 43 (2005), she began to have chest pain and back pain, which were persistent pain with paroxysmal severe exacerbations. Exertion could trigger worsening pain, and exertion was accompanied by dyspnea. ECG suggested myocardial ischemia (details of diagnosis and treatment unknown), with recurrent episodes.
At age 50 (2012), she was diagnosed with “hypertrophic cardiomyopathy” at an outside hospital and started oral diltiazem and other treatments; symptoms persisted with no obvious relief.
At age 60 (2022), in March, echocardiography and MRI at an outside hospital showed hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy. On June 20, she was hospitalized in the Department of Cardiology of our hospital for treatment.

### Past Medical History
No history of hypertension, coronary heart disease, diabetes mellitus, etc.

### Personal History
No history of smoking or alcohol consumption; no allergy history

### Marital/Obstetric and Menstrual History
Of appropriate reproductive age, with 1 son.

### Family History
Patient’s brother: echocardiography suggested changes of “hypertrophic cardiomyopathy”  
Patient’s son: echocardiography suggested changes of “hypertrophic cardiomyopathy”

## Physical Examination
① Temperature: 36.3°C, Heart rate: 76 beats/min, Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min, Blood pressure: 126/74 mmHg,  
② Breath sounds clear in both lungs; no dry or moist rales heard.  
③ Regular rhythm; a grade 3–4/6 systolic murmur heard at the left sternal border in the 3rd–4th intercostal spaces.  
④ Bilateral radial and femoral arteries with normal and symmetric strength; regular pulse. Good bilateral femoral artery pulsation; no vascular bruit; good dorsalis pedis artery pulsation bilaterally

## Initial Laboratory Tests
Complete blood count: WBC: 7.31*10^9/L, HGB: 134 g/L, PLT: 184*10^9/L  
Liver function: ALT: 15 U/L, AST: 33 U/L  
GGT: 23 U/L, TBIL: 10.6 umol/L, ALB: 46.5 g/L  
Renal function: Cr: 81 umol/L, eGFR: 68.4 ⬇️  
D-D: 0.22 ug/mL  
cTnI: 0.249 ng/mL ⬆️  
NT-proBNP: 5397 pg/mL ⬆️  
Coagulation indices, blood electrolytes, routine urinalysis and stool tests, thyroid function, and infectious disease indices showed no obvious abnormalities.

## Initial Examinations
Admission ancillary examination: Electrocardiogram ECG showed sinus rhythm, HR 57 beats/min, left ventricular hypertrophy, ST-T changes, short P-R interval.  
Admission ancillary examination: Holter monitoring Sinus bradycardia (average HR: 58 beats/min), sinus arrhythmia; occasional premature atrial contractions, paired premature atrial contractions, short runs of atrial tachycardia, atrial bigeminy; occasional premature ventricular contractions, paired multiform premature ventricular contractions; ST-T changes;  

Ancillary examination: Echocardiography 2022.6 Ultrasound findings: diffuse marked thickening of the left ventricular myocardium, abnormal myocardial echogenicity, mid-ventricular obstruction of the left ventricle; decreased left ventricular diastolic function (Grade I); normal left ventricular ejection fraction;  

### Additional Examinations
Cardiac MRI: diffuse left ventricular myocardial hypertrophy; fibrosis of the left ventricular septal wall and anterior wall; please correlate clinically. Left heart enlargement, marked left atrial enlargement. Normal resting left ventricular systolic function.  
Echocardiography: Parasternal long-axis view: diffuse marked thickening of the interventricular septum and left ventricular posterior wall; myocardial echoes fine and enhanced. Parasternal short-axis view: diffuse marked thickening of the myocardium of all left ventricular walls; myocardial echoes fine and enhanced; marked papillary muscle hypertrophy. Subxiphoid four-chamber view: marked thickening of the inferior wall myocardium of the right ventricle; myocardial echoes fine and enhanced. Apical four-chamber view: biventricular myocardial thickening; increased mid-left ventricular flow velocity. Apical three-chamber view: diffuse marked thickening of left ventricular myocardium; increased mid-left ventricular flow velocity. Continuous-wave Doppler: mid-left ventricular obstruction, peak velocity ~3.9 m/s, peak pressure gradient ~61 mmHg. Decreased diastolic function: mitral valve E/A = 0.6; TDI e′ (septal) = 3.5 cm/s; TDI e′ (lateral) = 4.5 cm/s; E/e′ = 12.5. Reduced global longitudinal strain of the left ventricle, with more significant reduction in the anterior septum and anterior wall. Patchy delayed enhancement of the left ventricular anterior wall and anterior septum;

### Supplementary History
① Neurological symptoms: Since childhood, the patient has had numbness in the limbs, tip of the tongue, and lips, with occasional tingling pain; cannot tolerate excessive cold or heat; pain symptoms become more obvious after exposure to excessive cold or heat.  
② Fundus manifestations: The patient occasionally has conjunctival hemorrhage.  
③ Gastrointestinal symptoms: The patient had severe constipation from 2008 to 2010; severe diarrhea from 2020 to 2022, with up to 10 bowel movements per day. Overall, there is a trend of alternating constipation and diarrhea.

**Abnormal items listed:**  
1. Biomarker (Lyso-GL-3): 6.47 ng/mL (reference range: <1.11 ng/mL)  
2. α-galactosidase A (GLA): 1.24 μmol/L/h (reference range: 2.40–17.65 μmol/L/h)  

Follow-up—Enzymatic and biomarker tests:
**Abnormal items listed:**  
1. α-galactosidase A (GLA):  
- Pre-treatment: 1.24 μmol/L/h (reference range: 2.40–17.65 μmol/L/h)  
- Post-treatment: 1.22 μmol/L/h (reference range: 2.40–17.65 μmol/L/h)  
2. Biomarker (Lyso-GL-3):  
- Pre-treatment: 6.47 ng/mL (reference range: <1.11 ng/mL)  
- Post-treatment: 3.48 ng/mL (reference range: <1.11 ng/mL)  
3. NT-ProBNP:  
- Pre-treatment: 5397 pg/mL (reference range not explicitly stated; typically >125 pg/mL is abnormal)  
- Post-treatment: 3619 pg/mL (decreased from baseline, but still markedly elevated)  

Genetic testing result: The examinee carries a pathogenic mutation with X-linked inheritance: a heterozygous variant in the GLA gene, c.640-801G>A

## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Medical History Summary
### Patient
Female, 43 years old

### Chief Complaint
Intermittent headache for more than 5 years, blurred vision for 6 months, worsened for 1 day.

### History of Present Illness
More than 5 years ago, the patient developed intermittent headaches without an obvious trigger, mostly located in the right frontal region, accompanied by palpitations and diaphoresis; no dizziness or blurred vision; no nausea or vomiting; no chest pain or abdominal pain; no fatigue, hirsutism, or changes in appearance. Symptoms could completely resolve after 3–5 minutes of rest. Thereafter, the above symptoms occurred 1–2 times per year; the nature and relieving factors were the same as before. The patient did not take it seriously and did not measure blood pressure.
More than 6 months ago, the patient noted increased frequency of headache episodes, occurring daily, with the same severity as before, accompanied by blurred vision and right ocular distending pain; symptoms lasted for several hours and then remitted. An outside hospital considered myopia as a possibility (blood pressure not measured); symptoms did not improve after wearing myopic glasses.
One day ago, headache recurred again with the same severity and nature as before, accompanied by palpitations and profuse sweating; a shadow appeared in the right eye. The patient came to our hospital for planned fundus fluorescein angiography; systolic blood pressure was measured at 212 mmHg, and the patient was admitted to the CCU for further diagnosis and treatment. Since onset, mental status has been fair; sleep and appetite have been poor; stool is dry; urination shows no abnormalities, with normal urine volume; no significant change in body weight.

### Past Medical History
Thirteen years ago, due to scanty menstruation, cranial MRI and sex hormone testing revealed a pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma). Bromocriptine 1.25 mg qd was administered. Bromocriptine was discontinued after repeat sex hormone testing and cranial MRI more than 1 year ago.

### Personal History
Born in Hebei Province, long-term resident locally. No history of exposure to chemical substances, radioactive substances, or toxic substances. No history of illicit drug use. No history of smoking or alcohol consumption.

### Marital/Obstetric and Menstrual History
Menarche at age 13. Menstrual cycle 45 days, duration 7 days; scanty and irregular menses. Married, with 1 daughter; daughter and spouse are healthy.

### Family History
Father developed hypertension in his 40s; mother developed hypertension in her 50s. Grandfather had lung cancer; grandmother had breast cancer.

## Physical Examination
T 36.2°C, P 94 bpm, RR 18 bpm, BP 180/119 mmHg  
Height 161 cm, weight 60 kg, BMI 23.1 kg/m²; cool extremities  
Breath sounds clear bilaterally, no dry or moist rales; cardiac borders not enlarged, regular rhythm, A2>P2, no murmurs in any valve auscultation areas, no pericardial friction rub, and no extra heart sounds;  
Abdomen soft, bowel sounds 4/min, no tenderness or rebound tenderness.  
No edema in both lower extremities.  
Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring: 24-hour mean BP (mmHg): 162/98  
Daytime mean BP (mmHg): 158/96  
Nighttime mean BP (mmHg): 174/103  
Morning mean BP (mmHg): 170/107  
Higher BP at night and in the morning  
Limb arterial PWV test: vessels are stiffer compared with a healthy 45-year-old female

## Initial Laboratory Tests
Complete blood count: WBC 9.09×10^9/L, RBC 5.46×10^12/L, HGB 121 g/L, PLT 405×10^9/L  
Biochemistry: Na 142.7 mmol/L, K 3.69 mmol/L, Cr 65 umol/L, ALT 21 U/L  
Lipids: T-CHO 5.94 (mmol/L), TG 0.52 (mmol/L), HDL-C 2.21 (mmol/L), LDL-C 2.85 (mmol/L) ↑  
Blood glucose: Glu 6.7 (mmol/L) ↑  
Cardiopulmonary markers: D-dimer <0.15 μg/mL, NT-proBNP 634 pg/mL  
Thyroid function: TT3 1.01 (ng/mL), TT4 6.8 (ug/dL), FT3 2.66 (pg/mL), FT4 0.9 (ng/dL),  
TSH 0.59 (uIU/mL), TGAb 54 (IU/mL) ↑; TPOAb >1300.0 U/mL ↑  
Urinalysis: urine occult blood -, urine protein 1+; urine protein quantification: 24-hour urine protein 203.4 (mg/24 hr) ↑

## Initial Examinations
ECG diagnostic conclusion: 1. Sinus rhythm 2. T-wave abnormality  
Bedside chest radiograph: 2023-7-25 bedside chest radiograph  
Impression: Increased bilateral lung markings; bilateral hilar shadows not enlarged; mediastinum not widened; cardiac silhouette not enlarged; both hemidiaphragms smooth; bilateral costophrenic angles relatively sharp.  
Conclusion: Increased bilateral lung markings; please correlate clinically.  
Echocardiography: intracardiac structures essentially normal  
LVEF 69%  
Normal right ventricular systolic function  
Normal inferior vena cava diameter and respiratory variation rate  

Ambulatory blood pressure: higher BP at night and in the morning  
Limb arterial PWV test:  
**List of abnormal items:**  
1. **Right upper arm blood pressure**  
   - Systolic: 175 mmHg (reference range: <120 mmHg)  
   - Diastolic: 105 mmHg (reference range: <80 mmHg)  
2. **Left upper arm blood pressure**  
   - Systolic: 178 mmHg (reference range: <120 mmHg)  
   - Diastolic: 104 mmHg (reference range: <80 mmHg)  
3. **Right ankle blood pressure**  
   - Systolic: 202 mmHg (reference range: <120 mmHg)  
   - Diastolic: 96 mmHg (reference range: <80 mmHg)  
4. **Left ankle blood pressure**  
   - Systolic: 198 mmHg (reference range: <120 mmHg)  
   - Diastolic: 95 mmHg (reference range: <80 mmHg)  
5. **baPWV (brachial–ankle pulse wave velocity)**  
   - Right lower limb: 1788 cm/s (reference range: healthy females <1400 cm/s)  
   - Left lower limb: 1938 cm/s (reference range: healthy females <1400 cm/s)  

Vascular stiffness (baPWV) is higher (stiffer) compared with a healthy 43-year-old female.  
Lower-limb vascular occlusion status (ABI): results of this examination are within the normal range.

## Additional History
### Additional Laboratory Tests
RAAS (supine): plasma renin activity 6.99 (ng/mL/h) ↑, angiotensin II 66.61 (pg/mL) ↑, aldosterone 207.74 (pg/mL) ↑;  
Catecholamines and metabolites: epinephrine 16.6 (pg/mL), norepinephrine 9545.29 (pg/mL), dopamine 19.9 (pg/mL), metanephrine 35.21 (pg/mL), normetanephrine (NMN) 6624.44 (pg/mL) ↑, 3-methoxytyramine 10.67 (pg/mL);  
24-hour urinary VMA (vanillylmandelic acid; 3-methoxy-4-hydroxymandelic acid) 13.5 mg/L, urine volume 1800 mL  
Parathyroid hormone test: normal  
Cortisol rhythm:  
|   | ACTH | Cortisol |
|-------|------|--------|
| 8am   | 20.2 | 20     |
| 16pm  | 6.2  | 13.7   |
| 0am   | 23.3 | 15.0   |
| 8am   | 12.3 | 13.4   |
| 16pm  | 6.7  | 4      |
| 0am   | 10.6 | 6.3    |

### Additional Examinations
Contrast-enhanced abdominopelvic CT: no obvious abnormalities in the liver, gallbladder, or pancreas.  
Bilateral adrenal glands are of acceptable size; no obvious abnormal density.  
A mixed-density mass is seen posterior to the retroperitoneal horizontal (third) portion of the duodenum, with heterogeneous enhancement, measuring approximately 4.4 cm × 4.7 cm.  
Both kidneys are of acceptable size and morphology. A round-like, non-enhancing low-density lesion is seen at the lower pole of the right kidney, with a diameter of approximately 6 mm. No obvious hydronephrosis/dilatation or signs of calculi.  
PET-CT:  
Retroperitoneal space-occupying lesion with hypermetabolism; neurogenic tumor considered.  
Multiple foci of increased metabolic activity in brown adipose tissue throughout the body.  
No abnormal hypermetabolic findings in the brain.  
Diffuse hypermetabolism in both thyroid lobes; recommend correlation with thyroid function tests and ultrasound examination.  
Possible inflammatory reactive lymph nodes in the bilateral supraclavicular regions.  
Thyroid ultrasound: diffuse thyroid parenchymal disease—consistent with C-TIRADS category 1; Hashimoto’s thyroiditis considered.  
Heart: echocardiography shows no secondary changes such as cardiac hypertrophy or enlargement;  
Brain: cranial MRI suggests mild cerebral white matter demyelination; no typical manifestations of hemorrhagic or ischemic stroke;  
Kidneys: 24-hour urine protein quantification 203.4 (mg/24 hr) ↑; urine specific gravity normal; no manifestations of impaired concentrating function due to tubular injury such as increased nocturia;  
Eyes: ophthalmology consultation: hypertensive retinopathy in both eyes (grade III), right retinal disease;  
Vessels: arteriosclerosis

Subsequently, surgical resection of the tumor was performed. Postoperative immunohistochemistry results: Syn (+), CgA (+), GATA3 (+), S-100 (sustentacular cells +), SDHB (+), P53 (wild type), Ki-67 (5%), INSM1 (+), CAIX (-), FH (+), Inhibin-a (-), Ret (-)  
Peripheral blood genetic testing: 1. Through sequencing analysis of disease-related genes, no definite pathogenic variants related to the disease phenotype were identified.  
2. No large fragment deletions/duplications were detected (CNVs analysis results).  
3. No pathogenic genomic variants related to the subject’s clinical phenotype were detected.

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Retroperitoneal paraganglioma (PGL)
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Intermittent headache for over 5 years, accompanied by palpitations and diaphoresis, relieved by rest; increased headache frequency over the past 6 months with blurred vision and right ocular distension pain; 1 day prior, headache worsened with palpitations, profuse sweating, and blood pressure of 212 mmHg
Physical examination: BP 180/119 mmHg, cold extremities
Ancillary tests: Catecholamine metabolites significantly elevated (norepinephrine 9545.29 pg/mL, normetanephrine 6624.44 pg/mL)
Ancillary tests: Contrast-enhanced abdominopelvic CT and PET-CT revealed a retroperitoneal heterogeneous density mass with hypermetabolic activity, consistent with neurogenic tumor
Ancillary tests: Postoperative immunohistochemistry Syn (+), CgA (+), INSM1 (+), supporting the diagnosis of paraganglioma
Ancillary tests: Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring showed higher nocturnal and early morning blood pressure, correlated with catecholamine secretion rhythm
Ancillary tests: 24-hour urinary VMA 13.5 mg/L ↑

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Pituitary prolactinoma
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma) was discovered 13 years ago due to oligomenorrhea, treated with bromocriptine
Ancillary tests: Cranial MRI indicated pituitary adenoma

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Hashimoto's thyroiditis
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary tests: Thyroid function tests showed TPOAb >1300.0 U/mL ↑, TGAb 54 IU/mL ↑
Ancillary tests: Thyroid ultrasound demonstrated diffuse parenchymal changes consistent with probable Hashimoto's thyroiditis

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Hypertensive retinopathy
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Blurred vision for over 6 months, with scotoma in the right eye
Ancillary tests: Ophthalmology consultation confirmed bilateral hypertensive retinopathy (Stage III)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Primary hypertension
Supporting_evidence: History: Family history of hypertension (both parents with hypertension)
Ancillary tests: Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring showed significantly elevated blood pressure throughout the day
Non-supporting_evidence: History: Paroxysmal headache accompanied by palpitations and diaphoresis, suggestive of secondary hypertension
Ancillary tests: Presence of a definite retroperitoneal catecholamine-secreting tumor
Ancillary tests: Paroxysmal hypertension with headache, palpitations, and diaphoresis, typical manifestations of catecholamine crisis
Recommended_corresponding_tests: Family history, ambulatory blood pressure monitoring

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Primary aldosteronism
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary tests: Supine RAAS showed aldosterone 207.74 pg/mL ↑, plasma renin activity 6.99 ng/mL/h ↑
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary tests: Bilateral adrenal CT showed no abnormal density lesions
Ancillary tests: Serum potassium was normal (3.69 mmol/L)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Pheochromocytoma
Supporting_evidence: History: Paroxysmal hypertension accompanied by headache, palpitations, and diaphoresis
Ancillary tests: Normetanephrine 6624.44 pg/mL ↑
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary tests: Tumor located in the retroperitoneum rather than the adrenal gland (CT showed normal adrenal glands)
Ancillary tests: Immunohistochemistry SDHB (+) more supportive of PGL

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Cushing syndrome
Supporting_evidence: History: History of pituitary adenoma
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary tests: Normal cortisol circadian rhythm, no abnormal elevation of ACTH
Physical examination: Absence of characteristic findings such as central obesity or purple striae

## Medical History Summary
### Patient
Female, 26 years old

### Chief Complaint
Shortness of breath and cyanosis for more than 20 years

### History of Present Illness
The patient reported having had lower exercise tolerance than peers since childhood, experiencing shortness of breath with strenuous activity, without receiving specific diagnosis or treatment. During adolescence, shortness of breath gradually became more obvious, and she felt shortness of breath with slight activity. She subsequently visited local hospitals multiple times and was diagnosed as “etiology investigation of shortness of breath and cyanosis”; after treatment, her symptoms did not improve significantly. She presented to our hospital for further diagnosis and treatment and was admitted to our department with a provisional diagnosis of “etiology investigation of shortness of breath and cyanosis.” Since onset, the patient’s mental status has been fair; appetite normal; no significant change in body weight; urination and defecation normal.

### Past Medical History
No special history.

### Obstetric/Gynecologic and Menstrual History
Gravida 1, Para 1; daughter healthy.

## Physical Examination
T: 36.3°C, P: 109 beats/min, R: 19 breaths/min, BP: 92/72 mmHg, SpO2 55%. Generalized skin cyanosis; cutaneous and mucosal capillary dilatation/telangiectasia visible; clubbing of fingers (toes). Alert. Breath sounds clear in both lungs; no dry or moist rales. Palpation: apical impulse normal; no thrill at the apex; no pericardial friction rub on palpation. Cardiac dullness normal on percussion. Heart rate 109 beats/min, regular rhythm; normal heart sounds without splitting; P2 > A2; no extra heart sounds or murmurs. Abdomen soft, no tenderness or rebound tenderness; liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin; bowel sounds normal. No edema in both lower extremities.

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension
Supporting_evidence: History: Progressively worsening exertional dyspnea

Ancillary investigations: Echocardiography showed no evidence of right ventricular hypertrophy or direct signs of pulmonary arterial hypertension
Recommended_corresponding_tests: Right heart catheterization (etiology of pulmonary arteriovenous fistula already confirmed by imaging)

raw_query:
## Brief Medical History
### Patient
Female, 77 years old

### Chief Complaint
Recurrent dyspnea for 1 year after atrial fibrillation procedure

### History of Present Illness
2013: Shortness of breath after activity, accompanied by cough and expectoration of white mucoid sputum; worsened in winter/spring and after colds. The condition recurred repeatedly, with gradually decreased exercise tolerance. No medical evaluation or treatment was sought.

2015: Diagnosed at an outside hospital as “chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)”. Thereafter, she was hospitalized on average every 2 years due to acute exacerbations. She did not receive regular maintenance inhalation therapy outside the hospital.

2021-05: Hospitalized in the Department of Respiratory Medicine of our hospital. During hospitalization, pulmonary function test: moderate-to-severe obstructive ventilatory defect. Chest CT: multiple enlarged lymph nodes in the mediastinum and bilateral hila; multiple lobar/segmental bronchi compressed and narrowed; partial atelectasis of the right middle lobe and left upper lobe; enlarged pulmonary artery. Bronchoscopy: multiple areas of mucosal pigmentation in bilateral bronchi; multiple bronchial stenoses. After discharge, home oxygen therapy was initiated, and she used Budegofu inhalation irregularly.

The patient developed palpitations in 2021. In February 2022, she visited our outpatient clinic for “palpitations”; ECG suggested paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and she subsequently underwent radiofrequency catheter ablation for atrial fibrillation in our department.

After surgery, dyspnea repeatedly worsened. She was hospitalized at outside hospitals in 2022-05, 2022-08, 2023-02, and 2023-03 due to aggravated shortness of breath.

2023-04: Without obvious trigger, cough, sputum production, and dyspnea worsened again. Marked wheezing occurred with minimal activity; dyspnea and cough were significantly aggravated in the supine position. Symptoms were not significantly relieved after inhalation of Budegofu. She was admitted from our outpatient clinic with “cardiac insufficiency”.

2021-5-23 Chest CT (lung window):
**List of abnormal findings:**  
1. A 1.5 cm round-like nodule in the right upper lobe (smooth margin)  
2. Patchy high-density opacity in the left lower lobe (inhomogeneous density)  
3. Multiple small nodules in both lungs (largest in the right middle lobe, diameter 0.8 cm)  
4. A 2.8 cm lobulated soft-tissue density mass in the right middle lobe (short spiculation sign at the margin)  
5. Blunting of the left costophrenic angle with locally decreased lucency (suspected pleural effusion)  
6. Diffuse coarse and disordered lung markings in both lungs (more prominent in the right lower lobe)  

2021-5-23 Chest CT (mediastinal window):
**List of abnormal findings:**  
1. **Soft-tissue density lesion in the right hilar region** (approximately 4.5 × 3.2 cm, margin not smooth);  
2. **Multiple enlarged right paratracheal lymph nodes** (maximum diameter approximately 2.5 cm, partially conglomerated);  
3. **Patchy high-density opacity in the left upper lung** (ill-defined margin, approximately 3.0 × 2.8 cm);  
4. **Soft-tissue density nodule above the aortic arch** (approximately 1.8 cm, heterogeneous density);  
5. **Small pericardial effusion**;  
6. **Left pleural thickening with pleural effusion** (loculated, maximum thickness approximately 1.2 cm).  

Review of the atrial fibrillation radiofrequency ablation operative record (date of procedure: February 16, 2022)
Left and right pulmonary vein angiography was performed. ACT was monitored every 30 minutes intraoperatively, and heparin was supplemented according to ACT. The preoperative rhythm was atrial fibrillation. A PentaRay catheter was used to establish a left atrial model with FAN. An irrigated ablation catheter (orange-handle) was used, with settings of 55°C, 40 W, and saline irrigation at 17 mL/min for ablation. Sufentanil was administered intravenously for analgesia. Radiofrequency ablation was performed along the antra of the bilateral pulmonary veins, achieving bilateral circumferential pulmonary vein isolation. Intraoperatively, atrial flutter occurred; CARTO mapping was used to establish a right atrial model. The activation map showed a counterclockwise macroreentrant circuit in the right atrium. A cavotricuspid isthmus line ablation was performed again, with continuous ablation for 800 s. External electrical cardioversion at 200 J was performed, restoring sinus rhythm; heart rate: 75 beats/min. Mapping with a PentaRay catheter within the bilateral pulmonary veins showed no potentials, achieving bilateral pulmonary vein electrical isolation and cavotricuspid isthmus line ablation. The catheters were removed.

2022-2-14 Chest CT
Arterial phase:
**List of abnormal findings:**  
1. **Focal thickening of the aortic arch** (approximately 4.2 mm, irregular wall);  
2. **Soft-tissue density lesion in the right hilar region** (approximately 3.2 × 2.8 cm, spiculated margin);  
3. **Patchy high-density opacity in the left upper lung** (approximately 2.5 × 2.0 cm, ill-defined margin with cavitation);  
4. **Multiple enlarged right paratracheal lymph nodes** (maximum diameter approximately 1.8 cm, ring enhancement);  
5. **Small pericardial effusion** (surrounding the lateral wall of the left ventricle);

## Brief Medical History

Female, 77 years old

Recurrent dyspnea for 1 year after atrial fibrillation procedure

### History of Present Illness
2013: Shortness of breath after activity, accompanied by cough and expectoration of white mucoid sputum; worsened in winter/spring and after colds. The condition recurred repeatedly, with gradually decreased exercise tolerance. No medical evaluation or treatment was sought.

2015: Diagnosed at an outside hospital as “chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)”. Thereafter, she was hospitalized on average every 2 years due to acute exacerbations. She did not receive regular maintenance inhalation therapy outside the hospital.

2021-05: Hospitalized in the Department of Respiratory Medicine of our hospital. During hospitalization, pulmonary function test: moderate-to-severe obstructive ventilatory defect. Chest CT: multiple enlarged lymph nodes in the mediastinum and bilateral hila; multiple lobar/segmental bronchi compressed and narrowed; partial atelectasis of the right middle lobe and left upper lobe; enlarged pulmonary artery. Bronchoscopy: multiple areas of mucosal pigmentation in bilateral bronchi; multiple bronchial stenoses. After discharge, home oxygen therapy was initiated, and she used Budegofu inhalation irregularly.

The patient developed palpitations in 2021. In February 2022, she visited our outpatient clinic for “palpitations”; ECG suggested paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and she subsequently underwent radiofrequency catheter ablation for atrial fibrillation in our department.

After surgery, dyspnea repeatedly worsened. She was hospitalized at outside hospitals in 2022-05, 2022-08, 2023-02, and 2023-03 due to aggravated shortness of breath.

2023-04: Without obvious trigger, cough, sputum production, and dyspnea worsened again. Marked wheezing occurred with minimal activity; dyspnea and cough were significantly aggravated in the supine position. Symptoms were not significantly relieved after inhalation of Budegofu. She was admitted from our outpatient clinic with “cardiac insufficiency”.

Left and right pulmonary vein angiography was performed. ACT was monitored every 30 minutes intraoperatively, and heparin was supplemented according to ACT.
