## Medical History Summary
### Patient
Female, 53 years old

### Chief Complaint
Recurrent chest tightness and dyspnea for more than 2 years, exacerbated with fever for 1 day

### History of Present Illness
More than 2 years ago, the patient developed chest tightness and dyspnea after activity, accompanied by chest pain, occasional cough and sputum production, relieved after rest. Subsequently, symptoms worsened, occurring mostly at night, accompanied by bilateral eyelid edema. She presented to a “tertiary hospital in Jinan” and underwent relevant examinations. She was diagnosed with “myocardial lesion and heart failure” and was given symptomatic treatment including cardiotonic agents, diuretics, vasodilators, dexamethasone, IVIG, etc. She improved and was discharged.
Over the past more than 2 years, the patient had repeated hospitalizations due to “chest tightness and dyspnea.” Five months ago, for further clarification of the condition, she presented to a “tertiary hospital in Beijing.”
Muscle biopsy pathology (left quadriceps femoris): scattered individual CD4+ cells around individual small vessels in the endomysium and perimysium, a small number of CD68+ cells, mild increase of MHC-I expression on the sarcolemma of a small number of muscle fibers; anti-C5b-9 staining: positive granules around some muscle fibers, positive staining in a small number of intermuscular capillaries. Pathological conclusion: myogenic changes with abnormal muscle fiber proportion; in combination with immunohistochemical staining, immune-mediated etiology cannot be excluded.
An ICD was implanted due to “short runs of ventricular tachycardia,” and immunosuppressants and glucocorticoids were added to the treatment regimen.
After discharge, the patient regularly took heart failure medications such as “Entresto, carvedilol, furosemide, spironolactone,” etc., as well as prednisone acetate tablets. Five months ago, she was hospitalized for “bacteremia,” improved and was discharged. One day ago, after catching a chill, symptoms such as chest tightness and dyspnea worsened significantly, accompanied by cough, expectoration of white sputum, fever, and profuse sweating; temperature 38°C, with limb weakness, unable to lie flat. She self-administered “amoxicillin” without symptom relief, and then presented to the Emergency Department of our hospital. For further diagnosis and treatment, she was admitted to our department from the outpatient clinic with “heart failure.”

### Past Medical History
History of “subclinical hypothyroidism” for 2 years; self-administered “levothyroxine sodium tablets” 25 µg/day

### Personal History
Denies smoking and alcohol use

### Marital/Fertility and Menstrual History
One son; spouse and son are both in good health

### Family History
Both parents alive; denies a family history of major hereditary diseases

## Physical Examination
T 36.2°C, P 76 bpm, R 20 breaths/min, BP 107/77 mmHg  
Clear consciousness, poor general condition  
Coarse breath sounds in both lungs; moist rales at both lung bases  
Cardiac border enlarged; heart rate 76 bpm; irregular rhythm; premature beats audible; grade 3 systolic blowing murmur at the apex  
Mild pitting edema of both lower extremities

## Preliminary Laboratory Tests
Outside hospital: presented to a “tertiary hospital in Jinan”  
CK 1008 U/L, CK-MB 27 ng/mL; cTnI 188.22 ng/L; NT-proBNP 5066 pg/mL; CRP 30.62 g/L; myositis antibody panel: Ro-52 (+++), ANA 1:160; ANCA (-); rubella, herpes simplex virus, cytomegalovirus, and toxoplasma IgM (-); neuromuscular disease–related genetic testing (-).

Emergency department on admission  
Laboratory results: BNP 1170.0 pg/mL; CBC: WBC 10.55×10^9/L, neutrophil percentage 0.770, RBC 3.46×10^12/L, hemoglobin 98 g/L, platelet count 231×10^9/L; D-dimer 0.77 mg/L.

## Preliminary Examinations
Outside hospital: presented to a “tertiary hospital in Jinan”  
Echocardiography: LVEF 30%, LV 54 mm. Muscle biopsy pathology: consistent with myogenic injury (inflammatory myopathy to be ruled out)  
Outside hospital: “tertiary hospital in Beijing”  
Echocardiography: LVEF 22%, LV 58 mm

Electrocardiogram: sinus rhythm, partial T-wave changes  
Echocardiography: cardiomyopathy (etiology to be correlated clinically)  
EF: 30%  
LV 54 mm; left atrial and right atrial enlargement  
Mitral regurgitation (moderate)  
Tricuspid regurgitation (mild)  
Decreased left ventricular systolic function  
Abnormal left ventricular restrictive filling  
Pericardial effusion (trace)  
Left ventricular false tendon  
Coronary CTA: mild stenosis of the proximal LAD; etiology considered atherosclerotic  
Enlarged left atrium and left ventricle; please correlate clinically  
Chest and abdominal CT: a few fibrotic foci in both lungs; calcified lesion in the left lung  
Pulmonary bullae in the right lung  
Cardiomegaly, coronary artery calcification, pericardial effusion  
Localized bilateral pleural thickening  
Bilateral pleural effusions  
Abdominal CT: no abnormalities detected  
Cardiac MRI: enlarged left ventricle, decreased LV systolic function  
Multiple subepicardial myocardial perfusion reductions in the interventricular septum and LV myocardium, ischemia considered  
Abnormal delayed enhancement in the mid-segment of the interventricular septum and mid-segment of the inferior LV wall; possible fibrosis; correlate clinically; dilated cardiomyopathy to be ruled out  
Mitral regurgitation  
Small amount of pericardial effusion  
Muscle MRI: multiple abnormal signals in muscle groups of bilateral thighs, calves, upper arms, and gluteal muscles; myositis/myopathy? Further examination recommended  
Muscle biopsy pathology: scattered individual CD4+ cells around individual small vessels in the endomysium and perimysium, a small number of CD68+ cells, mild increase of MHC-I expression on the sarcolemma of a small number of muscle fibers; anti-C5b-9 staining: positive granules around some muscle fibers, positive staining in a small number of intermuscular capillaries  
Pathological conclusion: myogenic changes with abnormal muscle fiber proportion; in combination with immunohistochemical staining, immune-mediated etiology cannot be excluded

## Supplementary Medical History
### Supplementary Laboratory Tests
CBC: 10.55*10^9/mL  
Blood lipids: LDL-C 2.25 mmol/L  
Coagulation profile: D-dimer 0.77 mg/L  
Cardiac markers: troponin I 0.11 ng/mL, B-type natriuretic peptide 1170.0 pg/mL  
Rheumatology/immunology panel: C-reactive protein 31.6 mg/L, ANA 1:100 positive, cytoplasmic granular pattern, anti-Ro-52 antibody +++  
Myocardial enzymes: CK 2961.00 U/L, CK-MB 73.78 U/L, LDH 564.00 U/L, AST 80.80 U/L  
Routine urinalysis and stool test, liver function, renal function, electrolytes, blood glucose, and thyroid function tests (five items) (-)

Cardiac transplantation performed 2 months later. Postoperative pathology: heart, weight 284 g; chamber dilatation; uneven hypertrophy of cardiomyocytes with enlarged, hyperchromatic nuclei; degeneration and necrosis of some cardiomyocytes; fibrous tissue proliferation between cardiomyocytes in some areas; abundant lymphocyte and plasma cell infiltration throughout the full-thickness interstitium. Valvular fibrous tissue proliferation with focal hyaline degeneration and myxoid degeneration. Mild fibrous tissue proliferation in some areas of the coronary arteries, with hyaline degeneration.
Immunohistochemistry: CD38 (abundant +), CD138 (abundant +), IgG (partial +), IgG4 (50%+, highest area count approximately 53/HPF), IgG4/IgG (approximately 50%)

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Inflammatory myopathy-associated cardiomyopathy
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Recurrent chest tightness and dyspnea for over 2 years, with exacerbation accompanied by fever for 1 day; chest tightness and dyspnea on exertion, nocturnal episodes, bilateral eyelid edema; ICD implantation performed 5 months prior due to nonsustained ventricular tachycardia, with subsequent addition of immunosuppressants and corticosteroid therapy
History: Symptom improvement following immunosuppressant and corticosteroid therapy
History: Muscle biopsy revealed myopathic changes with immunohistochemistry suggesting immune-mediated etiology (CD4+, CD68+ cell infiltration and C5b-9 deposition)
Physical examination: Moist rales at bilateral lung bases, cardiac enlargement, grade 3/6 systolic blowing murmur at the apex, mild pitting edema of bilateral lower extremities
Ancillary investigations: Significantly elevated creatine kinase (2961 U/L) with Ro-52 antibody +++
Ancillary investigations: Antinuclear antibody positive at 1:160 (cytoplasmic granular pattern)
Ancillary investigations: Post-cardiac transplantation pathology revealed transmural myocardial interstitial infiltration with abundant lymphocytes and plasma cells, with an IgG4/IgG ratio of 50%
Ancillary investigations: Cardiac MRI showed delayed enhancement in the interventricular septum and mid-segment of the left ventricular inferior wall (characteristic of fibrosis)

Ancillary investigations: Echocardiography showed LVEF 30%, left ventricular dimension 54 mm, reduced left ventricular systolic function; cardiac MRI showed left ventricular enlargement with multifocal myocardial perfusion defects and delayed enhancement

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Chronic heart failure (HFrEF, NYHA Class IV)
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Recurrent hospitalizations for chest tightness and dyspnea, NT-proBNP 5066 pg/mL, BNP 1170.0 pg/mL
History: Recurrent paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea with bilateral lower extremity edema
Physical examination: Moist rales at bilateral lung bases, cardiac enlargement, bilateral lower extremity edema
Ancillary investigations: Echocardiography showed left ventricular enlargement (58 mm) with restrictive filling abnormality
Ancillary investigations: NT-proBNP 5066 pg/mL, EF persistently ≤30%

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Coronary atherosclerosis
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Coronary CTA showed mild stenosis of the proximal left anterior descending artery (atherosclerotic) with calcification
Ancillary investigations: Cardiac MRI showed abnormal delayed enhancement in the interventricular septum and mid-segment of the left ventricular inferior wall

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Status post ICD implantation
Diagnostic_evidence: History: ICD implantation performed due to nonsustained ventricular tachycardia

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Subclinical hypothyroidism
Diagnostic_evidence: History: 2-year history of subclinical hypothyroidism, self-administered levothyroxine sodium tablets 25 μg/day

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Dilated cardiomyopathy
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Echocardiography showed left ventricular enlargement (54 mm) with reduced LVEF (30%)
Ancillary investigations: Cardiac MRI showed reduced left ventricular systolic function and left ventricular enlargement (58 mm) with significantly reduced EF (22%)
Non-supporting_evidence: History: Effective response to immunotherapy
Ancillary investigations: Myositis antibody panel positive (Ro-52 +++)

Ancillary investigations: Post-cardiac transplantation pathology showed abundant lymphocyte and plasma cell infiltration in the transmural interstitium with significantly increased IgG4-positive cells
Ancillary investigations: Cardiac pathology demonstrated inflammatory cell infiltration rather than pure fibrosis

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Sarcoidosis
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Cardiac MRI showed multifocal myocardial perfusion defects and delayed enhancement
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: ANCA negative with no evidence of hilar lymphadenopathy
Ancillary investigations: Myocardial pathology showed no non-caseating granulomas, and significantly increased IgG4-positive cells more consistent with IgG4-related disease
Recommended_corresponding_tests: High-resolution chest CT for evaluation of hilar lymph nodes

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Viral myocarditis
Supporting_evidence: History: Fever accompanied by deterioration of cardiac function
Ancillary investigations: Elevated troponin I (0.11 ng/mL), elevated CK-MB (73.78 U/L)
Non-supporting_evidence: History: Prolonged disease course of 2 years with chronic progressive deterioration
Ancillary investigations: Rubella, herpes simplex virus, cytomegalovirus, and Toxoplasma IgM all negative
Ancillary investigations: Pathology demonstrated chronic inflammatory cell infiltration
Recommended_corresponding_tests: Myocardial viral PCR testing (note: limited sensitivity of negative results)

## Brief Medical History
### Patient
Male, 57 years old

### Chief Complaint
Chest tightness, shortness of breath, and palpitations after activity for more than 2 years

### History of Present Illness
The patient reported that more than 2 years ago he developed exertional shortness of breath, palpitations, and chest tightness, which could be relieved after rest. No fever, cough, or sputum; no chest pain or profuse sweating; no abdominal distension, nausea, or vomiting; no dizziness or syncope, etc. He was hospitalized and treated at a local hospital, after which the symptoms were relieved. Despite regular medication, symptoms still recurred and gradually worsened. The patient now presented to the outpatient clinic of our hospital for further diagnosis and treatment.

### Past Medical History
Diagnosed with “hypertrophic cardiomyopathy” 15 years ago; took medication for 1 year and then discontinued on his own (details unknown). Denies history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and coronary heart disease.

## Physical Examination
T: 36.4°C, P: 43 beats/min, R: 20 breaths/min, BP: 122/63 mmHg

Breath sounds coarse in both lungs; no obvious rales heard.

Cardiac borders not enlarged; HR 43 beats/min, regular rhythm; a grade 2/6–3/6 systolic murmur heard at the 3rd intercostal space along the left sternal border; no obvious murmurs heard in other valve areas.

No skin rash; no edema in both lower extremities.

## Preliminary Laboratory Tests
Admission laboratory indices  
**List of abnormal items:**

1. **White blood cell count (WBC)**  
- Result: 12.48 × 10⁹/L  
- Reference range: 3.5–9.5 × 10⁹/L  

2. **Red blood cell count (RBC)**  
- Result: 3.80 × 10¹²/L  
- Reference range: 4.3–5.8 × 10¹²/L  

3. **Hemoglobin (HGB)**  
- Result: 125.00 g/L  
- Reference range: 130–175 g/L  

### **Coagulation Panel (Four Items)**  
1. **Prothrombin time (PT)**  
- Result: 23.20 s  
- Reference range: 9–15 s  

2. **International normalized ratio (INR)**  
- Result: 2.06  
- Reference range: 0.8–1.4  

3. **Fibrinogen (FIB)**  
- Result: 5.08 g/L  
- Reference range: 2.00–5.00 g/L  

4. **Prothrombin activity (PTA)**  
- Result: 37%  
- Reference range: 70–130%  

### **Thyroid Function Tests (Five Items)**  
5. **Triiodothyronine (T3)**

- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];

    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Brief History of Present Illness
### Patient
Female, 34 years old

### Chief Complaint
Exertional dyspnea and fatigue with chest tightness for more than 5 months

### History of Present Illness
Five months ago, during menstruation, the patient developed exertional dyspnea and fatigue, accompanied by chest tightness, without symptoms such as bilateral lower-extremity edema, chest pain, cough, sputum production, dizziness, amaurosis, syncope, etc. Transthoracic echocardiography at an outside hospital indicated “massive pericardial effusion”; pericardiocentesis with drainage catheter placement was performed. After the procedure, the patient’s dyspnea and fatigue improved significantly.  
Routine testing and biochemistry of the pericardial effusion suggested weakly positive protein; no other abnormalities were noted.  
Tuberculosis antibody showed no obvious abnormality, acid-fast stain was negative; CA125: 250 U/mL (reference value <30 U/mL).  
The patient now has recurrent exertional dyspnea and fatigue with chest tightness. Transthoracic echocardiography in our outpatient clinic showed massive pericardial effusion. The patient was admitted from the outpatient clinic with “pericardial effusion”.

### Past Medical History
History of hypertension for 4 years, with a maximum blood pressure of 150/90 mmHg; treated with nifedipine controlled-release tablets 30 mg qd + losartan 50 mg qd for blood pressure control.  
Ten years ago, the patient underwent one cesarean section at a hospital in Chongqing. Several months postoperatively, two masses were found on the left side of the cesarean incision, gradually enlarging with pain; pain worsened during menstruation.  
Five years ago, for management of the masses on the left side of the cesarean incision, the patient underwent surgical excision at a hospital in Chongqing; the diameters were approximately 2 cm and 4 cm. Postoperative pathology indicated endometriosis. GnRH-a therapy was given for 3 injections; no regular follow-up ultrasound was performed.  
One year ago, the patient developed right-sided pain at the cesarean incision during each menstrual period, and reported palpable nodules; no medical consultation.  
Five months ago, the patient again developed chest tightness and dyspnea/fatigue; hospitalization revealed massive pericardial effusion. Pericardiocentesis was performed and specimens were sent for testing; SARS-CoV-2 was not detected. Subsequent follow-up ultrasound showed gradual increase in pericardial effusion.  
Two months ago, the patient visited the gynecology outpatient clinic at an outside hospital; PET-CT suggested a soft-tissue mass in the pericardial cavity at the lower left side of the main pulmonary artery trunk, with massive pericardial effusion; considerations: 1) endometriosis, 2) tumor. GnRH-a therapy was administered by gynecology.  
2023-02-15 First GnRH-a injection administered  
2023-03-01 Menstruation began; chest tightness and dyspnea/fatigue worsened during menstruation; no repeat ultrasound  
2023-03-15 After the second GnRH-a injection, the patient felt chest tightness and dyspnea/fatigue were somewhat relieved  
2023-03-20 Repeat ultrasound showed no significant change in the amount of pericardial effusion compared with before  
2023-04-01 Menstruation (end of menses) occurred, but the above symptoms again worsened; developed dyspnea on exertion when walking on level ground  
2023-04-11 Admitted to our department; repeat ultrasound still suggested massive pericardial effusion  
2023-04-12 Third GnRH-a injection administered

### Personal History
Denies smoking and alcohol use; denies exposure to radioactive substances and chemical toxins

### Marital/Reproductive History and Menstrual History
Menarche at age 13; menstrual duration 3–5 days; cycle 28 days; regular menses; moderate menstrual flow; dysmenorrhea, mild, without progressive worsening

### Family History
Nothing particular

## Physical Examination
W 85 kg, H 170 cm, BMI 29.4 kg/cm2  
T 36.6℃   P 123 beats/min   R 20 breaths/min   BP 142/96 mmHg  
On palpation, apical impulse weakened; on percussion, cardiac dullness border expanded bilaterally; heart rate 123 beats/min; heart sounds faint and distant.  
Jugular venous distension; no edema of bilateral lower extremities.  
A nodule approximately 2 cm in diameter palpable on the right side of the cesarean incision in the abdomen, without tenderness.  
Otherwise unremarkable.

## Supplementary History
### Supplementary Laboratory Tests
Ancillary examinations:  
History of upper respiratory infection before onset (×)  
Toxic symptoms, fever (×)

## Brief History of Present Illness
### Patient
Female, 34 years old

### Chief Complaint
Exertional dyspnea and fatigue with chest tightness for more than 5 months

### History of Present Illness
Five months ago, during menstruation, the patient developed exertional dyspnea and fatigue, accompanied by chest tightness, without symptoms such as bilateral lower-extremity edema, chest pain, cough, sputum production, dizziness, amaurosis, syncope, etc. Transthoracic echocardiography at an outside hospital indicated “massive pericardial effusion”; pericardiocentesis with drainage catheter placement was performed. After the procedure, the patient’s dyspnea and fatigue improved significantly.  
Routine testing and biochemistry of the pericardial effusion suggested weakly positive protein; no other abnormalities were noted.  
Tuberculosis antibody showed no obvious abnormality, acid-fast stain was negative; CA125: 250 U/mL (reference value <30 U/mL).  
The patient now has recurrent exertional dyspnea and fatigue with chest tightness. Transthoracic echocardiography in our outpatient clinic showed massive pericardial effusion. The patient was admitted from the outpatient clinic with “pericardial effusion”.

### Past Medical History
History of hypertension for 4 years, with a maximum blood pressure of 150/90 mmHg; treated with nifedipine controlled-release tablets 30 mg qd + losartan 50 mg qd for blood pressure control.  
Ten years ago, the patient underwent one cesarean section at a hospital in Chongqing. Several months postoperatively, two masses were found on the left side of the cesarean incision, gradually enlarging with pain; pain worsened during menstruation.  
Five years ago, for management of the masses on the left side of the cesarean incision, the patient underwent surgical excision at a hospital in Chongqing; the diameters were approximately 2 cm and 4 cm. Postoperative pathology indicated endometriosis. GnRH-a therapy was given for 3 injections; no regular follow-up ultrasound was performed.  
One year ago, the patient developed right-sided pain at the cesarean incision during each menstrual period, and reported palpable nodules; no medical consultation.  
Five months ago, the patient again developed chest tightness and dyspnea/fatigue; hospitalization revealed massive pericardial effusion. Pericardiocentesis was performed and specimens were sent for testing; SARS-CoV-2 was not detected. Subsequent follow-up ultrasound showed gradual increase in pericardial effusion.  
Two months ago, the patient visited the gynecology outpatient clinic at an outside hospital; PET-CT suggested a soft-tissue mass in the pericardial cavity at the lower left side of the main pulmonary artery trunk, with massive pericardial effusion; considerations: 1) endometriosis, 2) tumor. Denies smoking and alcohol use; denies exposure to radioactive substances and chemical toxins

## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];

    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Brief Medical History
### Patient
Male, 34 years old

### Chief Complaint
Hiccups and vomiting for more than 10 days; recurrent loss of consciousness for 1 day

### History of Present Illness
More than 10 days ago, after staying up late until 2 a.m. and consuming takeaway food and milk tea, the patient began to have hiccups, nausea, and vomiting, occurring once every 1–2 hours. The vomitus consisted of gastric contents, accompanied by fatigue. There was no abdominal pain or diarrhea, no chest tightness or chest pain, and no dizziness or other discomfort. He visited the gastroenterology department of a local hospital; pituitary MRI, contrast-enhanced abdominal CT, trace elements, etc. showed no obvious abnormalities. Symptomatic treatment such as gastric mucosal protection, antiemetics, and improvement of gastrointestinal motility was given at the local hospital, with poor effect; he still had recurrent hiccups and vomiting more than 20 times daily. One day ago, the patient developed loss of consciousness 4 times repeatedly after hiccups, nausea, and vomiting; during the episodes, there were upward rolling of both eyes and tonic stiffening of the limbs, with no response to calling; there was no urinary or fecal incontinence. Each episode lasted for more than 10 seconds to about 30 seconds, and he could not recall the process afterward. For further diagnosis and treatment, he came to our hospital and was admitted with a tentative diagnosis of “syncope, etiology to be determined”.
Since onset, he has been clear-minded, with low spirits; sleep and appetite were poor; urine and stool were unremarkable; body weight showed no significant change.

### Past Medical History
History of chronic hepatitis B for more than 20 years; taking entecavir 1 tablet qd.

### Personal History
Unremarkable.

### Marital/Reproductive History and Menstrual History
Unremarkable.

### Family History
Unremarkable.

## Physical Examination
T 37.6°C, P 65 bpm, R 18 bpm, BP 98/65 mmHg, height 173 cm, weight 115 kg, BMI: 38.4 kg/m2. Conscious, with low spirits. No jaundice of the skin or sclera. Cranial nerve examination negative; no nystagmus. No enlargement of superficial lymph nodes. No jugular venous distention; hepatojugular reflux negative. Breath sounds clear in both lungs; no obvious moist rales heard in bilateral lower lungs. Regular heart rhythm; no obvious pathological murmurs heard in all valve auscultation areas. Abdomen flat and soft; no tenderness or rebound tenderness; liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin; shifting dullness negative. No obvious edema in both lower limbs. Neurological examination negative.

## Initial Laboratory Tests
Day 1 (2023-11-14):
Complete blood count + high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (Emergency): WBC 10.05↑10E9/L, neutrophils (%) 76.5 ↑%, hemoglobin 139 g/L, platelet count 203 10E9/L, CRP 1.88 mg/L  
Troponin I 0.016 ng/mL; Natriuretic peptide: quantitative B-type natriuretic peptide (proBNP) 255↑ pg/mL; myocardial enzyme panel within normal range  
Biochemistry: ALT 54 U↑/L, AST 24 U/L, creatinine 115↑ μmol/L, potassium 3.95 mmol/L, sodium 134↓ mmol/L, chloride 104 mmol/L, total calcium 2.02↓ mmol/L, magnesium 1.05↑ mmol/L, inorganic phosphorus 1.03 mmol/L  
Coagulation function and D-dimer within normal range  
Stool OB negative  
HBsAg positive

## Initial Examinations
Electrocardiogram:
**List of abnormal findings:**  
1. Prolonged QTc interval (465 ms; abnormal if >450 ms in males/>460 ms in females)  
2. Suspected left ventricular hypertrophy (RV5/SV1 = 0.509/1.203 mV; increased voltage with deepened S waves in leads V5–V6)  
3. Low/negative T waves in leads V1–V3 (suggesting abnormal myocardial repolarization)  
4. Markedly deepened S waves in leads I, aVL, V5, V6 (may reflect increased left ventricular load)  

(Day 1 of admission) 22:35 While the patient was asleep, sudden hiccups and vomiting occurred, accompanied by decreased heart rate. There was no vomitus. Loss of consciousness occurred, unresponsive to calling, both eyes tightly closed, with increased limb muscle tone. The patient was immediately placed in the supine position; just as external chest compressions were about to be performed, the patient regained spontaneous consciousness, and could not recall the process afterward. Review of ECG monitoring showed sinus arrest for about 5.6 seconds. After communication with the patient and family, an emergency temporary pacemaker implantation was performed.

Day 1 (2023-11-14)
Echocardiography: left atrial enlargement (74*50 mm), mild mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, EF 68%  
Transcranial + cervical vascular color Doppler ultrasound (ultrasound imaging): 1. Uneven intima-media thickening with plaque in the left carotid artery 2. No obvious abnormalities on transcranial arterial ultrasound.  
Bilateral lower extremity venous ultrasound: blood flow in deep veins of both lower extremities unobstructed  
Non-contrast head CT: Emergency head CT showed no obvious acute findings. Incidental finding: mild inflammation in both maxillary sinuses.  
Dynamic EEG (<24 h) + brain topographic mapping (routine): borderline state. Brain topographic mapping showed: slightly increased power in θ and δ frequency bands in all regions.

## Supplementary History
### Supplementary Laboratory Tests
Day 2 (2023-11-15):
Thyroid function, parathyroid hormone, glycated hemoglobin, cortisol, adrenocorticotropic hormone, epinephrine + norepinephrine, etc. showed no abnormalities  
ANA panel, anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody, ANCA-related antibodies, APS-related antibodies, etc. were all unremarkable

### Supplementary Examinations
Day 2 (2023-11-15):
Holter monitoring: average heart rate 76 bpm. Duration of tachycardia events (heart rate >100 bpm) accounted for 1.2% of total time; duration of bradycardia events (heart rate <60 bpm) accounted for 3.1% of total time. 1. Sinus rhythm 2. R-R ≥2.0 s occurred 73 times, including R-R ≥3.0 s occurred 30 times; the longest pause was 7.6 s, occurring at 11-14 17:57:47. 3. Total cardiac arrest occurred 30 times 4. Sinus arrest 5. Slow junctional escape beats, sometimes presenting as slow junctional escape rhythm (the above were seen before pacemaker implantation) 6. Dual-source premature atrial contractions, sometimes in pairs, sometimes as short runs of atrial tachycardia (a total of 60 PACs occurred, accounting for <1% of total beats, including 44 isolated PACs, 3 paired PACs, and 2 atrial tachycardia episodes.) 7. Multiform premature ventricular contractions, sometimes in pairs, sometimes as short runs of ventricular tachycardia (a total of 82 PVCs occurred, accounting for <1% of total beats, including 4 isolated PVCs, 3 paired PVCs, and 14 VT episodes.) 8. Intermittent ST-segment changes and T-wave changes 9. VVI pacing rhythm; pacemaker function showed no abnormalities.

## Brief Medical History
### Patient
Male, 34 years old

### Chief Complaint
Hiccups and vomiting for more than 10 days; recurrent loss of consciousness for 1 day

### History of Present Illness
More than 10 days ago, after staying up late until 2 a.m. and consuming takeaway food and milk tea, the patient began to have hiccups, nausea, and vomiting, occurring once every 1–2 hours. The vomitus consisted of gastric contents, accompanied by fatigue. There was no abdominal pain or diarrhea, no chest tightness or chest pain, and no dizziness or other discomfort. He visited the gastroenterology department of a local hospital; pituitary MRI, contrast-enhanced abdominal CT, trace elements, etc. showed no obvious abnormalities. Symptomatic treatment such as gastric mucosal protection, antiemetics, and improvement of gastrointestinal motility was given at the local hospital, with poor effect; he still had recurrent hiccups and vomiting more than 20 times daily. One day ago, the patient developed loss of consciousness 4 times repeatedly after hiccups, nausea, and vomiting; during the episodes, there were upward rolling of both eyes and tonic stiffening of the limbs, with no response to calling; there was no urinary or fecal incontinence. Each episode lasted for more than 10 seconds to about 30 seconds, and he could not recall the process afterward. For further diagnosis and treatment, he came to our hospital and was admitted with a tentative diagnosis of “syncope, etiology to be determined”.
Since onset, he has been clear-minded, with low spirits; sleep and appetite were poor; urine and stool were unremarkable; body weight showed no significant change.

### Past Medical History
History of chronic hepatitis B for more than 20 years; taking entecavir 1 tablet qd.

### Personal History
Unremarkable.

### Marital/Reproductive History and Menstrual History
Unremarkable.

### Family History
Unremarkable.

Breath sounds clear in both lungs; no obvious moist rales heard in bilateral lower lungs. Regular heart rhythm; no obvious pathological murmurs heard in all valve auscultation areas. **List of abnormal findings:**

- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;

    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:

### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;

## Medical History Summary

Male, 19 years old

### Chief Complaint
Intermittent blackout episodes for 2 years, chest pain for 3 months.

### History of Present Illness
The patient began having blackout episodes 2 years ago without an obvious trigger, which resolved spontaneously within a few seconds, with no loss of consciousness, no nausea or vomiting, no dizziness, headache, palpitations, chest tightness, or chest pain. The above symptoms occurred intermittently 5 times, without diagnosis or treatment. Three months ago, chest pain developed, related to exertion, presenting as pinpoint dull pain in the precordial area, usually lasting tens of seconds, without radiation to the shoulder or back, and without dyspnea, bilateral lower-extremity edema, sweating, palpitations, blackout episodes, syncope, or other symptoms.

### Past Medical History
Previously healthy.

### Personal History
Denies history of toxic exposure.

## Physical Examination
Physical examination on admission: Temperature: 36.5°C, Pulse: 73 beats/min, Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min, Blood pressure: 142/83 mmHg. Conscious and alert. No vascular bruits heard in the neck; no jugular venous distention or engorgement. Breath sounds clear bilaterally; no rales. Primary_diagnosis.Name: PRKAG2 cardiac syndrome
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Intermittent amaurosis for 2 years, chest pain for 3 months, associated with exertion; family history of hypertrophic non-obstructive cardiomyopathy in the mother, sudden death of maternal uncle at age 45
History: Mother diagnosed with hypertrophic non-obstructive cardiomyopathy and sinus bradycardia due to 'syncope', underwent permanent pacemaker implantation; maternal uncle died suddenly
Physical examination: Cardiac border enlarged toward the left and inferiorly
Ancillary examination: Genetic testing revealed PRKAG2 gene mutation (NM_016203.4: c.905G>A: p.R302Q, maternal heterozygous carrier)
Ancillary examination: Echocardiography showed left ventricular hypertrophy (interventricular septum thickness D/S 2.6/2.9 cm, diffuse thickening of left ventricular walls)

Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) showed left ventricular hypertrophic phenotype cardiomyopathy with focal myocardial edema and fibrosis (hyperintense areas in the left ventricular anterior septum, lateral wall, and inferior wall)
Ancillary examination: Holter monitoring indicated intermittent ventricular pre-excitation
Ancillary examination: Exercise stress test positive (downsloping ST-segment depression ≥0.1 mV in leads V2-V4)
Ancillary examination: Cardiopulmonary exercise testing indicated reduced stroke volume (O2 pulse <80%)

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Intermittent ventricular pre-excitation
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Holter monitoring indicated intermittent ventricular pre-excitation; transesophageal electrophysiology study confirmed the electrocardiographic diagnosis of intermittent ventricular pre-excitation

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Right bundle branch block
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Holter monitoring showed right bundle branch block

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Hyperuricemia
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Uric acid 704 μmol/L ↑

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Liver function abnormality
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Alanine aminotransferase 70.4 ↑

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Subclinical hypothyroidism
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Thyroid-stimulating hormone 6.226 ↑

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Cardiac amyloidosis
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Free light chain KAPPA 29.10 mg/L ↑, LAMBDA 27.98 mg/L ↑; CMR showed mildly elevated left ventricular wall T1 values
Ancillary examination: NT-proBNP elevated (559.7 ↑)
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: CMR did not show typical diffuse myocardial infiltration or LGE pattern characteristic of amyloidosis; genetic testing confirmed PRKAG2 mutation
Ancillary examination: No low-voltage electrocardiographic pattern (commonly seen in amyloidosis)
Ancillary examination: CMR T2 mapping showed focal myocardial edema (not the typical diffuse late gadolinium enhancement pattern of amyloidosis)

Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: CMR did not show right ventricular fatty infiltration or fibrofatty replacement; genetic testing did not identify ARVC-related mutations
Ancillary examination: No sustained ventricular tachycardia or epsilon waves
Ancillary examination: Echocardiography showed no right ventricular structural abnormalities (typical fatty infiltration or aneurysm seen in ARVC)

Ancillary examination: Genetic testing did not identify LAMP2 mutation (the causative gene for Danon disease)
History: No myopathy or intellectual disability (typical manifestations of Danon disease)

## Medical History Summary
### Patient
Male, 19 years old

### Chief Complaint
Intermittent blackout episodes for 2 years, chest pain for 3 months.

### History of Present Illness
The patient began having blackout episodes 2 years ago without an obvious trigger, which resolved spontaneously within a few seconds, with no loss of consciousness, no nausea or vomiting, no dizziness, headache, palpitations, chest tightness, or chest pain. The above symptoms occurred intermittently 5 times, without diagnosis or treatment. Three months ago, chest pain developed, related to exertion, presenting as pinpoint dull pain in the precordial area, usually lasting tens of seconds, without radiation to the shoulder or back, and without dyspnea, bilateral lower-extremity edema, sweating, palpitations, blackout episodes, syncope, or other symptoms.

### Past Medical History
Previously healthy.

### Personal History
Denies history of toxic exposure.

## Physical Examination
Physical examination on admission: Temperature: 36.5°C, Pulse: 73 beats/min, Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min, Blood pressure: 142/83 mmHg. Conscious and alert. No vascular bruits heard in the neck; no jugular venous distention or engorgement. Breath sounds clear bilaterally; no rales. Regular rhythm, heart rate 73 beats/min; cardiac dullness border enlarged to the left and downward; no murmurs heard in any valvular auscultation areas. Abdominal examination: no positive findings. No edema in both lower extremities.

## Initial Laboratory Tests
Renal function:
Uric acid 704 umol/L↑
Thyroid function:
Thyroid-stimulating hormone 6.226↑
Liver function:
Alanine aminotransferase 70.4↑
High-sensitivity troponin I: 0.164↑
NT-proBNP: 559.7↑
Complete blood count, coagulation function, D-dimer, glycated hemoglobin, infectious disease screening, creatine kinase, electrolytes: no abnormalities

In-hospital laboratory tests:
**Abnormal items:**  
1. **Free light chain KAPPA**: 29.10 mg/L (reference range: 3.99–19.40 mg/L)  
2. **Free light chain LAMBDA**: 27.98 mg/L (reference range: 5.71–26.30 mg/L)  

Echocardiography: Homogeneous thickening of the interventricular septum and left ventricular wall: interventricular septal thickness D/S 2.6/2.9 cm; left ventricular anterior wall 2.2 cm, lateral wall 2.0 cm, posterior wall 2.0 cm, inferior wall 2.2 cm; SAM negative; systolic LVOT jet velocity not fast: 1.3 m/s, pressure gradient: 9 mmHg
Conclusion:
1. Left ventricular enlargement
2. Left ventricular hypertrophy
3. Reduced left ventricular diastolic function

### Additional Examinations
2024.2.22 Holter monitoring:  HRave 61 bpm, HRmax 105 bpm, HRmin 35 bpm (02:19:22),
Duration of bradycardia events (heart rate <50 beats/min) accounted for 14.8% of total time
PAC 25, PVC 4
T: lead inversion or bidirectional changes in some periods; ST: depression in leads III and aVL in some periods
Conclusion: 1. Sinus arrhythmia 2. Occasional premature atrial contractions, atrial ectopic beats, occasional premature ventricular contractions 3. Right bundle branch block, intermittent ventricular pre-excitation 4. ST-T changes

2024.2.26 Exercise stress electrocardiogram:
Conclusion: During exercise at 05:00, at power 75 W and heart rate 110 bpm, downsloping ST-segment depression ≥0.1 mV was observed in leads V2–V4, lasting >2 min; during recovery at 07:00, at heart rate 89 bpm, it returned to baseline; no significant arrhythmia was observed
Exercise test result: Positive

Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR):
Short-axis whole-heart cine:
LVEF 55.8%
RVEF 51%
LVED/BSA 123 ml/m2
RVED/BSA 119 ml/m2
4-chamber, 2-chamber, and 3-chamber cine images showed diffuse left ventricular thickening, with no accelerated blood flow in the LVOT
Black-blood fat-suppressed sequence showed focal edematous high signal in the inferior wall of the left ventricle; T2 mapping measured myocardial value in this region of 66.3 ms;
CMR-LGE:
**Abnormal findings:**  
1. Focal areas of high signal in the anterior septum, lateral wall, and inferior wall of the left ventricle (suggestive of myocardial scar/fibrosis).  
2. Focal high-signal area in the right ventricular free wall (suggestive of myocardial involvement).  
3. Abnormally increased signal in the apical and papillary muscle regions.  

Conclusion: 1. Left ventricular hypertrophy phenotype cardiomyopathy, focal myocardial edema injury and fibrosis in hypertrophied regions, with slightly increased left ventricular wall T1 value (reference range 900–1050 ms); 2. Biventricular enlargement; 3. Mild mitral regurgitation; 4. Small bilateral pleural effusions, small pericardial effusion;
Genetic testing and family pedigree investigation were recommended; CMR re-examination every 3–5 years.

Transesophageal electrophysiological study: 1. SNRT = 1120 ms; Wenckebach point >130 bpm; ERP (AVN-HPS) = 290 ms.
2. No supraventricular tachycardia was induced by graded incremental stimulation and S1S2 programmed stimulation; no jump phenomenon was observed.
3. The patient’s ECG diagnosis was intermittent ventricular pre-excitation; graded incremental stimulation and S1S2 programmed stimulation showed no obvious fully pre-excited QRS waves;
4. Intravenous isoproterenol was not administered.
Conclusion: 1. Normal sinus node recovery time 2. Normal atrioventricular nodal conduction function 3. No paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia induced

Exercise stress echocardiography:
Conclusion: Treadmill test up to 125 W
Global enhancement of wall motion; no wall motion abnormalities were induced during exercise stress
Peak LVOT PGmax 18 mmHg (recovery phase (1 min)), peak pressure gradient at the apical segment of the left ventricular cavity 20 mmHg (exercise phase stage 5 (125 W))
Mild mitral regurgitation after stress
3D echocardiography measured left ventricular EF 64%

Cardiopulmonary exercise testing:
1. RER >1.10, indicating adequate effort; exercise test valid
2. peakVO2/kg: 19.3 ml/min/kg, Weber classification: Class B
3. peakVO2/predicted: 42%, indicating moderately reduced cardiopulmonary exercise tolerance
4. Breathing reserve: >50%, suggesting exercise limitation due to cardiovascular factors while breathing reserve remained high
5. O2 pulse during exercise <80%, suggesting reduced stroke volume; the O2 pulse trajectory showed a downward trend at 10 min 28 s, power 138 W, heart rate 125 bpm
6. VE/vco2 slope: 22.4, suggesting normal gas exchange function;
7. Resting PETCO2: 37.1 mmHg, peak during exercise 48.2 mmHg, showing a normal upward trend;
8. Abnormal blood pressure increment during exercise, maximum blood pressure 231/84 mmHg;
9. During exercise, the △VO2/△WR trajectory continued to rise, indicating normal peripheral muscle oxygen uptake capacity;
10. No oscillatory ventilation during exercise;
11. HRR1: 25 bpm, suggesting normal vagal tone;
12. Oxygen saturation: 100%.

Heart failure risk assessment:
VE/Vco2 slope (ventilatory equivalent for carbon dioxide slope): 22.4
peakVO2 (peak oxygen uptake)/predicted: 42%
PETCO2 (end-tidal carbon dioxide) peak during exercise: 48.2 mmHg

Genetic testing:
Gene: PRKAG2
Variant location: chr7:151576412-151576412
Gene subregion: exon 7
HGVS: NM_016203.4: c.905G>A: p.R302Q
Variant type: missense_variant

## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Response Format
- When generating the final answer: you must output the diagnostic results directly in Json format (example below), and do not output any other irrelevant content;

- {"Preliminary diagnosis": {"Primary diagnosis": {"Name": "XXX", "Etiologic subtype": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}, "Secondary diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}]}, "Differential diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Selection rationale": "Consider rare but potentially fatal diseases", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": [], "Excludable": true, "Recommended corresponding tests": []}, {"Name": "YYY", "Selection rationale": "Consider differential diagnosis of adjacent organ lesions", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1"], "Excludable": false, "Recommended corresponding tests": ["Recommendation 1", "Recommendation 2"]}]}

## Medical History Summary
### Patient
Male, 19 years old

### Chief Complaint
Abnormal liver function detected on physical examination for more than 2 years

### History of Present Illness
More than 2 years ago, elevated transaminases were found on physical examination. No fever or chills, no abdominal pain or distension, no fatigue, no jaundice, no history of alcohol consumption, and no history of special medication use. He visited a local hospital and was given oral “Bicyclol Tablets” and “Diammonium Glycyrrhizinate Enteric-coated Capsules”. Re-examination 2 months ago showed: ALT 213 U/L; AST 211 U/L; glutamyl transpeptidase 13.7 KU/L; direct bilirubin 7 mmol/L; indirect bilirubin 9.6 mmol/L. For further treatment, he presented to our hospital and was admitted from the outpatient clinic with “etiological investigation of liver injury”. Since onset, he has been clear-minded, in fair spirits, with average diet and sleep, normal urination and defecation, and no obvious change in body weight compared with before.

### Past Medical History
No history of hypertension or heart disease; no history of diabetes mellitus or cerebrovascular disease; no history of hepatitis, tuberculosis, or malaria. Vaccinations were administered according to the national planned immunization program. No history of trauma or blood transfusion. No history of food or drug allergy.

### Personal History
Born in his native place and has lived locally for a long time; no special travel/residence history; no exposure to toxic substances; no smoking or alcohol consumption.

### Marital/Fertility History and Menstrual History
Unmarried, nulliparous.

### Family History
Father is healthy. Mother died of heart disease at the age of 35 (details unknown). The patient is an only child. No similar diseases in the family; denies a family history of hereditary disease.

## Physical Examination
Temperature 36.50°C, pulse 84/min, respiration 21/min, blood pressure 129/84 mmHg, height 170 cm, weight 65.9 kg, BMI = 22.8 kg/m2.
Clear consciousness, fair mental status, normal intelligence, autonomous position, normal facial appearance, cooperative during examination. No jaundice of the skin or mucosa; no rash or subcutaneous bleeding. Heart rate 84/min, regular rhythm; no pathological murmurs heard in auscultation areas of cardiac valves. Abdomen flat; no abdominal wall varices; no abdominal tenderness or rebound tenderness. Abdomen soft, no palpable masses. Liver not palpable below the costal margin; spleen not palpable below the costal margin; shifting dullness negative. No edema of both lower extremities.

## Initial Laboratory Tests
Complete blood count (CBC):
White blood cell count 7.40×10^9/L, red blood cell count 4.95×10^12/L, hemoglobin 146.0 g/L, platelet count 346×10^9/L, neutrophil percentage 41.6%.
Coagulation function:
Prothrombin time activity 100.00%, fibrinogen 2.37 g/L, D-dimer 0.02 mg/L.
Liver function:
ALT 229 U/L, AST 302 U/L, gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) 25 U/L, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) 86 U/L, total bilirubin 18.5 μmol/L, direct bilirubin 8.2 μmol/L, total protein 76 g/L, albumin 49.2 g/L, prealbumin 287 mg/L.
Myocardial enzymes + B-type natriuretic peptide precursor:
AST 194↑ U/L, creatine kinase (CK) 992↑ U/L, creatine kinase-MB (CK-MB) 37.9↑ U/L, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) 809↑ U/L, alpha-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase (α-HBDH) 785↑ U/L, myoglobin 591.50↑ ng/mL; N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP) 1109↑ Pg/mL.
Thyroid function, autoimmune hepatitis panel, hepatitis B five-item panel, and hepatitis C antibody: no abnormalities detected.

Inflammation and immunity panel (7 items):
C-reactive protein 11.60↑ mg/L, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 3.00 mm/h;
Complement C3 1.38 g/L, complement C4 0.11 g/L;
Serum immunoglobulin A 2.51 g/L, serum immunoglobulin G 12.56 g/L, serum immunoglobulin M 0.89 g/L.

Liver needle biopsy
Two core liver biopsy specimens; 10 portal tracts identified. Scattered lymphocytic infiltration in portal areas; focal hepatocellular necrosis. Masson stain showed fibrous tissue proliferation in portal areas; reticulin stain showed preserved hepatic plate architecture.
Diagnosis: mild chronic hepatitis, G1S1.
Immunohistochemistry: CK7 (bile duct +), CK19 (bile duct +), CD38 (-), CD138 (-). Special stains: D-PAS (-), copper stain (-), iron stain (-).

## Initial Examinations
Electrocardiogram (ECG): 1) Occasional premature atrial contractions; 2) Ventricular pre-excitation cannot be excluded; 3) ST-T changes suggesting left ventricular hypertrophy; 4) Coved ST-segment elevation in anteroseptal leads; please correlate clinically.

Holter monitoring:
Baseline rhythm was sinus rhythm; total beats, average heart rate, and slowest heart rate throughout the recording were within normal ranges.
Occasional premature atrial contractions, some occurring in pairs.
Short runs of atrial tachycardia.
Suggestive of left ventricular hypertrophy.
Persistent ST-T changes in some leads; no obvious abnormal dynamic changes observed.

Echocardiography:
Ultrasound suggested: diffuse thickening of the left ventricular wall; consider non-obstructive symmetric hypertrophic cardiomyopathy?

Hepatobiliary-pancreatic-splenic ultrasound:
Ultrasound suggested: diffuse hepatic echogenicity changes.

Echocardiography:
Ultrasound suggested: thickened left ventricular wall with heterogeneous echogenicity and patchy slightly increased echogenicity; trace aortic regurgitation; trace tricuspid and mitral regurgitation.

Cardiac MRI:
Myocardial involvement disease; left ventricular myocardial hypertrophy with multiple patchy enhancements, considering non-obstructive symmetric hypertrophic cardiomyopathy;
Reduced left ventricular diastolic function;
Relatively widened main pulmonary artery diameter; please correlate clinically.

## Supplementary Medical History
### Supplementary Laboratory Tests
Myocardial PYP radionuclide scintigraphy:
Not suggestive of myocardial ATTR amyloidosis.
Quantification of light chains κ and λ:
Serum total κ light chain <8.84 g/L; serum total λ light chain <5.56 g/L. Urine total κ light chain <18.50 mg/L; urine total λ light chain <50.00 mg/L.

Skin biopsy
Test conclusion: The mean fluorescence signal of misfolded TTR protein in the lateral malleolus skin sample was below the threshold line, negative for misfolded TTR proteinopathy.

### Supplementary Examinations
Coronary CTA: no obvious abnormalities.

Coronary angiography + left ventriculography + left ventricular pressure measurement
CAG: Right-dominant coronary circulation; normal origins and distribution of LCA and RCA. Smooth intima of LM, LAD, LCX, RCA, PL, and PD; no significant stenosis or dissection; antegrade flow TIMI grade 3.

Left ventriculography + left ventricular pressure measurement
Left ventriculography: generally normal wall motion of the anterior wall and apex of the left ventricle.
Left ventricular pressure measurement: apical pressure 92/17 mmHg, mid-ventricular pressure 97/17 mmHg, subvalvular pressure 90/20 mmHg, supravalvular pressure 102/78 mmHg.

Electromyography (EMG):
EMG showed no abnormalities in motor and distal sensory conduction function of the examined muscles and nerves in both lower extremities; no abnormalities in bilateral tibial nerve H-reflex.
Motor evoked potentials showed no abnormalities in corticospinal tract conduction in both lower extremities.
Somatosensory evoked potentials showed no abnormalities in deep sensory pathway conduction in both lower extremities.

ECG: Atrial flutter:

Holter monitoring
Baseline rhythm was ectopic rhythm; total beats, average heart rate, and slowest heart rate throughout the recording were within normal ranges.
Persistent atrial flutter with atrioventricular conduction ratio 2:1 to 6:1.
Occasional premature ventricular contractions.
Complete left bundle branch block.
Persistent ST-T changes, with no obvious abnormal dynamic changes.

Radiofrequency ablation for atrial flutter + endomyocardial biopsy
1. Intracardiac electrogram showed atrial flutter, with CS9-10 A-wave leading. Under CARTO guidance, three-dimensional reconstruction and activation mapping of the right and left atria were performed, suggesting cavotricuspid isthmus reentry. Cavotricuspid isthmus line ablation was performed; atrial flutter terminated and converted to sinus rhythm. Pacing confirmed cavotricuspid isthmus line block.
2. An endomyocardial biopsy forceps was introduced via a VIZIGO sheath; biopsy specimens were obtained from the interventricular septum of the left and right ventricles and fixed for pathological examination.

Endomyocardial biopsy:
Pathological diagnosis: partial loss of Lamp2 expression in cardiomyocytes,
Increased autophagosomes in cardiomyocytes; genetic testing is recommended to exclude metabolic cardiomyopathy.

Genetic testing:
Hemizygous variant in the LAMP2 gene c.864+1G>T identified. Comprehensive analysis in conjunction with clinical symptoms and family history is recommended.

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Danon disease
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Abnormal liver function detected during physical examination for over 2 years, with no history of alcohol consumption or use of specific medications
History: No risk factors for secondary myocardial hypertrophy such as hypertension or diabetes mellitus
Ancillary examination: Genetic testing revealed a hemizygous variant c.864+1G>T in the LAMP2 gene
Ancillary examination: Endomyocardial biopsy demonstrated partial loss of Lamp2 expression in cardiomyocytes with increased autophagosomes
Ancillary examination: Cardiac MRI showed left ventricular myocardial thickening with multiple patchy areas of enhancement, consistent with features of metabolic cardiomyopathy
Ancillary examination: Echocardiography indicated diffuse left ventricular wall thickening, suggestive of non-obstructive symmetric hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
Ancillary examination: Significantly elevated creatine kinase (992↑ U/L) and elevated myoglobin (591.50↑ ng/mL)

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Atrial flutter
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Holter monitoring showed persistent atrial flutter with atrioventricular conduction ratios ranging from 2:1 to 6:1
Ancillary examination: Intracardiac electrograms during atrial flutter radiofrequency ablation confirmed cavotricuspid isthmus-dependent reentrant atrial flutter
Ancillary examination: ECG indicated atrial flutter

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Complete left bundle branch block
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Both ECG and Holter monitoring showed complete left bundle branch block

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Post-radiofrequency ablation
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Radiofrequency ablation for atrial flutter was performed; atrial flutter terminated after cavotricuspid isthmus linear ablation

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Hepatic insufficiency
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Persistently elevated alanine aminotransferase (229 U/L) and aspartate aminotransferase (302 U/L)
Ancillary examination: Liver biopsy demonstrated mild chronic hepatitis (G1S1)
Ancillary examination: Liver function tests showed total bilirubin of 18.5 μmol/L

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Cardiac amyloidosis
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Cardiac MRI showed multiple patchy areas of enhancement
Ancillary examination: Cardiac MRI showed left ventricular myocardial thickening with multiple patchy areas of enhancement
Ancillary examination: Left ventricular wall thickening with heterogeneous echogenicity (echocardiography)
Ancillary examination: Elevated cardiac enzymes (creatine kinase 992↑ U/L, lactate dehydrogenase 809↑ U/L)
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Skin biopsy was negative for misfolded TTR protein
Ancillary examination: Serum/urine free light chain quantification was within normal limits
Ancillary examination: Myocardial PYP nuclear scintigraphy did not suggest cardiac ATTR amyloidosis

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Diffuse left ventricular wall thickening (echocardiography)
Ancillary examination: Cardiac MRI showed symmetric myocardial hypertrophy
Ancillary examination: Cardiac MRI showed left ventricular myocardial thickening with multiple patchy areas of enhancement
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Genetic testing confirmed LAMP2 mutation (not a gene typically associated with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy)
Ancillary examination: Endomyocardial biopsy demonstrated loss of Lamp2 expression and increased autophagosomes; genetic testing identified LAMP2 gene variant
History: No family history of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Viral myocarditis
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Elevated cardiac enzymes (creatine kinase 992↑ U/L, myoglobin 591.50↑ ng/mL)
Ancillary examination: Holter monitoring showed persistent atrial flutter and premature ventricular complexes
Non-supporting_evidence: History: No prodromal symptoms such as fever or infection
Ancillary examination: Endomyocardial biopsy showed no evidence of inflammatory cell infiltration
Ancillary examination: Among the 7-item inflammatory and immunological panel, C-reactive protein was only mildly elevated (11.60 mg/L), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate was normal (3.00 mm/h)
