Primary_diagnosis.Name: Weaving coronary artery
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Male, 32 years old, no traditional cardiovascular risk factors (no hypertension, diabetes mellitus, or smoking history)
History: Sudden loss of consciousness for 25 days, retrosternal burning sensation for 40 days

Auxiliary examination: Coronary CTA showed irregular moderate-to-severe stenosis in the proximal-to-mid segment of the left anterior descending artery (LAD) and the right coronary artery (RCA); coronary angiography showed weaving pattern changes in the LAD and RCA, with approximately 70% stenosis in the mid-segment of the RCA and approximately 50%-60% stenosis in the proximal LAD

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Old myocardial infarction
Diagnostic_evidence: Auxiliary examination: ECG showed poor R-wave progression in the anteroseptal leads with ST-T changes

Auxiliary examination: Cardiac MRI showed subendocardial late gadolinium enhancement in the proximal-to-mid segment of the left ventricular inferior wall, left ventricular anterior wall, and mid-to-distal interventricular septum, consistent with myocardial infarction

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Ventricular thrombus
Diagnostic_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Echocardiography showed a mural hyperechoic mass at the left ventricular apex (19 mm × 5.6 mm); cardiac MRI showed abnormal signal in the left ventricular apical cavity, consistent with mural thrombus formation

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Post coronary artery bypass grafting
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Coronary artery bypass grafting (LIMA-LAD, RIMA-RCA) was performed on July 7, 2023

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Cerebral infarction (post-thrombolysis, post-interventional thrombectomy)
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Sudden loss of consciousness with right-sided limb weakness; intravenous thrombolysis and interventional thrombectomy were performed
Auxiliary examination: Brain MRI + MRA (October 15, 2021) showed old lesion in the left frontal lobe and recent cerebral infarction in the right frontal lobe

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Cerebral hemorrhage
Diagnostic_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Brain MRI showed subacute-phase hemorrhage in the left basal ganglia region; SWI showed hemosiderin deposition

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Hyperlipidemia
Diagnostic_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Total cholesterol 5.61 mmol/L↑, low-density lipoprotein 3.86 mmol/L↑

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Hepatic insufficiency
Diagnostic_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Alanine aminotransferase 73 U/L↑, gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase 85 U/L↑

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Hypofibrinogenemia
Diagnostic_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Fibrinogen 0.76–1.22 g/L↓ (reference range: 2–4 g/L)

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Thrombophilia
Diagnostic_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Protein S activity 45.80%↓ (reference range: 76–135%); PAI-1 gene 5G/4G heterozygous mutation, MTHFR c.677C>T CT heterozygous mutation

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Dilated cardiomyopathy
Supporting_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Reduced left ventricular function (EF: 47.78%), left ventricular apical mural thrombus
Non-supporting_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Echocardiography did not indicate ventricular dilatation (LV 46–50 mm, within normal range)
Auxiliary examination: Coronary CTA and angiography clearly demonstrated coronary artery stenotic lesions; cardiac MRI did not indicate ventricular dilatation or diffuse myocardial disease

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Coronary artery embolism
Supporting_evidence: History: Left ventricular thrombus and cerebral embolic events
Auxiliary examination: Cardiac MRI showed myocardial infarction in multiple territories
Non-supporting_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Antiphospholipid antibodies were negative
Auxiliary examination: Coronary angiography clearly showed weaving vascular malformation without typical embolic features
Recommended_corresponding_tests: Transesophageal echocardiography is recommended (to evaluate for cardiogenic emboli)
Intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) to assess coronary plaque characteristics
Screening for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation is recommended (extended Holter monitoring)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Antiphospholipid syndrome
Supporting_evidence: History: Multiple thrombotic events (cerebral infarction, ventricular thrombus)
Non-supporting_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Anticardiolipin antibodies and anti-β2-glycoprotein I antibodies were both negative (high specificity)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Acute myocarditis
Supporting_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Cardiac MRI showed abnormal myocardial enhancement
Non-supporting_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Myocardial injury markers (troponin, CK-MB) were normal
History: No fever or prodromal infectious symptoms

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Infective endocarditis
Supporting_evidence: Auxiliary examination: Left ventricular mural thrombus
Non-supporting_evidence: History: No fever, blood cultures were negative
Auxiliary examination: Echocardiography did not reveal valvular vegetations (high specificity)

## History Summary
### Patient
Male, 54 years old

### Chief Complaint
Chest tightness on exertion for more than 2 months, aggravated with lower-extremity edema for 1 month

### History of Present Illness
For more than 2 months, the patient had recurrent chest tightness and shortness of breath on exertion (exercise tolerance: approximately climbing 2–3 flights of stairs), relieved by rest. Over the past 1 month, symptoms worsened with bilateral lower-extremity edema.

Chest CT at an outside hospital: bilateral pleural effusions with bilateral lower-lobe atelectasis; TTE: LVEF 57%, PASP 54 mmHg, left ventricular wall thickening, small pericardial effusion

Admitted to the Department of Respiratory Medicine of our hospital on 2018-11-12. TTE: LVPW 15 mm, IVS 16 mm, LVEF 63.3%, PASP 45 mmHg; ECG: low voltage in limb leads, ST-T changes in some leads. After consultation by physicians in our department, RCM was considered, and the patient was transferred to our department for treatment.

### Past Medical History
No significant history

### Personal History
No significant history

### Family History
His mother died of “lung cancer”; otherwise unremarkable

## Physical Examination
T 36.4℃   P 88 beats/min   R 18 breaths/min  BP 114/86 mmHg  
Clear consciousness, fair general condition. No palpable superficial lymphadenopathy. Neck supple; jugular venous distention; positive hepatojugular reflux. Dullness to percussion over bilateral lower lungs; decreased breath sounds over bilateral lower lungs on auscultation. Heart rate 88 beats/min, regular rhythm; splitting of the second heart sound on auscultation, with mildly accentuated P2. Pitting edema present in both lower extremities.

## Preliminary Laboratory Tests
NT-proBNP 8034.7 pg/mL  
cTnT 196.1 ng/L  
β2-MG 3.10 mg/L (reference range 1.09–2.53 mg/L)  
Serum free light chains:  
F-KAP  22.7  
F-LAM 492.5  
K/L 0.05  
Urine light chains:  
u-KAP  28.80  
u-LAM 44.00  
uK/L 0.65  
Complete blood count, biochemistry, coagulation function, rheumatology panel (3 items), immunology panel (5 items), antinuclear antibody, and serum immunofixation electrophoresis were all negative.  
Pleural effusion was transudative; tumor markers in pleural fluid, tuberculosis-related indices, exfoliated cells, etc. were all negative.  
Bone marrow smear: increased proportion of plasma cells (4.8%).  
Tongue muscle biopsy: Congo red staining (+).

## Preliminary Examinations
Electrocardiogram (ECG):  
**Abnormal findings:**
1. **Right axis deviation**   
2. **Poor R-wave progression in leads V1–V3**   

Two-dimensional echocardiography: LAD 42 mm, RAD 42 mm, LVDd 40 mm, IVS 14 mm, LVPW 15 mm, LVEF 32.2%, E 60 cm/s, A 9 cm/s, septal e’ 3.6 cm/s, lateral wall e’ 2.9 cm/s, E/e’ 18.5, PASP 38 mmHg  
Slow intracavitary flow in both atria; relatively small left ventricular cavity; diffuse thickening of the interventricular septum and left ventricle, with a minimal A wave; inferior vena cava blood flow stasis

Cardiac MR: from the mid-ventricle to the apex, myocardial thickening of the interventricular septum and basal left ventricular free wall; extensive delayed enhancement of the left ventricular myocardium, predominantly subendocardial, partially involving the right ventricle

**Abnormal findings:**
1. Sinus arrest (long RR interval)
2. Sinus bradycardia (baseline heart rate <60 beats/min)

ECG during the episode:  
**Abnormal findings:**
1. **Premature atrial beats**   
2. **Premature ventricular beats**   

ECG 5 min after the episode:  
**Abnormal findings:**
1. Sinus arrest (long RR interval)
2. Sinus bradycardia (baseline heart rate <60 beats/min)

## Additional History
### Additional Examinations
Angiography:  
**Abnormal findings:**
1. Severe stenosis/occlusion of the distal right internal carotid artery (RICA)   
2. Poor visualization of the trunk and branches of the right middle cerebral artery (RMCA) (suggestive of stenosis or occlusion)   

Left atrial CT: no abnormalities in the coronary arteries; hypodense areas in the right atrial appendage and superior vena cava, thrombus possible; slow flow in the left atrial appendage

## Brief Medical History
### Patient
Female, 58 years old

### Chief Complaint
Recurrent palpitations and fatigue for 4 years

### History of Present Illness
Since 2014, the patient has repeatedly experienced palpitations and discomfort, accompanied by chest tightness and fatigue, which were明显 after activity. There was no obvious chest pain, no dizziness or transient visual blackout, and no lower-extremity edema. ECG showed atrial fibrillation, which was not taken seriously.
In 2017, due to frequent episodes of palpitations, the patient visited another hospital. Holter monitoring showed paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, with an average heart rate of 67 beats/min; multiform premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) 893 beats, 2 runs of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (NSVT), and the longest R–R interval of 1.4 seconds. Coronary CTA showed the coronary arteries were essentially normal. After oral administration of Qili Qiangxin Capsules, trimetazidine, etc., the patient felt symptomatic improvement.
In 2018, the patient had frequent palpitations and fatigue. Holter monitoring showed ectopic rhythm and atrial fibrillation, with an average ventricular rate of 58 beats/min; polymorphic PVCs 935 beats, 10 runs of PVC couplets and 3 runs of PVC triplets, 3 runs of NSVT, 20 pauses >2 seconds, and the longest R–R interval of 3.2 seconds. To seek further diagnosis and treatment, the patient presented to our hospital and was admitted with a tentative diagnosis of “atrial fibrillation”.

### Past Medical History
History of hypertension for 10 years, with a maximum of 168/98 mmHg; taking telmisartan 80 mg qd for control, with acceptable blood pressure control. Denies diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, and other chronic diseases.

### Personal History
Denies smoking and alcohol use.

### Family History
The patient denies a family history of relevant hereditary diseases.

## Physical Examination
Temperature: 36.5°C  Pulse: 54 beats/min  Respiratory rate: 22 breaths/min  Blood pressure: 136/73 mmHg  
Clear consciousness, fair general condition. No obvious jugular venous distension. Breath sounds coarse bilaterally; no dry or moist rales heard. Heart rate 58 beats/min, rhythm absolutely irregular; S1 varied in intensity; no pathological murmurs heard. Abdomen flat and soft; no tenderness or rebound tenderness; liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin; bowel sounds normal; no edema in both lower extremities.

## Preliminary Laboratory Tests
[Cardiac biomarkers] CK 270 ↑ IU/L, CK-MB 6 ng/mL, TNI 20 pg/mL  
[NT-proBNP] 1476 ↑ pg/mL  
[Coagulation] APTT 32.4 s, PT 12.9 s, INR 1.10, TT 17.80 s, Fg 2.6 g/L, fibrin degradation products 0.5 ↑ mg/L, D-dimer (quantitative) 0.1 mg/L  
[Biochemistry] Glucose 5.05 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.7%, ALT 17 IU/L, AST 18 IU/L, triglycerides 1.04 mmol/L, total cholesterol 4.92 mmol/L, HDL-C 1.64 mmol/L, LDL-C 3.06 mmol/L, creatinine 49 μmol/L, estimated GFR 103.5 mL/min/1.73 m2  
[Thyroid function] T3 1.54 nmol/L, T4 112.72 nmol/L, FT3 4.14 pmol/L, FT4 14.38 pmol/L, TSH 1.2524 μIU/mL, TGAb 5.37 ↑ IU/mL, TPOAb 65.94 ↑ IU/mL  
[Complete blood count] Essentially normal

## Preliminary Examinations
[Left atrial CTA] Left atrial enlargement  
[Transesophageal echocardiography] No thrombus formation in the left atrium observed  
ECG: Atrial fibrillation, premature ventricular beats  
Echocardiography: Left heart enlargement with mild-to-moderate mitral regurgitation;  
Pulmonary hypertension with mild tricuspid regurgitation; right atrium mildly enlarged  
Chest X-ray: Slightly increased, coarsened, disordered, and indistinct lung markings in both lungs; linear opacities in both lower lungs; enlarged cardiac silhouette; tortuous aorta; please correlate with clinical presentation, medical history, and other examinations.

Atrial fibrillation ablation was performed. Postoperative voltage mapping:
**Abnormal findings:**  
1. **Low-voltage areas in the left atrium**: Multiple areas with voltage ≤0.463 mV (purple/blue), suggesting post-ablation scar formation or conduction block.  
2. **Uneven voltage distribution**: Voltage in local areas was significantly higher than in adjacent areas (red/yellow), suggesting possible residual viable myocardium not completely ablated or residual conduction pathways.

Post–atrial fibrillation ablation ECG: Immediate postoperative ECG showed sinus bradycardia, first-degree AV block, junctional escape beats, and premature atrial contractions without ventricular conduction. After the patient returned to the ward, ECG telemetry revealed intermittent high-grade atrioventricular block. Due to an excessively slow heart rate and intermittent high-grade atrioventricular block, after 2 days of observation, permanent pacemaker implantation was performed.

### Supplementary History
The patient had fatigue symptoms since 7 years prior to this admission and visited another hospital; electromyography suggested myogenic damage.  
The patient concealed the family history due to consanguineous marriage of the maternal grandparents.  
The mother had atrioventricular conduction block with pacemaker implantation and a history of chronic heart failure; the eldest sister and second sister had atrial fibrillation and concomitant atrioventricular conduction block and dilated cardiomyopathy, both with pacemakers implanted; the third sister had clinical symptoms such as palpitations and had not been diagnosed with DCM. The mother and the eldest sister both died suddenly.

Suspected affected individuals in the pedigree:
Ⅰ-2 and Ⅲ-4 had symptoms such as palpitations and discomfort; no echocardiography, ECG, or other examinations were performed;  
Ⅱ-3 died suddenly of unknown cause at 6 years old.

Completed genetic sequencing results: LMNA mutation site (c.C1443G/p.Y481*).

History: Consanguineous marriage between maternal grandparents; cardiac-related diseases (atrial fibrillation, atrioventricular block, dilated cardiomyopathy) or history of sudden cardiac death in the mother, eldest sister, second eldest sister, third eldest sister, and individuals Ⅰ-2 and Ⅲ-4 within the family

raw_query:
## Brief History
### Patient
Female, 36 years old

### Chief Complaint
Poor exercise tolerance for more than 2 years; fever, productive cough, and shortness of breath for 26 days

### History of Present Illness
The child began to have poor exercise tolerance at 10 months of age, with shortness of breath and labored breathing even at rest. The above symptoms worsened with slight activity. There was no obvious cyanosis at rest; cyanosis of the lips was observed after crying and activity. No history of syncope. She was diagnosed with “pulmonary hypertension” at an outside hospital. After related treatment (details unknown), symptoms slightly improved but recurred repeatedly. Twenty-six days prior, fever developed with a maximum temperature of 40.1°C, accompanied by frequent cough with sputum that was difficult to expectorate. Mental status and physical strength deteriorated significantly, oxygen saturation decreased markedly, and shortness of breath and dyspnea were obvious. The local hospital provided anti-infective therapy, cardiac function support, targeted therapy, and invasive mechanical ventilation, with poor response. She was then transferred to our ICU on mechanical ventilatory support at 120.

### Past Medical History
2015.07.16 (birth): After birth, hospitalized in the local hospital NICU due to “neonatal pneumonia, neonatal asphyxia.” Hypoxemia occurred during hospitalization.  
Physical examination: hypotonia; generalized muscle laxity.  
Investigations: Ultrasound suggested “congenital heart disease: ventricular septal defect (perimembranous type, 3 mm), atrial septal defect (7 mm).” Thyroid function: “elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone.” Tandem mass spectrometry of blood: “increased carnitine.” Chromosomal analysis: “no abnormality.”  
SpO2 at discharge: 90–95%.  
Diagnosed at birth with “congenital hypothyroidism”; took “Euthyrox (levothyroxine)” orally for half a year; subsequent repeated thyroid function tests were all normal.

2016.05-1 (10 months): Local follow-up echocardiography: “congenital heart disease: ventricular septal defect, atrial septal defect, severe tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary arterial hypertension,” and was admitted to a higher-level hospital.

2016.05-2 (10 months): Repeat echocardiography at the higher-level hospital: dilated pulmonary artery with severe pulmonary arterial hypertension; right heart enlargement; right ventricular wall thickening (free wall 0.4 cm); mild-to-moderate tricuspid insufficiency; small atrial septal defect (0.5 cm); small pericardial effusion (right ventricular free wall about 0.3 cm). RA 2.6 cm, RV 2.7 cm, LA 1.3 cm, LV 2.1 cm. Recommended further diagnosis and treatment at a specialty hospital.

2017.09 (2 years 2 months): Hospitalized for heart failure to adjust treatment.

2016.06 (11 months): Genetic testing at an outside hospital  
Results:  
No pathogenic variants were found in genes related to the current clinical symptoms.  
Genes analyzed in focus included “pulmonary arterial hypertension–related disease genes,” “hereditary cardiovascular disease genes,” “hereditary syndrome disease genes,” etc. (references: Human Gene Mutation Database [HGMD], Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man [OMIM]).

2016.06 (11 months) Outside-hospital ECG  
1. Sinus rhythm  
2. Right ventricular hypertrophy  
3. ST–T changes  

Outside-hospital echocardiography  
Findings on routine 2D echocardiography:  
Normal cardiac position and connections. Marked enlargement of the right atrium and right ventricle; right ventricular wall hypertrophy; interventricular septum slightly bulging toward the left ventricle; normal left ventricular systolic motion. Tricuspid aortic valve with good mobility. Dilated pulmonary artery; main trunk inner diameter 1.47 cm; valve opening and motion acceptable. Atrioventricular valves opened and moved acceptably; mitral annulus 1.08 cm; tricuspid annulus 1.80 cm; mild tricuspid regurgitation; regurgitant jet width 0.27 cm; regurgitation velocity 5.08 m/s; pressure gradient 103.2 mmHg. Atrial septal defect (type II), upper margin 0.54 cm, bidirectional shunt. A bidirectional shunt was suspected in the muscular interventricular septum, located below the moderator band; shunt width 0.12 cm; distance to apex 0.91 cm. Left aortic arch.

Outside-hospital contrast-enhanced cardiac and pulmonary CT  
After spiral scanning and 3D reconstruction: mediastinum essentially midline; trachea patent; no stenosis of the left and right main bronchi. Lung window showed increased markings in both lungs, without obvious interstitial changes.  
Situs solitus; systemic and pulmonary venous return normal; atrioventricular septal defect; right atrial enlargement.  
Concordant atrioventricular connection; whether the ventricular septum was intact should be combined with ECHO; right ventricular enlargement.  
Concordant ventriculoarterial connection; left aortic arch; aberrant right subclavian artery; no obvious patent ductus arteriosus or coarctation of the aorta. Dilatation of the main pulmonary artery; bilateral pulmonary arteries developed acceptably.

Outside-hospital right heart catheterization  
Pulmonary artery pressure 90/54/71 mmHg; descending aortic pressure 94/51/69 mmHg; left pulmonary arteriolar wedge pressure 7 mmHg; pulmonary blood flow/systemic blood flow 0.63; pulmonary vascular resistance index 82.5 Wood units·m². Descending aortic oxygen saturation 74.8%; mixed venous oxygen saturation 50%.  
Left pulmonary arteriolar angiography showed tortuous small pulmonary arteries with a withered-branch appearance and decreased filling of the capillary bed.

2017.09 (2 years 2 months)  
Echocardiographic impression:  
Severe pulmonary arterial hypertension  
Right heart enlargement  
Moderate tricuspid regurgitation  
Congenital heart disease  
Secundum atrial septal defect (central type)  
Bidirectional shunt at the atrial level  
Patent ductus arteriosus not excluded

2016.06 (11 months)  
Outside-hospital diagnosis: idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension  
Treatment plan: 1. Targeted therapy for pulmonary arterial hypertension (bosentan).  
2. Cardiac function support (furosemide + spironolactone).

2017.09 (2 years 2 months)  
Outside-hospital diagnosis: idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension  
Treatment plan: 1. Targeted therapy for pulmonary arterial hypertension: ambrisentan + tadalafil + remodulin (discontinued in 2018.04)  
2. Cardiac function support (torasemide + spironolactone)

## Physical Examination
Admission examination: Temperature 38.3°C, pulse 156/min, respiratory rate 18/min, blood pressure: left upper limb 86/46 mmHg; left lower limb 100/55 mmHg; right upper limb 90/50 mmHg; right lower limb 103/40 mmHg. Limb SpO2: left upper 66%; left lower 71%; right upper 72%; right lower 71%. Under sedation, endotracheal tube in place, on continuous mechanical ventilatory support. Poor development. Bilaterally large and protruding ears. Bilateral single transverse palmar creases. Marked generalized cyanosis. Breath sounds coarse in both lungs, symmetric bilaterally; rales audible in the lungs. No palpable thrill along the parasternal area. Enlarged cardiac borders. Heart rate 156 bpm, regular rhythm. P2 markedly accentuated. No obvious murmur heard along the parasternal area. Abdomen soft; liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin. Bilateral dorsalis pedis pulses weak. Peripheral vascular signs negative. Extremities warm.

## Initial Laboratory Tests
Admission tests:  
**Abnormal items:**  
1. **Partial pressure of carbon dioxide (PaCO2)**: 49 mmHg (reference range 35–48 mmHg)  
2. **Sodium**: 135 mmol/L (reference range 137–147 mmol/L)  
3. **Glucose**: 8.9 mmol/L (reference range 3.9–5.6 mmol/L)  
4. **Hematocrit**: 24% (reference range 38–50.8%)  
5. **Oxygen saturation**: 60.0% (reference range 91.9–99%)  
6. **Partial pressure of oxygen (T)**: 33 mmHg (reference range 83–108 mmHg)  
7. **Plasma lactate**: 6.0 mmol/L (reference range 0.5–2.2 mmol/L)  
8. **Hemoglobin**: 82 g/L (reference range 110–160 g/L)  
9. **Ionized calcium**: 1.28 mmol/L (reference range 1.15–1.27 mmol/L)

Complete blood count: WBC 6.58*10^9, neutrophils 18.7%, RBC 2.474*10^12/L, hemoglobin 71.7 g/L, platelets 68.7*10^9/L; eosinophils and basophils within normal range.  
Liver function: ALT 240 IU/L, AST 486 IU/L, r-GT 468 U/L, total bilirubin 43.7 umol/L, direct bilirubin 12.7 umol/L.  
Troponin I 0.201 ng/mL  
High-sensitivity C-reactive protein 19.83 mg/L  
NT-proBNP 3907 pg/mL  
Procalcitonin 1.12 ng/mL  
Immunologic tests (IgG, IgE, IgM) normal  
Urinalysis, stool routine, coagulation function, electrolytes, renal function, rheumatologic tests, infectious disease screening: normal.

Treatment effect:  
**Abnormal items:**  
1. **Sodium**: 135 mmol/L (reference range: 137–147 mmol/L)  
2. **Potassium**: 3.0 mmol/L (reference range: 3.5–5.3 mmol/L)  
3. **Ionized calcium**: 1.43 mmol/L (reference range: 1.15–1.27 mmol/L)  
4. **Plasma lactate**: 0.4 mmol/L (reference range: 0.5–2.2 mmol/L)  
5. **Hemoglobin**: 95 g/L (reference range: 110–160 g/L)  
6. **Standard ionized calcium (7.4)**: 1.44 mmol/L (reference range: 1.15–1.27 mmol/L)  
7. **Alveolar–arterial oxygen gradient (A–aDO2)**: 198 mmHg (normal <150 mmHg)  
8. **PaO2/FiO2 ratio**: 214 mmHg (normal >300 mmHg)

Complete blood count + 5-part differential:  
**Abnormal items:**  
1. **RBC**: 2.796×10¹²/L (reference range: 3.8–5.1×10¹²/L)  
2. **Hemoglobin**: 79.6 g/L (reference range: 115–150 g/L)  
3. **Hematocrit**: 23.61% (reference range: 35–45%)  
4. **Lymphocyte percentage**: 16.02% (reference range: 20–50%)  
5. **Absolute lymphocyte count**: 0.61×10⁹/L (reference range: 1.1–3.2×10⁹/L)  
6. **Monocyte percentage**: 10.85% (reference range: 3–10%)  
7. **Red cell distribution width (RDW)**: 16.55% (reference range: 0–14.5%)

Liver function:  
**Abnormal items:**  
1. **Aspartate aminotransferase (AST)**  
- Result: ▲36.1 IU/L  
- Reference range: 0–35 IU/L  
2. **Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT)**  
- Result: ▲316 U/L  
- Reference range: 9–64 U/L  
3. **Total bile acids**  
- Result: ▲18.8 μmol/L  
- Reference range: 0–15 μmol/L  
4. **Alanine aminotransferase (ALT)**  
- Result: ▲35.7 IU/L  
- Reference range: 0–35 IU/L  
5. **Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)**  
- Result: ▲427 U/L  
- Reference range: 120–250 U/L  
6. **N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP)**  
- Result: ▲373.90 pg/mL  
- Reference range: 0–125 pg/mL

2021.09.13 Follow-up  
NT-proBNP 300–500 pg/mL  
SpO2 approximately 93%

## Initial Examinations
Admission examinations:  
**Abnormal items:**  
1. **Electrocardiogram (ECG) abnormality**  
- ST-segment elevation with convex upward morphology in leads V1–V3  
- Pathologic Q waves visible (leads V2–V3)  
2. **Chest X-ray abnormality**  
- Enlarged cardiac silhouette (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5)  
- Increased density with indistinct shadowing in the left lower lung field  
- Focal discontinuity of bone cortex of the right 4th rib (suspected fracture)

Pediatric color Doppler echocardiography + left ventricular function assessment (2D + M-mode) + bedside examination:  
**Abnormal items:**  
1. **Tricuspid systolic velocity**  
- Result: 4.9 m/s  
- Reference range: (not clearly indicated; typically >2.8 m/s suggests moderate/severe regurgitation)  
2. **Tricuspid systolic pressure gradient**  
- Result: 97 mmHg  
- Reference range: (not clearly indicated; typically >30 mmHg suggests significantly elevated right ventricular pressure)  
3. **Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF)**  
- Result: 58%  
- Reference range: (not clearly indicated; typically <55% suggests reduced left ventricular systolic function)

Bedside ultrasound: congenital heart disease: atrial septal defect (central type, bidirectional shunt), moderate-to-severe tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary arterial hypertension (high).  

Right heart catheterization:  
Pulmonary artery pressure 90/54/71 mmHg,  
descending aortic pressure 94/51/69 mmHg,  
left pulmonary arteriolar wedge pressure 7 mmHg,  
right atrial pressure 10 mmHg,  
pulmonary blood flow/systemic blood flow 0.63, pulmonary vascular resistance index 82.5 Wood units·m², descending aortic oxygen saturation 74.8%.  
Left pulmonary arteriolar angiography showed tortuous small pulmonary arteries with a withered-branch appearance and decreased filling of the capillary bed.

Chest radiographs at admission and after treatment:  
**Abnormal items:**  

**At admission (left image):**  
1. Extensive exudative lesion in the left lung (pneumonia)  
2. Exudative lesion in the right upper lung  
3. Enlarged cardiac silhouette (cardiomegaly)  
4. Blunting of the left costophrenic angle (suspected small pleural effusion)  

**After treatment (right image):**  
1. Marked absorption of the exudative lesion in the left lung  
2. Partial absorption of the exudative lesion in the right upper lung  
3. Cardiac silhouette smaller than before  
4. Pleural effusion reduced compared with before

Echocardiography at admission and before treatment:  
**Abnormal items on echocardiography after treatment:**  
1. **Right atrium**: 2.9 cm (reference range should be age-adjusted; enlargement if above normal)  
2. **Tricuspid valve**: velocity 5.5 m/s, pressure gradient 120 mmHg (significantly elevated, indicating severe regurgitation)  
3. **Left ventricular ejection fraction**: 60% (pediatric normal range usually ≥60%–70%; if below this suggests reduced systolic function)  
**Abnormal items on echocardiography before treatment:**  
1. **Left ventricle**: 2.0 cm (if above pediatric normal range suggests enlargement)  
2. **Tricuspid valve**: velocity 4.9 m/s, pressure gradient 97 mmHg (indicating moderate-to-severe regurgitation)  
3. **Left ventricular ejection fraction**: 58% (below the lower limit of pediatric normal range)

2021.09.13 Follow-up  
Findings suggested:  
Congenital heart disease: atrial septal defect (central type, bidirectional shunt), moderate tricuspid regurgitation, dilated pulmonary artery, pulmonary arterial hypertension (severe).  
Intermittent infection; cardiac function class II–III.

Review of outside-hospital contrast-enhanced chest CT: no bronchopulmonary dysplasia, pulmonary arteriovenous malformation, PVOD/PCH, thromboembolic lesions, or other pulmonary diseases. Outside-hospital left pulmonary arteriolar angiography showed no other abnormalities.

Genetic testing indicated a KMT2D gene mutation.

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Pulmonary arterial hypertension
Primary_diagnosis.Etiologic_subtype: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension most likely
Diagnostic_evidence: History: The patient began to exhibit decreased exercise tolerance at 10 months of age, presenting with tachypnea and labored breathing at rest, with symptoms worsening upon minimal activity, and perioral cyanosis observed after crying and activity
History: Right heart catheterization at an outside hospital revealed pulmonary artery pressure of 90/54/71 mmHg, pulmonary vascular resistance index of 82.5 Wood units per square meter
History: Genetic testing revealed no pathogenic variants in pulmonary arterial hypertension-related genes (**excluding hereditary factors**)
Physical examination: Marked generalized cyanosis, significantly accentuated P2, enlarged cardiac silhouette, SpO2 of extremities 66-72%

Ancillary investigations: Echocardiography showed right atrial and right ventricular enlargement (RA 2.6 cm, RV 2.7 cm), tricuspid regurgitation pressure gradient >100 mmHg (**regurgitant velocity 5.08 m/s, pressure gradient 103.2 mmHg**), pulmonary artery dilation with severe pulmonary arterial hypertension
Ancillary investigations: Right heart catheterization revealed pulmonary artery pressure of 90/54/71 mmHg, pulmonary vascular resistance index of 82.5 Wood units·m²
Ancillary investigations: Left pulmonary arteriography demonstrated tortuous small pulmonary arteries with a pruned-tree appearance and diminished capillary bed filling
Ancillary investigations: Pulmonary structural abnormalities excluded (CT showed no bronchial dysplasia, PVOD/PCH)

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Kabuki syndrome
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Congenital hypothyroidism (**a common comorbidity of Kabuki syndrome**)
History: Postnatal hypotonia and generalized muscle laxity (**typical tone abnormality**)
Physical examination: Bilateral large and protruding ears, simian crease (**characteristic dysmorphic features**)
Ancillary investigations: Genetic testing identified KMT2D gene mutation (**the major causative gene for Kabuki syndrome**)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)
Supporting_evidence: History: Right heart catheterization at an outside hospital revealed pulmonary artery pressure of 90/54/71 mmHg, pulmonary vascular resistance index of 82.5 Wood units per square meter
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Pruned-tree appearance of small pulmonary arteries (more consistent with idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension)
Ancillary investigations: Contrast-enhanced CT and pulmonary angiography showed no evidence of thromboembolism (**direct evidence excluding CTEPH**)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Connective tissue disease-associated pulmonary arterial hypertension
Supporting_evidence: History: Right heart catheterization at an outside hospital revealed pulmonary artery pressure of 90/54/71 mmHg, pulmonary vascular resistance index of 82.5 Wood units per square meter
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Rheumatologic panel and immunoglobulins were within normal limits (**no evidence of connective tissue disease**)
History: No clinical manifestations of connective tissue disease such as joint swelling and pain or rash

- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);

    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Brief Medical History
### Patient
Female, 60 years old

### Chief Complaint
Recurrent facial edema for 1 year, chest tightness and shortness of breath for 3 months, worsened for more than 10 days

### History of Present Illness
One year ago, the patient developed facial edema without an obvious trigger, worse in the morning and relieved in the evening, without redness or pruritus; without limb edema; without chest tightness or dyspnea; without yellowing of the skin or dark urine; without urinary frequency, urgency, or dysuria. The patient did not pay attention at that time. Three months ago, the patient developed chest tightness and dyspnea after activity, often occurring after climbing stairs and improving after rest, accompanied by mild pitting edema of both lower limbs; other features were the same as previously. More than 10 days ago, the symptoms of chest tightness and shortness of breath worsened, also occurring during normal walking on flat ground; she could not lie flat at night, accompanied by abdominal distension and increased bowel movements, up to more than 10 times/day, with yellow loose stool or watery stool containing food residues; abdominal pain occurred before defecation and was relieved after defecation. No hematemesis or melena; no acid regurgitation or belching. She self-administered probiotic medications without obvious improvement, and then visited the outpatient clinic of our hospital. Echocardiography (2022-04-07) showed: diffuse hypokinesis of the left ventricular wall; decreased left ventricular systolic function (LVEF: 37%); enlarged left heart, thickened left ventricular wall, mild tricuspid and mitral regurgitation, pericardial effusion. She was admitted with a provisional diagnosis of “heart failure”.
Since onset, the patient has been conscious and alert, in fair spirits, with fair appetite; urination normal; bowel movements as above; body weight increased by 5 kg in the past week.

### Past Medical History
None.

### Marital/Reproductive and Menstrual History
At age 23, spouse is healthy; has 1 child, the child is healthy.

### Family History
None.

## Physical Examination
Temperature: 37.5°C, pulse: 94 beats/min, respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min, blood pressure: 169/97 mmHg. Conscious and alert, fair spirits, mild facial edema, no cyanosis of lips, no palpable superficial lymphadenopathy, no rales heard in both lungs, decreased breath sounds in the left lung. Regular heart rhythm, no obvious murmurs in the auscultatory areas of the valves. Abdomen distended, firm on palpation, no tenderness; liver and spleen not palpable below costal margins; mild edema in both lower limbs; no obvious jugular venous distention; normal muscle strength and tone in all limbs; Babinski sign negative.

【Post-admission temperature changes】(based on the highest temperature; may decrease after physical cooling):
The temperature trend showed fluctuating changes, overall characterized by alternating phased rises and falls. Key points are as follows:

1. **First rise**: During 2022/4/8–4/10, temperature gradually increased from 37.5°C to a peak (approximately 38.5°C), then decreased to around 37.5°C during 4/11–4/13.
2. **Second rise**: On 4/14, temperature rose again to a similar peak (approximately 38.5°C), and fluctuated within 37.5–38.0°C during 4/15–4/19.
3. **Third rise**: On 4/20, temperature reached the peak for the third time (approximately 38.5°C), followed by a sustained decline phase, decreasing to below 37.0°C by 4/24.
4. **Late fluctuation**: During 4/25–4/26, temperature slightly rebounded but remained below the previous peaks.

**Values corresponding to relapse**: All three temperature elevations reached approximately 38.5°C; the interval between relapses was approximately 3–5 days, accompanied by phased decreases after physical cooling.

【Serologic indices】
Complete blood count: neutrophil percentage 75.6%. Hemoglobin, platelets, red blood cells: normal.

## Brief Medical History
### Patient
Female, 60 years old

### Chief Complaint
Recurrent facial edema for 1 year, chest tightness and shortness of breath for 3 months, worsened for more than 10 days

### History of Present Illness
One year ago, the patient developed facial edema without an obvious trigger, worse in the morning and relieved in the evening, without redness or pruritus; without limb edema; without chest tightness or dyspnea; without yellowing of the skin or dark urine; without urinary frequency, urgency, or dysuria. The patient did not pay attention at that time. Three months ago, the patient developed chest tightness and dyspnea after activity, often occurring after climbing stairs and improving after rest, accompanied by mild pitting edema of both lower limbs; other features were the same as previously. More than 10 days ago, the symptoms of chest tightness and shortness of breath worsened, also occurring during normal walking on flat ground; she could not lie flat at night, accompanied by abdominal distension and increased bowel movements, up to more than 10 times/day, with yellow loose stool or watery stool containing food residues; abdominal pain occurred before defecation and was relieved after defecation. No hematemesis or melena; no acid regurgitation or belching. She self-administered probiotic medications without obvious improvement, and then visited the outpatient clinic of our hospital. Echocardiography (2022-04-07) showed: diffuse hypokinesis of the left ventricular wall; decreased left ventricular systolic function (LVEF: 37%); enlarged left heart, thickened left ventricular wall, mild tricuspid and mitral regurgitation, pericardial effusion. She was admitted with a provisional diagnosis of “heart failure”.
Since onset, the patient has been conscious and alert, in fair spirits, with fair appetite; urination normal; bowel movements as above; body weight increased by 5 kg in the past week.

### Past Medical History
None.

### Marital/Reproductive and Menstrual History
At age 23, spouse is healthy; has 1 child, the child is healthy.

### Family History
None.

## Physical Examination
Temperature: 37.5°C, pulse: 94 beats/min, respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min, blood pressure: 169/97 mmHg. Conscious and alert, fair spirits, mild facial edema, no cyanosis of lips, no palpable superficial lymphadenopathy, no rales heard in both lungs, decreased breath sounds in the left lung. Regular heart rhythm, no obvious murmurs in the auscultatory areas of the valves. 4. **Late fluctuation**: During 4/25–4/26, temperature slightly rebounded but remained below the previous peaks.

- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Response Format
- When generating the final answer: you must output the diagnostic results directly in Json format (example below), and do not output any other irrelevant content;

- {"Preliminary diagnosis": {"Primary diagnosis": {"Name": "XXX", "Etiologic subtype": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}, "Secondary diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}]}, "Differential diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Selection rationale": "Consider rare but potentially fatal diseases", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": [], "Excludable": true, "Recommended corresponding tests": []}, {"Name": "YYY", "Selection rationale": "Consider differential diagnosis of adjacent organ lesions", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1"], "Excludable": false, "Recommended corresponding tests": ["Recommendation 1", "Recommendation 2"]}]}

## Brief History of Present Illness
### Patient
Male, 52 years old

### Chief Complaint
Fatigue and shortness of breath for 3 years, aggravated after exertion for 1 month; admitted on 2021-07-06

### History of Present Illness
The patient developed fatigue and shortness of breath during activity 3 years ago, which were aggravated after exertion. He visited a local hospital and improved after medication treatment (details unknown). Over the past month, the patient has felt fatigued with mild activity and weakness of both lower limbs; dyspnea on exertion has also worsened compared with before, accompanied by edema of both lower limbs. No chest pain, no cough or sputum, no dizziness or headache, no amaurosis or syncope, and no disturbance of consciousness. He visited the outpatient clinic of the Cardiology Department of our hospital. ECG showed: left ventricular hypertrophy with ST-T changes. Echocardiography: biatrial enlargement, ventricular wall thickening (IVS 20 mm, PW 17 mm), left ventricular outflow tract obstruction (estimated pressure gradient 39 mmHg), EF 75.4%. He was admitted to our department with “hypertrophic cardiomyopathy”.

### Past Medical History
History of hypertension for more than 1 year, with maximum blood pressure 155/93 mmHg; usually takes oral nifedipine sustained-release tablets, with acceptable control. Denies history of diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, and cerebrovascular disease; denies history of peptic ulcer and glaucoma; denies history of infectious diseases such as hepatitis and tuberculosis; denies history of surgery, trauma, and blood transfusion; denies food and drug allergy history.

### Personal History
Occupation: mason. No smoking, alcohol drinking, or other adverse habits.

### Marital and Reproductive History / Menstrual History
Married at age 25; has 1 son and 1 daughter; spouse and children are all healthy.

### Family History
Denies history among other family members of coronary heart disease, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, heart failure, and unexplained sudden death.

## Physical Examination
Height 176 cm, weight 75 kg, BMI 24.21 kg/㎡  
T 36.5℃  P 98 beats/min   R 18 breaths/min   BP 100/69 mmHg  
Spontaneous position, alert with clear speech, cooperative on examination. Jugular venous distension, positive hepatojugular reflux. Breath sounds clear bilaterally; no dry or wet rales heard in both lungs. Heart rate 98 beats/min, regular rhythm; no obvious murmurs heard in any valvular auscultation areas. Abdomen soft, no tenderness, rebound tenderness, or muscle guarding; liver and spleen not palpable below costal margins. Elevated pulmonary artery systolic pressure (estimated 42 mmHg)

## Additional History
### Additional Laboratory Tests
Main laboratory results on the day of admission

Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 68↑

pH: 7.206↓   pCO2: 103.2 mmHg↑  pO2: 65.9 mmHg↓

pO2: 75 mmHg↓  
pCO2: 91.6 mmHg↑  
sO2: 92.7%

Lac: 2.74 mmol/L↑  

Changes in arterial blood gas (after 3 days of noninvasive assisted ventilation, condition improved):  
**Trend and key time-point description:**  
1. **pH**: gradually increased from 7.277 (↓) to 7.374, indicating alleviated acidosis.

Over the past month, the patient has felt fatigued with mild activity and weakness of both lower limbs; dyspnea on exertion has also worsened compared with before, accompanied by edema of both lower limbs. No chest pain, no cough or sputum, no dizziness or headache, no amaurosis or syncope, and no disturbance of consciousness. He visited the outpatient clinic of the Cardiology Department of our hospital. ECG showed: left ventricular hypertrophy with ST-T changes. Echocardiography: biatrial enlargement, ventricular wall thickening (IVS 20 mm, PW 17 mm), left ventricular outflow tract obstruction (estimated pressure gradient 39 mmHg), EF 75.4%. ### Past Medical History
History of hypertension for more than 1 year, with maximum blood pressure 155/93 mmHg; usually takes oral nifedipine sustained-release tablets, with acceptable control. Denies history of diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, and cerebrovascular disease; denies history of peptic ulcer and glaucoma; denies history of infectious diseases such as hepatitis and tuberculosis; denies history of surgery, trauma, and blood transfusion; denies food and drug allergy history.

Occupation: mason. No smoking, alcohol drinking, or other adverse habits.

Denies history among other family members of coronary heart disease, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, heart failure, and unexplained sudden death.

## Brief Medical History
### Patient
Male, 61 years old

### Chief Complaint
“Myocardial hypertrophy found on physical examination for more than 20 years; chest tightness for 6 years, worsened for half a year.”

### History of Present Illness
Twenty years ago, an abnormal electrocardiogram was found on physical examination; echocardiography revealed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (non-obstructive type); asymptomatic. Six years ago, recurrent chest tightness with shortness of breath occurred, induced by activity and exertion; myocardial enzyme profile, serum cardiac troponin I, and NT-proBNP were elevated; urine free light chains and urine immunofixation electrophoresis were normal.  
Six years ago, chest tightness with shortness of breath occurred after bathing; at an outside hospital, coronary angiography showed normal findings; no other discomfort. Half a year ago, chest tightness and dyspnea worsened; exercise tolerance declined. Echocardiography: restrictive cardiomyopathy, possible amyloidosis, EF: 35%. Cardiac MRI: suggested dilated phase of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.  
Holter ECG: non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, maximum duration 8 seconds. The patient visited the outpatient clinic of our hospital;  
Outside-hospital ECG:  
**Abnormal items:**  
- Mild ST-segment elevation in lead V3 (approximately 0.1 mV)  

Outside-hospital echocardiography Ultrasound diagnosis: 1. Global ventricular wall thickening and mitral leaflet thickening; left ventricle and right atrium enlarged; decreased left and right ventricular systolic function; left ventricular diastolic dysfunction grade III; severe mitral regurgitation, considered mixed type; small pericardial effusion. Suggestive of restrictive cardiomyopathy, possible amyloidosis; further examinations recommended. 2. Mild tricuspid regurgitation with moderate pulmonary hypertension;  
Outside-hospital ECT PYP radionuclide Imaging diagnosis: acute myocardial infarction focus imaging: 1. No obvious acute myocardial infarction focus changes; 2. Mild myocardial radiotracer uptake, with uptake lower than ribs (grade 1), H/CL < 1.5; no typical ATTR cardiac amyloidosis. In conjunction with the medical history, early ATTR type or AL type cardiac amyloidosis should be differentiated; follow-up recommended.;  
Outside-hospital cardiac MRI Imaging diagnosis: 1. Left ventricular enlargement; diffuse thickening of the interventricular septum; focal thinning of the left ventricular lateral wall; biventricular systolic dysfunction predominantly involving the left ventricle. Late gadolinium enhancement suggests diffuse linear fibrotic scar formation in the mid-myocardial layer of the interventricular septum; decompensated phase of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is the first consideration. 2. Small pericardial effusion.;  
Outside-hospital bone marrow aspiration pathology Diagnostic conclusion: (bone marrow aspiration) hypocellular bone marrow;  
Outside-hospital bone marrow cytogenetic karyotype report interpretation and recommendations: No chromosomal numerical or structural abnormalities related to tumor were found; it is recommended to perform a comprehensive analysis and judgment in combination with other examination results;  
Outside-hospital abdominal wall fat biopsy Diagnostic conclusion: (abdominal wall fat) adipose tissue and small fragments of squamous epithelium; focally, amorphous eosinophilic material was seen. The specimen was sent out for mass spectrometry amyloidosis typing; it was returned due to an unqualified specimen.

### Past Medical History
Blurred vision occurred 3 years ago and was not taken seriously; history of tonsillectomy and appendectomy; no other medical history

### Personal History
Alcohol drinking habit; drinking for more than 30 years, 0.5 liang of liquor/day; occasional smoking.

### Marital/Reproductive History and Menstrual History
Married, no children

### Family History
Parents deceased, cause of death unknown; two younger sisters are healthy; no family history of hereditary predisposition diseases

## Physical Examination
T: 36.5°C, P: 75 beats/min, R: 20 breaths/min, BP: 100/70 mmHg. Clear consciousness, good general condition. No jaundice of skin or sclera. No palpable enlargement of superficial lymph nodes. Trachea midline. No jugular venous distention; hepatojugular reflux negative. Breath sounds clear in both lungs; no obvious moist rales heard at both lower lungs. Heart rate 75 beats/min, regular rhythm; a grade 3/6 systolic murmur was heard at the mitral auscultation area; no obvious murmurs in other valve auscultation areas. Abdomen flat and soft; no tenderness or rebound tenderness; liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin; shifting dullness negative. No obvious edema in both lower extremities. Neurological examination negative.

## Supplementary History
### Supplementary Laboratory Tests
Laboratory/examination results in our hospital: complete blood count, liver and renal function, coagulation function, D-dimer, thyroid function, tumor markers, high-sensitivity C-reactive protein, etc. showed no obvious abnormalities. High-sensitivity cardiac troponin I 0.566 ng/mL (0.000–0.034) B-type natriuretic peptide 1815 pg/mL (0–80) Six-minute walk test: 500 m Fundus examination suggested cataract; no other obvious abnormalities;

**Abnormal items:**  
1. α-galactosidase A (GLA): 0.79 μmol/L/h (reference range: 2.40–17.65 μmol/L/h)

Poor R-wave progression in the anterolateral wall 6. Marked ST-segment and T-wave changes (widespread coved ST-segment changes/T-wave inversion across multiple leads); please correlate clinically  
CMR cardiac imaging:  
**Abnormal items:**  
- Mid-myocardial late gadolinium enhancement (on PSMDE sequences, circumferential mid-myocardial enhancement was seen at the basal level and papillary muscle level)

## Brief History
### Patient
Female, 67 years old

### Chief Complaint
“Recurrent chest tightness for 2 months”

### History of Present Illness
Chest tightness and shortness of breath after activity, relieved after rest, accompanied by bilateral lower-extremity edema.  
First hospitalization (2018.5.31–2018.06.25)  
2018-06-21 Holter: Third-degree atrioventricular block, junctional escape rhythm.  
2018-06-21 Echocardiography: Left atrial enlargement with suspected thrombus formation, aortic sclerosis, severe aortic regurgitation, discordant left ventricular wall motion, pericardial effusion.  
2018-06-24 Complete blood count: WBC 5.8*10^9/L, CRP 23.8 mg/L.  
Coronary angiography: 40%–50% stenosis in the mid LAD; distal myocardial bridging (80%–90% systolic compression); 80% stenosis in the proximal-to-mid LCX; 30% stenosis in the proximal-to-mid RCA.  
A pacemaker implantation was planned; however, fever developed during hospitalization, mainly low-grade, with a maximum temperature of 38°C. Anti-infective therapy with cefuroxime, piperacillin-tazobactam, and moxifloxacin was given, but recurrent fever persisted.  
Second hospitalization (2018.6.25–2018.07.18): due to recurrent fever at a local hospital, the patient was transferred to a provincial tertiary hospital on 2018-06-25.  
After admission, the patient occasionally had paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea and orthopnea.  
ESR 38 mm/h, CRP 17.8 mg/L, CA125 35.2 U/mL, NSE 16.5 ng/mL  
PPD and T-SPOT negative; Mycobacterium tuberculosis antibody (+); blood culture (-); antinuclear antibody (-)  
2018-06-28 Echocardiography: Left atrial enlargement, moderate aortic regurgitation, moderate pericardial effusion.  
2018-07-03 Echocardiography: Segmental left ventricular wall-motion abnormalities (anterior wall, anterior septum), left atrial enlargement, degenerative aortic valve changes with mild regurgitation, pericardial effusion.  
After admission, metformin + glimepiride were given for glycemic control; atorvastatin for lipid regulation; nifedipine + metoprolol for blood pressure and heart rate control; nadroparin for anticoagulation; furosemide + spironolactone for diuresis; biapenem for anti-infective therapy.  
2018-07-09 Dual-chamber pacemaker implantation.  
2018-07-10 Pericardial effusion analysis: Light yellow fluid, Pandy test positive, predominantly lymphocytes; smear and centrifuged microscopic examination found no malignant cells.  
2018-07-12 ECHO: Visceral pericardial thickening, blood clot? Aortic sclerosis, widened ascending aorta, left atrial enlargement, degenerative aortic valve changes with mild regurgitation, pericardial effusion.  
2018-07-18 Discharged after symptom relief.  
Discharge diagnoses at the local hospital: 1. Third-degree atrioventricular block 2. Coronary atherosclerotic heart disease 3. Pericardial effusion (tuberculous?) 4. Cardiac function class IV 5. Hypertension (extremely high risk) 6. Type 2 diabetes mellitus  
Before the third hospitalization (2018.7.25–7.27)  
2018-7-25 The patient had recurrent chest tightness and dyspnea and presented to the emergency department of our hospital.  
Physical examination: Enlarged cardiac dullness, distant heart sounds.  
Emergency echocardiography: Aortic valve insufficiency (moderate), possible prolapse of the right coronary cusp; abnormal motion of the apical segment of the interventricular septum and the apical segment of the inferior wall of the left ventricle; widened ascending aorta; left atrial enlargement; decreased left ventricular diastolic function; mild tricuspid and mitral regurgitation; pericardial effusion (large).  
Further history: Chest tightness persisted, with no obvious correlation with activity, respiration, or food intake; the patient preferred the right lateral decubitus position at night.  
Transferred to our department on 2018-7-27 for further treatment.

### Past Medical History
Past medical history: Hypertension for 30 years; diabetes mellitus for 10 years.

## Supplementary History
### Additional Laboratory Tests
After admission (2018.7.27–)  
2018-7-27 Pericardiocentesis: Massive pericardial effusion; at end-diastole, fluid width at the left ventricular apex was approximately 1.6 cm; entered a hypoechoic fluid area in the pericardial cavity; 100 mL of light bloody fluid was aspirated. Pericardial effusion: Rivalta test (weakly positive), RBC 8000, nucleated cells 500 (lymphocytes 80%, neutrophils 20%), bacterial culture (-), acid-fast bacilli not found, protein 38.74 g/L, ADA 21.7 mmol/L, LDH 998 U/L.  
Complete blood count: No significant abnormality. Blood biochemistry: LDH 749 U/L, BNP 1409 pg/mL.  
Thyroid function, autoimmune tests, TSOPT: No positive findings.  
Tumor markers: CA125 253.2 U/mL.  
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): ESR 30 mm/1 hour; high-sensitivity C-reactive protein 35.88 mg/L. TNI: 0.427 ng/mL.  
Bone marrow pathologic biopsy: (Bone marrow biopsy) hematopoietic tissue proliferation was relatively active.

**Abnormal items:**  
1. CD3 negative (loss of T-cell marker)  
2. CD20 strongly positive (significant expression of B-cell marker)

**List of abnormal items:**  
1. CD10 negative expression  
2. Ki67 high expression (>80%)

**Abnormal items:**  
1. Bcl2 positive expression  
2. Bcl6 positive expression

**Abnormal items:**  
- cMyc positive expression (cMyc+)  
- MUM1 positive expression (MUM1+)

### Additional Examinations
After admission (2018.7.27–)  
2018-07-30 Transvaginal color Doppler ultrasound: Atrophic uterus; fluid in the uterine cavity; pelvic effusion.  
2018-07-30 Non-contrast chest CT: Inflammatory focus in the left lower lobe. Fibroproliferative lesions in both lungs considered. Left pleural effusion and pericardial effusion. Cardiomegaly; post–cardiac pacemaker implantation changes. Coronary artery calcifications.  
2018-08-01 Color Doppler echocardiography: Space-occupying lesion of the left atrial wall. Aortic valve insufficiency (moderate). Hypokinesis of the apical segment of the interventricular septum and the apical segment of the inferior wall of the left ventricle; please correlate with other examinations such as coronary CTA. Widened ascending aorta; left atrial enlargement. Mild tricuspid and mitral regurgitation. Pericardial effusion (trace). Atrial fibrillation. Post–pacemaker implantation.  
An isoechoic mass measuring approximately 104*36 mm was detected at the posterior wall of the left atrium.  
2018-08-02 Contrast-enhanced ultrasound of organs: Huge left atrial mass, hyperperfusion, tumor considered.  
2018-08-02 Whole-abdomen CT (non-contrast + contrast-enhanced): Focal gallbladder wall thickening, adenomyomatosis considered. Right renal angiomyolipoma. Nodular lesion in the left kidney, possible benign neoplastic lesion; further examination recommended. Nodular lesion in the left adrenal region, tumor considered. Pelvic effusion. Multiple subcutaneous nodules in the lower abdominal wall and right perineum; please correlate clinically. Incidental findings: Left atrial wall mass, pericardial effusion, left pleural effusion.  
2018-08-07 Whole-body PET-CT: Left atrial enlargement; a mass-like soft tissue density in the left atrium with increased FDG uptake, suggesting malignancy (lymphoma highly possible), with pericardial effusion; mild soft-tissue thickening with increased FDG uptake in the pericardial intravascular space between the great vessels and locally in the inferior vena cava, with unclear boundaries with the cardiac chambers; nodular increased FDG uptake in the left ventricle, infiltration considered; the lesion in the hepatic segment of the inferior vena cava had an indistinct boundary with the liver; soft tissue density with increased FDG uptake at the right cardiophrenic angle and at the anterior margin of the heart within the pericardium, para-aortic region, and deep surface of the right diaphragmatic crus; focal mild thickening of the right posteroinferior pleura with increased FDG uptake; slight soft-tissue thickening within the glandular tissue of both breasts with mild FDG uptake, tumor infiltration considered; a left adrenal nodular shadow with increased FDG uptake; a small nodular increased FDG uptake focus in the right adrenal gland; nodular soft tissue density shadows in both kidneys with increased FDG uptake; a soft tissue density shadow within the gallbladder lumen with increased FDG uptake; flocculent/nodular soft tissue density shadows in the abdominal mesentery, omentum posterior to the right kidney, and mesentery posterior to the left uterus with increased FDG uptake; multiple subcutaneous soft tissue nodules in the right lower abdomen with mild FDG uptake, tumor infiltration considered; focal mild thickening of the wall on the greater curvature side of the gastric body with increased FDG uptake; mild increased FDG uptake in the left anteroinferior chest wall; focal increased FDG uptake in the left tibialis anterior muscle, tumor infiltration considered.

2018-08-09 Superficial ultrasound: No obvious abnormality in the subcutaneous soft-tissue layer of the perineum. No suspicious superficial lymph nodes detected.  
Ultrasound Department: 2018.8.2 parasternal four-chamber two-dimensional image  
2018.8.2 contrast-enhanced ultrasound, parasternal four-chamber  
2018.8.2 parasternal four-chamber two-dimensional image  
Right atrial thrombus, 2D; right atrial thrombus, non-perfusion; left atrial myxoma, 2D; left atrial myxoma, static display of interatrial septal pedicle;  
Left ventricular thrombus, 2D; left ventricular thrombus, perfusion image  
2018.8.6 PET-CT:  
**Abnormal items:**  
1. Multiple enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes with hypermetabolism (SUVmax > 2.5)  
2. Multiple abnormal hypermetabolic foci in the liver and spleen  
3. Multiple osteolytic destructive lesions in the skeletal system with hypermetabolism  

After repeated communication with the patient’s family, the department decided to have a surgeon perform a cardiac mass biopsy. The patient underwent cardiac tumor biopsy + pericardial window under general anesthesia on 2018-8-20. Intraoperative findings: The heart was distended; massive light yellow pericardial effusion; a firm mass was palpable in the left atrium. Under transesophageal echocardiographic guidance, several strips of tumor specimens were obtained using a core biopsy needle. The procedure was uneventful, and hemostasis was ensured. One chest drainage tube was placed on each side, and the patient was transferred postoperatively to the surgical ICU.

2018-8-27 (Heart) Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (diffuse large B-cell, non-GCB type)  
CD3 (-), CD20 (+), Ki-67 (+90%), CD79a (+), CD30 (-), CD5 (-), CD10 (-), Bcl-2 (+), Bcl-6 (+), MUM1 (+), PAX-5 (+), CD21 (-), CyclinD1 (partially +), c-Myc (+60%), ALK (-), EBER (-), CK(pan) (-).

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Cardiac diffuse large B-cell lymphoma
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Recurrent chest tightness for 2 months, exertional chest tightness and dyspnea accompanied by bilateral lower extremity edema; recurrent fever during hospitalization with poor response to anti-infective therapy
Physical examination: Enlarged cardiac borders, distant heart sounds
Ancillary examination: Organ contrast-enhanced ultrasound on 2018-08-02 revealed a large left atrial mass (hyperperfusion, tumor suspected)

Ancillary examination: PET-CT on 2018-08-07 demonstrated a mass-like soft tissue density shadow in the left atrium with increased FDG metabolism (lymphoma highly suspected), with multiple metastatic lesions

Ancillary examination: Cardiac tumor biopsy pathology on 2018-08-27 confirmed non-Hodgkin lymphoma (diffuse large B-cell, NON-GCB subtype), immunohistochemistry showing strongly positive CD20, Ki-67 (+90%)

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Aortic valve regurgitation
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Echocardiography on 2018-06-21 showed severe aortic valve regurgitation; echocardiography on 2018-08-01 indicated aortic valve insufficiency (moderate)

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Third-degree atrioventricular block
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Holter monitor on 2018-06-21 showed third-degree atrioventricular block with junctional escape rhythm

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Post-pacemaker implantation
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Dual-chamber pacemaker implantation performed on 2018-07-09; echocardiography on 2018-08-01 indicated post-pacemaker implantation status

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Hypertension
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Hypertension for 30 years

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Type 2 diabetes mellitus
Diagnostic_evidence: History: Diabetes mellitus for 10 years; blood glucose controlled with metformin (Glucophage) and repaglinide (Fuladi) after admission

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Coronary atherosclerotic heart disease
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Coronary angiography showed 40%-50% stenosis in the mid-segment of LAD, 80% stenosis in the proximal-to-mid segment of LCX

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Cardiac myxoma
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Echocardiography on 2018-08-01 showed a 104×36 mm isoechoic mass on the posterior wall of the left atrium; contrast-enhanced ultrasound on 2018-08-02 indicated left atrial mass
Non-supporting_evidence: History: The patient's chest tightness symptoms were unrelated to body position (myxoma commonly presents with positional symptoms)
Ancillary examination: Pathology did not reveal the typical spindle cell structure of myxoma
Ancillary examination: PET-CT on 2018-08-07 showed increased FDG metabolism (myxoma typically does not exhibit hypermetabolism); pathology on 2018-08-27 confirmed lymphoma

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Metastatic cardiac tumor
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: PET-CT on 2018-08-07 showed multiple metastatic lesions in the liver, spleen, and bones
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: No superficial lymph node enlargement (superficial ultrasound)
Ancillary examination: Pathological immunohistochemistry confirmed diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (primary cardiac origin)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Tuberculous pericarditis
Supporting_evidence: History: Prolonged low-grade fever, elevated ESR/CRP
Ancillary examination: Tuberculosis antibody (+)
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: No acid-fast bacilli found in pericardial effusion, T-SPOT negative; PPD and T-SPOT negative; pericardial effusion ADA 21.7 mmol/L (<40 mmol/L does not support tuberculosis)
Ancillary examination: No granuloma found on pathology
Ancillary examination: PET-CT on 2018-08-07 showed features characteristic of neoplastic lesion
Recommended_corresponding_tests: Tuberculosis PCR testing of pericardial effusion recommended
