raw_query:
### Brief Medical History
Patient Information  
Sex: Female  
Age: 18 years  
Chief complaint: Recurrent post-exertional shortness of breath for 3 years; one episode of sudden syncope.

History of present illness:  
3 years ago (February 2020):  
The patient began to experience recurrent shortness of breath after activity. A local hospital considered “asthma” and provided “anti-asthmatic therapy”; the symptoms did not improve significantly, and no further evaluation or treatment was pursued.

2 years ago (January 2021):  
The patient dropped out of school and stayed at home without going out to work. She often felt fatigued; post-exertional shortness of breath was obvious and could be relieved by rest. There was no amaurosis fugax or syncope, no dizziness, no sensory or motor abnormalities of the limbs, and no vertigo. On detailed history taking, it was found that there had also been a sense of fatigue at rest over the past nearly 1 year; therefore, she preferred to stay quiet and move less.

This admission (April 2023):  
5 hours prior to admission, the patient suddenly experienced syncope while walking outdoors, fell to the ground, and, according to family members, regained consciousness spontaneously in about 2 minutes, without convulsions during the episode. An electrocardiogram in the emergency department suggested “third-degree atrioventricular block.” She was admitted after implantation of a temporary pacemaker.

Past medical history: Denies chronic diseases such as hypertension, diabetes mellitus, cerebral infarction, etc. Denies history of surgery, trauma, or infectious diseases.  
Obstetric and gynecologic history: Unmarried and nulliparous.

Menstrual history, personal history, family history: Regular menses with normal menstrual flow. Denies smoking and alcohol use; denies a history of long-term medication use. Both parents are healthy; no siblings; no similar medical history in the family; no family history of premature cardiovascular disease.

### Physical Examination
Vital signs: Temperature 36.5°C, pulse 60 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, blood pressure 105/70 mmHg.  
General condition: Normal facial appearance, normal development, no skin erythema, warm extremities, facial abrasions/contusions visible.  
Neck examination: No jugular venous distention; hepatojugular reflux negative; temporary pacemaker in place via the right internal jugular vein, with no local redness, swelling, or exudation.  
Chest examination: Scattered moist rales heard in both lower lungs.  
Cardiac examination: Cardiac borders enlarged bilaterally; heart rate 60 beats/min, regular rhythm; no murmurs in any valve auscultation area; no pericardial friction rub.  
Abdomen and extremities: Liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin; shifting dullness (-); mild edema of both lower extremities.  
Neurological examination: Normal muscle tone in all extremities; bilateral biceps and triceps muscle strength grade 5, bilateral handgrip strength grade 5; quadriceps strength grade 4+; plantar flexion strength grade 4; dorsiflexion strength grade 4.

### Ancillary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission  
Laboratory tests:  
Complete blood count: WBC 10.4×10^9/L, neutrophils 75.6%, hemoglobin 139 g/L, platelets 155×10^9/L.  
Inflammatory markers: C-reactive protein <0.08 mg/L.  
Biochemistry: ALT 20.1 U/L, AST 37.5 U/L, albumin 35.6 g/L, creatinine 49.5 µmol/L, uric acid 584.2 µmol/L.  
Myocardial biomarkers: Troponin T 0.074 ng/mL, myoglobin 75.16 ng/mL, CK-MB 10.28 ng/mL, NT-proBNP 8230 pg/mL, creatine kinase 201.5 U/L.  
Immunity and metabolism: No abnormalities in autoimmune-related indices; no abnormalities in serum free light chains; urine protein electrophoresis negative; immunofixation electrophoresis negative; no abnormalities in serum iron and transferrin saturation.

Electrocardiogram (ECG):  
Emergency ECG: Sinus rhythm, third-degree atrioventricular block, junctional escape rhythm with a heart rate of 42 beats/min.  
Holter ECG (day 3 after admission): Paced rhythm combined with intrinsic rhythm; average heart rate 68 beats/min; frequent premature atrial contractions 14,260 beats; short runs of atrial tachycardia; frequent multiform premature ventricular contractions 12,497 beats; no long RR intervals and no long QT intervals observed.

Imaging studies:  
Chest CT: Multiple patchy ground-glass opacity exudative lesions in both lungs, with interlobular septal thickening and diffuse thickening of bronchial walls in all lobes bilaterally (suggestive of pulmonary edema as a high possibility); marked cardiomegaly, temporary cardiac pacemaker in situ; small bilateral pleural effusions; mild bilateral pleural thickening.  
Echocardiography (day 2 after admission): Left atrium 45 mm, right atrium 50×39 mm, LV end-diastolic diameter 38 mm, LV end-systolic diameter 23 mm, interventricular septal thickness 18–20 mm, LV posterior wall thickness 14–15 mm, LVEF 48%. At rest, global LV wall motion was dyssynchronous; myocardial echoes were coarse and granular; myocardial thickening; restricted systolic and diastolic function.

Coronary angiography (day 2 after admission):  
No significant stenosis in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, or right coronary artery; distal flow TIMI grade 3.

2. Further examinations after admission (multimodal imaging and pathology)  
Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR, day 9 after admission):  
Morphology and function: Biatrial enlargement (left atrium 40 mm, right atrium 51 mm); symmetric LV wall thickening, maximal thickness approximately 19 mm; LVEF 45%.  
Tissue characteristics: Late gadolinium enhancement demonstrated transmural LGE of the LV wall; relatively absent enhancement from the basal to mid interventricular septum; significant involvement of the apex and free wall, with a non-ischemic distribution; no edematous changes of acute-phase myocarditis; no signal of fatty infiltration; no T2-weighted findings suggestive of iron overload.

Radionuclide imaging (day 10 after admission):  
99mTc-PYP scintigraphy: Visual score 1.

Electromyography (day 11 after admission):  
Suggestive of myogenic injury; proximal muscles of both upper and lower extremities were more prominently involved; partial motor unit potentials were narrowed in multiple muscles.

Gastrocnemius muscle biopsy (day 12 after admission):  
Pathology results: H&E staining showed slight swelling of striated muscle, with indistinct borders and striations; widened perinuclear spaces; no inflammatory cell infiltration.  
Immunofluorescence: Staining for desmin encoded by DES showed uneven desmin distribution with abnormal accumulation.

Genetic testing (day 12 after admission):  
Whole-exome sequencing: The subject carried a heterozygous DES gene variant NM_001927.3 c.1360C>T, resulting in substitution of arginine with tryptophan at amino acid position 454 of the encoded protein (p.Arg454Trp).

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Desmin-related cardiomyopathy
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Recurrent exertional dyspnea for 3 years, one episode of sudden syncope
Symptoms: Frequent self-perceived fatigue, exertional dyspnea relieved by rest
Physical examination: Cardiac borders enlarged bilaterally, heart rate 60 beats/min, regular rhythm
Physical examination: Scattered moist rales heard in bilateral lower lung fields
Physical examination: Mild bilateral lower extremity edema
Ancillary examination: Electrocardiogram indicating third-degree atrioventricular block
Ancillary examination: NT-proBNP significantly elevated to 8230 pg/mL
Ancillary examination: Troponin T 0.074 ng/mL

Ancillary examination: Echocardiography showing left atrium 45 mm, right atrium 50×39 mm, left ventricular end-diastolic diameter 38 mm, left ventricular end-systolic diameter 23 mm, interventricular septal thickness 18–20 mm, left ventricular posterior wall thickness 14–15 mm, left ventricular ejection fraction 48%
Ancillary examination: Echocardiographic conclusion of myocardial involvement, ventricular wall thickening, mildly reduced left ventricular function, and biatrial enlargement

Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging revealed biatrial enlargement, left atrial diameter 40 mm, right atrial diameter approximately 51 mm; symmetric left ventricular wall thickening with maximum thickness of approximately 19 mm

Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance late gadolinium enhancement showing transmural late gadolinium enhancement of the left ventricular wall, relative absence of enhancement in the basal to mid interventricular septum, with significant involvement of the apex and free wall, demonstrating a non-ischemic distribution pattern

Ancillary examination: Whole exome sequencing identified a heterozygous variant in the DES gene NM_001927.3 c.1360C>T, located in exon 8, resulting in substitution of arginine to tryptophan at amino acid position 454 of the encoded protein (p.Arg454Trp), classified as pathogenic by ACMG criteria

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Desmin myopathy
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Frequent self-perceived fatigue, exertional dyspnea relieved by rest
Symptoms: Fatigue at rest for approximately one year, leading to a sedentary lifestyle with reduced physical activity

Physical examination: Decreased muscle strength in all four extremities, with prominent involvement of proximal lower extremity muscles; quadriceps muscle strength grade 4+, plantar flexion and dorsiflexion muscle strength both grade 4
Ancillary examination: Creatine kinase mildly elevated to 201.5 U/L

Ancillary examination: Electromyography results indicating myopathic changes, with predominant involvement of proximal muscles of bilateral upper and lower extremities, and narrowed motor unit potentials in multiple muscles

Ancillary examination: Gastrocnemius muscle biopsy with H&E staining showing mild swelling of striated muscle fibers, indistinct borders and cross-striations, widened perinuclear spaces, and absence of inflammatory cell infiltration, suggestive of skeletal muscle degenerative changes
Ancillary examination: Immunofluorescence staining targeting desmin encoded by the DES gene revealed uneven distribution of desmin with abnormal accumulation

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Heart failure (mid-range ejection fraction, NYHA functional class III)
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Recurrent exertional dyspnea for 3 years
Symptoms: Frequent self-perceived fatigue, exertional dyspnea relieved by rest
Physical examination: Scattered moist rales heard in bilateral lower lung fields
Physical examination: Mild bilateral lower extremity edema
Ancillary examination: NT-proBNP significantly elevated to 8230 pg/mL

Ancillary examination: Chest CT plain scan revealed bilateral multiple patchy ground-glass opacity exudative shadows, with bilateral interlobular septal thickening and extensive thickening of bronchial walls in all lobes of both lungs, highly suggestive of pulmonary edema
Ancillary examination: Echocardiography showing left ventricular ejection fraction 48%
Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showing left ventricular ejection fraction 45%

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Third-degree atrioventricular block
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: One episode of sudden syncope
Physical examination: Heart rate 60 beats/min (paced rhythm)
Ancillary examination: Emergency electrocardiogram indicating third-degree atrioventricular block
Ancillary examination: Emergency electrocardiogram showing sinus rhythm, third-degree atrioventricular block, junctional escape rhythm at 42 beats per minute

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Cardiac arrhythmia (frequent atrial premature complexes, paroxysmal atrial tachycardia, frequent multifocal ventricular premature complexes)
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Ambulatory electrocardiographic monitoring on the third day of admission showed paced rhythm alternating with intrinsic rhythm, average heart rate 68 beats per minute, frequent atrial premature complexes totaling 14,260, paroxysmal atrial tachycardia, and frequent multifocal ventricular premature complexes totaling 12,497

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Facial soft tissue contusion
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Sudden syncope with fall to the ground
Physical examination: Visible fall-related injury on the face

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Acute coronary syndrome
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Consideration of a disease that may lead to serious consequences; the patient had an upward trend in cardiac enzyme levels after admission, and electrocardiographic changes were present
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Recurrent exertional dyspnea
Ancillary examination: Cardiac biomarker testing revealed troponin T of 0.074 ng/mL

Ancillary examination: Cardiac enzyme levels showed an upward trend after admission (troponin T increased from 0.074 ng/mL on the day of admission to 0.991 ng/mL on the following day; CK-MB increased from 10.28 ng/mL to 57.48 ng/mL)
Ancillary examination: Electrocardiogram showing ST-T changes in precordial leads
Non-supporting_evidence: Symptoms: The patient is a young female without risk factors such as hypertension or diabetes mellitus

Ancillary examination: Coronary angiography showed no significant stenosis in the left main coronary artery, left anterior descending artery, left circumflex artery, or right coronary artery, with distal TIMI grade 3 flow

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Myocarditis
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Consideration of common and frequently occurring diseases; the patient presented with dyspnea, electrocardiographic changes, and elevated myocardial injury biomarkers
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Recurrent exertional dyspnea
Ancillary examination: Electrocardiogram indicating third-degree atrioventricular block
Ancillary examination: Cardiac biomarker testing revealed troponin T of 0.074 ng/mL
Ancillary examination: Echocardiography showing ventricular wall thickening and mildly reduced left ventricular function
Non-supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Denied recent history of prodromal infection such as upper respiratory tract infection or diarrhea

Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed no edematous changes characteristic of acute-phase myocarditis; late gadolinium enhancement was predominantly in the left ventricular lateral wall, transmural in distribution, rather than confined to the subepicardial region

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Cardiac amyloidosis (AL type and ATTR type)
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Differential diagnosis from diseases sharing common features (restrictive cardiomyopathy phenotype)
Supporting_evidence: Physical examination: Mild bilateral lower extremity edema
Ancillary examination: Echocardiography showing ventricular wall thickening, mildly reduced left ventricular function, and biatrial enlargement
Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showing symmetric left ventricular wall thickening with fibrotic changes, restrictive phenotype cardiomyopathy
Non-supporting_evidence: Symptoms: No systemic manifestations such as carpal tunnel syndrome, nephrotic syndrome, autonomic or peripheral neuropathy, gastrointestinal symptoms, liver disease, macroglossia, or vitreous involvement
Ancillary examination: Serum free light chain assay showed no abnormalities; urine protein electrophoresis negative; immunofixation electrophoresis negative
Ancillary examination: Electrocardiogram showed myocardial hypertrophy but QRS complexes did not demonstrate low voltage
Ancillary examination: 99mTc-PYP nuclear scintigraphy with a visual score of 1, inconsistent with ATTR-type cardiac amyloidosis

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Cardiac sarcoidosis
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Consideration of a disease that may lead to serious consequences and involves the cardiac conduction system
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Electrocardiogram indicating third-degree atrioventricular block
Ancillary examination: Echocardiography showing ventricular wall thickening
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Chest CT plain scan showed no mediastinal lymphadenopathy, and no pulmonary micronodules or fibrotic changes

Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed diffuse, symmetric myocardial hypertrophy rather than the focal myocardial thickening typical of sarcoidosis; late gadolinium enhancement did not demonstrate the patchy, multifocal, mid-myocardial and epicardial pattern typical of cardiac sarcoidosis

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Cardiac hemochromatosis
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Differential diagnosis of restrictive cardiomyopathy
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Echocardiography showing ventricular wall thickening and mildly reduced left ventricular function
Non-supporting_evidence: Symptoms: No manifestations such as hepatomegaly, skin hyperpigmentation, arthralgia, or diabetes mellitus
Ancillary examination: Serum iron and transferrin saturation showed no abnormalities
Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance T2-weighted imaging showed no findings suggestive of iron overload

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Consideration of a disease that may lead to serious consequences (arrhythmia)
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Frequent multifocal ventricular premature complexes totaling 12,497
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed no fatty infiltration signal changes; right ventricular wall contractile function was preserved; no typical findings of triangle of dysplasia involvement, regional right ventricular wall motion abnormalities, or fatty infiltration were observed

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Autoimmune disease with cardiac involvement
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Consideration of common etiologies in young females
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Recurrent exertional dyspnea
Ancillary examination: Evidence of myocardial involvement
Non-supporting_evidence: Symptoms: No typical rash, photosensitivity, or other characteristic manifestations
Physical examination: No cutaneous erythema
Ancillary examination: Autoimmune-related laboratory markers showed no abnormalities

99mTc-PYP scintigraphy: Visual score 1.

Gastrocnemius muscle biopsy (day 12 after admission):  
Pathology results: H&E staining showed slight swelling of striated muscle, with indistinct borders and striations; widened perinuclear spaces; no inflammatory cell infiltration.  
Immunofluorescence: Staining for desmin encoded by DES showed uneven desmin distribution with abnormal accumulation.

Genetic testing (day 12 after admission):  
Whole-exome sequencing: The subject carried a heterozygous DES gene variant NM_001927.3 c.1360C>T, resulting in substitution of arginine with tryptophan at amino acid position 454 of the encoded protein (p.Arg454Trp).

### Medical History Summary
Patient Information  
Sex: Female  
Age: 13 years  
Chief complaint: Worsening chest tightness and shortness of breath with aggravated fatigue for more than 1 month.

History of Present Illness:  
One month prior, the patient developed chest tightness and shortness of breath without an obvious trigger, described as a sensation of suffocation/distension, accompanied by worsening fatigue and weakness of both lower extremities, markedly decreased exercise tolerance, significant tiredness, poor appetite, and dizziness, with progressive aggravation. No fever or diarrhea; no cough or sputum production; no palpitations, transient visual blackouts, or syncope. She was able to lie flat; no paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea.  
She visited the outpatient clinic of our hospital. Echocardiography showed decreased left and right ventricular systolic function (EF 46.8%), moderate pericardial effusion, and suspected persistent left superior vena cava; NT-proBNP 151 pg/mL. She was admitted to our department for further diagnosis and treatment.

Past Medical History: History of “primary carnitine deficiency” diagnosed 5 years ago; on regular oral levocarnitine oral solution. History of “autoimmune thyroiditis, hypothyroidism” for 5 years; on regular oral levothyroxine tablets. History of “primary hypoparathyroidism” for 5 years; long-term oral calcium zinc gluconate oral solution and calcitriol. History of bilateral anterior buttock osteophyte pain with bilateral lower-extremity weakness for 7 years; diagnosed as “autoimmune osteomyelitis”; previously treated with immunosuppressants, discontinued after 2 years of use. History of surgical treatment for “megacolon, intestinal obstruction” 7 years ago. History of tonsillectomy for “snoring during sleep” 7 years ago.  
Obstetric and Gynecologic History: No relevant records.

Menstrual History, Personal History, Family History:  
Personal history: Menarche at 13 years old; normal menstrual volume; no dysmenorrhea.  
Family history: Parents are consanguineous, first cousins. Great-grandmother had a history of thyroid tumor; great-grandfather had a history of osteomyelitis; younger sister has a history of asthma.

### Physical Examination
Vital signs: Temperature 36.4°C, pulse 92 beats/min, respiration 22 breaths/min, blood pressure 80/60 mmHg.  
General condition: Normal development, clear consciousness, fair nutritional status. Generalized rough skin; scattered freckles visible on both cheeks. No jaundice of skin or mucosa; no petechiae, purpura, or ecchymosis. No generalized lymphadenopathy palpable.  
Head and neck: No obvious thyroid enlargement. No abnormalities in carotid pulsation; no obvious jugular venous distention.  
Chest examination: Clear breath sounds in both lungs; no dry or moist rales.  
Cardiac examination: Heart sounds low; regular rhythm; heart rate 92 beats/min.  
Abdominal examination: Pulsus paradoxus positive. Abdomen flat and soft; laparoscopic surgical scars visible in the lower abdomen; no tenderness, rebound tenderness, or muscle guarding. Liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin.  
Extremities examination: No obvious edema of both lower extremities. Extremities cool. Muscle strength grade V; normal muscle tone. Tendon reflexes decreased; Achilles tendon reflex time markedly prolonged. Kernig sign and Babinski sign negative bilaterally.

### Ancillary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission  
Laboratory tests:  
Serum markers: NT-proBNP 151 pg/mL; C-reactive protein 8.35 mg/L; erythrocyte sedimentation rate 24 mm/h.  
Myocardial injury markers: hs-cTnI 10.66 ng/L; MYO 51 ng/mL; CK 108 U/L; CK-MB 20 U/L.  
Routine biochemistry and immunology: No abnormalities in complete blood count, urinalysis, stool routine, liver and renal function, electrolytes, blood lipids and glucose, routine coagulation tests, preoperative four-item panel, autoantibodies, immunoglobulins, or rheumatology four-item panel.

Imaging and electrophysiological examinations:  
Echocardiography (pre-admission): LA 27 mm, LV 41 mm, RA 23 mm, RV 18 mm; decreased left and right ventricular systolic function, EF 46.8%; moderate pericardial effusion; suspected persistent left superior vena cava.  
Chest CT: Diffuse mild thickening of bronchial walls in both lungs; linear opacities in both lungs; scattered micronodules in both lungs.  
Electrocardiogram: Sinus rhythm, ventricular rate 88 beats/min; flat T waves in leads V3–V6; T-wave inversion in leads III and aVF.

2. Further examinations after admission (multimodal imaging and pathology)  
Thyroid function and antibody tests:  
Thyroid function: s-TSH >150 mIU/L; total triiodothyronine 0.96 nmol/L; total thyroxine 28.6 nmol/L; FT3 2.22 pmol/L; FT4 5.19 pmol/L.  
Thyroid antibodies: TPOAb 104.7 IU/mL; TgAb >1300 IU/mL; thyroglobulin 0.18 ng/mL; anti-TSH receptor antibody 0.73 IU/L.  
Thyroid ultrasound: Coarsened and decreased, heterogeneous echogenicity of the thyroid gland; multiple small patchy hypoechoic areas with local reticular pattern; suggestive of diffuse thyroid disease.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR):  
Chamber size and cardiac function: LA 26 mm, LV 42 mm, RA 32 mm, RV 18 mm; IVS 6 mm, LVPW 7 mm; LVEF 57.6%; global left ventricular systolic function at the lower limit of normal.  
Tissue characteristics: Increased myocardial extracellular matrix content (ECV 31.9%).  
Pericardial effusion: Small-to-moderate pericardial effusion (2.3 mm outside the free wall of the right ventricle, 2.0 mm at the roof of the right atrium, 2.3 mm outside the lateral wall of the left ventricle).  
Other findings: Persistent left superior vena cava; no obvious abnormal enhancement of the left and right ventricular myocardium.

Growth, development, and endocrine-related examinations:  
Bone age assessment: Bone age 11 years (chronological age 14 years).  
Parathyroid function: Parathyroid hormone 3.44 pmol/L; serum calcium 2.08 mmol/L; serum phosphorus 1.44 mmol/L; serum alkaline phosphatase normal; parathyroid ultrasound showed no obvious abnormality.  
Sex hormone testing (luteal phase): Follicle-stimulating hormone 6 mIU/mL; luteinizing hormone 10.52 mIU/mL; estradiol 96 pg/mL; testosterone 0.36 ng/mL.  
Pituitary MRI: Pituitary gland plump; no obvious space-occupying lesion.

Neurological and neuromuscular examinations:  
Cranial MRI: Multiple punctate T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, FLAIR hypointense lesions in the bilateral frontoparietotemporal lobes, with signals consistent with cerebrospinal fluid; adhesive exudation considered.  
Electromyography: Left deltoid, right quadriceps femoris, bilateral tibialis anterior, and bilateral gastrocnemius muscles suggest myogenic injury.  
Lower-limb MRI: Partial bone marrow edema in the femur and tibia/fibula; linear hyperintense signal at the distal metaphysis of the tibia; small amount of ankle joint effusion; no fatty infiltration or T2 hyperintense signal in the surrounding muscle groups of the thighs and calves.  
Lower-limb plain radiographs: Metaphyseal sclerotic lines/sclerotic bands.

Metabolism-related examinations:  
Carnitine level testing: Free carnitine 19.33 μmol/L; no significant abnormalities in acylcarnitines.  
Abdominal ultrasound: No obvious abnormalities in the liver, spleen, kidneys, or bilateral renal organs; no obvious hepatic fatty infiltration.

raw_query:
### Medical History Summary
Patient Information  
Sex: Male  
Age: 34 years  
Chief complaint: Chest tightness and shortness of breath after activity for more than 1 week; dizziness with transient visual blackout for 17 hours.

History of Present Illness:  
Onset and first visit (1 week ago – Jan 11):  
One week ago, the patient developed chest tightness and shortness of breath after activity without an obvious trigger, specifically occurring when lifting his daughter, lasting for several minutes and resolving spontaneously. There was no chest pain or back pain, no radiating pain, no nausea or vomiting, and no dizziness or headache or other discomfort. Two days ago, intermittent chest pain during breathing occurred without an obvious trigger. Yesterday (01-11) at 21:30, the patient developed dizziness with transient visual blackout without an obvious trigger, accompanied by transient loss of consciousness, cyanosis of the lips, profuse cold sweating, marked fatigue, chest tightness and shortness of breath, nausea without vomiting, persisting for half an hour without relief. He then presented to Xiaoshan Hospital. ECG showed slight ST-segment elevation in the inferior leads; BNP 173, D-dimer 0.66, TNI unremarkable, CK-MB 29.6. Coronary artery disease was considered to be ruled out; aspirin and ticagrelor loading were given, and the dizziness did not improve.

First hospitalization (Jan 12 – Jan 16):  
For further diagnosis and treatment, he presented to the emergency department of our hospital. D-dimer was 1860 ug/L FEU, high-sensitivity C-reactive protein 9.55 mg/L. Pulmonary artery CTA showed no obvious pulmonary embolism; pericardial effusion was noted (slightly increased density, approximately 40 Hu), inflammatory and exudative changes in both lungs, and small bilateral pleural effusions. Echocardiography showed a large amount of pericardial effusion, with small amounts of mitral and tricuspid regurgitation. The patient had marked chest tightness and shortness of breath, tachycardia, no obvious chest pain, and occasional nausea without vomiting. After admission, pericardiocentesis and drainage were performed; the drainage fluid was bloody. After symptom relief, he was discharged on Jan 16.

Recurrence and progression (Jan 24 – Jan 31):

## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Response Format
- When generating the final answer: you must output the diagnostic results directly in Json format (example below), and do not output any other irrelevant content;

- {"Preliminary diagnosis": {"Primary diagnosis": {"Name": "XXX", "Etiologic subtype": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}, "Secondary diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}]}, "Differential diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Selection rationale": "Consider rare but potentially fatal diseases", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": [], "Excludable": true, "Recommended corresponding tests": []}, {"Name": "YYY", "Selection rationale": "Consider differential diagnosis of adjacent organ lesions", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1"], "Excludable": false, "Recommended corresponding tests": ["Recommendation 1", "Recommendation 2"]}]}

### History Summary
Patient Information  
Sex: Female  
Age: 50 years  
Chief complaint: Paroxysmal palpitations for 3 years, worsened for 1 month.

History of present illness:  
Initial onset and first visit (3 years ago):  
The patient developed palpitations 3 years ago without any obvious precipitating factors, with no chest pain, no chest tightness or shortness of breath, no dizziness or fatigue, no profuse sweating, no nausea or vomiting, no loss of consciousness, and no obvious association with physical activity. Self-administered oral medications (Suxiao Jiuxin Pills, Danshen Dripping Pills, etc.) with poor effect.

Recurrence and progression (1 month ago):  
One month ago, the patient again developed palpitations, accompanied by marked fatigue, with no chest pain, no chest tightness or shortness of breath, no dizziness, no profuse sweating, no nausea or vomiting, no loss of consciousness, and no relation to overwork or emotional agitation. The above symptoms recurred repeatedly.

This admission (February 10, 2025):  
For further diagnosis and treatment, the patient presented to our hospital.  
Disease course after admission: Potassium supplementation was given (0.9% sodium chloride 250 mL + potassium chloride injection 0.75 g). After potassium supplementation, serum potassium did not rise but instead decreased and showed a progressive downward trend; even treatment with spironolactone was ineffective.

Past medical history: Elevated blood pressure was noted 3 years ago, with a maximum blood pressure of 160/100 mmHg. The patient is currently taking telmisartan, nifedipine controlled-release tablets, and metoprolol sustained-release tablets regularly for blood pressure control, with suboptimal control: blood pressure fluctuates between 130–160/90–100 mmHg. History of diabetes mellitus for 4 years; currently taking dapagliflozin orally combined with subcutaneous polyethylene glycol loxenatide injection for glycemic control; glycemic control is acceptable.  
Obstetric and gynecologic history: No relevant records.

Menstrual history, personal history, family history: Denies smoking and alcohol use. Mother has a history of hypertension.

### Physical Examination
Vital signs: T 36.8°C, P 96 bpm, R 16 bpm, BP 162/96 mmHg.  
General condition: BMI 22.3. Alert, in fair spirits.  
Head and neck: No jugular venous distension.  
Chest examination: Breath sounds clear bilaterally; no dry or moist rales and no pleural friction rub.  
Cardiac examination: Cardiac dullness not enlarged on percussion. Heart rate 96 beats/min, regular rhythm; no pathological murmurs over any valve areas; no pericardial friction rub. No abnormal vascular signs.  
Abdomen and extremities: Abdominal examination (-). No pitting edema in both lower extremities.  
Neurological system: Physiological reflexes present; pathological reflexes not elicited. Meningeal irritation signs negative.

### Auxiliary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission  
Complete blood count: Hemoglobin 165 g/L, red blood cells 5.96×10¹²/L, platelets 396×10⁹/L.  
Biochemistry: Uric acid 416 μmol/L, potassium 3.48 mmol/L, fasting blood glucose 8.67 mmol/L.  
Urinalysis: Urine protein 3+, urine leukocytes increased.  
Thyroid function: No obvious abnormalities.  
Electrocardiogram: Sinus rhythm, HR 96 beats/min, QT/QTc 360/454 ms.  
Echocardiography: Aortic valve sclerosis, decreased left ventricular diastolic function.  
Coronary CT: Left anterior descending artery stenosis 30%–40%, left circumflex artery stenosis 20%–30%.

2. Further examinations after admission  
Serial electrolyte monitoring:  
2025-02-13: Serum potassium 2.80 mmol/L, serum sodium 143.3 mmol/L, serum chloride 107.5 mmol/L, serum calcium 2.15 mmol/L, serum phosphorus 1.34 mmol/L.  
2025-02-14: Serum potassium 2.83 mmol/L, serum sodium 143.8 mmol/L, serum chloride 109.3 mmol/L, serum calcium 2.03 mmol/L, serum phosphorus 1.06 mmol/L.  
2025-02-15: Serum potassium 2.77 mmol/L, serum chloride 105.1 mmol/L, serum calcium 2.03 mmol/L, serum phosphorus 0.89 mmol/L.

Five-item hormonal testing for hypertension:  
Supine (2025-02-12): Adrenocorticotropic hormone 34.60 pg/mL, cortisol 15.00 μg/dL, angiotensinogen 79.80 pg/mL, aldosterone 49.00 pg/mL, renin 3.35 pg/mL, aldosterone/renin ratio 14.63.  
Upright (2025-02-13): Adrenocorticotropic hormone 14.20 pg/mL, cortisol 5.14 μg/dL, angiotensinogen 85.00 pg/mL, aldosterone 56.80 pg/mL, renin 3.35 pg/mL.

Adrenal CT (2025-02-13): Thickening of the medial limb and the junction of the left adrenal gland; the right adrenal gland is normal in morphology and size, with homogeneous density.  
24-hour urinary potassium measurement: 86.56 mmol/L.  
Arterial blood gas analysis: Metabolic alkalosis.  
Genetic testing: Heterozygous missense mutation at codon 616 of the SCNN1B gene.

## Medical History Data
### Brief History
Patient Information  
Sex: Female  
Age: 66 years  
Chief complaint: Chest pain for 1 week.

History of present illness:  
Onset and first visit (1 week ago):  
The patient developed dull pain in the anterior chest region 1 week ago without obvious precipitating factors, radiating to the back, accompanied by chest tightness and shortness of breath, and fatigue. The symptoms persisted without relief. One day ago, she visited the Emergency Department of our hospital; laboratory testing indicated elevated troponin (14.38 ng/mL). She was then transferred to our department for further diagnosis and treatment. Since onset, the patient has occasionally had headache and dizziness, no fever or chills, occasional cough with sputum, no nausea or vomiting, no abdominal pain or diarrhea, no urinary frequency, urgency, or dysuria. Appetite was poor, sleep was acceptable, bowel and bladder function were normal, and no significant change in body weight was noted.

Review of past medical history (January 2025 - July 2025):  
Review of the patient’s prior laboratory results revealed that when she visited an outside hospital on January 20, 2025, the eosinophil percentage had already increased to 17.4%, with an absolute value of 1.45×10⁹/L. Subsequently, across multiple complete blood counts performed from then until July 14, 2025, eosinophils consistently remained at a relatively high level.

Disease course after admission (July 2025):  
On the third day after admission, coronary angiography was performed. The results showed 50% stenosis in the proximal right coronary artery and 50% stenosis in the mid left anterior descending artery; medical therapy was recommended.

Past medical history:  
History of hypertension for more than 6 years, with a maximum blood pressure of 200/180 mmHg; treated with oral nifedipine controlled-release tablets and telmisartan, among other medications; blood pressure controlled at approximately 140/90 mmHg.  
History of asthma for 3 years; during attacks, treated with Seretide, aminophylline, licorice tablets, and nebulized steroids; attacks occur approximately once every 1–3 months, and symptoms can be relieved after medication use each time.  
History of COPD for 3 years; no oral medications.  
History of allergic rhinitis for 15–16 years; occurs each year during the transitions of spring and autumn/winter; no medication treatment.  
In 2022, she was initially diagnosed with COPD and asthma at a primary healthcare facility; in May 2025, diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia; in early July 2025, initially diagnosed with segment-elevation myocardial infarction.

Obstetric/gynecologic history: Postmenopausal; has 1 son, in good health.

Menstrual history, personal history, family history:  
Personal history: No travel to pastoral areas or epidemic areas. Occupational history of dust exposure.  
Family history: Father died of acute myocardial infarction.

### Physical Examination
Vital signs: Temperature 36.5°C, pulse 78 beats/min, respiratory rate 19 breaths/min, blood pressure 101/68 mmHg.  
General: Clear consciousness, poor general condition; enlarged lymph nodes palpable in the neck; no jaundice of the skin or sclera. Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally.  
Chest: Moist rales audible at both lung bases.  
Cardiac: Regular rhythm; no cardiac murmurs heard in any valve auscultation area.  
Abdomen and extremities: Abdomen soft, no tenderness; no edema in both lower extremities. Muscle strength and tone of the extremities were normal, with no limitation of movement.

### Ancillary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission  
Laboratory tests:  
Cardiac biomarkers: Cardiac troponin I (cTnI) elevated to 14.38 ng/mL; BNP elevated to 709 pg/mL.  
Biochemistry: Creatinine elevated to 0.67 μg/mL; hyponatremia and hypochloremia.  
Inflammatory markers: PCT elevated to 0.124 pg/mL.  
Arterial blood gas analysis: No obvious abnormalities.

Imaging studies:  
Electrocardiogram (ECG): No obvious abnormalities; no ST-segment elevation or depression; low frequency in limb leads; ST-segment depression in leads V2–V3.  
Echocardiography: Segmental wall motion abnormalities in the inferior wall of the left ventricle and adjacent posterior wall; mildly reduced left ventricular diastolic function (EF 55%); reduced ejection fraction.  
Initial chest imaging suggested: Bilateral interstitial lung disease, micronodules, and old lesions.

2. Further examinations after admission (multimodal imaging and pathology)  
Coronary angiography (2025-07-10):  
50% stenosis in the proximal right coronary artery; 50% stenosis in the mid left anterior descending artery; medical therapy recommended.

Serial cardiac biomarker testing:  
cTnI: 14.38 ng/mL (07-08) -> 14.727 ng/mL (07-14) -> 10.217 ng/mL (07-15).  
BNP: 709 pg/mL (07-08) -> 3104 pg/mL (07-10) -> 1733 pg/mL (07-15).

Progression of cardiac imaging:  
Repeat echocardiography: Marked left ventricular enlargement (end-diastolic diameter 51 mm); left atrial enlargement (anteroposterior diameter 44 mm); ejection fraction further decreased to 49%; pulmonary artery systolic pressure 59 mmHg; decreased left ventricular lateral wall motion.  
Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging: Ejection fraction 39.95%; biventricular myocardial edema; diffuse subendocardial delayed enhancement throughout the heart; mural thrombus formation.

Detailed pulmonary assessment:  
Pulmonary function test: FEV1 36.6% (normal 80%); FVC 47.3% (normal 80%); bronchodilator test negative; mixed ventilatory defect (predominantly obstructive).  
High-resolution chest CT: Bilateral interstitial lung disease; bilateral micronodules; bilateral lungs and pleura with old lesions; bilateral bronchial inflammatory changes; multiple enlarged mediastinal and cervical lymph nodes (grade 2); small amount of pericardial effusion.

Lymph node evaluation:  
Ultrasonography: Bilateral cervical lymph nodes (grade 2).  
Abdominal ultrasonography: No obviously enlarged lymph nodes in the posterior abdominal wall.

In-depth hematologic evaluation:  
Repeat complete blood count: Elevated white blood cell count; eosinophils: 5.31×10⁹/L (46.2%); eosinophils on multiple repeat tests (from January 20, 2025 to July 14) persistently remained at a high level.  
Bone marrow aspiration: Completely negative.

Comprehensive immunologic evaluation:  
Rheumatology/immunology markers: ANCA-related antibodies: negative; rheumatoid antibody panel: negative; immunoglobulins and complement: negative.  
Allergy-related indices: IgE: 1812 U/mL (markedly elevated); ESR: 80 mm/h (elevated).  
Tumor markers: negative.  
Parasite testing: negative.

Neurologic assessment:  
Electromyography: Slowed motor nerve conduction velocity of the right peroneal nerve; other nerve conduction velocities were normal.

Otorhinolaryngology examination:  
Paranasal sinus CT: High likelihood of bilateral sinusitis.

Urinalysis:  
Routine urinalysis: Protein (+), urine occult blood (+).

## Medical History
### Brief History
Patient Information  
Sex: Male  
Age: 61 years  
Chief complaint: Intermittent chest pain and shortness of breath after exertion for more than 3 years, worsened for 1 week.

History of present illness:  
2020:  
The patient developed chest pain and shortness of breath after exertion, and was able to lie flat at night. No discomfort such as nausea, vomiting, dizziness, or headache. He visited a hospital and was considered to have “old myocardial infarction, heart failure”, and was treated with “sacubitril/valsartan sodium tablets, enteric-coated aspirin tablets, atorvastatin calcium tablets, perindopril”. Thereafter, there was no further exertional shortness of breath and no paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea; he did not seek further diagnosis or treatment during this period.

April 2023:  
Intermittent chest pain and shortness of breath after exertion recurred, occurring after walking about 500 meters, occasionally accompanied by generalized fatigue. Subsequently, nocturnal orthopnea gradually developed; it improved upon sitting up, lasting from several minutes to several hours. Symptoms improved with rest or self-administration of sublingual “nitroglycerin”. In mid-April, he visited a hospital where ECG and myocardial enzyme tests were performed, and he was diagnosed with “acute non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction, grade 1 hypertension (very high risk), type 2 diabetes mellitus, complete left bundle branch block, second-degree atrioventricular block”. He improved after treatment including antiplatelet therapy, lipid-lowering and plaque stabilization, coronary dilation, blood pressure and glucose control, improvement of myocardial remodeling, and improvement of cardiac function. After discharge, he regularly took “aluminum-magnesium aspirin, atorvastatin, isosorbide mononitrate sustained-release tablets, sacubitril/valsartan (50 mg twice daily), spironolactone, furosemide”.

November 2023 (first admission for this visit):  
Shortness of breath recurred; symptoms occurred after walking about 100 meters, accompanied by paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, relieved by sitting up. Exercise tolerance progressively declined. He presented to our hospital.

May 2024 (readmission and disease progression):  
The patient was readmitted due to worsening shortness of breath, manifested as dyspnea after walking 100 meters, accompanied by fatigue and poor appetite; he self-measured a markedly slowed heart rate. After admission, monitoring revealed persistent second-degree 2:1 atrioventricular block, with a heart rate in the 40s bpm. After ICD implantation, on June 18 he developed slurred speech and cognitive abnormalities; during this period, he successively developed limb convulsions (considered epileptic seizures), gastrointestinal bleeding, renal insufficiency, and other multisystem complications.

Past medical history:  
In 2009, laboratory tests and examinations at a local hospital suggested old myocardial infarction.  
History of diabetes mellitus for more than 20 years, with early onset (around age 30). Currently uses insulin and voglibose to control blood glucose; he does not routinely monitor in daily life.  
History of hypertension for more than 10 years; highest blood pressure reached 150/70 mmHg. Currently taking sacubitril/valsartan for control.

The family history shows an obvious maternal inheritance pattern: father deceased, had diabetes mellitus; mother deceased, had pulmonary tuberculosis; one younger sister has diabetes mellitus.

### Physical Examination
Vital signs: Temperature 36.3°C, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, pulse 60 beats/min, blood pressure 90/58 mmHg.

### Ancillary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission (December 2023)

Myocardial injury markers: myoglobin 112.8 ng/mL (elevated), CK-MB 14.52 ng/mL (elevated), high-sensitivity troponin I 309.65 pg/mL (elevated), BNP 904.8 pg/mL (markedly elevated). Repeat testing showed persistent elevation of myocardial injury markers.  
Renal function and electrolytes: urea 9.6 mmol/L (mildly elevated), creatinine 115 μmol/L (mildly elevated).  
Lipids: LDL-C 1.67 mmol/L.  
Immunology: immunoglobulin testing showed a kappa/lambda light chain ratio of 1.86 (>1.65).  
Bone marrow aspiration: hypoplastic marrow; no abnormal plasma cell proliferation identified.

Electrocardiographic examinations:  
Standard ECG: sinus rhythm, complete left bundle branch block, second-degree Mobitz type II atrioventricular block.  
Holter monitoring: sinus bradycardia (average heart rate 52 beats/min), frequent multiform premature ventricular contractions (1,512 beats), short runs of multiform ventricular tachycardia, second-degree atrioventricular block (2:1 conduction observed), complete left bundle branch block.

Imaging studies:

2. Further examinations after admission (May–July 2024)

Myocardial injury markers: myoglobin 103 ng/mL, CK-MB 13.9 ng/mL, high-sensitivity troponin I 106.0 pg/mL, NT-proBNP 1676.0 pg/mL (peak 31314.0 pg/mL), persistently elevated.

## Medical History
### Brief History
Patient Information  
Sex: Male  
Age: 61 years  
Chief complaint: Intermittent chest pain and shortness of breath after exertion for more than 3 years, worsened for 1 week.

History of present illness:

The patient developed chest pain and shortness of breath after exertion, and was able to lie flat at night. No discomfort such as nausea, vomiting, dizziness, or headache. He visited a hospital and was considered to have “old myocardial infarction, heart failure”, and was treated with “sacubitril/valsartan sodium tablets, enteric-coated aspirin tablets, atorvastatin calcium tablets, perindopril”. Thereafter, there was no further exertional shortness of breath and no paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea; he did not seek further diagnosis or treatment during this period.

April 2023:  
Intermittent chest pain and shortness of breath after exertion recurred, occurring after walking about 500 meters, occasionally accompanied by generalized fatigue. ### Ancillary Examinations

Myocardial injury markers: myoglobin 112.8 ng/mL (elevated), CK-MB 14.52 ng/mL (elevated), high-sensitivity troponin I 309.65 pg/mL (elevated), BNP 904.8 pg/mL (markedly elevated). Repeat testing showed persistent elevation of myocardial injury markers.

## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Response Format
- When generating the final answer: you must output the diagnostic results directly in Json format (example below), and do not output any other irrelevant content;

- {"Preliminary diagnosis": {"Primary diagnosis": {"Name": "XXX", "Etiologic subtype": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}, "Secondary diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}]}, "Differential diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Selection rationale": "Consider rare but potentially fatal diseases", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": [], "Excludable": true, "Recommended corresponding tests": []}, {"Name": "YYY", "Selection rationale": "Consider differential diagnosis of adjacent organ lesions", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1"], "Excludable": false, "Recommended corresponding tests": ["Recommendation 1", "Recommendation 2"]}]}

## Medical History
Patient Information  
Sex: Female  
Age: 25 years  
Chief complaint: Chest tightness, shortness of breath, and fatigue for 3 years, worsening for half a month

History of Present Illness:  
Initial onset and previous visits (3 years ago – 1 year ago):  
At 28+ weeks of gestation 3 years ago, the patient developed exertional chest tightness. At 36+ weeks, cesarean section was performed, delivering one fetus, and the fetus did not survive. Postpartum, the patient’s chest tightness and shortness of breath gradually resolved. One year ago, at 28+ weeks of gestation, the patient developed exertional chest tightness and shortness of breath again; at 32+ weeks, the chest tightness worsened compared with before, and she presented to our hospital. She was diagnosed with: acute heart failure, breech presentation; she was admitted to the Emergency ICU of our hospital, and an emergency cesarean section was performed. Postoperatively, her symptoms were relieved.

Recent recurrence and treatment at an outside hospital (half a month ago – 2 days ago):  
Half a month ago, after a cold, the patient again developed chest tightness, shortness of breath, and fatigue, accompanied by nausea, vomiting, headache, and dizziness; she had severe night sweats. Local treatment was ineffective, so she came to the Emergency Department of our hospital and was kept for observation. After admission, treatments including correction of heart failure and myocardial nutrition were given, and her chest tightness and shortness of breath were relieved compared with before. She returned to the local hospital for continued treatment. Two days ago, ventricular tachycardia was noted on ECG monitoring, without disturbance of consciousness, accompanied by fever with a temperature of 38.4°C; sinus rhythm was restored after intravenous infusion of amiodarone.

This admission and disease progression (to date):  
Given the complexity of the patient’s condition, she presented urgently to our hospital and was admitted for further diagnostic clarification. During routine blood pressure monitoring, paroxysmal elevations in blood pressure were observed, with maximal systolic blood pressure up to 186 mmHg and maximal diastolic blood pressure up to 135 mmHg, showing a pattern of intermittent (non-sustained) hypertension.

Past Medical History: Previously healthy; denies history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, or heart disease.  
Marital and Obstetric History: Married at appropriate age; gravida 2, para 2; fetuses did not survive.

Menstrual History, Personal History, Family History: No history of smoking or alcohol use; family history unremarkable.

### Physical Examination
General: T 37.2°C P 80/min R 21/min BP 117/70 mmHg.  
Specialty examination: Young female; heart and lungs (−); no edema of both lower extremities.

### Auxiliary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission  
Laboratory tests:  
Complete blood count: WBC 13.97×10⁹/L, RBC 6.81×10¹²/L, hemoglobin 161 g/L, platelets 358×10⁹/L.  
Liver function: ALT 60 U/L, AST 103 U/L, ALP 304 U/L.  
Blood lipids: total cholesterol 8.65 mmol/L, LDL-C 6.01 mmol/L, triglycerides 3.98 mmol/L.  
Coagulation: D-dimer 3.62 mg/L.  
Urinalysis: urine protein 1+, occult blood 1+.  
Myocardial markers and heart failure index: high-sensitivity cardiac troponin T 123.5 pg/mL, CK-MB 32.36 ng/mL, myoglobin 830.9 ng/mL, NT-proBNP 13869 pg/mL.  
Others: renal function, electrolytes, amylase, stool routine, viral panel, etc. showed no significant abnormalities.

Imaging studies:  
ECG: sinus rhythm, T-wave changes.  
Echocardiography: thickened interventricular septal myocardium; maximal septal thickness approximately 1.36 cm; the thickened myocardium showed mild ground-glass–like echogenic changes; segmental wall motion abnormalities; left ventricular ejection fraction normal (approximately 60%).  
Pulmonary artery CTA: no obvious abnormalities; tiny nodular lesion in the right lower lobe, considered a fibrotic nodule; no evidence of pulmonary embolism.  
Abdominal ultrasound: gallbladder sediment; mild bilateral hydronephrosis.

2. Further examinations after admission (multimodality imaging and pathology)  
Laboratory tests (readmission):  
Complete blood count: platelets 450×10⁹/L.  
Liver function: AST 71 U/L, ALT 36 U/L, γ-GGT 77 U/L.  
Blood lipids: total cholesterol 9.3 mmol/L, LDL-C 6.59 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.51 mmol/L.  
Urinalysis: urine protein 2+, microalbumin 579.7 mg/L.  
Thyroid function: free triiodothyronine 2.82 pmol/L.  
Respiratory pathogen IgM: Legionella pneumophila weakly positive.  
Myocardial markers: high-sensitivity cardiac troponin T 79.05 pg/mL, CK-MB 29.21 ng/mL, myoglobin 56.7 ng/mL, NT-proBNP 21156 pg/mL.  
Others: EBV and CMV DNA quantification, anticardiolipin antibody, cortisol, renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system, viral panel, etc. showed no special abnormalities.

Special examinations and imaging:  
ECG and monitoring: sinus rhythm, paroxysmal junctional tachycardia, T-wave changes, extensive anterior-wall T-wave inversion; ECG monitoring detected paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia with right bundle branch block morphology and left axis deviation.  
Bedside echocardiography: thickening of the upper interventricular septal myocardium (approximately 1.25 cm); decreased myocardial motion amplitude in the mid-to-lower interventricular septum and the apical segment of the left ventricular inferior wall; a muscular band was detected within the left ventricular cavity connecting the upper interventricular septum and the left ventricular apex; segmental wall motion impairment; LVEF 60%.  
Chest X-ray: findings suggestive of bronchitis.  
Abdominal ultrasound: solid nodule anterior to the abdominal aorta at the level of the crossing, approximately 3.9×2.3×3.5 cm.  
Abdominal MRI: an abnormal-signal mass lesion seen inferior to the abdominal aortic bifurcation.  
Endocrine tests: plasma catecholamines showed markedly elevated dopamine and norepinephrine, and markedly elevated plasma normetanephrine; 24-hour urinary vanillylmandelic acid markedly elevated.

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Paraganglioma; Catecholamine cardiomyopathy; Paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Chest tightness, dyspnea, and fatigue for 3 years, with exacerbation for half a month

Ancillary examination: Plasma catecholamine testing: Dopamine and norepinephrine significantly elevated; plasma metanephrine (normetanephrine) significantly elevated

Ancillary examination: Cardiac monitoring revealed paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia with right bundle branch block morphology and left axis deviation

Ancillary examination: Chest X-ray: Findings consistent with bronchitis

## Medical History
Patient Information  
Sex: Female  
Age: 25 years  
Chief complaint: Chest tightness, shortness of breath, and fatigue for 3 years, worsening for half a month

History of Present Illness:  
Initial onset and previous visits (3 years ago – 1 year ago):  
At 28+ weeks of gestation 3 years ago, the patient developed exertional chest tightness. At 36+ weeks, cesarean section was performed, delivering one fetus, and the fetus did not survive. Postpartum, the patient’s chest tightness and shortness of breath gradually resolved. One year ago, at 28+ weeks of gestation, the patient developed exertional chest tightness and shortness of breath again; at 32+ weeks, the chest tightness worsened compared with before, and she presented to our hospital. She was diagnosed with: acute heart failure, breech presentation; she was admitted to the Emergency ICU of our hospital, and an emergency cesarean section was performed. Postoperatively, her symptoms were relieved.

Recent recurrence and treatment at an outside hospital (half a month ago – 2 days ago):  
Half a month ago, after a cold, the patient again developed chest tightness, shortness of breath, and fatigue, accompanied by nausea, vomiting, headache, and dizziness; she had severe night sweats. Local treatment was ineffective, so she came to the Emergency Department of our hospital and was kept for observation. After admission, treatments including correction of heart failure and myocardial nutrition were given, and her chest tightness and shortness of breath were relieved compared with before. She returned to the local hospital for continued treatment. Two days ago, ventricular tachycardia was noted on ECG monitoring, without disturbance of consciousness, accompanied by fever with a temperature of 38.4°C; sinus rhythm was restored after intravenous infusion of amiodarone.

This admission and disease progression (to date):  
Given the complexity of the patient’s condition, she presented urgently to our hospital and was admitted for further diagnostic clarification. During routine blood pressure monitoring, paroxysmal elevations in blood pressure were observed, with maximal systolic blood pressure up to 186 mmHg and maximal diastolic blood pressure up to 135 mmHg, showing a pattern of intermittent (non-sustained) hypertension.

Past Medical History: Previously healthy; denies history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, or heart disease.  
Marital and Obstetric History: Married at appropriate age; gravida 2, para 2; fetuses did not survive.

Menstrual History, Personal History, Family History: No history of smoking or alcohol use; family history unremarkable.

### Physical Examination
General: T 37.2°C P 80/min R 21/min BP 117/70 mmHg.  
Specialty examination: Young female; heart and lungs (−); no edema of both lower extremities.

### Auxiliary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission  
Laboratory tests:  
Complete blood count: WBC 13.97×10⁹/L, RBC 6.81×10¹²/L, hemoglobin 161 g/L, platelets 358×10⁹/L.  
Liver function: ALT 60 U/L, AST 103 U/L, ALP 304 U/L.  
Blood lipids: total cholesterol 8.65 mmol/L, LDL-C 6.01 mmol/L, triglycerides 3.98 mmol/L.  
Coagulation: D-dimer 3.62 mg/L.  
Urinalysis: urine protein 1+, occult blood 1+.  
Myocardial markers and heart failure index: high-sensitivity cardiac troponin T 123.5 pg/mL, CK-MB 32.36 ng/mL, myoglobin 830.9 ng/mL, NT-proBNP 13869 pg/mL.  
Others: renal function, electrolytes, amylase, stool routine, viral panel, etc. showed no significant abnormalities.

Imaging studies:  
ECG: sinus rhythm, T-wave changes.  
Echocardiography: thickened interventricular septal myocardium; maximal septal thickness approximately 1.36 cm; the thickened myocardium showed mild ground-glass–like echogenic changes; segmental wall motion abnormalities; left ventricular ejection fraction normal (approximately 60%).  
Pulmonary artery CTA: no obvious abnormalities; tiny nodular lesion in the right lower lobe, considered a fibrotic nodule; no evidence of pulmonary embolism.  
Abdominal ultrasound: gallbladder sediment; mild bilateral hydronephrosis.

2. Further examinations after admission (multimodality imaging and pathology)  
Laboratory tests (readmission):  
Complete blood count: platelets 450×10⁹/L.  
Liver function: AST 71 U/L, ALT 36 U/L, γ-GGT 77 U/L.  
Blood lipids: total cholesterol 9.3 mmol/L, LDL-C 6.59 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.51 mmol/L.  
Urinalysis: urine protein 2+, microalbumin 579.7 mg/L.  
Thyroid function: free triiodothyronine 2.82 pmol/L.  
Respiratory pathogen IgM: Legionella pneumophila weakly positive.  
Myocardial markers: high-sensitivity cardiac troponin T 79.05 pg/mL, CK-MB 29.21 ng/mL, myoglobin 56.7 ng/mL, NT-proBNP 21156 pg/mL.  
Others: EBV and CMV DNA quantification, anticardiolipin antibody, cortisol, renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system, viral panel, etc. showed no special abnormalities.

Special examinations and imaging:  
ECG and monitoring: sinus rhythm, paroxysmal junctional tachycardia, T-wave changes, extensive anterior-wall T-wave inversion; ECG monitoring detected paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia with right bundle branch block morphology and left axis deviation.  
Bedside echocardiography: thickening of the upper interventricular septal myocardium (approximately 1.25 cm); decreased myocardial motion amplitude in the mid-to-lower interventricular septum and the apical segment of the left ventricular inferior wall; a muscular band was detected within the left ventricular cavity connecting the upper interventricular septum and the left ventricular apex; segmental wall motion impairment; LVEF 60%.  
Chest X-ray: findings suggestive of bronchitis.  
Abdominal ultrasound: solid nodule anterior to the abdominal aorta at the level of the crossing, approximately 3.9×2.3×3.5 cm.  
Abdominal MRI: an abnormal-signal mass lesion seen inferior to the abdominal aortic bifurcation.  
Endocrine tests: plasma catecholamines showed markedly elevated dopamine and norepinephrine, and markedly elevated plasma normetanephrine; 24-hour urinary vanillylmandelic acid markedly elevated.
## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Response Format
- When generating the final answer: you must output the diagnostic results directly in Json format (example below), and do not output any other irrelevant content;

- {"Preliminary diagnosis": {"Primary diagnosis": {"Name": "XXX", "Etiologic subtype": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}, "Secondary diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}]}, "Differential diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Selection rationale": "Consider rare but potentially fatal diseases", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": [], "Excludable": true, "Recommended corresponding tests": []}, {"Name": "YYY", "Selection rationale": "Consider differential diagnosis of adjacent organ lesions", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1"], "Excludable": false, "Recommended corresponding tests": ["Recommendation 1", "Recommendation 2"]}]}

### History Summary
Patient Information  
Sex: Male  
Age: 33 years  
Chief complaint: Elevated blood pressure for 5 years; paroxysmal dyspnea for 2 years, worsened for 4 days.

History of present illness:  
Onset and prior diagnosis and treatment (5 years ago – 2 years ago):  
The patient noted elevated blood pressure 5 years ago, with a maximum of 200/100 mmHg. He is currently taking sacubitril/valsartan 100 mg orally twice daily on a regular basis; blood pressure control is suboptimal, maintained at 130–140/90–100 mmHg. Two years ago, dyspnea developed without an obvious trigger, worsened in the supine position and relieved after sitting up, without chest pain. He presented to our hospital and was diagnosed with “dilated cardiomyopathy with heart failure”; symptoms improved after pharmacologic treatment. After discharge, he continued to take anti–heart failure medications regularly; during this period, dyspnea still recurred repeatedly. He was hospitalized multiple times for conditions including “heart failure, cerebral infarction, pulmonary embolism,” etc.

This admission (4 days ago to present):  
Four days ago, after catching a cold, he developed worsening dyspnea, poor blood pressure control, inability to lie flat, bilateral lower-extremity pitting edema, decreased urine output, and occasional cough with white frothy sputum. He presented to our hospital and was admitted for inpatient treatment.  
Disease course after admission: Upon repeated history-taking by physicians, the patient ultimately disclosed a long-term history of injectable anabolic-androgenic steroid (AAS) use (500–700 mg/week, started at age 15 and continuing to the present).

Past medical history: History of renal insufficiency for 2 years; taking Uremiqing Granules regularly, 1 sachet tid. Two years ago, he had a history of intraventricular thrombus, lower-extremity venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, cerebral infarction, and popliteal artery embolism.  
Marital and reproductive history: No relevant records.

Menstrual history, personal history, family history:  
Personal history: The patient is Russian and denies a history of smoking and alcohol consumption. The patient is a foreign national and a bodybuilder. He has engaged in bodybuilding since age 13, with >2–3 hours of high-intensity resistance training and aerobic exercise daily. At approximately age 15, he began injecting steroid hormones: testosterone 500–700 mg once weekly, continuing to the present.  
Family history: No relevant records.

### Physical Examination
Weight: 125 kg, Height: 180 cm, BMI: 38.58 kg/m², Body fat percentage: 20%  
Temperature: 37.0°C, Pulse: 100 beats/min, Respiratory rate: 22 breaths/min, Blood pressure: 157/100 mmHg  
No cyanosis of the lips; no jugular venous distension; no thyroid enlargement.  
Moist rales auscultated in both lungs; no pleural friction rub.  
Cardiac dullness enlarged; occasional premature beats audible; heart rate 100 beats/min; grade 3/6 blowing systolic murmur at the mitral and tricuspid areas; no pericardial friction rub.  
No abnormalities on abdominal examination.  
Mild edema of both lower extremities.  
Physiological reflexes present; pathological reflexes not elicited.

### Auxiliary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission  
Hematology:  
Hemoglobin: 181 g/L  
Platelets: 498×10⁹/L  
Total cholesterol: 7.21 mmol/L  
Low-density lipoprotein cholesterol: 4.61 mmol/L  
Triglycerides: 2.48 mmol/L  

Renal function indices:
