### History Summary
#### Patient
Female, 42 years old. Admitted to the Department of Cardiovascular Medicine, The First Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University (our hospital) on July 17, 2015, for “recurrent chest tightness and shortness of breath for more than 8 months.”

#### History of Present Illness
In November 2014, the patient developed recurrent chest tightness and shortness of breath without an obvious precipitating factor. Symptoms were more pronounced at night in the supine position and were not clearly related to activity. There was no palpitation, dizziness, fatigue, or other discomfort. No regular treatment was given during this period. In July 2015, the above symptoms recurred, accompanied by right-sided chest and back pain, characterized as persistent dull pain, without obvious correlation with activity or respiration, with no radiation pain to other sites, and without fever, fatigue, or other discomfort. She then went to a local hospital where a lung CT showed a small amount of right pleural effusion and a large amount of pericardial effusion. Since onset, her mental status has been acceptable, sleep has been poor, appetite decreased, bowel and bladder function normal, and there has been no significant recent change in body weight.

#### Past Medical History
General health status is average. Underwent “cesarean section” in 2005; sustained a right lower extremity fracture after trauma in 2014, without surgical treatment; history of “hepatitis B, HBeAg-positive (triple positive)” for half a year, without antiviral therapy; history of penicillin allergy. Denies history of tuberculosis, denies history of coronary heart disease, denies history of hypertension, denies history of diabetes mellitus, denies history of hepatitis, denies history of other infectious diseases, denies history of blood transfusion; has a history of vaccination, details unknown.

### Physical Examination
Temperature 36.5°C, heart rate 78 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, blood pressure 105/66 mmHg. Conscious and alert. No jaundice or petechiae of the skin and mucosa. No palpable superficial lymphadenopathy. No jugular venous distension. Breath sounds slightly coarse in both lungs; no obvious dry or moist rales. Cardiac dullness markedly enlarged; first heart sound low and muffled; heart sounds distant; heart rate 78 beats/min, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Abdomen soft, no tenderness or rebound tenderness; liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin. No edema of both lower extremities. Dorsalis pedis pulses normal. Muscle strength normal in all extremities; muscle tone normal. Hip joints of all extremities normal. Babinski sign negative bilaterally. No neck stiffness; Kernig sign negative.

### Laboratory Tests
1. T-SPOT test positive; multiple sputum smears for acid-fast staining showed no acid-fast bacilli; blood Mycobacterium tuberculosis antibody negative.
2. Routine analysis of pericardial effusion: yellow, clear and transparent, no clots, Rivalta test negative, nucleated cell count 130 cells/µL, segmented neutrophils 0.3%, lymphocytes 0.7%.
3. Biochemistry of pericardial effusion: total protein 60.6 g/L, albumin 31.2 g/L, globulin 29.4 g/L, glucose 7.7 mmol/L, lactate dehydrogenase 100 U/L, [K\^+] 3.10 mmol/L, [Na\^+] 138 mmol/L, [Cl\^-] 108 mmol/L, [Ca\^{2+}] 1.98 mmol/L, adenosine dehydrogenase 12 U/L.
4. Exfoliative cytology of pericardial effusion: no tumor cells seen. Hepatitis B five-item panel: HBeAg-positive (triple positive). Quantitative hepatitis B surface antigen: 56.180 IU/mL. Immunoglobulin G 23.20 g/L. Immunoglobulin A, immunoglobulin M, complement C3 and C4 normal. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate normal. No significant abnormalities in complete blood count, blood biochemistry, thyroid function, ANA profile items 3–14 (immunoblot), rheumatic four items (erythrocyte sedimentation rate, anti-streptolysin O, rheumatoid factor, C-reactive protein), tumor markers, HIV, syphilis, and hepatitis C.

### Ancillary Examinations
1. Echocardiography  
   Echocardiography (July 18, 2015) showed: ① pericardial effusion (large); ② left atrial enlargement, LVEF 75%.

2. Bilateral pleural ultrasound  
   Bilateral pleural ultrasound (July 23, 2015) showed: ① pericardial effusion; ② bilateral pleural effusions.

3. Chest CT  
   Chest CT (July 25, 2017) showed: ① increased-density opacities in the left lower lobe, lingular segment, and right middle lobe, considered chronic infection; ② large pericardial effusion; ③ small left pleural effusion (Figure 1).

A. Echocardiography (July 18, 2015) shows a large pericardial effusion (an anechoic area behind the left ventricular posterior wall during systole, depth approximately 5.2 cm)  
B. Chest CT (July 25, 2015) shows increased-density opacities in the left lower lobe, lingular segment, and right lung, large pericardial effusion, and a small amount of left pleural effusion

Figure 1. Results of echocardiography and chest CT on admission (July 2015)

raw_query:
### Brief History
#### Patient
Male, 55 years old. Admitted on November 26, 2018.

#### Chief Complaint
Cardiomegaly identified for more than 5 years; intermittent transient loss of consciousness for more than 2 months.

#### History of Present Illness
Five years ago, an abnormal electrocardiogram (ECG) was found on physical examination, manifested as occasional premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) and right bundle branch block (RBBB); echocardiography showed left atrial enlargement (anteroposterior diameter 4.5 cm), left ventricular enlargement (left ventricular end-diastolic diameter [LVEDD] 7.0 cm), reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF 43%), and suspected segmental wall motion abnormality. At that time, the patient had no obvious discomfort, could climb 17 floors, and did not seek further evaluation or treatment.

More than 1 year ago, the patient intermittently developed palpitations, nausea, sweating, and other discomfort in enclosed spaces or when taking a bus, without dizziness, vomiting, chest tightness, or dyspnea. Since March 2017, he had intermittent cough with yellow sputum, without fever, chills, abdominal pain, diarrhea, urinary frequency, or urgency; subsequently, he developed nocturnal paroxysmal dyspnea with palpitations, chest tightness, dyspnea, and expectoration of yellow foamy sputum; symptoms could be relieved spontaneously after sitting up for 5 minutes, recurred after lying flat, and occurred 3–4 times per night. His exercise tolerance decreased, with shortness of breath when climbing 3–4 floors. He presented to the Emergency Department of our hospital; brain natriuretic peptide (BNP) was 892 ng/L, and serum cardiac troponin I (cTnI) was (−). Heart failure was considered highly likely. After treatment with torasemide for diuresis, isosorbide mononitrate for coronary dilation, aspirin tablets for antiplatelet therapy, and cefaclor sustained-release tablets for anti-infection therapy, the above symptoms did not completely resolve, and he was admitted to our department.

(1) Auxiliary examinations after admission in March 2017

Myocardial injury markers: BNP 320 ng/L. No elevation of myocardial injury markers was observed.

Electrocardiogram: sinus rhythm, frequent premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) (Figure 1).

Figure 1. ECG on admission in March 2017: sinus rhythm with frequent premature ventricular contractions.

Chest X-ray: enlarged cardiac silhouette; old lesions in both lungs.

Echocardiography: left atrial enlargement (anteroposterior diameter 4.4 cm, transverse diameter 5.3 cm), left ventricular enlargement (LVEDD 7.4 cm), diffuse hypokinesis, reduced LVEF (32%), left ventricular diastolic dysfunction (E’ 4 cm/s, E/E’ 32.6, E/A 3.2), and moderate-to-severe mitral regurgitation.

Coronary angiography: 30% stenosis in the proximal left anterior descending artery (LAD), 10% stenosis in the mid right coronary artery (RCA), and no stenosis in the remaining vessels.

Ambulatory electrocardiogram (Holter): sinus rhythm (53–92 bpm, mean 67 bpm), 11,146 PVCs/24 h, including 63 couplets/24 h; 16 episodes of nonsustained ventricular tachycardia (NSVT)/24 h (maximum 3 beats), with multiple morphologies.

Treatment course: Based on the above examinations, combined with the patient’s birthplace being an endemic area for Keshan disease and a past history of Kashin–Beck disease, Keshan disease could not be excluded. Selenium supplementation (selenium yeast tablets) was given; spironolactone, metoprolol sustained-release tablets, and benazepril were administered to improve ventricular remodeling; torasemide for diuresis; aspirin for antiplatelet therapy; and amiodarone (200 mg once daily) for antiarrhythmic therapy. Symptoms of palpitations, cough, and sputum production were relieved. For half a year after discharge, there was no recurrence of nocturnal awakening with dyspnea or exertional dyspnea, and exercise tolerance gradually returned to the pre-onset level.

(2) Fourteen months ago (September 2017), the patient was readmitted to our department for follow-up. BNP was 204 ng/L; the ECG is shown in Figure 2; chest X-ray showed no cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio 0.48); echocardiography showed diffuse hypokinesis, reduced LVEF (32% → 36.9%), left atrial enlargement (anteroposterior diameter 4.4 cm → 4.1 cm), left ventricular enlargement (LVEDD 7.4 cm → 6.7 cm), mild-to-moderate mitral regurgitation, and mild tricuspid regurgitation.

Figure 2. ECG on admission in September 2017.

Cardiac MRI: marked left ventricular enlargement (LVEDD 75 mm), mild left atrial enlargement (left atrial area 12 cm²), diffuse left ventricular myocardial dyskinesia, severely impaired left ventricular systolic function (ejection fraction 20%), and multifocal left ventricular myocardial fibrosis (scattered multiple subendocardial and mid-myocardial linear and nodular hypoperfusion foci in the left ventricle; on delayed imaging, a few linear and nodular delayed enhancement foci were seen in the mid-myocardium of the basal and mid interventricular septum; linear and nodular non-enhancing foci were seen in the basal and mid anterior wall and lateral wall and part of the anterior septum), consistent with changes of dilated cardiomyopathy.

Holter was completed: sinus rhythm (53–91 bpm, mean 68 bpm), complete right bundle branch block, partial first-degree atrioventricular block, 4,187 PVCs/24 h, including 6 couplets/24 h (PVCs decreased compared with before).

Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) was completed: 24-hour average 96/68 mmHg, daytime average 102/72 mmHg, nighttime average 90/59 mmHg.

Because the patient had reduced LVEF and ventricular arrhythmias, implantation of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) for primary prevention was recommended; however, the patient and his family requested conservative pharmacologic treatment temporarily, and the above medications were continued.

On regular follow-up, the patient did not have recurrent chest tightness, dyspnea, or other discomfort; exercise tolerance was normal; occasional palpitations occurred during emotional agitation and resolved spontaneously.

(3) Seven months ago (April 2018), due to abnormal renal function (serum creatinine 420 μmol/L), amiodarone (Cordarone) was reduced to 100 mg once daily; torasemide was reduced to 10 mg every other day. Starting 6 months ago, he began to experience intermittent dizziness upon standing after prolonged sitting (about half an hour), accompanied by seeing stars, without sweating, palpitations, chest pain, or chest tightness; symptoms resolved spontaneously after resting for 2–3 seconds. After symptom relief, self-measured blood pressure was: systolic <95 mmHg, diastolic <65 mmHg. The frequency of episodes had no obvious regularity. More than 2 months ago, while bending his head down to shower, he experienced dizziness with seeing stars, without sweating, palpitations, chest tightness, trismus, muscle convulsions, or other discomfort, followed by loss of consciousness, which resolved spontaneously after 2–3 seconds. After recovery of consciousness, there were no residual symptoms such as urinary/fecal incontinence, sweating, or deviation of the mouth angle. Two months ago, upon standing after prolonged sitting (half an hour), he again developed dizziness with seeing stars, followed by loss of consciousness, which resolved spontaneously after 2–3 seconds.

He was admitted to our department for further diagnosis and treatment.

Since onset, the patient’s mental status, appetite, and physical strength have been normal; sleep has been poor; urination and defecation have been normal; body weight has decreased by approximately 2 kg compared with 2 months ago.

#### Past Medical History
History of diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, Kashin–Beck disease, reflux esophagitis, right total hip arthroplasty, right renal mass needle biopsy + radiofrequency ablation, and left adrenal adenoma; no history of hypertension.

#### Personal History
Born in Xiaobeigou Village, Datan Town, Fengning Manchu Autonomous County, Hebei Province. He reported that most villagers over 30 years old locally had multiple joint enlargement, and there were almost no survivors over 70 years old locally. He moved to and has lived in Beijing for 30 years. Smoking history for more than 20 years, 10 cigarettes/day, quit. Intermittent small amount of alcohol consumption for 2 years, quit.

#### Family History
No family history of coronary heart disease; mother died of heart disease at age 28, and elder brother died of heart disease at age 12 (details unknown).

### Physical Examination on Admission
Temperature 36.5°C; blood pressure: right 132/80 mmHg, left 133/75 mmHg; heart rate 54 bpm; pulse 69 bpm.

No jugular venous distention. Lips without pallor; no fine rales. Breath sounds clear in both lungs; no obvious dry or moist rales. Cardiac dullness enlarged to the left; irregular rhythm; occasional premature beats audible; no obvious murmurs in the auscultation areas of the cardiac valves. Abdomen soft; no tenderness or rebound tenderness; liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin; bowel sounds 4/min. Bony enlargement of the proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands with shortened fingers (Figure 3). No obvious edema of both lower extremities.

Figure 3. Bony enlargement of the proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands with shortened fingers.

### Ancillary Examinations on Admission
1. Hematology tests

(1) Complete blood count: WBC 11.70×10°/L, RBC 4.11×10°/L, hemoglobin 129 g/L, platelets 222×10°/L.

(2) Routine urine and stool tests: (−).

(3) Routine stool test: yellow soft stool; fecal occult blood by dual method (−).

(4) Blood biochemistry: alanine aminotransferase 15 IU/L, aspartate aminotransferase 17 IU/L, serum creatinine 85.13 umol/L, glomerular filtration rate 88.287 ml/(min·1.73 m²), blood urea nitrogen 6.33 mmol/L, [K+ 5.00 mmol/L, high-sensitivity C-reactive protein 3.25 mg/L, total cholesterol 0.83 mmol/L, triglycerides 2.43 mmol/L, low-density lipoprotein cholesterol 1.29 mmol/L, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol 0.74 mmol/L.

(4) Myocardial injury markers: creatine kinase-MB 1.2 μg/L, serum cardiac troponin I 0.002 μg/L, BNP 81 ng/L.

(5) Coagulation function: prothrombin time 10.90 s, activated partial thromboplastin time 26.5 s, D-dimer 0.13 mg/L.

(6) Thyroid function: normal.

(7) Glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c): 6.90%.

(8) Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 19 mm/h.

2. Imaging examinations

(1) Chest X-ray: clear bilateral lung markings; high-density linear opacities in both lower lungs; cardiac silhouette not enlarged (Figure 4); cardiothoracic ratio 0.46.

A. Posteroanterior view  
B. Lateral view  
Figure 4. Chest X-ray.

(2) Echocardiography: left ventricular enlargement (LVEDD 6.5 cm, left ventricular end-systolic diameter [LVESD] 5.4 cm), diffuse hypokinesis, reduced LVEF (43.5%), mild aortic regurgitation, moderate mitral regurgitation, left ventricular diastolic dysfunction (Grade I), inferior vena cava not dilated, inspiratory collapse rate >50%.

### 3. Electrocardiogram
Showed sinus bradycardia, premature ventricular contractions, first-degree atrioventricular block, intraventricular conduction block, T-wave inversion in V3–V6. Low voltage in limb leads (Figure 5).

Figure 5. ECG on admission: sinus bradycardia, premature ventricular contractions, first-degree atrioventricular block, intraventricular conduction block, T-wave inversion in V3–V6. Low voltage in limb leads.

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Chronic Keshan disease
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: The patient was born in a high-prevalence area of Keshan disease (Daba Town, Fengning Manchu Autonomous County, Hebei Province), where the majority of local residents over 30 years of age exhibit polyarticular enlargement, and virtually no survivors over 70 years of age exist; reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF 43%) with cardiac enlargement was identified 5 years ago; multiple episodes of position-related transient loss of consciousness occurred over the past 2 months

Physical examination: Cardiac border enlarged to the left, irregular cardiac rhythm, occasional premature beats audible on auscultation; bony enlargement of the proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands (suggestive of coexisting Kashin-Beck disease)

Ancillary investigations: Cardiac MRI revealed multifocal myocardial fibrosis of the left ventricle (delayed enhancement foci and non-enhanced foci), consistent with dilated cardiomyopathy changes; serial echocardiography demonstrated progressive left ventricular dilation (end-diastolic diameter from 7.0 cm decreased to 6.5 cm) and fluctuating ejection fraction (32%-43.5%); ambulatory electrocardiography revealed frequent premature ventricular complexes and non-sustained ventricular tachycardia

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Type 2 diabetes mellitus
Diagnostic_evidence: Past medical history: Definite history of diabetes mellitus
Ancillary investigations: Glycated hemoglobin 6.90% (above normal range)

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Dyslipidemia
Diagnostic_evidence: Past medical history: Definite history of dyslipidemia
Ancillary investigations: Triglycerides 2.43 mmol/L (elevated), high-density lipoprotein cholesterol 0.74 mmol/L (decreased)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Ischemic cardiomyopathy
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Consideration of common etiologies of heart failure
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Decreased exercise tolerance accompanied by paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea
Ancillary investigations: Coronary angiography revealed 30% stenosis of the proximal left anterior descending artery
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: The degree of stenosis in the major coronary vessels did not meet the criteria for ischemic cardiomyopathy (stenosis <50%)
Ancillary investigations: Cardiac MRI demonstrated scattered nodular pattern of myocardial fibrosis rather than segmental ischemic distribution

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Alcoholic cardiomyopathy
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Need to exclude toxic factor-induced myocardial injury
Supporting_evidence: Personal history: History of intermittent light alcohol consumption
Non-supporting_evidence: Personal history: Alcohol cessation for 2 years and cumulative alcohol intake did not reach toxic threshold

Ancillary investigations: The fluctuating pattern of left ventricular ejection fraction during the disease course is inconsistent with the progressive deterioration characteristic of alcoholic cardiomyopathy

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Thyroid dysfunction-related cardiomyopathy
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Need to exclude endocrine-related cardiac dysfunction
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Palpitations, diaphoresis, and other autonomic dysfunction manifestations
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Thyroid function test results were within normal limits

## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Response Format
- When generating the final answer: you must output the diagnostic results directly in Json format (example below), and do not output any other irrelevant content;

- {"Preliminary diagnosis": {"Primary diagnosis": {"Name": "XXX", "Etiologic subtype": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}, "Secondary diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}]}, "Differential diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Selection rationale": "Consider rare but potentially fatal diseases", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": [], "Excludable": true, "Recommended corresponding tests": []}, {"Name": "YYY", "Selection rationale": "Consider differential diagnosis of adjacent organ lesions", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1"], "Excludable": false, "Recommended corresponding tests": ["Recommendation 1", "Recommendation 2"]}]}

### Medical History Summary
#### Patient
Zhang [surname], male, 13 years old, student.

#### Chief Complaint
Shortness of breath after exertion for 4 months, aggravated for 2 weeks.

#### History of Present Illness
The patient developed shortness of breath after exertion 4 months ago (needed to rest after climbing 2 flights of stairs), without chest tightness or chest pain; without dizziness or syncope; without cough, sputum production, fever, etc.; able to lie supine at night; did not seek medical attention. Two weeks ago, the patient’s shortness of breath after exertion worsened compared with before, manifested as shortness of breath after walking 50 m. He visited a local hospital and underwent transthoracic echocardiography, which suggested right heart enlargement and severe pulmonary arterial hypertension (SPAP 95 mmHg), mild mitral regurgitation, moderate tricuspid regurgitation, and mild pulmonary valve regurgitation. He came to our hospital for further diagnostic clarification and treatment.

During the course of the disease, there was no chest pain, cough, or syncope; no photosensitivity, alopecia, xerostomia/xerophthalmia, facial erythema, Raynaud phenomenon, or arthralgia. He can lie supine at night and occasionally snores at night. No history of prolonged sitting, malignancy, or surgery; no history of schistosomiasis exposure; no history of long-term use of weight-loss drugs or other medications. Since onset, the patient’s mental status has been acceptable; sleep is good; appetite is slightly poor; bowel movements are normal; urine output has been decreased over the past week; body weight has not been monitored.

#### Past Medical History
Previously healthy.

#### Personal History
No allergy history; no history of exposure to special substances. Full-term spontaneous vaginal delivery; no special issues during infancy and early childhood. No history of smoking or alcohol consumption.

#### Family History
Both parents are healthy; has one younger brother who is healthy. Denies a family history of hereditary diseases.

### Physical Examination
Temperature 36.3°C, pulse 91 beats/min, respiratory rate 19 breaths/min, blood pressure 90/70 mmHg. Height 180 cm, weight 54 kg, body mass index (BMI) 16.7; normal development. Alert with clear speech; cooperative during examination. Breath sounds slightly coarse bilaterally; no dry or moist rales heard. Cardiac borders enlarged; heart sounds forceful; loud P2; heart rate 91 beats/min; regular rhythm; no pathological murmurs heard in any valvular auscultation areas. Abdomen soft; liver and spleen not palpable; no edema of the lower extremities bilaterally.

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Exertional dyspnea for 4 months, worsening over the past 2 weeks (dyspnea upon walking 50 meters)
Physical examination: Enlarged cardiac borders, accentuated P2

raw_query:
### Medical History Summary
The patient is a 52-year-old male, admitted to the Department of Gastroenterology on 2022-04-01 due to “recurrent abdominal pain and abdominal distension for more than 2 weeks”.

History of present illness: More than 2 weeks prior, the patient developed recurrent abdominal pain and abdominal distension without obvious triggers. The pain was a persistent distending pain in the left lower abdomen, unrelated to activity or body position, which could worsen after eating and could be relieved after defecation and passage of flatus. There was no nausea or vomiting, no acid regurgitation or heartburn, no hematemesis or melena, no chest tightness or chest pain, no urinary frequency, dysuria, or dark yellow urine, and no fever or chills. The patient self-administered medications including “Chenxiang Huaqi Capsules, amoxicillin, simethicone, digestive enzymes”, with poor efficacy. The above symptoms recurred, and he presented to the emergency department of our hospital. Emergency laboratory tests: total bilirubin 51.92 μmol/L; blood urea nitrogen 15.99 mmol/L; creatinine 189 μmol/L; uric acid 621 μmol/L; C-reactive protein 138.6 mg/L; D-dimer 69.29 mg/L. Abdominal CT on 2022-03-31: postoperative findings of inferior vena cava–right internal jugular vein artificial vascular graft shunt; findings consistent with liver cirrhosis, splenomegaly, ascites, and formation of collateral circulation; gallstones; slightly thickened gallbladder wall, cholecystitis not excluded; splenic infarction considered highly likely, please correlate clinically; exudative changes and fluid accumulation in the abdominal cavity. The patient was admitted to the gastroenterology ward for further treatment.

Past medical history: Hypertension for 3 years, with maximum blood pressure up to 160/100 mmHg; currently taking “losartan potassium”, with blood pressure controlled at 140/90 mmHg. Nephrotic syndrome and paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) for 2 years; given renoprotective medications (details unknown), with regular follow-up treatments at the hospital. Hepatitis C for more than 2 years; after standard anti-HCV therapy, follow-up testing was negative. The patient denied a history of other infectious diseases such as tuberculosis and typhoid fever and denied close contact exposure history. Twenty-nine years ago, due to “Budd–Chiari syndrome”, he underwent an “inferior vena cava–right internal jugular vein artificial vascular graft shunt”, with a history of blood transfusion during the same period.

Personal history: No special history; denied smoking and alcohol use.

Marital and reproductive history: Married at appropriate age; has one son.

Family history: Father died of “myocardial infarction”; mother is alive. Has one younger brother, who is healthy. Denied any family history of hereditary diseases or major diseases.

### Physical Examination
Temperature 37.0°C, heart rate 96 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, blood pressure 144/93 mmHg. Pale appearance, thin body habitus. A surgical scar approximately 4 cm in length was visible on the right side of the neck; a longitudinal surgical scar approximately 15 cm in length was visible in the midline of the abdomen. Breath sounds were coarse bilaterally, with no dry or moist rales auscultated. No precordial bulging or depression; cardiac borders not enlarged. Heart rate 96 beats/min, regular rhythm; no pathological murmurs auscultated in any valvular area. Abdominal distension, increased abdominal wall tension; tenderness in the left abdomen with rebound tenderness. Liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin; Murphy’s sign negative; tympany on percussion throughout the abdomen; no costovertebral angle tenderness; bowel sounds not hyperactive; shifting dullness negative. Mild pitting edema of both lower extremities.

### Auxiliary Examinations
After admission, complete blood count indicated anemia (hemoglobin 83 g/L) and thrombocytopenia (platelets 71×10<sup>9</sup>/L), with concomitant infection (neutrophil percentage 82.2%, C-reactive protein 138.6 mg/L). Liver and renal function tests indicated elevated bilirubin (total bilirubin 37.5 μmol/L, direct bilirubin 27.2 μmol/L), hypoalbuminemia (27.3 g/L), and renal insufficiency (blood urea nitrogen 15.3 mmo/L, creatinine 183 μmol/L). Coagulation tests indicated coagulopathy, with prolonged plasma prothrombin time (PT) (16.2 s) and prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) (43 s), and markedly elevated D-dimer (52.15 mg/L).

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH)
Diagnostic_evidence: Past medical history: PNH for 2 years
Laboratory findings: anemia (Hb 83 g/L), thrombocytopenia (71×10^9/L)

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Splenic infarction
Diagnostic_evidence: Laboratory/Imaging findings: CT suggestive of splenic infarction
Symptoms: persistent left lower abdominal pain
Laboratory findings: D-dimer 69.29 mg/L (emergency) / 52.15 mg/L (on admission)

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Chronic kidney disease stage 3
Diagnostic_evidence: Laboratory findings: blood urea nitrogen 15.3 mmol/L, creatinine 183 μmol/L
Past medical history: nephrotic syndrome for 2 years

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Cholelithiasis with chronic cholecystitis
Diagnostic_evidence: Imaging findings: CT showing gallbladder stones with mildly thickened gallbladder wall
Laboratory findings: total bilirubin 37.5 μmol/L, direct bilirubin 27.2 μmol/L

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Hypertension grade 2 (high risk)
Diagnostic_evidence: Physical examination: blood pressure 144/93 mmHg
Past medical history: hypertension for 3 years, with peak blood pressure of 160/100 mmHg

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Decompensated liver cirrhosis (portal hypertensive type): hepatitis C-related liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: recurrent abdominal pain and distension, aggravated by food intake, relieved by defecation and flatus
Symptoms: 29 years post inferior vena cava-right internal jugular vein prosthetic graft shunt surgery
Physical examination: abdominal distension, increased abdominal wall tension, tenderness and rebound tenderness in the left abdomen
Physical examination: 4 cm surgical scar on the right side of the neck, 15 cm longitudinal midline abdominal surgical scar
Imaging findings: CT showing liver cirrhosis, splenomegaly, ascites, and collateral circulation formation
Laboratory findings: hypoalbuminemia (27.3 g/L), coagulopathy (PT 16.2 s / APTT 43 s)
Laboratory findings: thrombocytopenia (71×10^9/L), anemia (Hb 83 g/L)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Mesenteric ischemia
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Consideration of potentially life-threatening conditions
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: abdominal pain aggravated after food intake
Laboratory findings: markedly elevated D-dimer (69.29 mg/L)
Non-supporting_evidence: Imaging findings: no signs of bowel wall ischemia on CT
Symptoms: symptom relief after defecation

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Differential diagnosis of common complications of liver cirrhosis
Supporting_evidence: Laboratory findings: CRP 138.6 mg/L
Laboratory findings: neutrophils 82.2%
Non-supporting_evidence: Physical examination: negative shifting dullness
Symptoms: no fever

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Acute cholecystitis
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Differential diagnosis of adjacent organ pathology
Supporting_evidence: Imaging findings: mildly thickened gallbladder wall
Laboratory findings: elevated bilirubin
Non-supporting_evidence: Physical examination: negative Murphy's sign
Symptoms: pain located in the left lower abdomen

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Portal vein thrombosis
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Common complication of portal hypertension
Supporting_evidence: Laboratory findings: markedly elevated D-dimer
Imaging findings: CT findings suggestive of splenic infarction
Non-supporting_evidence: Imaging findings: no direct evidence of thrombus on imaging
检查推荐: Doppler ultrasound of the portal venous system

raw_query:
### History Summary
The patient is a 59-year-old female who was admitted on 2023-05-29 due to “plantar numbness and rash for more than 1 year, and chest tightness and shortness of breath for more than 4 months”.

History of present illness: In 2021-11, after receiving the third dose of the COVID-19 vaccine, the patient developed numbness of the left sole and facial rash; thereafter, she gradually developed numbness of the right sole accompanied by swelling of both feet and pain after prolonged standing. Due to progressively worsening numbness, she visited an outside hospital, where electromyography indicated peripheral nerve injury in both lower extremities (more pronounced in the left lower extremity); high-sensitivity troponin I 0.069 μg/L; symptoms did not improve significantly after additional treatment with mecobalamin, vitamin B<sub>1</sub>, and vitamin B<sub>6</sub>. Cryotherapy and laser therapy were given for the rash; symptoms receded but recurred repeatedly. In 2022-08, she again visited the neurology department of an outside hospital for the above symptoms; symptoms did not improve significantly after additional treatments including neurotrophic and analgesic medications. In 2023-01, after SARS-CoV-2 infection, her exercise tolerance declined markedly; she developed chest tightness and dyspnea after fast walking or climbing 2 flights of stairs, which was relieved after resting for several minutes; she could lie flat at night to sleep. She visited a local hospital, where creatine kinase isoenzymes and natriuretic peptide were elevated; contrast-enhanced chest CT showed multiple enlarged lymph nodes in the neck, hilum, and mediastinum; Holter monitoring showed multifocal premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) 17,497 times; echocardiography showed LVEF 46% and left heart enlargement; superficial lymph node ultrasound revealed bilateral supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Further right supraclavicular lymph node aspiration showed granulomatous lymphadenitis (all special stains negative); T-cell test for tuberculosis infection was positive. After additional treatment with aspirin, metoprolol succinate extended-release tablets (Betaloc ZOK), spironolactone, furosemide, etc., chest tightness improved slightly. In 2023-04, she visited an outside hospital due to intermittent dull precordial pain; high-sensitivity troponin I and natriuretic peptide were elevated, and angiotensin-converting enzyme was elevated; echocardiography showed LVEF 28% and left heart enlargement; Holter monitoring showed frequent ventricular ectopic beats; chest CT showed multiple enlarged lymph nodes in the neck, bilateral hila, and mediastinum. Thereafter, she visited the neurology outpatient clinic of our hospital; neuropathological biopsy showed mixed-type pathological changes of peripheral nerves with vasculitic changes (left sural nerve). In the dermatology outpatient clinic, a skin biopsy of the facial rash showed naked epithelioid cell granulomas on pathology, and sarcoidosis was considered first. On 2023-05-11, an outside hospital started prednisone acetate 50 mg once daily (taper by 2.5 mg weekly; currently 45 mg once daily); meanwhile, spironolactone, Betaloc ZOK, sacubitril/valsartan, dapagliflozin, etc. were given to improve ventricular remodeling. After adding glucocorticoids, chest tightness and rash improved significantly compared with before; plantar numbness showed no significant change. The patient was admitted for further diagnosis and treatment. Over the past half year, her mental status and appetite were acceptable; sleep was poor; urination and defecation were normal; body weight decreased by 2 kg.

Past medical history: Denies history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and valvular heart disease. Hyperlipidemia for more than 1 year; taking atorvastatin 10 mg once nightly. In 2023-01, gastroscopy at an outside hospital suggested chronic gastritis; underwent descending colon polypectomy with forceps. Thyroid nodules; the patient reported prior thyroid surgery, details unknown. Severe osteoporosis; currently taking calcium carbonate and calcitriol (Rocaltrol). Gallbladder polyp, left renal calculus, lumbar degenerative changes. Status post tubal ligation.

Personal history: No special history; denies exposure to drugs or toxic substances.

Marital and reproductive history: Not menopausal; three daughters, healthy.

Family history: One brother in the family has antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA)-associated vasculitis, with numbness of both feet; father and two uncles died of lung cancer (>70 years old).

### Physical Examination
Temperature 36.5°C, pulse 56 beats/min, respiration 20 breaths/min, left upper limb blood pressure 87/49 mmHg, right upper limb blood pressure 86/52 mmHg. Height 167.5 cm, weight 46.3 kg, body mass index (BMI) 16.5. Conscious and alert; scattered yellow and yellow-red papules and nodules on the face; no enlarged superficial lymph nodes palpable throughout the body. Jugular venous distension; positive hepatojugular reflux. Breath sounds clear bilaterally; no obvious dry or moist rales heard. Apical impulse located 0.5 cm lateral to the left midclavicular line at the 5th intercostal space; no abnormal impulse observed; no palpable thrills; cardiac borders enlarged; irregular rhythm; P2 < A2; no obvious murmurs in any valvular auscultation areas and no pericardial friction rub. Abdomen soft, no tenderness; bowel sounds 3 times/min. No edema in both lower extremities.

### Auxiliary Examinations
1. Pre-admission examinations

2022-05:

Complete blood count: white blood cell count 3.2×10<sup>9</sup>/L, red blood cell count 3.47×10<sup>12</sup>/L, hemoglobin 101 g/L.

High-sensitivity troponin I 0.069 μg/L.

High-sensitivity C-reactive protein 1.13 mg/L.

Tumor markers and natriuretic peptide: no abnormalities.

Echocardiography: LVEF 72%, left ventricular end-diastolic diameter 3.9 cm.

Holter monitoring: sinus rhythm (mean heart rate 73 beats/min), premature atrial contractions 24 times.

Non-contrast chest CT: small amount of chronic inflammation in the right upper and middle lobes with mild bronchiectasis in some branches; ground-glass nodules in both upper lobes.

Electromyography: peripheral nerve injury in both lower extremities (more pronounced in the left lower extremity), involving both sensory and motor nerves, predominantly axonal injury.

2022-08:

ANCA (-).

Serum κ light chain 4.11 g/L ↑.

Transthyretin (TTR) gene: no abnormalities.

2023-01:

Creatine kinase isoenzyme 10.1 U/L.

Natriuretic peptide 661.50 pg/mL.

Antinuclear antibody (-).

Cervical lymph node color Doppler ultrasound: bilateral supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Echocardiography: decreased left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 46%), left heart enlargement (left ventricular end-diastolic diameter 5.8 cm).

Holter monitoring: sinus rhythm (mean heart rate 69 beats/min); multifocal premature ventricular contractions 17,497 times, 1,805 couplets, and 166 episodes of nonsustained paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia (“VT”), sometimes in bigeminy, trigeminy, or interpolated patterns.

Contrast-enhanced chest CT: multiple enlarged lymph nodes in the neck, hilum, and mediastinum.

Coronary computed tomography angiography (CTA): calcified plaque in the proximal left anterior descending artery (LAD) wall with mild luminal stenosis; calcified plaque in the mid-segment wall with mild luminal stenosis.

Right supraclavicular lymph node aspiration: granulomatous lymphadenitis; PAS stain, acid-fast stain, and methenamine silver stain all negative.

T-cell test for tuberculosis infection: positive.

2023-04:

Angiotensin-converting enzyme 98 U/L ↑.

High-sensitivity troponin I 100 ng/L.

B-type natriuretic peptide 394 pg/mL.

Echocardiography: reduced left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 28%), left heart enlargement (left ventricular end-diastolic diameter 5.9 cm), decreased left ventricular diastolic function, normal right ventricular systolic function, moderate mitral regurgitation.

Holter monitoring: sinus rhythm (mean heart rate 72 beats/min), frequent ventricular ectopic beats (including premature beats and escape beats) (multifocal): some couplets, some bigeminy, some trigeminy; total premature ventricular contractions 30,239 times; 607 episodes of nonsustained VT.

Nerve ultrasound: no abnormalities.

Electromyography: slowed motor nerve conduction velocity of the left tibial nerve; slowed motor nerve conduction velocity of bilateral common peroneal nerves with decreased compound muscle action potential (CMAP) amplitude; otherwise no abnormalities.

Left sural nerve pathology: mixed pathological changes of peripheral nerve with vasculitic changes.

Dermatology visit: Physical examination: scattered yellow and yellow-red papules and nodules on the face; tuberculin test (PPD test) (-); skin biopsy: epithelioid cell aggregates throughout the full thickness of the dermis, no caseous necrosis, relatively well-demarcated. Pathology showed naked epithelioid cell granulomas; sarcoidosis was considered first, and special infection needed to be excluded.


2. Additional post-admission examinations

Creatine kinase isoenzyme 1.5 ng/mL, high-sensitivity troponin I 10.3 ng/L.

B-type natriuretic peptide 527 pg/mL ↑.

High-sensitivity C-reactive protein 0.13 mg/L.

Hepatitis B virus DNA quantification 1.13×10<sup>3</sup> U/mL.

Thyroid function: no abnormalities.

Antinuclear antibody positive (speckled pattern, 1:100), anti-ENA profile all negative, ANCA (-).

24-hour urine electrolytes: 24-hour urine volume 2450 mL, 24-hour urinary calcium quantification 8.50 mmol/L.

Rubella virus IgM, EB virus antibody IgM, and parvovirus B19 antibody IgM all negative.

Free light chains, serum immunofixation electrophoresis, and protein electrophoresis: no abnormalities.

Pulmonary function: no obvious abnormalities. Diffusing capacity corrected for hemoglobin 12.7 g/L was normal. Carbon monoxide diffusing capacity as percentage of predicted value 83.1%.

Electrocardiogram: sinus rhythm, indeterminate axis, polymorphic premature ventricular contractions, low voltage in limb leads.


Holter monitoring: sinus rhythm (45–110 beats/min, mean 66 beats/min), premature atrial contractions 18 times, premature ventricular contractions 13,080 times, including 205 episodes of nonsustained VT (multiple morphologies, predominantly one morphology).

Echocardiography: reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF 22.2%); left ventricular enlargement (left ventricular end-diastolic diameter 5.9 cm); interventricular septum 0.84 cm, left ventricular posterior wall 0.77 cm; pulmonary artery systolic pressure 25.3 mmHg; inferior vena cava not dilated, inspiratory collapse rate >50%.

Speckle-tracking echocardiography: reduced global longitudinal strain of the left ventricle at rest, more pronounced reduction in the mid-segments of the lateral wall and posterior wall; left ventricular global longitudinal strain (LV-GLS) = -11.9%. Conclusion: reduced left ventricular global longitudinal strain.


Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging: see Case 2 Figure 4.

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Sarcoidosis (cardiac involvement)
Primary_diagnosis.疾病分型: Multisystem sarcoidosis with cardiac and peripheral nerve involvement
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: facial rash, numbness of the soles of feet, chest tightness, dyspnea, decreased exercise tolerance
Physical examination: scattered yellow and yellowish-red papules and nodules on the face, jugular venous distension, positive hepatojugular reflux, enlarged cardiac borders, cardiac arrhythmia

Ancillary investigations: skin biopsy pathology revealed naked epithelioid cell granulomas (characteristic of sarcoidosis); lymph node aspiration showed granulomatous inflammation; serum angiotensin-converting enzyme 98 U/L↑; cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed reduced left ventricular global longitudinal strain (LV-GLS = -11.9%); echocardiography showed progressive decline in LVEF (72% → 46% → 28% → 22.2%) with left ventricular dilation; ambulatory electrocardiography revealed multifocal ventricular arrhythmias
Ancillary investigations: electromyography showed bilateral lower extremity peripheral nerve damage (predominantly axonal); nerve biopsy revealed mixed pathological changes with vasculitic changes

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Left heart failure (NYHA Class IV)
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: chest tightness and dyspnea on brisk walking or climbing 2 flights of stairs, able to lie flat at night

Ancillary investigations: persistently elevated brain natriuretic peptide (394 → 527 pg/mL↑); echocardiography showed LVEF 22.2%, left ventricular end-diastolic diameter 5.9 cm; speckle tracking imaging showed reduced left ventricular global longitudinal strain

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Coronary atherosclerosis, hyperlipidemia, hepatitis B virus infection, chronic gastritis, severe osteoporosis, thyroid nodule, gallbladder polyp, left renal calculus, lumbar degenerative disease, post-thyroidectomy, post-tubal ligation, post-descending colon polypectomy
Diagnostic_evidence: The patient has a history of the aforementioned comorbidities

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Tuberculous granuloma
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Considering granulomatous inflammation of the lymph node and positive T-cell test for tuberculosis infection
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: positive T-SPOT (T-cell test for tuberculosis infection); chest CT showed mediastinal lymphadenopathy
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: special stains on lymph node aspiration (PAS, acid-fast, GMS) were all negative; PPD test was negative; corticosteroid therapy was effective, but anti-tuberculosis treatment was not administered

Differential_diagnoses.Name: ANCA-associated vasculitis
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Family history of ANCA-associated vasculitis
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: bilateral foot numbness (similar symptoms in family members); vasculitic changes in peripheral nerves
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: multiple ANCA tests were all negative; no evidence of renal involvement

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Cardiac amyloidosis
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Considering progressive cardiac function deterioration and hypotensive state
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: concomitant peripheral neuropathy; echocardiography showed normal left ventricular wall thickness (interventricular septum 0.84 cm)
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: serum free light chains were normal; transthyretin gene showed no abnormalities; cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed no typical late gadolinium enhancement pattern

## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

### Brief History
#### Patient
Male, 29 years old. Admitted on October 24, 2019.

#### Chief Complaint
Intermittent chest pain for 6 years; thrombocytopenia discovered for 2 years.

#### History of Present Illness
Six years ago (September 12, 2013), the patient developed sudden severe chest pain without obvious precipitating factors; the pain was severe and intolerable, radiating to the back and both shoulders, persisting for 6 hours without relief, accompanied by profuse sweating and nausea without vomiting. An ECG in the emergency department of our hospital showed sinus rhythm; ST-segment elevation of 0.05–0.2 mV in leads II, III, aVF, and V₇–V₉; Q waves were seen in leads III, aVF, and V₇–V₉ (Figure 1). Emergency coronary angiography (CAG) showed complete occlusion of the mid right coronary artery (RCA), mild myocardial bridging in the mid left anterior descending artery (LAD), and no obvious abnormality in the left circumflex artery (LCX). After thrombus aspiration, 1 stent was implanted at the RCA lesion; post-procedure distal flow was TIMI grade 3 (Figure 2). After discharge, the patient took aspirin 100 mg + clopidogrel 150 mg as dual antiplatelet therapy for 1 year, atorvastatin 20 mg for lipid-lowering therapy, and an ACE inhibitor and a β-blocker. After 1 year, clopidogrel was discontinued; the remaining treatments were continued as before. He usually had no symptoms such as chest pain or chest tightness.
Figure 1 ECG on admission on September 12, 2013  
Figure 2 Coronary angiography (CAG) and percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) (September 2013)

Four years ago, after staying up late, the patient developed severe chest pain again, without radiation to the shoulder/back, accompanied by chest tightness, shortness of breath, and profuse sweating, without palpitations; he did not seek medical attention at that time. He presented 38 hours after onset. ECG showed: sinus rhythm; QS pattern in leads II and aVF; rSr’ pattern in lead III; troponin (cTnI) 8.22 ng/mL. The diagnosis was coronary heart disease, acute non–ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), Killip class I, and old myocardial infarction. CAG showed: mild myocardial bridging in the mid left main (LAD), no obvious abnormality in the left anterior descending branch (LCX), complete occlusion with thrombus formation at the proximal segment near the proximal end of the original stent in the RCA; collateral circulation from the LAD to the distal RCA. After repeated thrombus aspiration in the RCA and intracoronary tirofiban injection, due to heavy intracoronary thrombus burden and high risk of no-reflow, no stent was implanted; intensified medical therapy was recommended, with repeat CAG after 1 week and PCI if necessary. One week later, the patient and his family refused repeat CAG and requested discharge. After discharge, he took medications regularly: aspirin and ticagrelor dual antiplatelet therapy for 1 year; ticagrelor was discontinued after 1 year; the remaining treatments were continued as before.

Three years ago, the patient had recurrent dull precordial pain with chest tightness and shortness of breath, lasting several minutes each time and related to exertion; symptoms could be relieved within minutes by rest or sublingual nitroglycerin (Xiaoxintong); exercise tolerance decreased. After admission, he refused CAG and requested coronary CTA. Coronary CTA showed: status post proximal RCA stent implantation; in-stent restenosis with severe luminal stenosis, nearly occluded. The family refused cardiac interventional surgery and requested conservative medical treatment. After discharge, he took medications regularly: aspirin and ticagrelor dual antiplatelet therapy; rosuvastatin 10 mg and ezetimibe 10 mg for lipid-lowering therapy; ACE inhibitor; β-blocker; isosorbide mononitrate. He occasionally had episodes of chest tightness, each lasting several minutes and resolving spontaneously.

Two years ago, he again developed sudden severe chest pain, persisting for 3 hours without relief. ECG showed sinus rhythm with frequent premature ventricular contractions; QS pattern in leads II, III, and aVF; ST-segment depression of 0.05 mV in leads I, aVL, and V₇–V₉; mild T-wave inversion in leads V₅, V₆, and V₇–V₉. Troponin (cTnI): 59.323 ng/mL. Diagnosis: coronary heart disease; acute NSTEMI; Killip class I; old myocardial infarction; arrhythmia—frequent premature ventricular contractions. Emergency CAG showed: complete occlusion of proximal RCA; complete occlusion of proximal LAD with thrombus shadow; no obvious abnormality in LCX. With intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) support, 1 stent was successfully implanted at the LAD lesion; post-procedure distal flow was TIMI grade 3 (Figure 3). Platelet count on admission was 151×10⁹/L. On day 4 of IABP, platelets decreased to 94×10⁹/L; after IABP removal, platelets recovered spontaneously to normal at 225×10⁹/L (Figure 4). It was recommended that 15 days later, elective treatment of the RCA in-stent chronic total occlusion (CTO) lesion be performed. The patient was readmitted 15 days later; repeat platelet count was 53×10⁹/L, and surgery was not performed due to thrombocytopenia. Rheumatoid antibody panel and anticardiolipin antibodies were negative (Figure 5). The patient’s family refused further evaluation with bone marrow aspiration. Hematology prescribed oral traditional Chinese medicine Weixuening (ingredients: Agrimonia pilosa, Spatholobus suberectus, Polygonum cuspidatum, Rehmannia glutinosa [prepared], Rehmannia glutinosa, Paeonia lactiflora, Pseudostellaria heterophylla, Eclipta prostrata). Platelets at discharge were 117×10⁹/L (see Figure 5). After discharge, he continued aspirin + ticagrelor dual antiplatelet therapy for 1 year; ticagrelor was discontinued after 1 year; the remaining treatments were continued as before. During 2 years of outpatient follow-up after discharge, platelet counts fluctuated between (40–90)×10^9/L (mostly around 50×10^9/L). After discharge, the patient had no episodes of chest pain or chest tightness.

Figure 3 Coronary angiography (CAG) and percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) (August 2017)

Suggestive of impaired maturation of platelet-producing megakaryocytes

One month ago (October 24, 2019), repeat platelet count was 24×10^9/L, and he was admitted again.

Over the past month, appetite was acceptable; sleep was poor (frequent staying up late); mental status and physical strength were normal; urination and defecation were normal; no significant change in body weight.

#### Past Medical History
Denies history of hypertension and diabetes mellitus.

#### Personal History
Smoking history of approximately 15 years, about 1 pack/day. Began smoking cessation on August 21, 2019 and has remained abstinent to date. Usually stays up late frequently.

#### Family History
No family history of coronary heart disease. No family history of hereditary diseases.

### Physical Examination on Admission
Temperature 36.5°C, heart rate 87 beats/min, blood pressure 129/92 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, height 170 cm, weight 80 kg, body mass index 27.68^. Clear consciousness; no cyanosis of the lips; no jugular venous distension; breath sounds clear bilaterally; no dry or moist rales. No precordial bulging or depression; normal apical impulse range; no precordial thrill or pericardial friction rub palpated; heart rate 87 beats/min, regular rhythm; no murmurs in any valve auscultation areas. Bilateral radial pulses were normal in strength, symmetrical, with regular rhythm. Primary_diagnosis.Name: Antiphospholipid syndrome
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Recurrent in-stent thrombosis (thrombus 1, thrombus 2, thrombus 3, thrombus 4, thrombus 5)
Symptoms: Multifocal arterial thrombosis (recurrent occlusion of RCA and LAD)
Ancillary tests: Anti-β2-glycoprotein I antibody (β2-GPI) positive (quantitative assay on October 25, 2019: 9.20 U/mL)
Ancillary tests: Bone marrow aspiration suggesting maturation arrest of platelet-producing megakaryocytes (leukemia and other bone marrow diseases excluded)

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Drug-refractory thrombocytopenia
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Thrombocytopenia identified 2 years ago with persistent fluctuations (nadir PLT 16×10⁹/L)
Ancillary tests: Bone marrow aspiration showing thrombocytopenia with maturation arrest of platelet-producing megakaryocytes (Figure 6)
Ancillary tests: PLT fluctuated significantly during antiplatelet therapy (151→94→225→53→117→24×10⁹/L)
Treatment course: Dexamethasone pulse therapy and oral eltrombopag administered, yet platelet counts continued to fluctuate significantly

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Coronary atherosclerotic heart disease, old myocardial infarction
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Recurrent chest pain over 6 years with multiple episodes of acute myocardial infarction (STEMI, NSTEMI)
Ancillary tests: Coronary angiography showing recurrent occlusion of RCA/LAD with thrombus formation (Figures 2, 3)
Ancillary tests: Multiple coronary CTA/PCI examinations demonstrating in-stent restenosis (2016 CTA showing severe in-stent stenosis of RCA)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP)
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Considering thrombocytopenia as the primary concern
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary tests: Bone marrow aspiration showing maturation arrest of platelet-producing megakaryocytes (consistent with ITP features)
Ancillary tests: Rheumatic antibody panel negative (SLE-secondary etiology excluded)
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary tests: Multiple arterial thrombotic events (ITP is typically not associated with thrombosis)
Ancillary tests: Anti-β2-glycoprotein I antibody positive (suggestive of antiphospholipid syndrome)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Considering platelet decline following heparin administration
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary tests: PLT decreased from 151 to 94×10⁹/L during IABP use in 2017 (Figure 4)
Non-supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Thrombocytopenia persisted for 2 years after IABP removal (PLT 24×10⁹/L in 2019)
Ancillary tests: Anti-PF4 antibody not tested (critical indicator missing)

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Considering the combination of thrombosis and thrombocytopenia
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Coexistence of thrombosis and thrombocytopenia
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary tests: Schistocyte detection within normal limits (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia excluded)
Ancillary tests: Absence of neurological symptoms (inconsistent with the classic pentad)

- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

### Medical History Summary
#### Patient
Female, 56 years old, self-employed.

#### Chief Complaint
Chest tightness and shortness of breath on exertion for 2 months, progressively worsening for 7 days.

#### History of Present Illness
Since January 2017, the patient developed chest tightness and shortness of breath on exertion, accompanied by palpitations and poor appetite, without chest pain or shoulder/back discomfort; without cough, sputum production, or hemoptysis; without dizziness or syncope. Symptoms could improve spontaneously after rest and were not taken seriously. The above symptoms progressively worsened, occurring with mild activity, and gradually neck edema developed, followed by inability to lie supine at night.

On February 8, 2017, the patient visited an outside hospital and underwent echocardiography: left ventricular internal diameter in diastole (LVIDd) 52 mm, right ventricle (RV) 25 mm, left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) 32%. Resting myocardial radionuclide imaging showed slight enlargement of the left ventricular cavity, with no obvious abnormality in left ventricular myocardial perfusion. Diagnosed with “dilated cardiomyopathy, cardiomegaly, cardiac function class III (NYHA classification).” After treatment (specific regimen unknown), symptoms were slightly relieved. She continued regular oral treatment outside the hospital with “ramipril, spironolactone, torasemide,” etc. She still had chest tightness and shortness of breath on exertion, which were tolerable.

On March 3, 2017, symptoms of chest tightness and shortness of breath progressively worsened, accompanied by inability to lie supine. She presented to our hospital on March 10.

#### Past Medical History
Hypertension for 1 year, with a maximum of 140/90 mmHg. She previously took “levamlodipine besylate, 2.5 mg each time, once daily” for blood pressure control; blood pressure fluctuated between 120–130/70–80 mmHg. One month before onset, she discontinued the medication on her own due to blood pressure fluctuations of 80–100/60–70 mmHg. Denies history of joint redness, swelling, heat, and pain; denies history of hepatitis and tuberculosis; no history of special drug exposure.

#### Personal History
Denies smoking and alcohol abuse.

#### Marital and Obstetric History
Married at age 25; 2 pregnancies and 1 delivery (G2P1); one son. No history of spontaneous abortion.

#### Family History
Mother had dermatomyositis at age 65 and died 6 months after onset.

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Antiphospholipid syndrome; Dilated cardiomyopathy
Primary_diagnosis.疾病分型: Chronic systolic heart failure (NYHA Class IV)
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Chest tightness and dyspnea on exertion for 2 months, progressively worsening with orthopnea at night
Physical examination: Cardiac dullness enlarged bilaterally on percussion, muffled heart sounds, blood pressure 80/60 mmHg, cold extremities

Ancillary examination: Echocardiography showing global cardiac enlargement (LA 35 mm, RA 48×53 mm, RV 27 mm, LVIDd 52 mm), left ventricular ejection fraction 26%, diffuse hypokinesis of both left and right ventricular walls

Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showing multiple delayed gadolinium enhancement foci in the left and right ventricular myocardium, with mural thrombus formation at the right ventricular apex
Ancillary examination: NT-proBNP 7,991.0 ng/L↑

Case characteristics: (1) Middle-aged female, previously healthy, with a maternal history of dermatomyositis.

(2) Presented with heart failure, rapidly declining exercise tolerance, rapid disease progression, and recurrent hospitalizations; poor response to pharmacological therapy. Global cardiac enlargement predominantly involving the right ventricle, with late gadolinium enhancement on cardiac MRI suggestive of myocardial fibrosis.

(3) Manifestations of multi-organ arterial and venous thrombosis: ① Right ventricular intracardiac thrombus formation; ② Pulmonary artery embolism; ③ Lower extremity superficial femoral vein thrombosis; ④ Cutaneous venous thrombosis, manifesting as livedo reticularis; ⑤ Cutaneous small artery embolism, manifesting as digital gangrene; ⑥ Possible cardiac small artery microthrombi, leading to myocardial microinfarction and myocardial fibrosis.

(4) Multi-organ involvement: heart, skin, hematologic system, lungs, etc.

(5) Thrombocytopenia, elevated D-dimer, and coagulation dysfunction.

(6) Immunological markers: Anticardiolipin antibody (ACL), anti-β2-glycoprotein I (β2-GPI) antibody, and antinuclear antibody (ANA) were all positive.

Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Echocardiography showing severe tricuspid regurgitation (regurgitant Vmax = 164 cm/s)
Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showing severe regurgitation at the tricuspid valve

Supporting_evidence: Family history: Mother diagnosed with dermatomyositis

question:
### Medical History Summary
#### Patient
Female, 56 years old, self-employed.

#### Chief Complaint
Chest tightness and shortness of breath on exertion for 2 months, progressively worsening for 7 days.

#### History of Present Illness
Since January 2017, the patient developed chest tightness and shortness of breath on exertion, accompanied by palpitations and poor appetite, without chest pain or shoulder/back discomfort; without cough, sputum production, or hemoptysis; without dizziness or syncope. Symptoms could improve spontaneously after rest and were not taken seriously. The above symptoms progressively worsened, occurring with mild activity, and gradually neck edema developed, followed by inability to lie supine at night.

On February 8, 2017, the patient visited an outside hospital and underwent echocardiography: left ventricular internal diameter in diastole (LVIDd) 52 mm, right ventricle (RV) 25 mm, left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) 32%. Resting myocardial radionuclide imaging showed slight enlargement of the left ventricular cavity, with no obvious abnormality in left ventricular myocardial perfusion. Diagnosed with “dilated cardiomyopathy, cardiomegaly, cardiac function class III (NYHA classification).” After treatment (specific regimen unknown), symptoms were slightly relieved. She continued regular oral treatment outside the hospital with “ramipril, spironolactone, torasemide,” etc. She still had chest tightness and shortness of breath on exertion, which were tolerable.

On March 3, 2017, symptoms of chest tightness and shortness of breath progressively worsened, accompanied by inability to lie supine. She presented to our hospital on March 10.

#### Past Medical History
Hypertension for 1 year, with a maximum of 140/90 mmHg. She previously took “levamlodipine besylate, 2.5 mg each time, once daily” for blood pressure control; blood pressure fluctuated between 120–130/70–80 mmHg. One month before onset, she discontinued the medication on her own due to blood pressure fluctuations of 80–100/60–70 mmHg. Denies history of joint redness, swelling, heat, and pain; denies history of hepatitis and tuberculosis; no history of special drug exposure.

#### Personal History
Denies smoking and alcohol abuse.

#### Marital and Obstetric History
Married at age 25; 2 pregnancies and 1 delivery (G2P1); one son. No history of spontaneous abortion.

#### Family History
Mother had dermatomyositis at age 65 and died 6 months after onset.

### Physical Examination on Admission
Temperature 36.5°C, heart rate 80 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, blood pressure 80/60 mmHg. Clear consciousness, acute facies, propped-up position with high pillow, listless, cooperative with examination. No distention of bilateral jugular veins. Bilateral lungs resonant to percussion; coarse breath sounds bilaterally; no dry/wet rales or pleural friction rub. Cardiac dullness enlarged bilaterally on percussion. Heart rate 80 beats/min; heart sounds low and muffled; regular rhythm; no pathological murmurs or third heart sound heard in any valve auscultation areas. Abdomen flat; no tenderness, rebound tenderness, or muscle guarding throughout; liver and spleen not palpably enlarged below the costal margin. No edema of bilateral lower extremities; low skin temperature of the extremities.

### Laboratory Tests
1. Complete blood count: WBC 7.4×10⁹/L, hemoglobin 171 g/L, hematocrit 52.5%, platelets 110×10⁹/L.

2. Liver function: ALT 35 U/L, total bilirubin (TBIL) 36.3 μmol/L ↑, direct bilirubin (DBIL) 10.0 μmol/L ↑, indirect bilirubin (IBIL) 26.3 μmol/L ↑  
Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) 100 U/L ↑, albumin (ALB) 38 g/L ↓, total protein (TP) 64 g/L ↓.

(3) Renal function: creatinine 81.3 μmol/L ↑, urea (UREA) 9.0 mmol/L, uric acid (UA) 749 μmol/L ↑.

(4) Coagulation function: prothrombin time (PT) 22.7 s ↑, international normalized ratio (INR) 1.77, D-dimer (DD₂) 4 137 μg/L ↑.

(5) N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP) 7 991.0 ng/L ↑.

(6) Blood gas analysis: pH 7.54 ↑, PCO₂ 29 mmHg, PO₂ 70 mmHg, BE 2.7 mmol/L, HCO₃⁻ 24.0 mmol/L, SO₂ 95%.

(7) High-sensitivity C-reactive protein 37.6 mg/L ↑.

(8) Blood glucose, blood lipids, cholinesterase, troponin, antistreptolysin O, rheumatoid factor, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, thyroid function, hepatitis panel, tumor marker panel, procalcitonin (PCT), routine urine and stool tests: no obvious abnormalities.

### Auxiliary Examinations
1. **Electrocardiogram (ECG)**  
   Sinus rhythm, heart rate 80 beats/min, low voltage QRS complexes in widespread leads (Figure 1).

### Figure 1 Admission ECG
Sinus rhythm, heart rate 80 beats/min, low voltage QRS complexes in widespread leads

2. **Echocardiography**  
   Left atrium 35 mm, left ventricular internal diameter in diastole (LVIDd) 52 mm, right atrium (RA) 48 mm × 53 mm, right ventricle 27 mm, left ventricular ejection fraction 26%. Four-chamber enlargement; diffuse hypokinesis of left and right ventricular walls; mild mitral regurgitation; severe tricuspid regurgitation; decreased left ventricular systolic function; small pericardial effusion (Figure 2).
