## Medical History
Patient Information  
Sex: Male  
Age: 72 years  
Chief complaint: Bilateral lower extremity edema for >1 month; chest tightness and shortness of breath for 4 days

History of present illness:  
Onset and initial presentation (1 month ago):  
One month ago, the patient developed bilateral lower extremity edema without an obvious trigger. The edema in both lower extremities could improve after activity. There were no symptoms such as paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, palpitations, chest pain, palpitations, cough with sputum, abdominal pain, diarrhea, periorbital/facial edema, proteinuria, etc. No specific treatment was given.

Worsening of condition (4 days ago – 2 days ago):  
Four days ago, the patient developed retrosternal chest tightness, accompanied by palpitations and shortness of breath. The above symptoms could be aggravated after activity and relieved with rest.  
Two days ago, the patient experienced one episode of chest pain, accompanied by profuse sweating, shortness of breath, palpitations, cough with sputum, etc. The sputum was white mucoid sputum and was easy to expectorate. Symptoms were relieved after approximately 20 minutes. The patient went to Fuwai Hospital for consultation (details of diagnosis and treatment are unknown).

This admission:  
For further treatment, the family visited our hospital’s chest pain clinic. B-type natriuretic peptide was 388.14 pg/mL. The patient was admitted to our department from the outpatient clinic with “cardiac insufficiency.”  
Disease course after admission: Further examinations after admission revealed low-level elevation of myocardial enzymes that remained essentially stable on repeated testing, mild abnormalities of hepatic and renal function, marked leukocytosis, and mildly elevated baseline blood pressure. The clinical presentation comprised progressively worsening heart failure symptoms; discordant myocardial hypertrophy with low-voltage ECG findings; preserved ejection fraction; moderate pericardial effusion; hepatic and renal multi-organ dysfunction; persistent low-level troponin elevation; and markedly elevated BNP.

Past medical history: History of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease; history of bilateral renal stone surgery 3 years ago; history of tonsillectomy 8 years ago. Denies history of cardiovascular/cerebrovascular, renal, endocrine, and other major organ diseases, and denies history of infectious diseases. Denies history of trauma.  
Marital and reproductive history: No relevant records.

Menstrual history, personal history, family history: Denies history of blood transfusion. Allergy history: fish, shrimp and other aquatic foods; sulfonamides. Family history: no relevant records.

### Physical Examination
Vital signs: Temperature 36.3°C, pulse 120 bpm, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, blood pressure 133/69 mmHg, oxygen saturation 96% (with oxygen via nasal cannula at 2 L/min).  
General: No cyanosis of the lips; no large ecchymoses, pallor, or cyanosis of other skin and mucosa; no jugular venous distention; hepatojugular reflux negative.  
Chest examination: No trauma or herpes on the chest wall skin; no obvious tenderness; widened intercostal spaces; decreased breath sounds bilaterally; no dry or moist rales.  
Cardiac examination: No enlargement of cardiac dullness on percussion; heart rate 120 bpm, regular rhythm; no pathological murmurs in all valvular auscultation areas; distant heart sounds.  
Abdomen and extremities: Abdomen flat and soft; no tenderness, rebound tenderness, or muscle guarding; liver and spleen not palpable; mild edema of both lower extremities. Physiological reflexes present; pathological reflexes not elicited.

### Ancillary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission  
Laboratory tests:  
Cardiac enzyme markers: Troponin I (cTnI) 0.589 ng/mL (positive on multiple rechecks); myoglobin 145.28 ng/mL.  
Cardiac function marker: B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) 388.14 pg/mL.  
Hepatic and renal function: Creatinine (Cr) 112.63 μmol/L; alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 53.58 IU/L; aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 88.10 IU/L.  
Inflammatory marker: C-reactive protein (CRP) 247.78 mg/L.  
Hematology: White blood cell count (WBC) 17.24×10⁹/L; red blood cell count (RBC) 4.84×10¹²/L; hemoglobin (Hb) 151 g/L.  
Coagulation: D-dimer 0.75 mg/L.  
Screening tests: Respiratory tract specimen tests, T-cell test for tuberculosis infection, Mycoplasma, and G test were all negative; glucose metabolism, thyroid function tests, and rheumatology/immunology panel were negative.  
Tumor markers: CA125↑, CA153↑.

Electrocardiogram (ECG):  
Low voltage in limb leads (QRS amplitude <0.5 mV); poor R-wave progression in precordial leads, with premature ventricular complexes; sinus tachycardia (120 bpm).

Imaging studies:  
Chest CT: Scattered nodular and patchy consolidative exudative opacities in both lungs (predominantly in the right lower lung); multiple tiny nodules in both lungs; right apical pleural thickening with fibrotic strands; dependent changes in both lower lobes; emphysema in both lungs with a few small bullae; heart not enlarged, moderate pericardial effusion; calcification of the aortic valve and aortic wall.  
Coronary CTA: Coronary artery stenosis excluded.  
Bedside echocardiography: Marked interventricular septal thickening (16 mm); left ventricular posterior wall thickening (13 mm); ejection fraction (EF) 56%; moderate pericardial effusion.  
Bilateral lower extremity vascular ultrasound: Spontaneous echogenic contrast within the intramuscular veins of the soleus muscle.

2. Further examinations after admission (multimodality imaging and pathology)  
Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR):  
Morphologic changes: Symmetric left ventricular wall hypertrophy; right ventricular wall involvement (>4 mm); thickened interatrial septum; moderate pericardial effusion.  
Quantitative parameters: Native T1 1090 ms; extracellular volume fraction (ECV) 38%.  
Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE): Speckled/chalky-gray delayed enhancement; diffuse enhancement of the interventricular septum and lateral wall; early subendocardial enhancement; late transmural enhancement.

Hematologic tests:  
Immunofixation electrophoresis (IFE): Serum monoclonal Lambda light-chain band (positive); urinary Lambda light chain elevation (positive).  
Serum free light chain (sFLC) assay: Markedly increased Kappa (κ) free light chain; markedly increased Lambda (λ) free light chain, predominantly elevated; κ/λ ratio 0.01.  
Intact light chain assay: Normal.

Bone marrow examination:  
Bone marrow aspiration smear: Markedly increased plasma cells; plasma cell burden approximately 13%.  
Bone marrow biopsy: Increased abnormal plasma cells.  
Flow cytometry (FCM) immunophenotyping: Increased abnormal plasma cells; Lambda expression positive.

Radionuclide imaging:  
Technetium-99m pyrophosphate scintigraphy (99mTc-PYP): Increased cardiac uptake (Perugini grade 2–3); heart-to-contralateral lung ratio (H/CL): 1 hour >1.5, 3 hours >1.3.

Genetic testing:  
TTR gene analysis: No TTR gene mutation detected.

## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Response Format

### History Summary
Patient Information  
Sex: Male  
Age: 19 years  
Chief complaint: Recurrent cough with sputum and dyspnea for more than 1 month.

History of Present Illness:  
Onset and diagnosis/treatment at an outside hospital (1 month ago / March 2023):  
More than 1 month prior to admission, after catching a chill, the patient developed recurrent cough with sputum, accompanied by dyspnea, more obvious after activity, accompanied by diarrhea; no chills, no syncope or amaurosis, no hemoptysis, no nausea/vomiting, no limb movement disorder, no tidal fever, night sweats, etc.  
First hospitalization (March 21, 2023): Hospitalized at Chengdu Xindu District *** Hospital, diagnosed as “1. Acute myocarditis 2. Severe pneumonia 3. Massive bilateral pleural effusion 4. Right spontaneous chylothorax 5. Cardiac insufficiency 6. Aortic valve insufficiency 7. Mitral and tricuspid valve insufficiency 8. Pericardial effusion 9. Acute renal insufficiency”. Bilateral thoracentesis with catheter drainage was performed; piperacillin–tazobactam sodium + ciprofloxacin were given for anti-infective therapy; ambroxol for expectoration, pantoprazole for acid suppression and gastric protection, salbutamol + budesonide for antispasmodic and anti-asthmatic symptomatic treatment, after which the patient’s symptoms improved.  
Second hospitalization (March 29, 2023): Transferred to Chengdu University *** Affiliated Hospital for further diagnosis and treatment, diagnosed as “1. Sepsis 2. Respiratory failure 3. Heart failure 4. Severe pneumonia 5. Secondary pulmonary tuberculosis, sputum smear (-), initial treatment 6. Infective endocarditis 7. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection 8. Left ventricular apical mural mass (thrombus highly likely) 9. Left heart enlargement, mitral regurgitation (moderate), tricuspid regurgitation (mild), elevated pulmonary artery pressure (mild–moderate), pericardial effusion (small amount) 11. Splenomegaly 12. Bilateral pleural effusion 13. Venous hypertension 14. Severe malnutrition with emaciation, hypoproteinemia 15. Hyperuricemia 16. Hyperlipidemia 17. Mild anemia 18. Thrombocytopenia”. Treated with imipenem and moxifloxacin combined anti-infective therapy, Xuebijing, doxofylline and other symptomatic treatments, and enoxaparin for anticoagulation. TNGS suggested Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection; moxifloxacin anti-tuberculosis therapy was started on March 31, 2023. Considering infective endocarditis, vancomycin was added for anti-infective therapy.

This admission (April 4, 2023):  
The patient and family came to our hospital for further treatment.  
Disease progression after admission: During hospitalization, eosinophils continued to increase and were later considered a delayed allergic reaction to contrast medium. After multidisciplinary discussion, it was found that in addition to cardiac lesions, the patient also had systemic manifestations including increased joint range of motion, hyperextensible skin, scoliosis, and long slender fingers. Two months after discharge, the patient was re-admitted due to spontaneous pneumothorax.

Past Medical History: Generally healthy. Denies hepatitis, tuberculosis, or other infectious disease history. Immunization history unknown. No allergy history. No history of trauma. No surgical history. No history of blood transfusion. No other special medical history.  
Marital and Reproductive History: Unmarried, no children.

Menstrual History, Personal History, Family History: Non-smoker, does not drink alcohol, has not been to epidemic areas; no family history of cardiovascular disease.

### Physical Examination
Vital signs: T: 36.6°C, P: 109/min, R: 23/min, BP: 110/59 mmHg, HR: 109/min.  
General condition: Height 175 cm, weight 55 kg, BMI 17.96 kg/m2. No subcutaneous bleeding throughout the body. No enlarged superficial lymph nodes palpated. Multiple localized pigmentations were seen in the interdigital spaces. Rash was seen on the buttocks. Hyperelastic skin with high extensibility, relatively soft.  
Chest examination: No abnormality of thoracic cage. Decreased tactile fremitus in the right lower lung; normal tactile fremitus in the left lung. No pleural friction rub palpable. Decreased breath sounds in the right lower lobe. Moist rales were heard in the lungs; no obvious wheezing. Marked scoliosis.  
Cardiac examination: Hepatojugular reflux positive. Cardiac borders enlarged. Regular rhythm. No murmurs heard in any valve auscultation areas.  
Abdomen and extremities: No edema of extremities. Hyperextension of the finger joints and metacarpophalangeal joints; fingers in a “swan-neck” appearance; long slender fingers with reduced subcutaneous fat.

### Auxiliary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission  
Hematology:  
Platelets: 85×10⁹/L (mildly decreased)  
White blood cells: 16.13×10⁹/L (elevated)  
Absolute eosinophil count: 1.32×10⁹/L (elevated)

Biochemistry:  
Liver and renal function: normal  
Blood lipids: normal  
Calcium, magnesium: mildly decreased

Myocardial markers and cardiac function indices:  
NT-proBNP: 16,140 ng/L (markedly elevated)  
Troponin T: 166.0 ng/L (markedly elevated)  
Myoglobin: 21.61 ng/mL  
Creatine kinase-MB: 1.22 ng/mL

Other tests:  
Routine urinalysis, routine stool test, routine DIC panel, pre-transfusion panel: no obvious abnormalities.

Electrocardiogram:  
Sinus tachycardia, heart rate 109/min.  
Inferior leads (II, III, aVF): obvious abnormal Q waves.  
Anterior leads (V1–V6): abnormal Q waves with ST-segment elevation.

Transthoracic echocardiography (bedside):  
Left heart enlargement (LV 64 mm, LA 45 mm).  
Mural thrombus formation at the left ventricular apex.  
Thinning of the LV posterior wall; markedly reduced excursion of the lateral wall, posterior wall, inferior wall and apex.  
Moderate mitral regurgitation.  
Markedly reduced left ventricular systolic function (EF 32%).  
Normal right ventricular systolic function.

Chest CT:  
Scattered patchy, linear, and consolidation/atelectasis opacities in both lungs; thickening of some interlobular septa, suggesting infection with pulmonary edema.  
Pulmonary bulla in the left lower lobe.  
Moderate right pleural effusion.  
Splenomegaly with low-density nodules.  
Increased and enlarged mediastinal and bilateral hilar lymph nodes.

2. Further examinations after admission (multimodal imaging and pathology)  
Coronary angiography:  
Right coronary artery: occluded.  
Left main to left anterior descending artery: beaded and aneurysmal dilatation throughout.  
Mid-segment of the left anterior descending artery: thrombus present, distal occlusion.  
Left circumflex artery: occluded.

Contrast-enhanced cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR):  
Marked left heart enlargement; LV end-diastolic diameter 96.6 mm.  
LVEF: 22.7% (markedly reduced); RVEF: 52.0% (normal).  
Left ventricular apical thrombus formation; small amount of pericardial effusion.  
Late gadolinium enhancement: obvious enhancement in the LV free wall, mid-ventricle, apex, and mid-myocardial layer of the interventricular septum, suggesting ischemic changes are highly likely.

Extremity vascular examination:  
Extremity arterial Doppler ultrasound: segmental occlusion of the left radial artery; thickening of the distal right brachial artery with thrombus formation; decreased flow velocity in the right radial and ulnar arteries.  
Extremity vascular CTA: focal intraluminal filling defect of the right brachial artery with moderate-to-severe stenosis.

Pulmonary vascular CTA:  
Cardiomegaly, with prominent left ventricular enlargement; patchy filling defects within (thrombus possible).  
Distal LAD: coronary aneurysm formation and possible myocardial bridging.  
Main pulmonary artery slightly enlarged; four main pulmonary veins drain into the left atrium.  
Scattered patchy, linear, and consolidation/atelectasis opacities in both lungs; small bilateral pleural effusions.

Immunologic tests:  
IgE: 38,880 IU/mL (markedly elevated).  
CRP: 6.65 mg/L (mildly elevated).  
PCT, complement C3, IgG: normal or no obvious abnormalities.  
Rheumatoid factor, anti-ANA antibodies, anti-dsDNA antibodies, anti-ENA antibody profile, IgG4, ANCA: all negative.

Thrombosis-related tests:  
Thromboelastography (rapid TEG): clot formation rate parameter 175 sec.  
Protein C, protein S, lupus anticoagulant, antiphospholipid syndrome-related antibodies: no obvious abnormalities.

Special infection screening:  
TB infection T-cell test and PPD test: negative.  
Fungal G test and GM test: negative.  
Stool parasite microscopy, malaria antigen, Schistosoma IgG antibody, echinococcosis + fluke antibodies: negative.  
Respiratory pathogen nucleic acid tests: negative.

Hand imaging:  
DR (PA and oblique views) of both hands and CT of both hands: no bone destruction in the bones of both hands; fingers relatively long and slender with less subcutaneous fat; lateral views showed slight dorsal extension of some phalanges.

Other imaging:  
Chest X-ray: marked scoliosis.  
Aortic dissection CT: irregular filling defect at the LV apex (maximum thickness approximately 2.1 cm, thrombus formation possible); celiac trunk and its branches slightly dilated and tortuous.

Genetic testing:  
Whole-exome sequencing: heterozygous deletion in COL5A1 gene exon 9.

Physical examination: Hyperextension of interphalangeal joints and metacarpophalangeal joints, with fingers resembling a "swan-neck" deformity; scoliosis.

Ancillary investigations: Whole exome sequencing result: heterozygous deletion of exon 9 in the COL5A1 gene.

Ancillary investigations: Coronary angiography: occlusion of the right coronary artery; beaded and aneurysmal dilation throughout the left main artery to the left anterior descending artery (LAD); thrombus visible in the mid-segment of the LAD with distal occlusion; occlusion of the left circumflex artery.

Ancillary investigations: CT angiography of the extremity vessels: intraluminal filling defect in the right brachial artery with moderate-to-severe stenosis.
Ancillary investigations: Hand imaging: long slender fingers; lateral radiograph showing mild dorsal hyperextension of some phalanges; decreased subcutaneous fat.

Ancillary investigations: Pulmonary CT angiography: coronary artery aneurysm formation in the distal LAD with possible myocardial bridging.

Ancillary investigations: Cardiac biomarkers: Troponin-T 166.0 ng/L (markedly elevated); creatine kinase-MB isoenzyme 1.22 ng/mL.

Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Electrocardiogram: prominent pathological Q waves in inferior leads (II, III, aVF).

Ancillary investigations: Bilateral upper and lower extremity arterial ultrasonography: segmental occlusion of the left radial artery; dilation of the distal right brachial artery with thrombus formation.
Ancillary investigations: CT angiography of the extremity vessels: intraluminal filling defect in the right brachial artery with moderate-to-severe stenosis.

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Severe pneumonia
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Recurrent cough, expectoration, and dyspnea.

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Pleural effusion (history of chylothorax)
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Diagnosed at an outside hospital as '3. Bilateral large pleural effusion; 4. Right-sided spontaneous chylothorax.'

Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: The patient was readmitted for spontaneous pneumothorax 2 months after discharge.

Diagnostic_evidence: Physical examination: Scoliosis.

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Kawasaki disease
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Considered as the most common etiology of coronary artery aneurysmal dilation in children and adolescents
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Coronary angiography: beaded and aneurysmal dilation throughout the left main artery to the left anterior descending artery.
Non-supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Upon detailed history-taking, the patient denied any definitive history of Kawasaki disease.

Physical examination: Hyperextension of interphalangeal joints and metacarpophalangeal joints, with fingers resembling a "swan-neck" deformity, and scoliosis (Kawasaki disease cannot explain the multisystem involvement).

Ancillary investigations: On coronary angiography, the patient's aneurysms appeared pulsatile with non-thickened vessel walls (in classical Kawasaki disease, aneurysmal walls are typically thickened, with an eggshell-like appearance, and may be accompanied by calcification).

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Infective endocarditis
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Considered based on the outside hospital diagnosis and the patient's fever, cardiac murmur, and embolic manifestations
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Recurrent cough, expectoration, and dyspnea.
Physical examination: Moist rales (crackles) audible over the lungs.
Ancillary investigations: White blood cell count: 16.13 × 10⁹/L (elevated).
Ancillary investigations: Echocardiography: moderate mitral regurgitation; mural thrombus formation at the left ventricular apex.
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Late gadolinium enhancement cardiac MRI: significant enhancement in the left ventricular free wall, mid-segment, apex, and mid-layer of the interventricular septum, highly suggestive of ischemic changes (rather than inflammatory changes).

Ancillary investigations: Coronary angiography: occlusion of the right coronary artery; beaded and aneurysmal dilation throughout the left main artery to the LAD with mid-segment thrombus; occlusion of the left circumflex artery (myocardial ischemia and necrosis resulting from severe multivessel coronary artery disease, not consistent with simple bacterial endocarditis).

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Eosinophilic myocarditis (Loeffler endocarditis)
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Considered due to elevated eosinophil count accompanied by myocardial injury
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Absolute eosinophil count: 1.32 × 10⁹/L (elevated).
Ancillary investigations: IgE: 38,880 IU/mL (markedly elevated).
Ancillary investigations: Echocardiography: mural thrombus formation at the left ventricular apex.
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Cardiac MRI did not reveal characteristic fibrosis or endocardial thickening indicative of eosinophilic myocarditis.

Symptoms: The hematology department's opinion indicated that the degree of eosinophil elevation was not significantly high, and eosinophilia alone is insufficient to cause such severe coronary artery disease.

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Marfan syndrome
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Considered as a connective tissue disorder causing vascular pathology
Supporting_evidence: Physical examination: Long slender fingers (arachnodactyly).
Physical examination: Scoliosis.
Non-supporting_evidence: Symptoms: No family history of Marfan syndrome.

Physical examination: Aortic Z-score was −1.83, which did not meet the diagnostic threshold (≥2); no aortic root dissection or lens subluxation; systemic score was only 2 points (positive wrist sign and scoliosis), well below the diagnostic threshold (≥7 points).

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Takayasu arteritis
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Considered due to multivessel vascular disease in a young patient
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary investigations: Coronary angiography: occlusion of the right coronary artery and left circumflex artery; aneurysmal dilation of the left anterior descending artery.
Ancillary investigations: Bilateral upper and lower extremity arterial ultrasonography: segmental occlusion of the left radial artery.
Non-supporting_evidence: Physical examination: No characteristic vasculitis findings in the peripheral arteries.
Ancillary investigations: Immunological tests: anti-ANA antibody, anti-dsDNA antibody, anti-ENA antibody panel, IgG4, and ANCA: all negative.

Immunologic tests:  
IgE: 38,880 IU/mL (markedly elevated).  
CRP: 6.65 mg/L (mildly elevated).  
PCT, complement C3, IgG: normal or no obvious abnormalities.  
Rheumatoid factor, anti-ANA antibodies, anti-dsDNA antibodies, anti-ENA antibody profile, IgG4, ANCA: all negative.

Thrombosis-related tests:  
Thromboelastography (rapid TEG): clot formation rate parameter 175 sec.  
Protein C, protein S, lupus anticoagulant, antiphospholipid syndrome-related antibodies: no obvious abnormalities.

Special infection screening:  
TB infection T-cell test and PPD test: negative.  
Fungal G test and GM test: negative.  
Stool parasite microscopy, malaria antigen, Schistosoma IgG antibody, echinococcosis + fluke antibodies: negative.  
Respiratory pathogen nucleic acid tests: negative.

Hand imaging:  
DR (PA and oblique views) of both hands and CT of both hands: no bone destruction in the bones of both hands; fingers relatively long and slender with less subcutaneous fat; lateral views showed slight dorsal extension of some phalanges.

Other imaging:  
Chest X-ray: marked scoliosis.  
Aortic dissection CT: irregular filling defect at the LV apex (maximum thickness approximately 2.1 cm, thrombus formation possible); celiac trunk and its branches slightly dilated and tortuous.

Genetic testing:  
Whole-exome sequencing: heterozygous deletion in COL5A1 gene exon 9.
## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Response Format
- When generating the final answer: you must output the diagnostic results directly in Json format (example below), and do not output any other irrelevant content;

- {"Preliminary diagnosis": {"Primary diagnosis": {"Name": "XXX", "Etiologic subtype": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}, "Secondary diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}]}, "Differential diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Selection rationale": "Consider rare but potentially fatal diseases", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": [], "Excludable": true, "Recommended corresponding tests": []}, {"Name": "YYY", "Selection rationale": "Consider differential diagnosis of adjacent organ lesions", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1"], "Excludable": false, "Recommended corresponding tests": ["Recommendation 1", "Recommendation 2"]}]}

### Brief History of Present Illness
Patient Information  
Sex: Male  
Age: 20 years  
Chief complaint: Intermittent palpitations for half a year, worsened for 1 month

History of Present Illness:  
Three years ago, palpitations occurred after physical activity, accompanied by sweating, which could resolve spontaneously; however, the patient did not take it seriously.  
The patient reported that half a year ago, palpitations occurred after activity, lasting for several hours and then relieving, accompanied by sweating. He denied chest tightness, chest pain, abdominal pain, acid regurgitation, belching, nausea, and vomiting; no dizziness, headache, or syncope. He then visited a local hospital, where an electrocardiogram (ECG) showed atrial fibrillation. The patient did not take it seriously and did not undergo further diagnosis and treatment.  
One month ago, after an upper respiratory tract infection, the patient again developed palpitations; the symptoms persisted without relief. He visited a local hospital, where ECG showed atrial fibrillation. Echocardiography showed: LVEF 33%, LVED 65 mm, left heart enlargement, and diffuse reduction of left ventricular wall motion. The diagnosis was considered “atrial fibrillation, heart failure”. Symptomatic treatment such as correction of heart failure, ventricular rate control, and anticoagulation was given, but there was no significant improvement in symptoms.  
The patient was transferred to our hospital for further diagnosis and treatment and was admitted to our department with “cardiac insufficiency”. During the course of illness, the patient was conscious and clear, with poor spirits; sleep was acceptable; appetite was acceptable. He denied cough, sputum production, and hemoptysis; denied amaurosis, syncope, loss of consciousness, and urinary/fecal incontinence; denied tearing pain in the chest and back; denied abdominal pain, abdominal distension, nausea, and vomiting; denied diarrhea; denied bilateral lower-extremity edema. Urination and defecation were normal. There were no significant recent changes in body weight or physical strength.  
Disease progression after admission: Repeat echocardiography showed progression compared with before: ejection fraction further decreased to 15%, further enlargement of the left heart, and thickening of the interventricular septum and left ventricular wall to 10–13 mm. Electromyography revealed myogenic damage in the right deltoid and right biceps brachii. Muscle biopsy findings were consistent with the pathological changes of glycogen storage disease and desminopathy.

Past Medical History: Generally in good health. Denied hypertension; denied diabetes mellitus; denied history of cerebrovascular disease. No history of hepatitis or tuberculosis. Denied history of surgery, trauma, or blood transfusion. Denied history of food or drug allergy. Immunization history unknown.  
Marital and Reproductive History: Unmarried.

Menstrual History, Personal History, Family History: Junior high school education; self-employed. No history of drug abuse. Occasional history of smoking and alcohol use; currently has quit smoking and alcohol. No travel history. Parents are healthy; there are 5 siblings, all healthy. Denied family history of hereditary diseases.

### Physical Examination
Temperature 36.4°C, pulse 110 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, blood pressure 132/84 mmHg, height 168 cm, weight 52 kg, BMI 18.42 kg/m2.  
Respiratory movements were normal; breath sounds were clear; no dry or moist rales were heard. No precordial bulge. The point of maximal impulse was located 1 cm lateral to the left midclavicular line in the 5th intercostal space. The relative cardiac dullness border was enlarged. Heart rate 134 beats/min; rhythm was absolutely irregular. S1 varied in intensity. No cardiac murmurs were heard in any valvular auscultation areas. No extra heart sounds, no gallop rhythm, and no pericardial friction rub were heard. Abdomen was flat; no visible gastric peristaltic waves or intestinal pattern; no peristaltic waves; no abdominal wall varices; no surgical scars; no abdominal muscle guarding. No tenderness throughout the abdomen; no rebound tenderness. No fluid wave thrill; no succussion splash. No palpable abdominal mass; shifting dullness (-). No percussion tenderness over bilateral renal regions. Bowel sounds were normal; no vascular bruits were heard. No obvious abnormalities were found on neurological examination.

### Auxiliary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission  
Electrocardiogram (ECG): Atrial fibrillation, atrioventricular conduction block, ST-segment and T-wave abnormalities.  
Echocardiography: LVEF 15–33% (severely reduced ejection fraction); LVED 65 mm (increased left ventricular end-diastolic diameter); left heart enlargement; diffuse reduction of left ventricular wall motion. Re-examination (January 21): thickening of interventricular septum and left ventricular wall (10–13 mm).  
Hematologic tests:  
Biochemistry: Total bilirubin 32.1 μmol/L (elevated); direct bilirubin 6.83 μmol/L (elevated); aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 457.2 U/L (elevated); alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 609.6 U/L (elevated); lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) 1586 U/L (markedly elevated).  
Cardiac biomarkers: Creatine kinase (CK) 1287.2 IU/L (significantly elevated); creatine kinase-MB (CK-MB) 17.45 U/L; high-sensitivity troponin T 0.13 ng/L (within normal range).  
Other indices: N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP) 848 ng/L (elevated); D-dimer 324 ng/mL; C-reactive protein 5.336 mg/L.  
Complete blood count: White blood cells 7.84×10⁹/L (normal); hemoglobin 157 g/L (normal); platelets 311×10⁹/L (normal).  
Renal function and electrolytes: Potassium 4.06 mmol/L (normal); sodium 138.00 mmol/L (normal); creatinine 74.00 μmol/L (normal); uric acid 383.40 μmol/L (normal).  
Coagulation function: Normal coagulation function.

2. Further examinations after admission (multimodality imaging and pathology)  
Imaging examinations:  
Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR): Subendocardial myocardial injury (characteristic finding); EF 14%; myocardial injury of the left ventricular septum and left ventricular wall; myocardial injury of both atria.  
Coronary CT: Completely normal (coronary artery disease excluded).  
Electromyography: Right deltoid: myogenic damage; right biceps brachii: myogenic damage.  
Pathological examinations:  
Muscle biopsy: Findings consistent with the pathological changes of glycogen storage disease and desminopathy.  
Endomyocardial biopsy: Consider myocardial glycogen storage lesion; pathology showed glycogen granule deposition (characteristic finding).  
Genetic testing: Positive for LAMP2 gene mutation.  
Other special examinations: Serum immunofixation electrophoresis (to exclude amyloidosis); antinuclear antibody profile (to exclude autoimmune disease); respiratory virus panel of eight items (to exclude viral myocarditis); alpha-galactosidase A activity assay (to exclude Anderson–Fabry disease); blood lactate and pyruvate testing (to exclude mitochondrial myopathy).

### Brief History of Present Illness
The patient is a 64-year-old female who was admitted in 2022-12 due to “shortness of breath for 1 year, recurrence for 1 week”.

History of present illness: The patient developed chest tightness and shortness of breath 1 year prior to admission, which were more obvious at night; dry cough was pronounced in the supine position, accompanied by bilateral lower-extremity edema. She presented to a local hospital and was diagnosed with “hypertrophic cardiomyopathy” and “heart failure”. Symptoms improved after diuretic therapy and other treatments, but recurred repeatedly. One week prior, she visited our hospital; chest CT suggested pleural effusion and pericardial effusion, and she was diagnosed with “heart failure” and “pulmonary infection”. She underwent pleural catheter drainage, and improved after diuresis, anti-infective therapy, and other treatments; she was able to lie supine, but experienced shortness of breath with slight activity. She was admitted for further clarification of the etiology of heart failure. Since onset, the patient has had fatigue, decreased appetite, decreased urine output, and body weight was not monitored.

Past history: The patient developed numbness in both hands 10 years ago, with gradual worsening thereafter. Numbness in the lower extremities occurred 2 years ago; she visited the Department of Neurology and was treated with “mecobalamin”, among other measures. Five years ago, she presented to our hospital’s Department of Gastroenterology for “recurrent nausea and vomiting” and was diagnosed with “neurogenic vomiting”. She improved after taking “flupentixol and melitracen tablets” on a regular basis.

Family history: The patient’s mother and elder sister died of “heart failure”.

### Physical Examination
Temperature 36.8°C, pulse 77 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, blood pressure 102/71 mmHg, oxygen saturation 97% (oxygen concentration 33%). Conscious and alert; no cyanosis of the lips. A small amount of moist rales were heard in both lower lungs; no pleural friction rub was heard. Pleural puncture drainage tubes were visible in place and patent below the inferior angles of both scapulae; the drainage fluid was light yellow. Heart rate 77 beats/min, regular rhythm; no obvious murmurs were heard in the auscultatory areas of the cardiac valves. Abdomen soft, without tenderness or rebound tenderness; bowel sounds normal. Mild edema of both lower extremities. Pathological reflexes were not elicited.

### Auxiliary Examinations
Complete blood count, liver and kidney function tests, and myocardial enzymes showed no significant abnormalities. NT-proBNP 9877 pg/mL; high-sensitivity troponin 0.15 μg/L (reference: <0.1 μg/L). Serum and urine immunofixation electrophoresis and serum free light chain testing showed no abnormalities.

Electrocardiogram: low voltage in limb leads; poor R-wave progression in precordial leads.

Echocardiography: enlarged left and right atria; thickened left ventricular wall (interventricular septum 20 mm, left ventricular posterior wall 13 mm) with heterogeneous myocardial echogenicity; small amount of pericardial effusion; impaired left ventricular diastolic function; E/E’ 21.7; EF 60%.

CT: non-contrast chest CT suggested bilateral pulmonary inflammation and a small amount of pleural effusion; coronary CTA showed no significant coronary artery stenosis.

Myocardial radionuclide <sup>99m</sup>Tc-PYP scintigraphy: positive; heart-to-contralateral lung (H/CL) uptake ratio 2.18.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging suggested marked thickening of the ventricular walls, diffuse LGE enhancement in both left and right ventricles, and pericardial effusion.

TTR gene whole-exome sequencing revealed a heterozygous pathogenic variant c.349G>T (guanine>thymine) detected in exon 4. This variant is an autosomal dominant missense mutation, resulting in an amino acid change at position 117: p.Ala117Ser (alanine>serine). Genetic screening of the patient’s relatives showed that both of the patient’s two nieces carry this pathogenic gene.

## Medical History Information
Patient Information  
Sex: Male  
Age: 52 years  
Chief complaint: Intermittent palpitations and chest tightness for 11 months, worsened for 1 month  

Present illness:
Initial onset and first visit (11 months ago):  
11 months ago, the patient developed cardiac weakness without obvious precipitating factors, accompanied by dizziness, chest tightness, shortness of breath, and fatigue.  
First hospitalization: 24-hour ambulatory ECG indicated frequent premature ventricular contractions, paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia, and complete right bundle branch block. Echocardiography at an outside hospital suggested LVEF 60%. Further cardiac MRI showed abnormal signal enhancement in the interventricular septum with late gadolinium enhancement; at that time, a cardiac space-occupying lesion (hemangioma?) or arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) was considered.

At the outside hospital, the diagnosis was “multifocal premature ventricular contractions,” and catheter-based RF ablation was performed. Discharge medications: bisoprolol 5 mg QD PO, amiodarone 0.2 g QD PO.

Recurrence and progression (7 months ago):  
Symptoms were not completely relieved after the procedure; 7 months ago, the patient again developed palpitations and chest tightness.  
Second hospitalization: Repeat cardiac MRI showed that the space-occupying lesion in the right ventricular interventricular septum persisted, and late gadolinium imaging showed markedly increased enhancement on the right ventricular side of the interventricular septum compared with before, suggesting lesion progression. PET-CT indicated increased metabolic uptake in the interventricular septum.

This admission (from 1 month ago to present):  
Over the past month, chest tightness worsened compared with before, and the patient was admitted for further diagnosis and treatment.  
Disease course after admission: The patient had recurrent episodes of ventricular tachycardia; transesophageal pacing induced VT at a rate of approximately 150 beats/min, with symptoms consistent with usual episodes. Combined with the inflammatory metabolic activity and expanded lesion extent suggested by multimodal imaging (CMR, PET-CT, D-SPECT), a systemic or granulomatous disease was highly suspected clinically.

Past medical history: No special history.  
Marital and reproductive history: No relevant records.

Menstrual history, personal history, family history: No relevant records.

### Physical Examination
Vital signs: T 36.4°C, P 65 bpm, RR 19/min, BP 132/90 mmHg.  
General condition: Alert, in fair spirits; no obvious injury to skin or mucosa; no obvious enlargement of superficial lymph nodes throughout the body.  
Chest examination: Thorax symmetrically rising and falling; breathing unlabored; breath sounds clear in both lungs; no obvious dry or moist rales.  
Cardiac examination: No obvious precordial bulging or retraction; cardiac borders normal; irregular rhythm; no obvious pathological murmurs or pericardial friction rub.  
Abdomen and extremities: Abdomen flat; soft; no scars; no palpable mass; no tenderness, rebound tenderness, or muscle guarding; liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin; bowel sounds 4/min; no obvious edema of both lower extremities.

### Auxiliary Examinations
1. Routine admission examinations  
Laboratory tests:  
Biochemistry: Mildly elevated creatinine; mildly abnormal blood glucose; glycated hemoglobin 7%.  
Myocardial injury markers: Mildly elevated troponin.  
Inflammation and immunity: C-reactive protein, ESR, and autoantibodies all negative; immunoglobulins and complement showed no abnormalities; anticardiolipin antibody negative.  
Tumor markers: CEA 6.4 ng/mL (mildly elevated); other tumor markers without obvious abnormalities.

Electrocardiogram (ECG):  
Standard ECG: Complete right bundle branch block, QRS duration 150 ms, QTc interval 429 ms, with secondary ST-T changes.  
Holter: Sinus rhythm, average heart rate 57 beats/min; frequent premature ventricular contractions (92 beats) and paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia recorded.

Routine transthoracic echocardiography (TTE): LVEF 60%; cardiac structure basically normal; no obvious regional wall motion abnormality or left atrial/left ventricular enlargement.  
Abnormal finding: A filling defect area was seen in the left ventricular cavity near the interventricular septum (at initial diagnosis, it was once considered a normal chordae tendineae).

Coronary angiography (outside hospital): Mild stenosis of the right coronary artery; no obvious overall ischemic lesions; negative result.

2. Further examinations after admission (multimodal imaging and pathology)  
Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR - follow-up with contrast):  
Imaging features: An obvious protruding tissue shadow was present in the right ventricular interventricular septum.  
Dynamic changes: Compared with the previous imaging, this examination showed a markedly expanded range of late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) on the right ventricular side of the interventricular septum; T1 mapping and ECV sequences showed significantly increased inflammatory metabolism in the interventricular septum, suggesting significant inflammatory activity.

Metabolic imaging examinations:  
FDG-PET CT: A strip-like abnormally increased metabolic focus was present from the interventricular septum to the right ventricular apical region, with markedly increased FDG uptake; no abnormally increased metabolism was seen in other organs (lungs, liver, kidneys), suggesting the lesion was confined to the heart.  
D-SPECT (myocardial perfusion): Myocardial perfusion in the mid and basal interventricular septum was significantly decreased, accounting for approximately 8% of the total left ventricular area.

Contrast-enhanced chest CT: Multiple solid and ground-glass nodules in both lungs; bilateral maxillary sinusitis; reactive hyperplastic lymph nodes in both axillae; cyst at the lower pole of the left kidney; degenerative spinal disease.

Electrophysiological study: Transesophageal pacing induced ventricular tachycardia (150 bpm), confirming VT recurrence.

Myocardial biopsy (confirmatory examination):  
Sampling: Under transthoracic echocardiography guidance, multi-site sampling was performed at high, mid, and low levels of the right ventricular interventricular septum.  
Pathology results: Langhans-type multinucleated giant cells were observed microscopically; HE staining showed noncaseating epithelioid granulomas.

Primary_diagnosis.Name: Isolated cardiac sarcoidosis
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Intermittent palpitations and chest tightness for 11 months, with worsening over the past 1 month. Eleven months ago, the patient developed cardiac weakness without obvious precipitating factors, accompanied by dizziness, chest tightness, dyspnea, and fatigue. Seven months ago, the patient experienced recurrence of palpitations with a sense of alarm and chest tightness. Chest tightness has worsened over the past 1 month compared to before.
Physical examination: Irregular heart rhythm.
Ancillary examination: 24-hour Holter monitoring revealed frequent premature ventricular complexes (PVCs), paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia, and complete right bundle branch block.

Ancillary examination: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) demonstrated abnormal signal enhancement in the interventricular septum with late gadolinium enhancement (LGE). Cardiac MRI showed a prominently protruding tissue mass in the right ventricular interventricular septum across different imaging sequences, with mild delayed enhancement (LGE) visible on gadolinium imaging. Late gadolinium imaging indicated that the enhancement near the right ventricular surface of the interventricular septum had significantly increased compared to previous examinations, suggesting disease progression. Quantitative analysis, including T1 mapping and extracellular volume (ECV) sequences, revealed significantly increased inflammatory metabolism in the interventricular septum.

Ancillary examination: FDG-PET/CT: Whole-body PET/CT demonstrated a strip-shaped area of abnormally increased metabolism extending from the interventricular septum to the right ventricular apex, with significantly enhanced FDG uptake. No abnormal FDG uptake was identified in other organs throughout the body, including the lungs, liver, and kidneys.

Ancillary examination: D-SPECT examination revealed significantly reduced myocardial perfusion in the mid and basal segments of the interventricular septum, accounting for approximately 8% of the total left ventricular area.
Ancillary examination: Transesophageal pacing electrophysiology study induced ventricular tachycardia at a rate of approximately 150 beats per minute.

Ancillary examination: Endomyocardial biopsy (confirmatory test): Microscopic examination revealed Langhans-type multinucleated giant cells, with multiple nuclei observed at the periphery of the giant cells. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining demonstrated non-caseating epithelioid granulomas composed of multinucleated giant cells surrounded by lymphocytes and epithelioid cells.

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Cardiac arrhythmia (frequent premature ventricular complexes, paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia)
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Intermittent palpitations and chest tightness for 11 months, with worsening over the past 1 month. Palpitations with a sense of alarm.
Physical examination: Irregular heart rhythm.

Ancillary examination: 24-hour Holter monitoring revealed frequent premature ventricular complexes (PVCs) and paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia. The 24-hour Holter recording documented a total of 80,808 heartbeats, an average heart rate of 57 beats per minute, 92 premature ventricular complexes, and episodes of frequent PVCs and paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia.
Ancillary examination: Transesophageal pacing electrophysiology study induced ventricular tachycardia at a rate of approximately 150 beats per minute.

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Complete right bundle branch block
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Standard 12-lead electrocardiogram demonstrated complete right bundle branch block with a QRS duration of 150 milliseconds.

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Bilateral maxillary sinusitis
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: FDG-PET/CT report noted bilateral maxillary sinusitis.
Ancillary examination: Contrast-enhanced chest CT demonstrated bilateral maxillary sinusitis.

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Scattered focal emphysema in bilateral upper lobes
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: FDG-PET/CT report noted scattered focal emphysema in the bilateral upper lobes.

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Cyst of the lower pole of the left kidney
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Contrast-enhanced chest CT demonstrated a cyst in the lower pole of the left kidney.

Secondary_diagnoses.Name: Degenerative spinal disease
Diagnostic_evidence: Ancillary examination: Contrast-enhanced chest CT demonstrated degenerative spinal disease.

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Ischemic heart disease (coronary artery disease)
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Consideration of common and frequently occurring diseases
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Intermittent palpitations and chest tightness for 11 months, with worsening over the past 1 month.
Ancillary examination: D-SPECT examination revealed significantly reduced myocardial perfusion in the mid and basal segments of the interventricular septum.
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Coronary angiography (performed at an outside institution) revealed mild stenosis of the right coronary artery, with no significant ischemic lesions overall; the angiographic result was negative.

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC)
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Differentiation from diseases sharing common features or involving adjacent anatomical structures
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Intermittent palpitations and chest tightness.
Ancillary examination: 24-hour Holter monitoring revealed frequent premature ventricular complexes (PVCs) and paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia.
Ancillary examination: Cardiac MRI showed a prominently protruding tissue mass in the right ventricular interventricular septum across different imaging sequences.
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Echocardiography performed at an outside institution showed an LVEF of 60%. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed no significant wall motion abnormalities or left atrial or left ventricular enlargement, with a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 60% and essentially normal cardiac structure.
Ancillary examination: Endomyocardial biopsy pathology revealed non-caseating epithelioid granulomas.

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Cardiac tumor (primary or metastatic)
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Consideration of diseases potentially leading to serious consequences
Supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Cardiac MRI showed a prominently protruding tissue mass in the right ventricular interventricular septum across different imaging sequences.

Ancillary examination: FDG-PET/CT demonstrated a strip-shaped area of abnormally increased metabolism extending from the interventricular septum to the right ventricular apex, with significantly enhanced FDG uptake.
Non-supporting_evidence: Ancillary examination: Whole-body PET/CT showed no abnormal FDG uptake in other organs throughout the body, including the lungs, liver, and kidneys.
Ancillary examination: Regarding tumor markers, CEA was 6.4 ng/mL (mildly elevated), with no significant abnormalities in other tumor markers.
Ancillary examination: Endomyocardial biopsy pathology revealed non-caseating epithelioid granulomas.

Differential_diagnoses.Name: Sequelae of myocarditis
Differential_diagnoses.Selection_rationale: Consideration of common and frequently occurring diseases
Supporting_evidence: Symptoms: Palpitations and chest tightness.
Ancillary examination: Mildly elevated troponin levels.
Ancillary examination: Cardiac MRI demonstrated abnormal signal enhancement in the interventricular septum with late gadolinium enhancement (LGE).
Non-supporting_evidence: Symptoms: No definite prior history of viral infection or fever.
Ancillary examination: C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and autoantibodies were all negative.

Ancillary examination: The distribution pattern of LGE on cardiac MRI was not consistent with the typical scar pattern seen after myocarditis (as noted in the original text: "the LGE distribution on cardiac MRI was not consistent with the typical post-myocarditis scar pattern").
Ancillary examination: Endomyocardial biopsy pathology revealed non-caseating epithelioid granulomas.

## Medical History Information
Patient Information  
Sex: Male  
Age: 52 years  
Chief complaint: Intermittent palpitations and chest tightness for 11 months, worsened for 1 month  

Present illness:
Initial onset and first visit (11 months ago):  
11 months ago, the patient developed cardiac weakness without obvious precipitating factors, accompanied by dizziness, chest tightness, shortness of breath, and fatigue.  
First hospitalization: 24-hour ambulatory ECG indicated frequent premature ventricular contractions, paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia, and complete right bundle branch block. Echocardiography at an outside hospital suggested LVEF 60%. Further cardiac MRI showed abnormal signal enhancement in the interventricular septum with late gadolinium enhancement; at that time, a cardiac space-occupying lesion (hemangioma?) or arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) was considered.

At the outside hospital, the diagnosis was “multifocal premature ventricular contractions,” and catheter-based RF ablation was performed. Discharge medications: bisoprolol 5 mg QD PO, amiodarone 0.2 g QD PO.

Recurrence and progression (7 months ago):  
Symptoms were not completely relieved after the procedure; 7 months ago, the patient again developed palpitations and chest tightness.  
Second hospitalization: Repeat cardiac MRI showed that the space-occupying lesion in the right ventricular interventricular septum persisted, and late gadolinium imaging showed markedly increased enhancement on the right ventricular side of the interventricular septum compared with before, suggesting lesion progression. PET-CT indicated increased metabolic uptake in the interventricular septum.

This admission (from 1 month ago to present):  
Over the past month, chest tightness worsened compared with before, and the patient was admitted for further diagnosis and treatment.  
Disease course after admission: The patient had recurrent episodes of ventricular tachycardia; transesophageal pacing induced VT at a rate of approximately 150 beats/min, with symptoms consistent with usual episodes. Combined with the inflammatory metabolic activity and expanded lesion extent suggested by multimodal imaging (CMR, PET-CT, D-SPECT), a systemic or granulomatous disease was highly suspected clinically.

Past medical history: No special history.  
Marital and reproductive history: No relevant records.

Menstrual history, personal history, family history: No relevant records.

### Physical Examination
Vital signs: T 36.4°C, P 65 bpm, RR 19/min, BP 132/90 mmHg.  
General condition: Alert, in fair spirits; no obvious injury to skin or mucosa; no obvious enlargement of superficial lymph nodes throughout the body.  
Chest examination: Thorax symmetrically rising and falling; breathing unlabored; breath sounds clear in both lungs; no obvious dry or moist rales.  
Cardiac examination: No obvious precordial bulging or retraction; cardiac borders normal; irregular rhythm; no obvious pathological murmurs or pericardial friction rub.  
Abdomen and extremities: Abdomen flat; soft; no scars; no palpable mass; no tenderness, rebound tenderness, or muscle guarding; liver and spleen not palpable below the costal margin; bowel sounds 4/min; no obvious edema of both lower extremities.

### Auxiliary Examinations
1. Routine admission examinations  
Laboratory tests:  
Biochemistry: Mildly elevated creatinine; mildly abnormal blood glucose; glycated hemoglobin 7%.  
Myocardial injury markers: Mildly elevated troponin.  
Inflammation and immunity: C-reactive protein, ESR, and autoantibodies all negative; immunoglobulins and complement showed no abnormalities; anticardiolipin antibody negative.  
Tumor markers: CEA 6.4 ng/mL (mildly elevated); other tumor markers without obvious abnormalities.

Electrocardiogram (ECG):  
Standard ECG: Complete right bundle branch block, QRS duration 150 ms, QTc interval 429 ms, with secondary ST-T changes.  
Holter: Sinus rhythm, average heart rate 57 beats/min; frequent premature ventricular contractions (92 beats) and paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia recorded.

Routine transthoracic echocardiography (TTE): LVEF 60%; cardiac structure basically normal; no obvious regional wall motion abnormality or left atrial/left ventricular enlargement.  
Abnormal finding: A filling defect area was seen in the left ventricular cavity near the interventricular septum (at initial diagnosis, it was once considered a normal chordae tendineae).

Coronary angiography (outside hospital): Mild stenosis of the right coronary artery; no obvious overall ischemic lesions; negative result.

2. Further examinations after admission (multimodal imaging and pathology)  
Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR - follow-up with contrast):  
Imaging features: An obvious protruding tissue shadow was present in the right ventricular interventricular septum.  
Dynamic changes: Compared with the previous imaging, this examination showed a markedly expanded range of late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) on the right ventricular side of the interventricular septum; T1 mapping and ECV sequences showed significantly increased inflammatory metabolism in the interventricular septum, suggesting significant inflammatory activity.

Metabolic imaging examinations:  
FDG-PET CT: A strip-like abnormally increased metabolic focus was present from the interventricular septum to the right ventricular apical region, with markedly increased FDG uptake; no abnormally increased metabolism was seen in other organs (lungs, liver, kidneys), suggesting the lesion was confined to the heart.  
D-SPECT (myocardial perfusion): Myocardial perfusion in the mid and basal interventricular septum was significantly decreased, accounting for approximately 8% of the total left ventricular area.

Contrast-enhanced chest CT: Multiple solid and ground-glass nodules in both lungs; bilateral maxillary sinusitis; reactive hyperplastic lymph nodes in both axillae; cyst at the lower pole of the left kidney; degenerative spinal disease.

Electrophysiological study: Transesophageal pacing induced ventricular tachycardia (150 bpm), confirming VT recurrence.

Myocardial biopsy (confirmatory examination):  
Sampling: Under transthoracic echocardiography guidance, multi-site sampling was performed at high, mid, and low levels of the right ventricular interventricular septum.  
Pathology results: Langhans-type multinucleated giant cells were observed microscopically; HE staining showed noncaseating epithelioid granulomas.
## Task Description
- Given the patient information, please complete the clinical diagnostic process according to **clinical medical reasoning** (including primary and secondary diagnoses, and differential diagnosis).
- You need to focus on analyzing all of the patient’s negative and positive features, including all physical examination and ancillary test results, summarize the corresponding diagnostic evidence, then dynamically integrate the chain of evidence, and provide specific diagnostic conclusions through clinical reasoning.

## **Requirements for Diagnostic Evidence**:
  - **Completeness**: Ensure that all evidence relevant to the current diagnosis is listed, and **bold** the evidence with **high specificity**, marking it **separately**;
  - **Consistency**: Use the original wording as much as possible for all evidence descriptions; avoid modification, deletion, or summarization; fabrication of factual evidence is prohibited under any circumstances;
  - **Order**: Strictly classify and output evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**, and indicate the specific category; organize in order to make the results clearer;
    - Example: ["History: xxxx", "Physical Examination: xxxxxxx"];
    - Only the above three categories are allowed; do not use other fields;
  - **Evidence for Differential Diagnosis**: For differential diagnoses, list supporting and non-supporting evidence, summarized into the corresponding fields as follows:
    - **Supporting evidence**: Classify evidence by **History, Physical Examination, Ancillary Tests**; for related evidence, provide a combined description (e.g., signs + imaging features);
    - **Non-supporting evidence**:
      - Classify as “absolute exclusion evidence” and “evidence pending verification”; “evidence pending verification” must be labeled, example: ["Evidence pending verification (History): xxxx"];
      - Must consider and explain the limitations of negative results (e.g., insufficient test sensitivity);
    - Key tests that have not been completed must be labeled as “tests not completed”;
    - If there is currently no non-supporting evidence, output an empty list ('[]') is allowed;

## Clinical Medical Reasoning
### Primary Diagnosis
- Directly points to the root cause of the patient’s current clinical manifestations; use standard ICD-10 disease names;
- Etiologic subtype: Based on the primary diagnosis, further refine the subtype characteristics of the disease;

### Secondary Diagnoses
- Generally refer to one or more diseases in the patient’s past history, with relatively less harm to the patient’s health, and that do not occupy a major position during treatment;

### Differential Diagnosis
- Based on the preliminary diagnosis, consider other diseases with symptoms and signs similar to the patient’s, and rule out these possibilities through further examinations and assessments;
- Requires divergent and reverse thinking, able to infer possible related diseases from the patient’s clinical manifestations;
- A good differential diagnosis helps improve diagnostic accuracy and **reduce misdiagnosis and missed diagnosis**;

### Basis for Selecting Differential Diagnoses (According to Clinical Reasoning Methods)
  - Differentiate from diseases that share similarities with this disease and are in adjacent anatomical locations;
  - Consider common and frequently occurring diseases;
  - Consider diseases that may lead to serious consequences: diseases that may cause severe outcomes if not diagnosed and treated promptly, to ensure life-threatening conditions are not missed;
  - When necessary, consider regional or seasonal diseases (e.g., influenza);
  - If some secondary diagnoses may affect the differential diagnosis: determine their possible impact on comorbid symptoms (e.g., infection), disease manifestations and progression, and the differential process;
  - **If there is already clear non-supporting evidence**, the current diagnosis to be differentiated should be directly excluded to avoid an ineffective differential diagnosis process and output;

## Response Format
- When generating the final answer: you must output the diagnostic results directly in Json format (example below), and do not output any other irrelevant content;

- {"Preliminary diagnosis": {"Primary diagnosis": {"Name": "XXX", "Etiologic subtype": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}, "Secondary diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Diagnostic evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"]}]}, "Differential diagnoses": [{"Name": "XXX", "Selection rationale": "Consider rare but potentially fatal diseases", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": [], "Excludable": true, "Recommended corresponding tests": []}, {"Name": "YYY", "Selection rationale": "Consider differential diagnosis of adjacent organ lesions", "Supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1", "Evidence 2"], "Non-supporting evidence": ["Evidence 1"], "Excludable": false, "Recommended corresponding tests": ["Recommendation 1", "Recommendation 2"]}]}

### Brief History of Present Illness
Patient Information  
Sex: Female  
Age: 56 years  
Chief complaint: Intermittent chest tightness and shortness of breath for more than 5 years, aggravated for 3 days.

History of present illness:  
Initial onset and first medical consultation (>5 years ago / 2019):  
The patient developed chest tightness and shortness of breath during fast walking, which could be relieved after rest. There was no nocturnal dyspnea or chest pain. This was accompanied by progressive decrease in exercise tolerance; she experienced obvious shortness of breath after walking 100 meters. No diagnosis or treatment was given.

Current first admission (3 days ago / June 2024):  
Three days ago, the patient experienced left-sided back pain while doing housework, accompanied by chest tightness and shortness of breath, with a crushing sensation in the precordial area. Chest tightness improved after rest; the remaining symptoms were the same as before. Electrocardiogram at an outside hospital showed sinus rhythm and ST-segment depression of 0.05–0.1 mV in leads V4–V6; echocardiography showed LVEF 41%, left heart enlargement, mild tricuspid and mitral regurgitation, and decreased left heart function.

Recurrence and progression (post-discharge follow-up / September–December 2024):  
After discharge, the patient had multiple outpatient follow-ups. Hematologic tests repeated in September and December 2024 showed mildly elevated NT-proBNP. Ambulatory ECG monitoring suggested first-degree AV block, intermittent second-degree type I AV block, 1,633 premature ventricular contractions over 24 hours, and no sustained ventricular tachycardia. Echocardiography in September showed left ventricular ejection fraction <40%, suggesting continued deterioration of cardiac function.

Second hospitalization (January 2, 2025):  
The patient was rehospitalized due to worsening dyspnea accompanied by fever for 2 days.

Past medical history: Bronchiectasis with recurrent infections for more than 5 years. Status post cesarean section. Denies history of infectious diseases such as hepatitis and tuberculosis; denies history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, cerebrovascular disease, and psychiatric disorders; denies history of trauma and blood transfusion. Allergic to penicillin and sulfonamides.  
Marital and reproductive history: Married at appropriate age; has one son; husband and son are healthy.

Menstrual history, personal history, family history:  
Personal history: Long-term residence locally; denies exposure to chemical substances, radioactive substances, or toxic substances; denies smoking and alcohol abuse.  
Family history: Mother had coronary heart disease. Father died suddenly at age 78 and mother died suddenly at age 70; causes of death unknown. Two older brothers (in their 60s) and one older sister (at age 57) underwent pacemaker implantation. The patient also has one older sister who underwent ICD implantation due to ventricular dilation and heart failure and has records of 3 shocks; her second older brother died suddenly this year at age 62. Denies other familial hereditary diseases.

### Physical Examination
Vital signs: T 36.2°C, P 89/min, R 18/min, BP 133/81 mmHg.  
General condition: Height 168 cm, weight 80 kg, BMI 28.3, BSA 1.95 m². No enlargement of superficial lymph nodes; no jugular venous distention; hepatojugular reflux negative.  
Chest examination: No dry or moist rales heard in either lung; no pleural friction rub.  
Cardiac examination: Heart rate 89/min, regular rhythm; no pathological murmur heard in any valvular auscultation area; cardiac dullness percussed enlarged to the left.  
Abdomen and extremities: No edema in both lower extremities.  
Physical signs at the second hospitalization (January 2025): Body temperature 38.1°C; breath sounds loud in both lungs, with a small amount of moist rales bilaterally; heart rate 84/min with regular rhythm; cardiac dullness enlarged to the left; mild edema in both lower extremities.

### Ancillary Examinations
1. Routine examinations on admission  
Laboratory tests:  
Complete blood count: Essentially normal.  
Biochemical indices: Liver and renal function and electrolytes normal; creatine kinase (CK) within normal range.  
Markers of myocardial injury: Troponin I 5.9 pg/mL; B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) 48.62 pg/mL; N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP) mildly elevated.  
Immunologic tests: Autoantibody profile (ANA, ENA) negative; ANCA negative; ESR 23 mm/hr; blood catecholamines and serum free light chains negative.

Electrocardiogram (ECG):  
Sinus rhythm; first-degree atrioventricular block (AVB); low-amplitude and inverted T waves in leads V3–V6; ST-segment depression of 0.05–0.1 mV.

Imaging examinations:  
Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE): Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) 42%, mildly reduced; left ventricular end-diastolic diameter (LVEDD) 56 mm, left heart enlargement; left ventricular wall thickness 9 mm; mild mitral regurgitation.  
Chest X-ray: Prominent cardiac silhouette; mild scoliosis of the thoracic spine.  
Pulmonary function test: Moderately decreased ventilatory function, mixed ventilatory dysfunction predominantly obstructive; positive bronchodilator test.

2. Further examinations after admission (multimodal imaging and pathology)  
Coronary angiography (CAG):  
Right coronary dominant circulation; no significant stenosis in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, or right coronary artery.  
Left ventriculography: Globally reduced left ventricular systolic function; no obvious mitral regurgitation; left ventricular end-diastolic pressure (LVEDP) 18 mmHg.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR):  
Left heart enlargement (left ventricular long-axis 59 × 93 mm); left ventricular ejection fraction 41.7%; globally reduced wall motion.  
Key finding: Mid-myocardial linear late gadolinium enhancement in the basal interventricular septum.

Ambulatory electrocardiogram (Holter):  
First-degree AVB, intermittent second-degree type I AVB; 1,633 premature ventricular contractions over 24 hours; non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (NSVT): heart rate 50–60 bpm, ventricular rate not fast during VT episodes; prolonged QT interval.

Genetic testing:  
LMNA gene: Heterozygous missense mutation c.646C>T, protein change p.Arg216Cys, classified as pathogenic according to ACMG guidelines.  
SLC2A2 gene and PCSK9 gene variants of uncertain significance.

Examinations during the second hospitalization (January 2025):  
Infection-related: Influenza A virus nucleic acid positive; CRP 22.1 mg/L; PCT 0.11 ng/mL; blood culture negative.  
Blood gas analysis: pH 7.4, PCO₂ 46.9 mmHg, PO₂ 71.7 mmHg, suggesting mild carbon dioxide retention and hypoxemia.  
Cardiac biomarkers: D-dimer 0.178 mg/L; troponin I 9.52 pg/mL; NT-proBNP 125 pg/mL.  
Urinalysis: Ketones positive.  
ECG: Sinus rhythm, first-degree AVB, low-amplitude and inverted T waves in leads V4–V6, premature ventricular contractions, prolonged QT interval.

Echocardiographic monitoring (longitudinal comparison):  
September 2024: LVEF <40%, LVEDD 60 mm.  
December 2024: LVEF 37%, LVEDD 57 mm.  
January 2025: LVEF 38%, LVEDD 55 mm.

Primary_diagnosis.Name: LMNA-related dilated cardiomyopathy; acute exacerbation of chronic heart failure; heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF)
Diagnostic_evidence: Symptoms: Chief complaint: Intermittent chest tightness and dyspnea for over 5 years, with exacerbation for 3 days
Symptoms: History of present illness: Accompanied by progressive decline in exercise tolerance, with significant dyspnea after walking 100 meters

Symptoms: Family history: Father died suddenly at age 78, mother died suddenly at age 70... Two older brothers (in their 60s) and one older sister (at age 57) underwent pacemaker implantation... The second older brother died suddenly this year at age 62
Physical examination: Cardiac percussion revealed left-sided enlargement
Physical examination: Mild bilateral lower extremity edema
Ancillary examinations: Echocardiography showed: LVEF 41%, left heart enlargement, mild mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, reduced left ventricular function
